CuratorialText:
In the early 1940s, decades into his artistic career, Henri Matisse (1869–1954) turned his focus to a new medium: cut paper. 

He had become bedridden and unable to paint following an excruciating abdominal surgery, and cut paper allowed him to continue to create in his relatively sedentary state. Encouraged by his friend, the book publisher Tériade (the pseudonym of Stratis Eleftheriades), Matisse furthered his exploration of this new technique. He mined his memories of Parisian music halls, the circus, trips to Tahiti, folktales, and mythology, and produced a series of 20 cut-paper maquettes.
persona3_adapted:
In the early 1940s, many years into his artistic career, Henri Matisse (1869–1954) began working in a new way: cutting and arranging paper.

After a painful abdominal surgery left him confined to bed and unable to paint, Matisse turned to cut paper as a way to keep making art while staying relatively still. Encouraged by his friend Tériade (the pen name of Stratis Eleftheriades), a book publisher, Matisse continued to develop this technique. He drew on his memories of Parisian music halls, the circus, trips to Tahiti, folktales, and mythology, and created a series of 20 small-scale models made from cut paper.

CuratorialText: These vibrant original compositions were then faithfully reproduced using pochoir (stencil) and combined with an original text written by Matisse to form an unbound book, Jazz, that was published in 1947—when Matisse was 77 years old. Jazz caused an immediate international sensation and reinvigorated the aging artist, setting him on a new course of artistic discovery.
persona3_adapted: These lively, original designs were carefully copied using a stencil-based printing technique called pochoir. They were paired with an original text written by Matisse to create an unbound book titled *Jazz*, published in 1947, when the artist was 77 years old. The book caused an immediate international stir and gave the aging artist a burst of creative energy, setting him on a new path of artistic exploration.

CuratorialText: Matisse’s extraordinary images of Jazz are not only captivating and provocative in their own right but also reflect a practice devoted to the exploration of color and line. Throughout the artist’s decades-long career, he pushed color from the purely visual to the nearly transcendental and progressively simplified his line to the most elemental. But until his cut-outs, his color and line existed separately, one modifying the other but never fully unified—at least not to Matisse’s own satisfaction.
persona3_adapted: Matisse’s striking images in *Jazz* are not only captivating and thought-provoking on their own, but they also reveal a lifelong artistic practice focused on the study of color and line. Over the course of his long career, Matisse moved color beyond simply what we see, toward something almost spiritual, while gradually simplifying his line work to its most basic form. However, before he created his cut-outs, color and line remained separate elements in his art—each one influencing the other, but never fully coming together in a way that satisfied Matisse himself.

CuratorialText: This exhibition marks the first time Matisse’s Jazz will be shown in its entirety at the Art Institute; the museum acquired it in 1948. One of the most important livres d’artiste (artist books) of the 20th century, Jazz joins over 50 works from the museum’s renowned collection of paintings, sculpture, drawings, prints, illustrated books, and textiles to showcase the famed artist’s commitment across his 50-year career to continual innovation and the expressive power of color and line.
persona3_adapted: This exhibition marks the first time Matisse’s *Jazz* will be shown in its entirety at the Art Institute; the museum acquired it in 1948. One of the most important artist books (a type of work where an artist creates both the images and the overall design of the book) of the 20th century, *Jazz* joins over 50 works from the museum’s renowned collection of paintings, sculpture, drawings, prints, illustrated books, and textiles. Together, they highlight the famed artist’s lifelong dedication—over a 50-year career—to constant experimentation and the emotional impact of color and line.

CuratorialText: From 6th-century gilt bronze Buddhist sculpture through Joseon dynasty painting and white porcelain and contemporary paintings of the late 20th century, the works of art in this exhibition demonstrate the artistic legacy produced on the Korean peninsula over millennia.
persona3_adapted: From a 6th-century Buddhist sculpture covered in gold leaf, to paintings and white porcelain from the Joseon dynasty, to late 20th-century contemporary paintings, the artworks in this exhibition showcase the artistic heritage created on the Korean peninsula over thousands of years.

CuratorialText: Once privately held, these artworks now belong to the Korean people thanks to a single groundbreaking gift from the family of Lee Kun-Hee, late chairman of Samsung Group. In 2021 the family donated over 23,000 works to the Korean government for public audiences to study and appreciate in perpetuity. Lee Kun-Hee and his father, Lee Byung-Chull, collected exceptional and storied objects from throughout Korean history as a means of preserving and celebrating the nation’s cultural heritage. This exhibition marks the first time the works are on public display.
persona3_adapted: Once held in a private collection, these artworks now belong to the Korean people, thanks to an extraordinary gift from the family of Lee Kun-Hee, the late chairman of Samsung Group. In 2021, the family donated more than 23,000 works to the Korean government, so that the public could study and enjoy them forever. Lee Kun-Hee and his father, Lee Byung-Chull, collected remarkable and historically significant objects from across Korean history, as a way to preserve and celebrate the nation’s cultural heritage. This exhibition is the first time these works have been shown to the public.

CuratorialText: Among the 140 artworks on view—including modern and historical painting, ceramics, and objects made for Buddhist worship and scholarly study—22 are officially recognized as National Treasures or Treasures by the Korean government. These objects are recognized as remarkable examples of their type and distinguished for their exceptional historic, artistic, and academic value.
persona3_adapted: Among the 140 artworks on display—ranging from modern and historical paintings to ceramics and objects created for Buddhist worship and scholarly study—22 have been officially designated as National Treasures or Treasures by the Korean government. This designation means these pieces are considered outstanding examples of their kind, notable for their exceptional historical, artistic, and scholarly importance.

CuratorialText: The largest Art Institute exhibition devoted to Korean art in four decades, Korean National Treasures: 2,000 Years of Art charts the ideas, values, and traditions that have shaped the country’s creative production, from the Three Kingdoms period (57 BCE–676 CE) through the 1900s, offering insights into these objects’ rich meanings across centuries.
persona3_adapted: The largest exhibition of Korean art at the Art Institute in forty years, *Korean National Treasures: 2,000 Years of Art*, traces the ideas, values, and traditions that have shaped the country's artistic output—from the Three Kingdoms period (57 BCE–676 CE) through the 1900s. It offers a window into the deep and evolving meanings these objects have held over the centuries.

CuratorialText: Epic and intricate, monumental and meticulous—the paintings of Kashmir-raised, London-based artist Raqib Shaw offer fantastical meditations on identity, transformation, and the redemptive power of beauty.
persona3_adapted: Monumental in scale and painstakingly detailed, the paintings of Raqib Shaw—an artist raised in Kashmir and now based in London—offer imaginative reflections on identity, change, and how beauty can heal or transform us.

CuratorialText: Born in Calcutta and raised in the verdant Himalayan mountains of Kashmir, Shaw draws deeply on the landscapes and memories of his early life, many of which were fractured by political upheaval. Forced to leave Kashmir as a teenager, he relocated first to New Delhi and later to London, where he studied at Central Saint Martins College of Art and Design. His work is influenced by a broad range of sources—Mughal and Persian miniatures, Renaissance altarpieces, Old Masters paintings, Japanese arts of the Momoyama period, Kashmiri and Urdu poetry, and Hindu and Western mythology—and yet his visual vocabulary and technique are all his own. His signature method involves applying an acrylic liner on gesso to create a golden line almost like the leading of a stained glass window. He then applies automobile enamel paints with needle-fine syringes and manipulates those with a porcupine quill, often adding glittering inset stones and baubles that further enhance the magical and dreamlike quality of the depicted scenes.
persona3_adapted: Born in Calcutta and raised in the lush Himalayan mountains of Kashmir, Shaw draws deeply on the landscapes and memories of his early life—many of which were disrupted by political turmoil. Forced to leave Kashmir as a teenager, he moved first to New Delhi and later to London, where he studied at Central Saint Martins College of Art and Design. His work is influenced by a wide range of sources: paintings from the Mughal and Persian courts, religious altarpieces from the Renaissance, works by European masters from earlier centuries, Japanese art from the Momoyama period, Kashmiri and Urdu poetry, and stories from Hindu and Western mythology. Yet his own visual style and techniques are entirely original. His signature method involves using a fine acrylic applicator on a prepared surface (called gesso) to create a golden line, similar to the metal strips that hold stained glass together. He then applies car paint using needle-thin syringes and shapes it with a porcupine quill, often adding small sparkling stones and decorative ornaments that heighten the dreamlike, enchanted quality of the scenes he depicts.

CuratorialText: At the Art Institute, Shaw debuts his more-than 100-feet-wide, 21-panel Paradise Lost (2009–25), the artist’s most ambitious and personal project to date. This magnificent allegorical painting takes viewers on a spellbinding journey, from the nocturnal solitude of the artist’s childhood in Kashmir to the frenzied daylight of the art world and the West to finally a fragile, renewed dawn. Each panel is dense with symbolism: mythical beasts, anthropomorphic hybrids, collapsing kingdoms, and natural beauty in various states of transformation. Throughout, the work is dotted with images of the artist, sometimes as a humanoid creature with different animal heads, at another time as a monkey looking with awe at the gleaming edifices and the wealth of the West, and sometimes unambiguously in full human form seated on a bed of saffron under a blossoming cherry tree, lost deep in his thoughts
persona3_adapted:
At the Art Institute, Shaw unveils his most ambitious and personal project to date: *Paradise Lost* (2009–25), a work over 100 feet wide and composed of 21 panels. This richly symbolic painting takes viewers on a captivating journey—from the quiet, nighttime isolation of the artist’s childhood in Kashmir, through the chaotic, brightly lit world of Western art and culture, and finally to a fragile, hopeful new dawn.

Each panel is packed with meaning: mythical creatures, beings that are part-human and part-animal, crumbling empires, and natural landscapes in the midst of change. Throughout the work, the artist appears in many forms—sometimes as a humanoid figure with different animal heads, at other times as a monkey gazing in wonder at the gleaming buildings and wealth of the West, and occasionally in clear human form, sitting on a bed of saffron beneath a blossoming cherry tree, lost in thought.

CuratorialText: The work is not a direct retelling of Milton’s 17th-century poem Paradise Lost, but rather a reflection on the many paradises lost across a lifetime: childhood innocence, creative freedom, mental tranquility, cultural belonging. “This is not just my story,” Shaw explained. “It is the story of each of us, and the story of our times.”
persona3_adapted: This work does not directly retell John Milton’s 17th-century poem *Paradise Lost*. Instead, it reflects on the many kinds of paradise we lose over a lifetime: the innocence of childhood, the freedom to create, peace of mind, and a sense of belonging to a culture. As the artist Shaw explains, “This is not just my story. It is the story of each of us, and the story of our times.”

CuratorialText: While Shaw has previously shown the first chapter of Paradise Lost, this exhibition marks the first time all four chapters will be displayed, showcasing the fullest extent yet of this epic masterpiece. “This painting was made not only to document a life,” Shaw shared, “but to offer something to those who come after, to offer a space of reflection for emotional recognition and perhaps for quiet rebellion against forgetfulness. I do hope that this work invites the viewer to slow down and to look carefully and to feel without haste.”
persona3_adapted: While Shaw has previously exhibited the first chapter of *Paradise Lost*, this is the first time all four chapters are being shown together, revealing the full scope of this ambitious, large-scale work. “This painting was created not just to record a life,” Shaw explained, “but to give something to future generations—a place for thoughtful reflection, for emotional recognition, and perhaps for a quiet resistance against being forgotten. I hope it encourages viewers to pause, to observe closely, and to feel without rushing.”

CuratorialText: Exquisitely crafted and shrouded in mystery, Saint-Porchaire ceramics are among the rarest treasures of the French Renaissance.
persona3_adapted: Exquisitely made and wrapped in mystery, Saint-Porchaire ceramics are among the rarest treasures of the French Renaissance.

CuratorialText: Only around 70 examples are known to exist worldwide, and each one dazzles and intrigues with its refined and delicate modeling and astonishingly intricate decoration. Created between about 1520 and 1570 and closely associated with the royal courts of François I and Henri II, these extraordinary works bear the emblems of power, prestige, and desire—including the interlaced cipher of Henri II and his famed mistress, Diane de Poitiers.
persona3_adapted: Only about 70 of these objects are known to exist around the world. Each one stands out for its finely detailed shaping and surprisingly complex decoration. They were made between roughly 1520 and 1570, and are closely linked to the royal courts of King François I and King Henri II of France. These remarkable pieces display symbols of power, status, and longing—including the interwoven initials of Henri II and his famous mistress, Diane de Poitiers.

CuratorialText: Saint-Porchaire ceramics embody the sophistication and inventive spirit of Renaissance court culture. Fashioned from lead-glazed earthenware, their sculptural forms echo precious metalwork, while their ornamental patterns draw inspiration from textiles, bookbinding, and other luxury arts. Together, they express a unique and daring artistry, playful elegance, and refined taste at the height of French royal patronage.
persona3_adapted: Saint-Porchaire ceramics capture the refinement and creative energy of Renaissance court life. Made from lead-glazed earthenware—a type of pottery coated with a shiny, glass-like finish—these pieces take on sculptural shapes that recall the look of fine metalwork. Their decorative patterns borrow from textiles, bookbinding, and other luxury crafts. Together, these elements reveal a bold and original artistic vision, a sense of playful elegance, and the sophisticated preferences that flourished under French royal support at its peak.

CuratorialText: Beginning in March, the Art Institute will present an exceptional group of Saint-Porchaire ceramics from a private collection—most on public view for the first time since the late 1800s—in the Deering Family Galleries of Medieval and Renaissance Art, Arms, and Armor. These rare works will be shown alongside other notable examples of French Renaissance ceramics drawn from the Art Institute’s collection, including several recent acquisitions.
persona3_adapted: Beginning in March, the Art Institute will display a remarkable collection of Saint-Porchaire ceramics—a type of fine, decorative pottery from 16th-century France—on loan from a private collection. Most of these pieces have not been seen by the public since the late 1800s. They will be shown in the Deering Family Galleries of Medieval and Renaissance Art, Arms, and Armor, alongside other outstanding examples of French Renaissance pottery from the museum’s own collection, including several pieces acquired in recent years.

CuratorialText: Installed in the intimate James W. and Marilynn Alsdorf Gallery, this focused presentation invites close encounters with these remarkable jewel-like objects, while its placement within the broader context of the Deering Family Galleries highlights the rich dialogue between ceramics and the metalwork, textiles, and decorative arts that flourished during the Renaissance.
persona3_adapted: Installed in the intimate James W. and Marilynn Alsdorf Gallery, this focused exhibition invites you to get up close with these remarkable, jewel-like objects. Its location within the larger Deering Family Galleries emphasizes the lively exchange between ceramics and the metalwork, textiles, and decorative arts that thrived during the Renaissance.

CuratorialText: Over a long and distinguished career, Japanese artist Hamanishi Katsunori (born 1949) has focused on making mezzotint prints, perhaps the most demanding of all print techniques.
persona3_adapted: Over a long and celebrated career, Japanese artist Hamanishi Katsunori (born 1949) has specialized in creating mezzotint prints—a printmaking technique widely considered the most challenging of all.

CuratorialText: Mezzotints are known for their dark and atmospheric appearance. This is because the starting point for any mezzotint is the creation of a roughened surface, which produces a solid black background when printed. To draw the image that emerges from this dark background, the artist uses a series of burnishers and scrapers; the deepest gouges print as white areas on the finished print.
persona3_adapted: Mezzotints are known for their dark, moody look. This effect comes from the fact that the artist begins by roughening the entire surface of the printing plate, so that when ink is applied and the plate is pressed onto paper, the result is a deep, solid black background. To create the image that emerges from this darkness, the artist uses tools called burnishers and scrapers—smoothing or scraping away parts of the roughened surface. The deepest scratches or gouges leave no ink at all, so those areas print as white on the final image.

CuratorialText: This presentation includes earlier, smaller-format works done without color, as well as more recent larger work that boast many hues. Among the earlier monochromatic prints is Hamanishi’s 1997 Viva Chicago series, which focuses on the city’s beloved public monuments like the Picasso and Miró sculptures. As the artist recalled, “When I first came here, I was unprepared for the impact this city would leave on my mind, such a vivid impression etched deep in my memory… . This is my ode to Chicago.”
persona3_adapted: This selection includes both earlier, smaller works created without color, and more recent, larger pieces that feature a wide range of colors. Among the earlier single-color prints is Hamanishi’s 1997 *Viva Chicago* series, which highlights the city’s cherished public monuments, such as the sculptures by Picasso and Miró. As the artist recalled, “When I first came here, I was unprepared for the impact this city would leave on my mind, such a vivid impression etched deep in my memory… . This is my ode to Chicago.”

CuratorialText: The exhibition also celebrates several important gifts to the museum. Hamanishi’s 2022 Four Seasons series—shown here for the first time—is a recent gift from the artist. Each work in the series is reminiscent of the panels of a folding screen and features seasonal floral imagery. Many works in the show come from the 2013 gift to the museum from the Ninion and Sheldon Landy Collection, which gave the Art Institute the largest collection of Hamanishi’s prints in the world.
persona3_adapted: The exhibition also highlights several major donations to the museum. Hamanishi’s 2022 Four Seasons series—on display here for the first time—was recently given by the artist himself. Each piece in the series resembles the panels of a folding screen and depicts flowers associated with different seasons. Many of the works in the show come from a 2013 donation by the Ninion and Sheldon Landy Collection, which gave the Art Institute the world’s largest collection of Hamanishi’s prints.

CuratorialText:
“There is no work by Carroll Dunham that does not use drawing as a foundation.”  
—Carroll Dunham. Over the course of five decades, Dunham has engaged in wide-ranging formal and thematic experimentation across various media, yet his drawings represent a distinct, interconnected body of work. Dunham’s mature artistic career began in 1970s New York amid a scene dominated by Minimalist aesthetics. Using simple elements such as line, shape, and color, he made a tremendous impact with work that nevertheless signaled a return to subject matter. In the intervening decades, Dunham has continued to produce drawings that test, and ultimately collapse, the porous boundaries between abstraction and figuration: bodies shapeshift into buildings, trees pose like models, the cellular resembles the cosmic, and internal organs or private parts are made very public.
persona3_adapted:
“There is no work by Carroll Dunham that does not use drawing as a foundation.”  
—Carroll Dunham. Over five decades, Dunham has experimented widely with different forms and themes across many types of art, yet his drawings form a distinct and connected body of work. Dunham’s mature artistic career began in 1970s New York, during a time when the art world was heavily focused on Minimalism—an approach that emphasizes simple, stripped-down forms and materials. Using basic elements like line, shape, and color, he made a powerful impact with work that nonetheless brought back a focus on recognizable subjects. In the years since, Dunham has continued to create drawings that test, and ultimately break down, the blurry line between abstract and realistic imagery: bodies shift into buildings, trees strike poses like models, tiny cellular forms resemble vast cosmic scenes, and internal organs or private parts are made very public.

CuratorialText: Working in series, Dunham methodically tests out every possible outcome of a drawing’s composition or content. This approach seems to spontaneously yield new imagery and ideas that link one body of work to the next. His influences are likewise wide-ranging—art history, philosophy, psychoanalysis, and evolutionary science as well as science fiction and comic books—all of which inform his explorations of the tension between male and female, nature and culture, self and other.
persona3_adapted: Working in a series, Dunham carefully explores every possible variation in a drawing’s layout or subject matter. This method appears to naturally generate fresh images and ideas that connect one group of works to the next. His inspirations are similarly broad—ranging from art history, philosophy, psychoanalysis, and evolutionary science to science fiction and comic books—all of which shape his investigations into the conflict between male and female, nature and culture, and self and other.

CuratorialText: Dunham foregrounds the development of his work by precisely dating each drawing. This practice is evidenced in the thousands of drawings he has organized into an archive, from which this show is derived. Developed in conversation with the artist, this comprehensive survey is the first museum presentation focused exclusively on Dunham’s drawings. Encompassing a 50-year period and featuring many drawings making their public debut, it celebrates the medium central to Dunham’s expansive practice.
persona3_adapted: Dunham highlights the evolution of his work by carefully dating each drawing. This habit is clear in the thousands of drawings he has organized into a personal collection, from which this exhibition is drawn. Created in close collaboration with the artist, this broad overview is the first museum show focused solely on Dunham’s drawings. Spanning 50 years and including many works shown publicly for the first time, it honors the artistic method at the heart of Dunham’s wide-ranging creative practice.

CuratorialText: In fall of 2024, the Art Institute unveiled a newly imagined and installed gallery for the arts of Korea—our first space fully dedicated to this cultural region.
persona3_adapted: In fall of 2024, the Art Institute opened a newly designed and arranged gallery for Korean art—our first space entirely devoted to this cultural tradition.

CuratorialText: The new installation presents a wider range of objects than previous displays—extending from ceramics to textiles and painting and spanning 2,000 years from the Three Kingdoms period (about 57 BCE–676 CE) until today. Intentionally located between the Chinese and Japanese art galleries, the thoughtful display offers insight into how these artworks both reflect the religious and material culture of Korea and how they relate to the arts of Korea’s influential neighbors, China and Japan.
persona3_adapted: This new installation includes a wider variety of objects than previous displays—ranging from ceramics to textiles and painting, and covering 2,000 years from the Three Kingdoms period (about 57 BCE–676 CE) to the present day. The gallery is intentionally placed between the Chinese and Japanese art galleries, and the thoughtful arrangement helps visitors understand how these artworks reflect Korea’s religious and material culture, as well as how they relate to the arts of Korea’s influential neighbors, China and Japan.

CuratorialText: Six themes introduce Korea’s long and rich history as well as its religious, political, and material cultures. The first section introduces objects that were made to help understand and spread the teachings of the Buddha. The centerpiece, an 18th-century Buddha statue, is on view for the first time since its acquisition after extensive conservation treatment. A section devoted to celadon and tea culture provides a glimpse into the practice of tea ceremonies during the Goryeo dynasty (918–1392), and objects grouped around the idea of symbolism illustrate how beautiful embellishments represent desired outcomes, such as a blissful marriage, career advancement, and long life.
persona3_adapted: Six themes guide visitors through Korea’s long and rich history, including its religious, political, and material cultures. The first section features objects created to help people understand and share the teachings of the Buddha. The centerpiece is an 18th-century statue of the Buddha, on display for the first time since the museum acquired it and completed a careful conservation process. Another section focuses on celadon—a type of Korean pottery with a distinctive greenish-blue glaze—and tea culture, offering a window into the tea ceremonies practiced during the Goryeo dynasty (918–1392). Objects grouped around the idea of symbolism show how beautiful decorations were used to represent hopes for a happy marriage, career success, and a long life.

CuratorialText: The remaining three sections focus on how material culture responded to a major ideological, political, and cultural shift during the Joseon dynasty (1392–1897) as evidenced in the creation of the earthy grey stoneware known as buncheong, elegant white porcelain, and scholars’ objects. The new installation also includes select contemporary works that meaningfully engage with traditional forms and materials.
persona3_adapted: The final three sections explore how everyday and artistic objects reflected a major shift in ideas, politics, and culture during the Joseon dynasty (1392–1897). This change is seen in the creation of three types of items: a rough, grayish stoneware called *buncheong*; refined white porcelain; and objects used by scholars. The new display also includes a few modern works that thoughtfully connect with these traditional forms and materials.

CuratorialText: The transformed space explores aspects of life and the afterlife in the Nile Valley with the first new installation of works from the museum’s historic collection of ancient Egyptian art in a quarter-century. Striking artifacts—displayed along one wall of the gallery in a series of innovative cases that promote viewing from multiple vantage points—provide insight into the beliefs and practices of this illustrious North African culture.
persona3_adapted: This reimagined gallery space invites visitors to explore life and the afterlife in the Nile Valley, featuring the first major reinstallation of works from the museum’s historic collection of ancient Egyptian art in twenty-five years. Remarkable objects—displayed along one wall in a series of innovative cases designed to be viewed from multiple angles—offer a window into the beliefs and customs of this celebrated North African civilization.

CuratorialText: Recurring themes throughout this fresh presentation of the collection consider the impact of Egypt’s natural environment, including the Nile River, on its visual culture; reveal the processes of ancient Egyptian artists; and explore the centrality of gods and goddesses to life (and death) along the Nile. Arresting sculptures, such as the statue of Shebenhor, reveal how ancient Egyptians chose to present themselves so that they would be remembered for eternity, while funerary works—such as a gilded funerary mask—unveil practices for preparing for and protecting oneself in the afterlife.
persona3_adapted: This fresh look at the museum’s collection returns to a few key ideas again and again. One is how Egypt’s natural surroundings—especially the Nile River—shaped the art and objects people made. Another is the way ancient Egyptian artists worked. And a third is how central gods and goddesses were to life (and death) along the Nile. Powerful sculptures, like the statue of Shebenhor, show us how ancient Egyptians chose to present themselves so they would be remembered forever. Meanwhile, objects made for burial—such as a mask covered in gold leaf—reveal the rituals people used to prepare for and protect themselves in the afterlife.

CuratorialText: For the first time in over 30 years, the Art Institute of Chicago unveils an elegant and thought-provoking new display of architectural fragments from buildings in and around Chicago. Since the 19th century, Chicago’s urban landscape has experienced continuous change. Over time, many  transformations of the city’s streets and neighborhoods have become invisible, preserved only in collective memory and archival photographs. Architectural fragments provide another avenue to understanding our urban past, including historical building practices and technologies, our dynamic communities, and evolving ways of life.
persona3_adapted: For the first time in more than 30 years, the Art Institute of Chicago presents a refined and thought-provoking new exhibition featuring architectural pieces—such as sections of buildings, decorative details, or structural elements—taken from structures in and around Chicago. Since the 1800s, Chicago’s cityscape has been constantly evolving. Over the decades, many changes to the city’s streets and neighborhoods have faded from view, surviving only in shared memory and old photographs. These architectural fragments offer another way to connect with our urban history, revealing historical construction methods and technologies, the character of our changing communities, and the shifting patterns of daily life.

CuratorialText: In this new installation, sculptural elements from building facades and interiors are displayed in groups that allow visitors to learn about many architecturally or culturally significant structures and moments in Chicago. These range from celebrated buildings by well-known architects like Frank Lloyd Wright and Louis Sullivan to important local histories related to the South Side neighborhood of Bronzeville, labor, and preservation.
persona3_adapted: In this new installation, three-dimensional pieces taken from the outsides and insides of buildings are arranged in groups. This setup helps visitors explore many buildings and moments that are important to Chicago’s architecture or culture. The pieces range from famous structures designed by well-known architects like Frank Lloyd Wright and Louis Sullivan to significant local stories connected to the South Side neighborhood of Bronzeville, as well as themes of labor and preservation.

CuratorialText: Featuring well-loved works from the permanent collection alongside important new acquisitions—such as a stained-glass window by Marion Mahony Griffin and a terracotta bust of an Egyptian pharaoh by Walter Bailey—this installation, designed by the Chicago-based practice Norman Kelley, offers dynamic encounters with the museum’s rich holdings of architectural fragments, an integral part of our shared material history.
persona3_adapted: Featuring beloved pieces from the museum’s permanent collection alongside significant recent additions—including a stained-glass window by Marion Mahony Griffin and a terracotta bust of an Egyptian pharaoh by Walter Bailey—this exhibition, designed by the Chicago-based firm Norman Kelley, creates lively opportunities to engage with the museum’s extensive collection of architectural fragments. These fragments are an essential part of our shared history of physical objects and built environments.

CuratorialText: In the textiles of Southwest Asia and North Africa, embroidery is more than surface decoration. In this region, known as SWANA, artists have used, and continue to use, centuries-old embellishment techniques to transmit cultural expressions of identity, status, and long-held belief systems. Cultural groups throughout the region wear layers of dress and adornment to protect their bodies physically and spiritually. Garments and household items are ornamented with valuable materials that function as wearable wealth, including symbolic motifs and designs constructed with coins, thread, beads, amulets, ornaments, and jewelry.
persona3_adapted: In the textiles of Southwest Asia and North Africa, embroidery is much more than just surface decoration. In this region, often called SWANA, artists have used—and still use—traditional stitching and embellishment methods that are centuries old. Through these techniques, they express cultural ideas about identity, social standing, and long-held beliefs. Across different cultural groups in the region, people wear multiple layers of clothing and accessories to protect themselves, both physically and spiritually. Garments and household items are decorated with valuable materials that serve as a kind of portable wealth. These include symbolic patterns and designs made from coins, thread, beads, protective charms, ornaments, and jewelry.

CuratorialText: The vast area that spans from Morocco and Algeria in the west to Afghanistan and Uzbekistan in the east is connected by millennia-old trade networks. The works presented reveal pathways of deep cultural exchange shaped by diverse landscapes—from mountains and deserts to seas—along with the movement of nomadic peoples and the spread and interweaving of multiple religious traditions and urban fashions.
persona3_adapted: The vast region stretching from Morocco and Algeria in the west to Afghanistan and Uzbekistan in the east has been linked for thousands of years by trade routes. The artworks on display here show how these connections led to deep cultural sharing, influenced by a variety of landscapes—including mountains, deserts, and seas—as well as the travels of nomadic groups, the spread and blending of different religious traditions, and changing styles in city life.

CuratorialText: Embroidered Traditions from Morocco to Afghanistan is the first exhibition at the Art Institute of Chicago devoted exclusively to the artistic and cultural traditions of SWANA and spectacularly showcases the region’s distinctive beauty and iconic visual expressions. Drawn primarily from the Art Institute’s rich collection of textiles, jewelry, and adornment, this display presents over 70 works from the 18th through 20th century—most of which have never been exhibited publicly—including four rare face veils on loan from a private collector based in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.
persona3_adapted: **Embroidered Traditions from Morocco to Afghanistan** is the first exhibition at the Art Institute of Chicago to focus entirely on the artistic and cultural traditions of the SWANA region—an acronym for Southwest Asia and North Africa. The show highlights the area’s distinctive beauty and iconic visual styles, drawing primarily from the museum’s own extensive collection of textiles, jewelry, and adornment. It features more than 70 works dating from the 1700s to the 1900s, most of which have never been shown to the public before. Among these are four rare face veils on loan from a private collector based in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

CuratorialText: Among the many other highlights are elaborately decorated garments from Syria, Yemen, Niger, and Egypt; silver and coral “treasure necklaces” from Morocco’s Anti-Atlas Mountains; richly embroidered linen panels from the Art Institute’s exceptional collection of Ottoman Turkish textiles; and a wedding blanket embroidered by the Marsh Arab (Ahwari) peoples of Iraq.
persona3_adapted: Among the many other highlights are elaborately decorated garments from Syria, Yemen, Niger, and Egypt; silver and coral “treasure necklaces” from Morocco’s Anti-Atlas Mountains; richly embroidered linen panels from the Art Institute’s exceptional collection of textiles from the Ottoman Empire (a Turkish-ruled empire that lasted from the 1300s to the early 1900s); and a wedding blanket embroidered by the Marsh Arab (Ahwari) peoples of Iraq.

CuratorialText: Barbara Hammer’s experimental film Sanctus depicts human bodies at risk. It features archival moving X-rays taken by doctor and filmmaker James Sibley Watson Jr. (1894–1982) of people making simple gestures and engaging in everyday activities: applying lipstick, drinking liquids, shaking hands, and shaving. Hammer rephotographed the original film and spliced the images together in a visceral montage set to overwhelming requiems by Neil B. Rolnick. She further transformed the X-rays with flashes of light, hand-painted colors, and other visual effects and by speeding up, looping, and reversing the movements. In presenting images captured by medical technology that uses large amounts of radiation, Hammer underscored her idea that “science advances through the demise of animal, including human, subjects in experiments” and that imaging, and even treatment, can cause harm.
persona3_adapted: Barbara Hammer’s experimental film *Sanctus* shows human bodies in vulnerable states. The film uses archival moving X-rays taken by doctor and filmmaker James Sibley Watson Jr. (1894–1982), which capture people performing simple gestures and everyday actions: applying lipstick, drinking, shaking hands, and shaving. Hammer re-photographed the original footage and edited the images together in a powerful sequence set to overwhelming musical pieces (requiems) by Neil B. Rolnick. She further altered the X-rays with flashes of light, hand-painted colors, and other visual effects, as well as by speeding up, looping, and reversing the movements. By presenting images created by medical technology that uses large amounts of radiation, Hammer emphasized her view that “science advances through the demise of animal, including human, subjects in experiments” and that imaging—and even treatment—can cause harm.

CuratorialText: Sanctus emerged from Hammer’s initial engagement with medical themes, an interest incited in the early 1980s by the AIDS epidemic. Over the course of her 50-year career, Hammer worked in photography, 16mm film, and video with an unflinching focus on queer communities and on her encounters within the American medical system.
persona3_adapted: Sanctus grew out of Hammer’s early interest in medical subjects, sparked in the 1980s by the AIDS crisis. Over her 50-year career, Hammer worked in photography, 16mm film, and video, consistently focusing on queer communities and her own experiences within the American healthcare system.

CuratorialText: Step back in time and immerse yourself in the spiritual, domestic, and chivalric worlds of the Middle Ages and Renaissance. The Deering Family Galleries of Medieval and Renaissance Art, Arms, and Armor present nearly 700 objects from the museum’s rich holdings of art from 1200 to 1600: monumental altarpieces, exquisite jewelry, and the beloved arms and armor collection.
persona3_adapted: Step back in time and explore the spiritual, home, and knightly worlds of the Middle Ages and Renaissance. The Deering Family Galleries of Medieval and Renaissance Art, Arms, and Armor showcase nearly 700 objects from the museum’s extensive collection of art created between 1200 and 1600: large-scale religious paintings for altars, finely crafted jewelry, and the popular collection of weapons and armor.

CuratorialText: The sumptuous display begins with a large room featuring architectural sculpture, treasury objects, and early painting that convey the austere sanctity of earlier medieval art. The centerpiece of the gallery is the meticulously conserved Ayala Altarpiece, commissioned in 1396 by the chancellor of Castile for his family’s funerary chapel in northern Spain. Spanning nearly 24 feet across and eight feet high, the painted wood altarpiece has undergone extensive treatment over the last three years and makes its restored debut in brilliant form. Two linked vaulted galleries showcase altarpieces and altarpiece fragments from Italy, Spain, and northern Europe, including the Art Institute favorite Saint George and the Dragon by Bernat Martorell. Each is displayed to suggest its original religious space, highlighting the church as a center of public life.
persona3_adapted: The exhibition opens dramatically with a large room filled with architectural carvings, precious objects from church treasuries, and early paintings—all of which reflect the solemn, sacred quality of medieval art from its earlier centuries. The focal point of this gallery is the carefully restored Ayala Altarpiece, a large painted wooden structure commissioned in 1396 by the chancellor of Castile for his family’s burial chapel in northern Spain. Measuring nearly 24 feet wide and eight feet tall, this altarpiece has undergone extensive conservation work over the past three years and is now being shown in brilliant condition for the first time since its restoration. Two connected, vaulted rooms display altarpieces and fragments of altarpieces from Italy, Spain, and northern Europe, including the Art Institute’s beloved painting *Saint George and the Dragon* by Bernat Martorell. Each piece is arranged to evoke the religious setting it originally came from, emphasizing the church as a central hub of community life.

CuratorialText: The spaces that follow are more intimate, focusing on the texture of late Gothic and Renaissance life. One gallery presents luxury goods and the accessories of feasting, while another displays works of art for the bedchambers of Tuscany’s merchant elite. Everyday objects from northern Europe, along with jewelry and items of personal display, complete the domestic picture of the period.
persona3_adapted: The rooms ahead feel more personal, offering a closer look at the textures of daily life during the late Gothic and Renaissance periods. One gallery features luxury items and the tools used for feasting, while another showcases artworks made for the bedrooms of Tuscany’s wealthy merchant class. Everyday objects from northern Europe, together with jewelry and personal accessories, round out the domestic scene of the era.

CuratorialText: An elegant rotunda introduces the collection of arms and armor with equipment for the bodyguards of four rulers. From here, the space opens to a gallery filled with weaponry and armor dominated by two armored figures on horseback—one dressed for battle, the other for sport—and two armed and costumed figures engaged in foot combat. Finally, a gallery lined with cabinetry features the art of the sword, luxury firearms, and equipment for the hunt extending through the Baroque era.
persona3_adapted: An elegant, circular entry hall sets the stage for the collection of arms and armor, featuring equipment once used by the personal bodyguards of four different rulers. From this starting point, the space opens into a gallery filled with weapons and armor, where two striking figures on horseback immediately draw the eye—one outfitted for actual battle, the other for sport. Nearby, two more figures, dressed in costume and armed, are posed as if locked in combat on foot. The final gallery, lined with display cabinets, showcases the artistry of swords, luxury firearms, and hunting equipment, all extending through the Baroque period (a style known for its ornate, dramatic flair, roughly from the early 1600s to the mid-1700s).

CuratorialText: The new installation expands the display of art of this fascinating period sixfold, premiering several recently conserved works and many significant new acquisitions—an elegant late Gothic breastplate and backplate, a rare 15th-century German portrait, and an outstanding group of 17th- and 18th-century swords. Subtle changes in the architecture of the galleries propel visitors through time and from religious to secular works, while digital labels provide an interactive multimedia experience. At the heart of this display are the gifts of major collectors whose donations serve as the collection’s foundation: the Ayala Altarpiece and Saint George and the Dragon from Charles Deering, Sienese and Florentine gold-ground paintings and early Northern works given by Martin Ryerson, a terracotta altarpiece by Florentine Benedetto Buglioni and a South German carved and painted Saint Catherine from Kate Buckingham, and George F. Harding’s impressive collection of arms and armor. All come together in the new Deering Family Galleries, offering a transporting experience to a time that continues to enchant.
persona3_adapted: The newly expanded installation showcases six times more art from this captivating period, featuring several recently preserved works and many important new additions—an elegant late Gothic chest and back plate for armor, a rare 15th-century German portrait, and an outstanding collection of 17th- and 18th-century swords. Subtle changes in the gallery layout guide visitors through time, moving from religious to everyday objects, while digital labels offer an interactive multimedia experience. At the heart of this display are the gifts of major collectors whose donations form the foundation of the collection: the Ayala Altarpiece and Saint George and the Dragon from Charles Deering; paintings with gold backgrounds from Siena and Florence, along with early Northern European works, given by Martin Ryerson; a clay altarpiece by Florentine artist Benedetto Buglioni and a painted wooden sculpture of Saint Catherine from southern Germany, donated by Kate Buckingham; and George F. Harding’s impressive collection of weapons and armor. All come together in the new Deering Family Galleries, offering a transporting experience to a time that continues to fascinate.

CuratorialText: Jill Mulleady paints by breathing new life into unseen histories. Drawing from art-historical, cinematic, and literary sources, her memorable images can haunt the viewer long after encountering them. This exhibition—Mulleady’s first solo show at a major museum—brings together approximately 20 paintings by the artist.
persona3_adapted: Jill Mulleady creates paintings by reviving overlooked stories from the past. She draws inspiration from art history, film, and literature, and her striking images can linger in the viewer’s mind long after seeing them. This exhibition—Mulleady’s first solo show at a major museum—brings together about 20 of her paintings.

CuratorialText: Mulleady uses various methods including staining, and at times incorporating materials such as copper, to achieve fragile, luminous surfaces that feel impossibly thin yet visually deep, holding tension between flatness and depth. Not limited to canvas or linen, she employs a range of supports, including glass and velvet. The resultant works balance different degrees of transparency and opacity and can be looked at and, sometimes, seen through.
persona3_adapted: Mulleady works with a range of techniques—such as staining her materials, and occasionally adding elements like copper—to create surfaces that are delicate and glowing. These surfaces feel almost impossibly thin, yet they give the impression of great visual depth, balancing a sense of flatness with an illusion of space. She doesn't limit herself to traditional painting surfaces like canvas or linen; she also uses materials such as glass and velvet. The finished pieces play with varying levels of transparency and opacity, inviting the viewer to look at them and, in some cases, to see through them.

CuratorialText: Mulleady’s paintings often depict scenes that are enigmatic and dangerous, showing figures at risk or under threat—from appetite, predation, or compulsion. While these ambiguous scenes may imply narratives, they likewise refuse tidy resolutions. Her imagery draws actively on painting lineages—allegory and the Baroque, Symbolist unease, the grotesque—recasting genres and canonical images for the present. In contrast to the purity and rationality of 20th-century modernism, Mulleady prioritizes a tradition in which chaos, instinct, and history drive art making.
persona3_adapted: Mulleady’s paintings often show scenes that feel mysterious and dangerous, with figures who appear to be in danger—whether from hunger, being hunted, or some uncontrollable urge. Although these unclear scenes might hint at a story, they also resist neat or simple endings. Her imagery draws on traditions from art history—such as works that use symbolic storytelling (allegory), the dramatic style of the 17th-century Baroque period, the unsettling mood of Symbolist art, and the exaggerated, distorted forms of the grotesque—reworking these older genres and famous images for today. In contrast to the clean, orderly ideals of 20th-century modernism, Mulleady favors a tradition where chaos, instinct, and history are the driving forces behind making art.

CuratorialText: As Mulleady has said, she works with existing languages in order to invent a new one, and her staged tableaux are particularly resonant within the Art Institute galleries, as they rhyme with many works in the collection that have informed the artist.
persona3_adapted: Mulleady has said that she works with existing visual languages—the familiar styles and symbols of art history—in order to invent a new one. Her carefully arranged scenes, or tableaux, feel especially meaningful within the Art Institute galleries, because they echo or "rhyme with" many works in the collection that have influenced her.

CuratorialText: After decades as a representational painter, in her seventies Alma Thomas turned to abstraction, creating shimmering, mosaic-like fields of color with rhythmic dabs of paint that were often inspired by forms from nature. The artist had been fascinated with space exploration since the late 1960s, and her later paintings often referenced America’s manned Apollo missions to the moon. Although she had never flown, Thomas began to paint as if she were in an airplane, capturing what she described as shifting patterns of light and streaks of color. “You look down on things,” she explained. “You streak through the clouds so fast… . You see only streaks of color.”
persona3_adapted: After spending many years painting recognizable subjects—people, places, and objects—Alma Thomas shifted, in her seventies, to a style focused on color and form rather than direct representation. In this later work, she built up shimmering, mosaic-like surfaces using rhythmic dabs of paint, often inspired by shapes found in nature. Thomas had been captivated by space exploration since the late 1960s, and her later paintings frequently reference America’s manned Apollo missions to the moon. Though she had never flown herself, she began to paint as if she were looking down from an airplane, capturing what she described as shifting patterns of light and streaks of color. “You look down on things,” she explained. “You streak through the clouds so fast… . You see only streaks of color.”

CuratorialText: Starry Night and the Astronauts evokes the open expanse and celestial patterns of a night sky, but despite its narrative title, the work could also be read as an aerial view of a watery surface, playing with our sense of immersion within an otherwise flat picture plane. The viewer is immersed not only in the sense of organic expanse that this painting achieves, however, but also in an encounter with Thomas’s process: the surface here is clearly constructed stroke by stroke. Meanwhile, the glimpses of raw canvas between each primary-colored mark seem as vivid as the applied paint itself—almost as if the composition were backlit. Thomas relied on the enlivening properties of color throughout her late-blooming career. “Color is life,” she once proclaimed, “and light is the mother of color.” This painting was created in 1972, when the artist was eighty. In the same year, she became the first African American woman to receive a solo exhibition at a major art museum, the Whitney Museum of American Art in New York City.
persona3_adapted: Starry Night and the Astronauts evokes the vast, open feeling and starry patterns of a night sky. But despite its story-like title, the work could also be seen as an aerial view of a watery surface, playing with our sense of being drawn into a scene that otherwise stays flat on the canvas. The viewer is immersed not only in the sense of organic expanse this painting achieves, but also in an encounter with Thomas’s process: the surface here is clearly built up stroke by stroke. Meanwhile, the glimpses of raw canvas between each brightly colored mark seem as vivid as the paint itself—almost as if the composition were lit from behind. Thomas relied on the life-giving qualities of color throughout her career, which blossomed later in life. “Color is life,” she once said, “and light is the mother of color.” This painting was created in 1972, when the artist was eighty years old. That same year, she became the first African American woman to have a solo exhibition at a major art museum, the Whitney Museum of American Art in New York City.

CuratorialText: Perhaps the most famous depiction of a bedroom in Western art history, this vibrant painting documents Vincent van Gogh’s sleeping quarters in his beloved “Yellow House” in Arles, France. The composition exists in three versions, the first of which Van Gogh conceived in October 1888, a month after he moved into the home. In a letter to his brother Theo, he described having painted “the walls pale lilac, the floor in a broken and faded red, the chairs and the bed chrome yellow, the pillows and the sheet very pale lemon green, the bedspread blood-red, the dressing table orange, the washbasin blue, the window green.” With its bold colors, thick and broken brushwork, and sharply receding lines, the picture might suggest a nervous energy. But the artist understood it as a calming and restful image.
persona3_adapted: Perhaps the most famous image of a bedroom in Western art, this vivid painting shows the room where Vincent van Gogh slept in his beloved “Yellow House” in Arles, France. There are three versions of this composition. Van Gogh first imagined it in October 1888, a month after moving into the house. In a letter to his brother Theo, he described painting “the walls pale lilac, the floor in a broken and faded red, the chairs and the bed chrome yellow, the pillows and the sheet very pale lemon green, the bedspread blood-red, the dressing table orange, the washbasin blue, the window green.” With its strong colors, thick and uneven brushstrokes, and lines that seem to tilt sharply inward, the picture might suggest a restless energy. But the artist saw it as a calm and soothing scene.

CuratorialText: The painting in the Art Institute’s collection is Van Gogh’s second version of the scene, made nearly a year after the first, in September 1889. He produced a third, smaller version at the same time as a gift for his mother and sister.
persona3_adapted: This painting in the Art Institute’s collection is Van Gogh’s second version of this scene, created nearly a year after the first, in September 1889. At the same time, he made a third, smaller version as a gift for his mother and sister.

CuratorialText: This meditating Buddha comes from the coastal town of Nagapattinam in southern India, which was, as a result of settlers from Srivijaya (Indonesia), one of the few places where Buddhism was still flourishing in the twelfth century. The Buddha—with his elongated earlobes, the wheel marks on his palms, the urna between his brows, and the cranial protuberance covered with snail-shell curls—is seated in the posture of meditation, with his hands resting on his lap (dhyanamudra), wearing a seemingly diaphanous monastic garment. As in other images from Nagapattinam, a flame emerges out of the Buddha’s cranial protuberance, probably signifying wisdom. This monumental granite sculpture originally would have graced a monastic site at Nagapattinam, which is also well known for its Buddhist bronzes. The Tamil inscription covering its back is no longer legible.
persona3_adapted: This meditating Buddha comes from the coastal town of Nagapattinam in southern India. Because of settlers from Srivijaya (in present-day Indonesia), Nagapattinam was one of the few places where Buddhism was still thriving in the twelfth century. The Buddha has several traditional features: elongated earlobes, wheel-shaped marks on his palms, a small dot (often called an *urna*) between his eyebrows, and a bump on his head covered with spiral curls that resemble snail shells. He sits in the classic meditation pose, with his hands resting in his lap, and wears a monastic robe that appears almost transparent. As in other sculptures from Nagapattinam, a flame rises from the bump on his head, likely symbolizing wisdom. This large granite statue originally would have stood in a monastery at Nagapattinam, a site also famous for its Buddhist bronze works. The Tamil inscription on its back is no longer readable.

CuratorialText: Many Mansions is the first in Kerry James Marshall’s series of five large-scale paintings depicting public housing projects in Chicago and Los Angeles such as Rockwell Gardens, Wentworth Gardens, or, as in Many Mansions, Stateway Gardens. Struck by the absurdity of the term “garden” to describe these failed solutions to low-income housing, Marshall was inspired to represent the profound contradictions of living in such an environment. Many Mansions is filled with ironic and startling juxtapositions of the real and artificial—from the unnaturally cheerful landscape to the three haunting, ebony-skinned figures dressed in nostalgic Sunday best. The title of the painting, visible on the red ribbon above, is a variation on Christ’s oft-quoted remark found in John [14:2]: “In my Father’s house there are many mansions.”
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

*Many Mansions* is the first painting in Kerry James Marshall’s series of five large works that depict public housing projects in Chicago and Los Angeles—places like Rockwell Gardens, Wentworth Gardens, and, in this case, Stateway Gardens. Marshall was struck by the strangeness of calling these failed attempts at low-income housing “gardens,” and this irony inspired him to explore the deep contradictions of living in such an environment. The painting is filled with surprising and unsettling contrasts between what is real and what is artificial—from the unnaturally cheerful landscape to the three haunting figures with deep black skin, dressed in nostalgic Sunday best. The title, which appears on the red ribbon above, is a variation on a well-known line from the Bible, in John 14:2: “In my Father’s house there are many mansions.”

CuratorialText: In Ferris Bueller’s Day Off, Ferris’s best friend Cameron Frye intensely studies this nearly ten-foot-wide painting during a scene set at the Art Institute. Featuring people of every age and social class on the banks of the River Seine, A Sunday on La Grande Jatte—1884 has captivated visitors ever since its arrival at the museum in 1924.
persona3_adapted: In *Ferris Bueller’s Day Off*, Ferris’s best friend, Cameron Frye, stares intently at this nearly ten-foot-wide painting during a scene filmed at the Art Institute. The artwork shows people of all ages and social classes gathered along the banks of the River Seine. Since it arrived at the museum in 1924, *A Sunday on La Grande Jatte—1884* has fascinated visitors with its detailed, almost mosaic-like style—created by the artist using thousands of tiny dots of color, a technique that makes the scene feel both lively and still.

CuratorialText: If we, like Cameron, come closer to the painting, figures and forms dissolve into dots and dashes of complementary colors laid side by side, characteristic of Seurat’s pointillist technique. Many smaller painted and drawn sketches and several larger canvases, in which Seurat laid out the parameters for the landscape and figures, led up to this majestically composed scene. Seurat returned to the work two years after its start date, amplifying the silhouettes of some figures and adding others. Some of these, like the monkey on the leash, seem so integral to the final composition that it is hard to imagine them as add-ons, but others, like the man carrying a rolled newspaper in the furthest distance, are barely noticeable. To the artist, however, every decision was essential to his aim of making a painting of modern life equivalent to a classical Greek frieze.
persona3_adapted: If we, like Cameron, move in close to the painting, the figures and forms break apart into small dots and dashes of contrasting colors placed side by side. This is the hallmark of Seurat’s technique, known as pointillism, where the eye blends these tiny marks into a complete image. Before arriving at this grand, carefully arranged scene, Seurat created many smaller painted and drawn studies, as well as several larger canvases, to establish the setting and the people within it. Two years after he began the work, he returned to it, making some figures’ outlines more pronounced and adding new ones. Some of these additions—like the monkey on a leash—feel so essential to the final image that it’s hard to believe they weren’t there from the start. Others, such as the man carrying a rolled newspaper far in the background, are easy to overlook. Yet for Seurat, every choice mattered. His goal was to create a painting of modern life that carried the same weight and dignity as a classical Greek frieze.

CuratorialText: Laboring at a backstrap loom in this painting is Luz Jiménez, a master weaver and Nahua, one of the largest Indigenous groups in Mesoamerica. With expertise and dexterity, Jiménez threads weft through warp, slowly building her intricately patterned textile, the completed portion resting on her lap. Diego Rivera, a leading modernist painter who came to prominence in the years after the Mexican Revolution (1910–20), promoted a vision of Mexican national identity rooted in Indigenous and folk cultures, distinct from the legacies of Spanish colonialism. By centering Jiménez in Weaving, Rivera claimed her traditions as part of his own.
persona3_adapted: At a backstrap loom in this painting, Luz Jiménez works as both a master weaver and a member of the Nahua people, one of the largest Indigenous groups in Mesoamerica. With skill and steady hands, she guides the weft thread through the warp, slowly building a textile with an intricate pattern. The finished part of the cloth rests in her lap. Diego Rivera, a leading modernist painter who rose to fame in the years after the Mexican Revolution (1910–20), promoted a vision of Mexican national identity rooted in Indigenous and folk traditions—distinct from the legacy of Spanish colonialism. By placing Jiménez at the center of *Weaving*, Rivera claimed her traditions as part of his own.

CuratorialText: This monumental painting is Gustave Caillebotte’s largest, most ambitious work and has been a visitor favorite since the Art Institute acquired it in 1964, well before the artist’s contributions to the Impressionist movement were widely recognized. Caillebotte captured a busy intersection of Paris that was only a short walk from his home. In fact, his paintings of Paris rarely depict areas beyond his familiar neighborhoods.
persona3_adapted: This monumental painting is Gustave Caillebotte’s largest and most ambitious work. It has been a favorite among visitors ever since the Art Institute acquired it in 1964—long before the artist’s role in the Impressionist movement was fully appreciated. Caillebotte captured a busy Parisian intersection just a short walk from his home. In fact, his paintings of Paris rarely show areas beyond the neighborhoods he knew well.

CuratorialText: Caillebotte demonstrated his affinity with the Impressionists through his subject of modern urban life but approached these settings with a more Realist technique. The dramatic cropping of the scene may have been inspired by the newly popularized medium of photography; the two central figures seem to be walking straight into the viewer as something captures their attention outside of the frame. At the time, artists rarely attempted to depict people in motion; Caillebotte carefully placed each of the background figures to evoke the feeling of bustling city streets. When he started exhibiting with the Impressionists, Caillebotte also focused on portraying the atmosphere of his scenes; here, instead of explicitly painting raindrops to indicate the weather, he suggests it through the incorporation of newly available retractable umbrellas and the subtle reflections on the wet cobblestones.
persona3_adapted: Caillebotte shared the Impressionists' interest in depicting modern city life, but he painted these scenes with a more detailed, realistic style. The image is sharply cut off at the edges, a technique likely borrowed from the then-new art of photography. The two central figures appear to be walking directly toward the viewer, distracted by something just beyond the picture's edge. At the time, artists rarely tried to show people in motion; Caillebotte carefully positioned each figure in the background to create the sense of a busy, lively street. When he began showing his work alongside the Impressionists, he also focused on capturing the mood or feeling of a scene. Here, rather than painting raindrops directly to show it was raining, he suggests the weather by including the newly available collapsible umbrellas and by painting the subtle reflections on the wet cobblestones.

CuratorialText: Richard Hunt created Hero Construction in 1958, one year after graduating from the School of the Art Institute of Chicago. The sculpture is composed of found objects—old pipes, bits of metal, and automobile parts—that the artist discovered in junkyards and on the street. Using a torch like a paintbrush, Hunt welded these elements into a form that is abstract yet recognizable.
persona3_adapted: Richard Hunt created *Hero Construction* in 1958, just one year after finishing his studies at the School of the Art Institute of Chicago. The sculpture is made from found objects—old pipes, scraps of metal, and automobile parts—that the artist collected from junkyards and the street. Using a welding torch as if it were a paintbrush, Hunt fused these pieces together into a shape that is abstract but still recognizable.

CuratorialText: Hunt was fascinated with mythology, and Hero Construction can be related to other sculptures past and present, from ancient statues of Greek heroes to modern monuments of statesmen. The modest but monumental stance of the figure suggests a contemporary hero who maintains strength in the face of the uncertainties and dangers of the present age.
persona3_adapted: Hunt was deeply interested in mythology, and *Hero Construction* can be seen as part of a long tradition of sculptures—from ancient Greek statues of heroes to modern monuments of political leaders. The figure’s pose, both humble and grand, suggests a modern-day hero who holds onto strength even when faced with the uncertainties and dangers of today’s world.

CuratorialText: The Mexicas, or Aztec Empire, ruled central Mexico from 1428 until 1521. This monument uses glyphs to record the history of the Mexica universe up to 1503. On the back, 1 Rabbit represents the beginning of time. On the front, the four previous cosmic eras are inscribed in the corners, while the current cosmic era 4 Movement appears in the center. 1 Crocodile (above) and 11 Reed (in the square frame) indicate the date July 15, 1503, when the ninth emperor Moctezuma Xocoyotzin was coronated. Just as these glyphs recurred throughout the Mexica capital, Tenochtitlan, they can also be found in Chicago: 4 Movement appears in a mural at the 18th Street “L” stop and on manhole covers in Pilsen—a neighborhood that has long been home to Chicago’s vibrant Mexican community.
persona3_adapted: The Mexicas—often called the Aztec Empire—ruled central Mexico from 1428 until 1521. This carved stone monument uses picture symbols, known as glyphs, to record the history of the Mexica universe up to the year 1503. On the back of the monument, the symbol "1 Rabbit" marks the beginning of time. On the front, the four previous cosmic eras are inscribed in the corners, while the current cosmic era, "4 Movement," appears in the center. The symbols "1 Crocodile" (above) and "11 Reed" (in the square frame) together indicate the date July 15, 1503—the day the ninth emperor, Moctezuma Xocoyotzin, was crowned. Just as these glyphs were once common throughout the Mexica capital, Tenochtitlan, they can also be found in Chicago today: "4 Movement" appears in a mural at the 18th Street “L” stop and on manhole covers in Pilsen—a neighborhood that has long been home to Chicago’s vibrant Mexican community.

CuratorialText: A palpable energy and sense of movement enliven Nightlife, Archibald Motley’s portrayal of a crowded cabaret in the South Side neighborhood of Bronzeville in Chicago. With stylized figures, an array of diagonal lines, and heightened colors keyed to shades of magenta and violet, the artist captured the exuberance of city dwellers out on the town. Motley created a network of gestures and glances among the people, drawing attention to the various social interactions that animate the scene. The composition is an exploration of artificial lighting. Motley was inspired, in part, to paint Nightlife after having seen Edward Hopper’s Nighthawks (1942.51), which had entered the Art Institute’s collection the prior year.
persona3_adapted: A lively energy and sense of motion bring *Nightlife* to life. In this painting, Archibald Motley shows a crowded cabaret in Bronzeville, a neighborhood on Chicago’s South Side. Using simplified, slightly exaggerated figures, a network of diagonal lines, and bright colors centered on shades of magenta and violet, the artist captured the excitement of city residents enjoying a night out. Motley created a web of gestures and glances among the people, drawing our attention to the different social interactions that make the scene feel active. The painting also explores the effects of artificial light. Motley was partly inspired to paint *Nightlife* after seeing Edward Hopper’s *Nighthawks* (1942.51), which had entered the Art Institute’s collection the year before.

CuratorialText: Although influenced by Abstract Expressionist artists in New York in the early 1950s, Joan Mitchell did not prioritize self-expression: her often exuberant abstractions were “about landscape, not about me,” she once explained. Mitchell painted large, light-filled canvases animated by loosely applied skeins of bright color—here infused with the energy of a large metropolis. The title suggests a relationship between the painting’s network of pigments and the nerves or arteries of an urban space. The sense of spontaneity conveyed in City Landscape, however, belies Mitchell’s methods. Unlike many of her contemporaries, who were dubbed “action painters,” Mitchell worked slowly and deliberately. “I paint a little,” she said. “Then I sit and look at the painting, sometimes for hours. Eventually, the painting tells me what to do.”
persona3_adapted: Although Joan Mitchell was influenced by the Abstract Expressionist artists working in New York in the early 1950s, she did not focus on expressing her own emotions or inner state. Instead, she described her often lively and energetic abstract works as being "about landscape, not about me." Mitchell created large canvases filled with light, brought to life by loosely applied strands of bright color—here, those colors seem charged with the energy of a big city. The painting’s title suggests a connection between the web of paint and the nerves or arteries of an urban environment. However, the feeling of spontaneity in *City Landscape* hides the careful process behind it. Unlike many of her peers, who were called "action painters" for their fast, physical approach, Mitchell worked slowly and thoughtfully. "I paint a little," she said. "Then I sit and look at the painting, sometimes for hours. Eventually, the painting tells me what to do."

CuratorialText: Around 1920 Georgia O’Keeffe painted a number of oils exploring, as she later recalled, “the idea that music could be translated into something for the eye.” In Blue and Green Music, O’Keeffe’s colors and forms simultaneously suggest the natural world and evoke the experience of sound. She was drawn to the theories of the Russian Expressionist painter Vasily Kandinsky, who, in his 1912 text Concerning the Spiritual in Art, argued that visual artists should emulate music in order to achieve pure expression free of literary references.
persona3_adapted: Around 1920, Georgia O’Keeffe created a series of oil paintings based on what she later described as “the idea that music could be translated into something for the eye.” In *Blue and Green Music*, the colors and shapes she uses both remind us of the natural world and capture the feeling of hearing sound. O’Keeffe was interested in the ideas of the Russian painter Vasily Kandinsky, who was part of an artistic movement called Expressionism. In his 1912 book *Concerning the Spiritual in Art*, Kandinsky argued that visual artists should take inspiration from music in order to create pure expression—art that communicates emotion directly, without relying on stories or recognizable objects.

CuratorialText: Henri Matisse considered Bathers by a River to be one of the five most “pivotal” works of his career, and with good reason: it facilitated the evolution of the artist’s style over the course of nearly a decade. Originally, the work was related to a 1909 commission by the Russian collector Sergei Shchukin, who wanted two large canvases to decorate the staircase of his Moscow home. Matisse proposed three pastoral images, though Shchukin decided to purchase only two works, Dance II and Music (both State Hermitage Museum, Saint Petersburg).
persona3_adapted: Henri Matisse considered *Bathers by a River* to be one of the five most “pivotal” works of his career, and with good reason: it marked a major shift in his artistic style over nearly a decade. The painting originally began as part of a 1909 commission from the Russian collector Sergei Shchukin, who wanted two large canvases to decorate the staircase of his Moscow home. Matisse proposed three pastoral scenes, but Shchukin decided to purchase only two of them: *Dance II* and *Music* (both now housed at the State Hermitage Museum in Saint Petersburg).

CuratorialText: Four years later, Matisse returned to this canvas, the rejected third image, altering the idyllic scene and changing the pastel palette to reflect his new interest in Cubism. He reordered the composition, making the figures more columnar, with faceless, ovoid heads. Over the next years, Matisse transformed the background into four vertical bands and turned the formerly blue river into a thick black vertical band. With its restricted palette and severely abstracted forms, Bathers by a River is far removed from Dance II and Music, which convey a graceful lyricism. The sobriety and hint of danger in Bathers by a River may in part reflect the artist’s concerns during the terrible, war-torn period during which he completed it.
persona3_adapted: Four years later, Matisse returned to this canvas—the painting that had been rejected as the third image in the series. He reworked the peaceful scene, changing its soft, pastel colors to reflect his growing interest in Cubism, a style that breaks objects into geometric shapes and multiple viewpoints. He rearranged the composition, making the figures more like columns, with featureless, egg-shaped heads. Over the next several years, Matisse transformed the background into four vertical strips and turned what had been a blue river into a thick, black vertical band. With its limited color range and severely simplified forms, *Bathers by a River* feels very different from *Dance II* and *Music*, which convey a sense of flowing elegance and lightness. The seriousness and hint of threat in *Bathers by a River* may partly reflect the artist’s anxieties during the terrible, war-torn period in which he finished it.

CuratorialText: One of four posts sculpted for the palace of the king (ogoga) of Ikere, this evocative image of the Yoruba concept of a divine ruler is a masterwork of Olowe of Ise. The renowned artist’s dynamic style can be distinguished by the interrelationships between figures, their exaggerated proportions, and the use of open space. The sculptor’s diminutive portrayal of the king suggests his dependence on others, and his crown has been emphasized to evoke the spiritual power residing within it. The towering image of the king’s senior wife standing behind him underscores her superior status as a guardian of her husband’s interests, endowed with keen political understanding and supernatural knowledge.
