She would have placed the crown on the king’s head during his enthronement ceremony. The smaller figures at the king’s feet represent one of his junior wives and the flute-playing trickster god Eshu.
persona3_adapted: One of four carved posts originally made for the palace of the king (ogoga) of Ikere, this powerful image of a divine ruler in Yoruba culture is a masterwork by Olowe of Ise. The famous artist’s energetic style can be seen in how the figures relate to one another, their exaggerated sizes, and the use of empty space around them. The sculptor’s decision to show the king as small suggests his reliance on others, while his crown is made larger to highlight the spiritual power believed to reside within it. The towering figure of the king’s senior wife standing behind him emphasizes her higher status as a protector of her husband’s interests, someone with sharp political insight and spiritual knowledge. She would have placed the crown on the king’s head during his coronation ceremony. The smaller figures at the king’s feet represent one of his junior wives and the flute-playing trickster god Eshu.

CuratorialText: About Nighthawks Edward Hopper recollected, “unconsciously, probably, I was painting the loneliness of a large city.” In an all-night diner, three customers sit at the counter opposite a server, each appear to be lost in thought and disengaged from one another. The composition is tightly organized and spare in details: there is no entrance to the establishment, no debris on the streets. Through harmonious geometric forms and the glow of the diner’s electric lighting, Hopper created a serene, beautiful, yet enigmatic scene. Although inspired by a restaurant Hopper had seen on Greenwich Avenue in New York, the painting is not a realistic transcription of an actual place. As viewers, we are left to wonder about the figures, their relationships, and this imagined world.
persona3_adapted:
**About *Nighthawks***  
Edward Hopper later recalled that, “unconsciously, probably, I was painting the loneliness of a large city.” In an all-night diner, three customers sit at the counter across from a server. Each figure seems lost in thought and disconnected from the others.  

The scene is tightly arranged, with few details: there is no visible entrance to the diner, and the streets are empty. Through balanced shapes and the warm glow of the diner’s electric lights, Hopper created a calm, beautiful, yet mysterious image. Although the painting was inspired by a restaurant Hopper saw on Greenwich Avenue in New York, it is not a realistic copy of an actual place. As viewers, we are left to wonder about the people, their connections to one another, and this imagined world.

CuratorialText: The Assumption of the Virgin was the central image of El Greco’s first major commission, an altarpiece for the Church of Santo Domingo el Antiguo, a convent in Toledo, Spain. The painting’s subject, the raising of the Virgin Mary’s body into heaven, relates to the function of the church as the funerary monument of a wealthy noblewoman, Doña Maria de Silva; the iconography of the entire commission focuses on death and salvation.
persona3_adapted: The Assumption of the Virgin was the central image in El Greco’s first major commission: an altarpiece—a large artwork placed behind an altar—for the Church of Santo Domingo el Antiguo, a convent in Toledo, Spain. The painting shows the Virgin Mary’s body being lifted up into heaven, a subject that connects to the church’s role as a funerary monument for a wealthy noblewoman named Doña Maria de Silva. The overall theme of the commission centers on death and salvation.

CuratorialText: El Greco used broad, free brushwork, flickering hues, rich color harmonies, and bold figural arrangements to arouse devotional fervor in the viewer and impart a deep sense of faith. The composition is divided into two zones: the earthly sphere of the apostles and the celestial realm of the angels. El Greco masterfully compressed many figures into a tall and narrow format: In the earthly zone, the apostles’ faces and gestures express a range of emotions through their dynamic, agitated gestures and poses. They are so individually considered that it seems El Greco studied actual studio models. In contrast, the angels in the heavenly scene above are calm, graceful, and direct in their focus toward the looming figure of the Virgin Mary as she rises into their realm.
persona3_adapted: El Greco used broad, energetic brushstrokes, shifting colors, and carefully balanced color combinations to stir a sense of religious devotion in the viewer and convey a deep feeling of faith. The painting is split into two areas: the earthly space where the apostles stand, and the heavenly realm of the angels. El Greco skillfully packed many figures into a tall, narrow format: on the earthly level, the apostles’ faces and hand movements show a wide range of emotions through their lively, restless poses. Each figure seems so individually studied that it looks as though El Greco worked from real models in his studio. In contrast, the angels in the heavenly scene above are calm, graceful, and focused directly on the towering figure of the Virgin Mary as she rises into their realm.

CuratorialText: Katsushika Hokusai’s much celebrated series, Thirty-Six Views of Mount Fuji (Fugaku sanjûrokkei), was begun in 1830, when the artist was 70 years old. This tour-de-force series established the popularity of landscape prints, which continues to this day. Perhaps most striking about the series is Hokusai’s copious use of the newly affordable Berlin blue pigment, featured in many of the compositions in the color for the sky and water. Mount Fuji is the protagonist in each scene, viewed from afar or up close, during various weather conditions and seasons, and from all directions.
persona3_adapted: Katsushika Hokusai’s highly celebrated series, *Thirty-Six Views of Mount Fuji*, was begun in 1830, when the artist was 70 years old. This masterful series helped make landscape prints popular—a trend that continues to this day. What is perhaps most striking about the series is Hokusai’s extensive use of a newly affordable blue pigment called Berlin blue, which appears in many of the prints, coloring the sky and water. Mount Fuji is the central figure in every scene, whether seen from a distance or close up, in different weather and seasons, and from every direction.

CuratorialText: The most famous image from the set is the “Great Wave” (Kanagawa oki nami ura), in which a diminutive Mount Fuji can be seen in the distance under the crest of a giant wave. The three impressions of Hokusai’s Great Wave in the Art Institute are all later impressions than the first state of the design.
persona3_adapted: The most famous image from the series is the “Great Wave” (Kanagawa oki nami ura). In this print, a tiny Mount Fuji appears in the distance beneath the curling peak of a massive wave. The Art Institute holds three copies of Hokusai’s Great Wave, but all of them were printed later than the very first version of the design.

CuratorialText: Pablo Picasso made The Old Guitarist while working in Barcelona. In the paintings of his Blue Period (1901-04), the artist restricted himself to a cold, monochromatic blue palette, flattened forms, and emotional, psychological themes of human misery and alienation related to the work of such artists as Edvard Munch and Paul Gauguin. The elongated, angular figure of the blind musician also relates to Picasso’s interest in Spanish art and, in particular, the great 16th-century artist El Greco. The image reﬂects the twenty-two-year-old Picasso’s personal struggle and sympathy for the plight of the downtrodden; he knew what it was like to be poor, having been nearly penniless during all of 1902.
persona3_adapted: Pablo Picasso painted *The Old Guitarist* while he was living and working in Barcelona. During what is known as his Blue Period (1901–04), he limited his colors to a cold, mostly blue palette. He also flattened the shapes in his paintings and focused on emotional, psychological themes—human suffering and isolation—similar to the work of artists like Edvard Munch and Paul Gauguin. The blind musician’s long, angular body shows Picasso’s interest in Spanish art, especially the 16th-century painter El Greco. The image also reflects Picasso’s own struggles at age twenty-two and his empathy for people who were poor or marginalized; he knew what it was like to be nearly penniless, having spent most of 1902 in poverty.

CuratorialText: Illustrator Norman Rockwell was one of the most popular artists of the 20th century, beloved in particular for the hundreds of cover images he created for the Saturday Evening Post. The Dugout is the largest of several studies for one such cover, published on September 4, 1948. It was inspired by a springtime doubleheader that he attended during which the hometown Boston Braves swept the visiting Chicago Cubs. The artist created an image contrasting dejected Cubs players and elated Braves fans.
persona3_adapted: Norman Rockwell, an illustrator best known for the many cover images he created for the *Saturday Evening Post*, was one of the most popular American artists of the 1900s. *The Dugout* is the largest of several preparatory works—essentially practice versions—for one of those covers, which appeared on September 4, 1948. Rockwell got the idea after attending a spring baseball doubleheader, where the hometown Boston Braves won both games against the visiting Chicago Cubs. In the image, he sets up a clear contrast: the defeated Cubs players sit slumped in their dugout, while the Braves fans in the stands are full of excitement and joy.

CuratorialText: Rockwell staged the entire scene, which enabled him to take photographs for later reference. He recruited several family members of Braves players to act as fans. In the dugout, he positioned (from left to right) Cubs pitcher Bob Rush, manager Charlie Grimm, catcher Al “Rube” Walker, and pitcher Johnny Schmitz. The figure of the forlorn batboy, however, was not a member of the Cubs; Rockwell paid Braves batboy Frank McNulty $5 to don the Cubs uniform and pose.
persona3_adapted: Rockwell carefully arranged the entire scene, setting it up so he could take photographs to use as reference material later. He asked several family members of Braves players to pose as fans in the stands. In the dugout, he placed (from left to right) Cubs pitcher Bob Rush, manager Charlie Grimm, catcher Al “Rube” Walker, and pitcher Johnny Schmitz. The sad-looking batboy, however, was not actually a member of the Cubs; Rockwell paid Braves batboy Frank McNulty $5 to put on a Cubs uniform and strike a pose.

CuratorialText: By the time the issue was published, the Cubs had fallen to last place in their league. Rockwell’s cover helped bolster their growing reputation as the “loveable losers,” and it became one of the most recognized sports images of the 20th century.
persona3_adapted: By the time the magazine issue came out, the Cubs had dropped to the bottom of their league. Rockwell’s cover image helped strengthen their emerging image as the “loveable losers,” and it went on to become one of the most widely recognized sports pictures of the 1900s.

CuratorialText: The monumental stacks that Claude Monet depicted in his series Stacks of Wheat rose fifteen to twenty feet and stood just outside the artist’s farmhouse at Giverny. Through 1890 and 1891, he worked on this series both in the field, painting simultaneously at several easels, and in the studio, refining pictorial harmonies. In May 1891, Monet hung fifteen of these canvases next to each other in one small room in the Galerie Durand-Ruel in Paris. An unprecedented critical and financial success, the exhibition marked a breakthrough in Monet’s career, as well as in the history of French art. In this view, and in nearly all of the autumn views in the series, the conical tops of the stacks break the horizon and push into the sky. But in most of the winter views, which constitute the core of the series, the stacks seem wrapped by bands of hill and field, as if bedded down for the season. For Monet, the stack was a resonant symbol of sustenance and survival. He followed this group with further series depicting poplars, the facade of Rouen Cathedral, and, later, his own garden at Giverny. The Art Institute has the largest group of Monet’s Stacks of Wheat in the world.
persona3_adapted:
The towering stacks of wheat that Claude Monet painted in his series *Stacks of Wheat* stood about fifteen to twenty feet tall, just outside the artist’s farmhouse in Giverny, France. Between 1890 and 1891, Monet worked on this series both outdoors—painting at several easels at once to capture changing light—and indoors, where he fine-tuned the colors and composition of each piece. In May 1891, he hung fifteen of these canvases side by side in a single small room at the Galerie Durand-Ruel in Paris. The exhibition was an unprecedented success, both with critics and financially, and it marked a turning point in Monet’s career, as well as in French art history.

In this particular painting, and in nearly all the autumn scenes from the series, the cone-shaped tops of the stacks break the line of the horizon and rise into the sky. But in most of the winter scenes—which form the heart of the series—the stacks appear wrapped by bands of hills and fields, as if they are settling in for the season. For Monet, the stack was a powerful symbol of sustenance and survival. He followed this series with others depicting poplars, the facade of Rouen Cathedral, and, later, his own garden at Giverny. The Art Institute holds the largest collection of Monet’s *Stacks of Wheat* in the world.

CuratorialText: In 1886 Vincent van Gogh left his native Holland and settled in Paris, where his beloved brother Theo was a dealer in paintings. Van Gogh was apparently inspired by the energy of the city and by his introduction to Impressionism, and he created at least twenty-four self-portraits during his two-year stay in the French capital.
persona3_adapted: In 1886, Vincent van Gogh left his homeland of Holland and moved to Paris, where his beloved brother Theo worked as an art dealer. The city's vibrant energy and his first encounters with Impressionism—a style of painting that focuses on capturing fleeting moments of light and color—clearly inspired him. During his two years in the French capital, van Gogh produced at least twenty-four self-portraits.

CuratorialText: This early example is modest in size and was painted on prepared artist’s board rather than canvas. Its densely dabbed brushwork, which became a hallmark of Van Gogh’s style, reﬂects the artist’s response to Georges Seurat’s revolutionary pointillist technique in A Sunday on La Grande Jatte—1884. What was for Seurat a method based on the cool objectivity of science became in Van Gogh’s hands an intense emotional language. The surface of the painting dances with particles of color—intense greens, blues, reds, and oranges. Dominating this dazzling array of staccato dots and dashes are the artist’s deep green eyes and the intensity of their gaze. “I prefer painting people’s eyes to cathedrals,” Van Gogh once wrote to Theo. “However solemn and imposing the latter may be—a human soul, be it that of a poor streetwalker, is more interesting to me.” From Paris, Van Gogh traveled to the southern town of Arles for fifteen months. At the time of his death, in 1890, he had actively pursued his art for only five years.
persona3_adapted: This early work is small in scale and was painted on a prepared artist’s board instead of canvas. Its surface is covered in dense, dabbed brushstrokes—a technique that would become a signature of Van Gogh’s style. This approach was inspired by Georges Seurat’s revolutionary method in *A Sunday on La Grande Jatte—1884*, a technique known as pointillism, in which tiny dots of color are placed side by side to blend in the viewer’s eye. For Seurat, this was a cool, scientific way of painting. But in Van Gogh’s hands, it became a powerful emotional language. The painting’s surface seems to dance with particles of color—vivid greens, blues, reds, and oranges. Dominating this lively array of short, sharp marks are the artist’s deep green eyes and the intensity of their gaze. “I prefer painting people’s eyes to cathedrals,” Van Gogh once wrote to his brother Theo. “However solemn and imposing the latter may be—a human soul, be it that of a poor streetwalker, is more interesting to me.” From Paris, Van Gogh traveled to the southern town of Arles, where he stayed for fifteen months. By the time of his death in 1890, he had actively pursued his art for only five years.

CuratorialText: Van Gogh’s career as a painter was actually very brief. From Paris, he traveled to the southern town of Arles for ﬁ ft een months. At the time of his death, in 1890, he had actively pursued his art for just ﬁve years.
persona3_adapted: Van Gogh’s career as a painter was actually very short. After leaving Paris, he spent fifteen months in the southern town of Arles. By the time of his death in 1890, he had been actively working as an artist for only five years.

CuratorialText: In American Gothic, Grant Wood directly evoked images of an earlier generation by featuring a farmer and his daughter posed stiffly and dressed as if they were, as the artist put it, “tintypes from my old family album.” They stand outside of their home, built in an 1880s style known as Carpenter Gothic. Wood had seen a similar farmhouse during a visit to Eldon, Iowa. When it was exhibited at the Art Institute in 1930, the painting became an instant sensation, its ambiguity prompting viewers to speculate about the figures and their story. Many understood the work to be a satirical comment on midwesterners out of step with a modernizing world. Yet Wood intended it to convey a positive image of rural American values, offering a vision of reassurance at the beginning of the Great Depression.
persona3_adapted: In *American Gothic*, Grant Wood deliberately called to mind images of an earlier era by painting a farmer and his daughter standing stiffly and dressed as if they were, in the artist’s words, “tintypes from my old family album.” They pose outside their home, which is built in a style from the 1880s known as Carpenter Gothic—a type of architecture that uses decorative wooden details to imitate medieval stone churches. Wood had seen a similar farmhouse during a visit to Eldon, Iowa. When the painting was shown at the Art Institute in 1930, it became an immediate sensation. Its unclear meaning led viewers to wonder about the figures and what story they might tell. Many saw the work as a satirical comment on midwesterners who seemed out of touch with a rapidly changing world. However, Wood actually meant it to show a positive view of traditional rural American values, offering a sense of comfort and stability at the start of the Great Depression.

CuratorialText: Little did Claude Monet know that the water garden he created three years after buying his Giverny, France, property in 1890 would become his primary inspiration over the next two and a half decades. These paintings, numbering around 250, mark Monet’s artistic journey from more straightforward depictions of the pond spanned by the wooden Japanese bridge to the monumental and near-abstract series on the water lily theme he made in preparation for his murals at the Musée de l’Orangerie in Paris between 1914 and 1926.
persona3_adapted: Monet probably never imagined that the water garden he built three years after buying his property in Giverny, France, in 1890 would become his main source of creative inspiration for the next twenty-five years. He created about 250 paintings of this garden, and they show how his approach changed over time. Early works are more straightforward views of the pond, with its wooden Japanese bridge. Later, his paintings of water lilies became larger and more abstract—meaning they focused less on realistic detail and more on shape, color, and feeling. These later works were made between 1914 and 1926 as studies for the large wall paintings he planned for the Musée de l’Orangerie in Paris.

CuratorialText: Water Lilies, one of a group of paintings on the subject made between 1903 and 1908, comes at the midpoint of Monet’s developing style and spatial experimentations. The nearly square format underscores his move away from painting the conventional zones of land, sky, and water to focus solely on the water’s surface. Clusters of water lilies at bottom left and top right frame a watery path, while the water’s surface reflects trees and clouds. Although the dreamlike quality of floating forms might seem to be a natural development for the artist, he considered these works “an obsession,” a sentiment borne out by technical examinations on the Art Institute’s canvas, revealing many significant changes made to the painting in progress.
persona3_adapted: Water Lilies, part of a series of paintings on this theme created between 1903 and 1908, represents a middle point in Monet’s evolving artistic style and his experiments with space. The painting’s nearly square shape highlights his shift away from the traditional arrangement of land, sky, and water, focusing instead entirely on the water’s surface. Clusters of water lilies in the lower left and upper right frame a watery pathway, while the water reflects trees and clouds above. Although the floating forms give the work a dreamlike quality that might seem like a natural step for the artist, Monet himself described these paintings as “an obsession.” Technical studies of the Art Institute’s canvas confirm this, revealing many significant changes made to the painting as it was being created.

CuratorialText: Pierre-Auguste Renoir considered the Restaurant Fournaise in Chatou (a village along the Seine River) the perfect spot to enjoy nature and good company away from the urban distractions of Paris. His painting Two Sisters (On the Terrace), which he exhibited at the Impressionist exhibition of 1882, is among the last of his paintings set there.
persona3_adapted: Pierre-Auguste Renoir saw the Restaurant Fournaise in Chatou—a village along the Seine River—as the ideal place to enjoy nature and good company, away from the busy distractions of Paris. His painting *Two Sisters (On the Terrace)*, which he showed at the Impressionist exhibition of 1882, is one of the final works he created in that setting.

CuratorialText: Sometimes, as in his most famous work on this subject, Luncheon of the Boating Party (1882; Phillips Collection, Washington, DC), Renoir used his close friends as models. For Two Sisters, however, despite the title of the painting, he paid unrelated models to create this intimate and perfectly balanced pairing of youth and nature. The older girl, wearing the blue flannel of a female boater’s outfit, sports a luxurious red hat, but it is her younger companion who captivates. Her oversize headdress composed of a floral bouquet is echoed in the brightly colored skeins of yarn in the basket that she gently touches. As was typical for the artist, Renoir painted the faces of his models with controlled and precise brushwork creating a porcelain-like surface that contrasts brilliantly with the diaphanous, impressionist backdrop of river, boats, and blossoming trees just beyond the railing of the terrace.
persona3_adapted: Sometimes, as in his most famous painting on this subject, *Luncheon of the Boating Party* (1882; Phillips Collection, Washington, DC), Renoir used his close friends as models. For *Two Sisters*, however—despite the title—he paid unrelated models to create this intimate and perfectly balanced pairing of youth and nature. The older girl, wearing the blue flannel of a female boater’s outfit, sports a luxurious red hat, but it is her younger companion who captures our attention. Her oversized headdress, made of a floral bouquet, is echoed in the brightly colored bundles of yarn in the basket she gently touches. As was typical for the artist, Renoir painted the faces of his models with controlled and precise brushwork, creating a smooth, porcelain-like surface. This contrasts strikingly with the soft, almost transparent background of river, boats, and blossoming trees just beyond the terrace railing—a style that suggests the fleeting, light-filled atmosphere of the scene.

CuratorialText: Willem de Kooning was a central figure in Abstract Expressionism, an art movement that espoused the painterly actions of the artist as a sign of his or her emotions. De Kooning completed Excavation in June 1950, just in time for it to be exhibited in the twenty-fifth Venice Biennale. His largest painting up to that date, the work exemplifies the Dutch-born innovator’s style, with its expressive brushwork and distinctive organization of space into sliding planes with open contours. According to the artist, the point of departure for the painting was an image of women working in a rice field in Bitter Rice, a 1949 Neorealist film by Italian director Giuseppe de Santis. The mobile structure of hooked calligraphic lines defines anatomical parts—bird and fish shapes, human noses, eyes, teeth, necks, and jaws—that seem to dance across the painted surface, revealing the particular tension between abstraction and figuration that is inherent in de Kooning’s work. The original white pigment has yellowed over the years, diluting somewhat the flashes of red, blue, yellow, and pink that punctuate the composition. Aptly titled, the painting reflects de Kooning’s technically masterful painting process: an intensive building up of the surface and scraping down of its paint layers, often for months, until the desired effect was achieved.
persona3_adapted:
Willem de Kooning was a key figure in Abstract Expressionism, a movement in which artists used bold, physical brushwork to express their inner emotions. He finished *Excavation* in June 1950, just in time for it to be shown at the twenty-fifth Venice Biennale, a major international art exhibition. At that time, it was the largest painting he had ever made. The work shows de Kooning’s distinctive style, with energetic, visible brushstrokes and a unique way of organizing space into sliding, overlapping shapes whose edges remain open rather than sharply defined.

The artist said the painting was inspired by a scene from *Bitter Rice*, a 1949 Italian film by director Giuseppe de Santis, which showed women working in a rice field. Across the canvas, hooked, calligraphy-like lines create the suggestion of body parts—bird and fish shapes, human noses, eyes, teeth, necks, and jaws—that seem to move and dance. This blend of abstract shapes with hints of recognizable figures reveals a tension that runs throughout de Kooning’s work: he never fully abandoned the human form, even as he pushed toward abstraction.

Over time, the original white paint has yellowed, softening the flashes of red, blue, yellow, and pink that punctuate the composition. The painting’s title, *Excavation*, is fitting: it reflects de Kooning’s intense, technically demanding process of building up layers of paint and then scraping them back down, often over many months, until he achieved the effect he wanted.

CuratorialText: White Crucifixion is the first in Marc Chagall’s series of compositions that feature Jesus as a Jewish martyr and dramatically call attention to the persecution and suffering of Jews in 1930s Germany at the hands of the National Socialist Party. Chagall stressed Jesus’s religious identity by depicting him and the biblical figures above him in traditional Jewish garments. The surrounding images show the devastation of pogroms, violent attacks against Jewish communities often organized or sanctioned by local governments. Combining the Crucifixion with contemporary events, Chagall’s painting links the martyred Jesus with the Jewish people being persecuted across Europe and implicitly compares the Nazis with Jesus’s tormentors.
persona3_adapted: White Crucifixion is the first in a series of works by Marc Chagall that show Jesus as a Jewish martyr. The painting powerfully draws attention to the persecution and suffering of Jews in 1930s Germany under the National Socialist Party. Chagall emphasizes Jesus’s Jewish identity by showing him—and the biblical figures above him—wearing traditional Jewish clothing. The scenes around the central image depict the destruction caused by pogroms, which were violent attacks on Jewish communities, often organized or approved by local governments. By combining the Crucifixion with current events, Chagall’s painting connects the martyred Jesus to the Jewish people being persecuted across Europe. It also suggests a comparison between the Nazis and those who tormented Jesus.

CuratorialText: The Child’s Bath is a tender portrayal of familial closeness, a subject that Mary Cassatt explored throughout her career. The caregiver’s cheek brushing the child’s shoulder, her encircling embrace, and the child’s pudgy hand on her knee suggest an emotional bond between the two. Captivated by a large exhibition of Japanese prints in Paris in 1890, Cassatt set out to produce a series of color prints influenced by Japanese aesthetics. She then continued her investigation across media, culminating in this bold composition, with its dramatically flattened picture plane, decorative patterning, and bright palette.
persona3_adapted: The Child’s Bath is a gentle portrayal of family intimacy, a theme Mary Cassatt explored throughout her career. The caregiver’s cheek brushing the child’s shoulder, her arms wrapped around the child, and the child’s chubby hand resting on her knee all suggest a deep emotional connection between them. In 1890, Cassatt was captivated by a large exhibition of Japanese prints in Paris, and she decided to create a series of color prints inspired by Japanese artistic styles. She then continued exploring this approach across different art forms, resulting in this bold composition—with its strikingly flat sense of space, decorative patterns, and bright colors.

CuratorialText: René Magritte painted Time Transfixed at a moment when he was attempting to elicit “poetic secrets” through his works. With both philosophy and psychology in mind, the artist challenged himself to juxtapose two images in such a way that they would suggest in the viewer’s mind a third un-pictured thing. Here he connected two highly disparate objects—a train and a fireplace—in a composition that became one of his most iconic works. Although the fireplace is domestic and the train is industrial, they are linked by the smoke. Magritte understood that viewers would connect the locomotive’s billowing exhaust with the plumes of smoke that travel up chimneys, uncovering the painting’s poetic secret.
persona3_adapted: René Magritte painted *Time Transfixed* at a time when he was trying to uncover what he called “poetic secrets” through his art. Drawing on ideas from philosophy and psychology, he set himself a challenge: to place two images side by side in a way that would spark a third, unseen image in the viewer’s mind. In this work, he brought together two very different objects—a train and a fireplace—in a composition that became one of his most famous. Even though the fireplace belongs to the home and the train to the industrial world, they are connected by smoke. Magritte knew that viewers would link the train’s thick exhaust with the smoke rising from a chimney, revealing the painting’s hidden poetic meaning.

CuratorialText: One of the most avant-garde aspects of the Impressionists was their choice of subject matter, which frequently included scenes derived from modern, industrial Paris, from iron bridges to exhibition halls to train sheds. The train station at Saint-Lazare would have been a familiar, meaningful sight to Claude Monet in the 1870s. The terminal linked Paris to Normandy, where the artist developed his technique of painting outdoors in the 1860s. It was also the point of departure for the towns and villages west and north of Paris that the Impressionists frequently visited. Monet completed eight of his twelve known paintings of the Gare Saint-Lazare in time for the third Impressionist exhibition, in 1877, probably displaying them in the same gallery.
persona3_adapted: One of the most daring choices made by the Impressionists was what they decided to paint. Instead of the traditional subjects favored by earlier artists, they often turned to scenes from modern, industrial Paris—iron bridges, exhibition halls, and train sheds. For Claude Monet in the 1870s, the Saint-Lazare train station was a familiar and meaningful place. The station connected Paris to Normandy, where Monet had developed his method of painting outdoors in the 1860s. It was also the departure point for the towns and villages west and north of Paris that the Impressionists frequently visited. Monet completed eight of his twelve known paintings of the Gare Saint-Lazare in time for the third Impressionist exhibition in 1877, and he likely displayed them together in the same gallery.

CuratorialText: Monet chose to focus his attention here on the glass-and-iron train shed, where he found an appealing combination of artiﬁ cial and natural effects—the rising steam of locomotives trapped within the structure and the daylight penetrating large, glazed sections of the roof, for instance. Monet’s depictions of the station inaugurated what was to become for him an established pattern of painting a speciﬁc motif repeatedly in order to capture subtle and temporal atmospheric changes, as in his famous series of stacks of wheat. But the Saint-Lazare paintings also represented his last attempt to capture urban life: from this point on in his career, Monet largely devoted himself to landscapes.
persona3_adapted: Monet focused here on the train shed, a structure made of glass and iron. He was drawn to the way it combined man-made and natural elements—for example, the steam rising from the locomotives was trapped inside the building, while daylight streamed through the large glass sections of the roof. By painting the station repeatedly, Monet began a practice that would become a hallmark of his work: capturing the same subject over and over to record subtle, fleeting changes in light and atmosphere, much like his well-known series of haystacks. However, the Saint-Lazare paintings also marked a turning point. They were his last major effort to depict city life; after this, Monet largely turned his attention to landscapes.

CuratorialText: Salvador Dalí, Surrealism’s most publicized practitioner, created monstrous visions of a world turned inside out, which he made even more compelling through his extraordinary technical skills. When the Art Institute acquired Inventions of the Monsters in 1943, the artist wrote his congratulations and explained.
persona3_adapted: Salvador Dalí, the most widely known artist associated with Surrealism—a movement that explored dreamlike, irrational imagery—created unsettling visions of a world turned upside down. He made these images even more striking through his remarkable technical ability. When the Art Institute acquired *Inventions of the Monsters* in 1943, Dalí wrote to congratulate the museum and offered an explanation of the work.

CuratorialText: “According to Nostradamus the apparition of monsters presages the outbreak of war. The canvas was painted in the Semmering mountains near Vienna a few months before the Anschluss [the 1938 political union of Austria and Germany] and has a prophetic character. Horse women equal maternal river monsters. Flaming giraffe equals masculine apocalyptic monster. Cat angel equals divine heterosexual monster. Hourglass equals metaphysical monster. Gala and Dalí equal sentimental monster. The little blue dog is not a true monster.”
persona3_adapted: According to Nostradamus, the appearance of monsters foreshadows the outbreak of war. This painting was created in the Semmering mountains near Vienna just a few months before the Anschluss—the 1938 political union of Austria and Germany—and carries a prophetic quality. In the work, horse-women are equated with maternal river monsters. A flaming giraffe represents a masculine apocalyptic monster. A cat angel stands for a divine heterosexual monster. An hourglass symbolizes a metaphysical monster. Gala and Dalí themselves appear as a sentimental monster. The little blue dog, however, is not considered a true monster.

CuratorialText: Inventions of the Monsters has an ominous mood. It is rife with threats of danger, from the menacing fire in the distance to the sibylline figure in the foreground with an hourglass and a butterfly, both symbols of the inevitability of death. Next to this figure sit Dalí and his wife and muse, Gala. With his native Catalonia embroiled in the Spanish Civil War, the artist surely felt great anxiety over a world without a safe haven, a world that indeed had allowed for the invention of monsters.
persona3_adapted: Inventions of the Monsters carries a dark, foreboding atmosphere. The painting is filled with signs of danger, from the threatening fire in the distance to the mysterious figure in the foreground holding an hourglass and a butterfly—both objects that symbolize the certainty of death. Beside this figure sit Dalí and his wife and muse, Gala. With his home region of Catalonia caught up in the Spanish Civil War, the artist likely felt deep anxiety about a world that offered no safe place—a world that had, in fact, allowed monsters to be created.

CuratorialText: Shiva, one of the most important Hindu divinities, is here depicted as the Lord of the Dance (Nataraja), an iconic image in Indian art. Shiva’s cosmic dance sets in motion the rhythm of life and death; it pervades the universe, as symbolized by the ring of fire that is filled with the loose, snakelike locks of the god’s hair. One pair of his arms balances the flame of destruction and the hand drum (damaru) that beats the rhythm of life while another performs symbolic gestures: the raised right hand means “fear not,” and the left hand (gajahasta) pointing down toward his raised left foot signifies release from the ignorance that hinders realization of the ultimate reality. Shiva is shown perfectly balanced, with his right leg planted on the demon of darkness (Apasmara), stamping out ignorance. The tiny figure of the personified river goddess, Ganga, is caught up in his matted, flowing locks. Shiva was believed to break the fall of the great Ganges River as it descends from the Himalayas by standing beneath the waters, which divide over his hair, becoming the seven holy rivers of India. This classic bronze comes from the Chola period in the south of India. Icons such as this were carried in procession during religious ceremonies.
persona3_adapted: Shiva, one of the most important gods in Hinduism, appears here as the Lord of the Dance (Nataraja), a famous image in Indian art. His cosmic dance creates the rhythm of life and death, and it fills the entire universe. This idea is shown by the ring of fire around him, which is filled with the loose, snake-like strands of his hair. With one pair of arms, he holds the flame of destruction and a small hand drum (damaru) that beats the rhythm of life. With his other arms, he makes symbolic gestures: his raised right hand means “do not fear,” and his left hand, pointing down toward his raised left foot, represents freedom from the ignorance that blocks understanding of the ultimate reality. Shiva is shown perfectly balanced, with his right foot planted on the demon of darkness (Apasmara), crushing ignorance. The small figure of the river goddess Ganga is caught in his tangled, flowing hair. According to belief, Shiva stood beneath the great Ganges River as it fell from the Himalayas, breaking its fall. The water divided over his hair and became the seven holy rivers of India. This classic bronze sculpture comes from the Chola period in southern India. Images like this one were carried in processions during religious ceremonies.

CuratorialText: Albert Bierstadt is best remembered for his paintings of the American West, but during his early career, the ambitious artist also created New England landscapes, particularly of the White Mountains, as seen here. When this painting was first exhibited in 1863, critics declared it Bierstadt’s “best work” and praised the artist’s deft contrast of “light and shade,” which brought a heightened realism to the image. Bierstadt’s affinity for the White Mountains mirrored a growing interest in the region as one of America’s premier tourist attractions. Photographs of the area from the 19th century suggest that Bierstadt utilized landscape elements from the Flume, a popular New Hampshire tourist site, in this imaginary composition.
persona3_adapted: Albert Bierstadt is best known for his paintings of the American West, but early in his career, this ambitious artist also created landscapes of New England—especially the White Mountains, as shown here. When this painting was first displayed in 1863, critics called it Bierstadt’s “best work” and praised his skillful use of contrast between light and dark, which made the scene feel strikingly realistic. Bierstadt’s connection to the White Mountains reflected a growing public interest in the region as one of America’s top tourist destinations. Photographs of the area from the 1800s suggest that Bierstadt borrowed landscape features from the Flume, a popular New Hampshire tourist site, to create this imagined scene.

CuratorialText: In this large-scale canvas, one of several Paul Gauguin sent back to France in 1897, women of varying ages appear to go about daily life within a colorful, stylized outdoor environment. Like much of the artist’s work, this painting combines observed detail with artistic fantasy to create a dreamlike scene whose narrative resists simple interpretation. The work’s deliberately provocative title enhances this effect, leaving us to wonder if the “you” refers to someone in the painting or to the viewer.
persona3_adapted: In this large-scale painting—one of several Paul Gauguin sent back to France in 1897—women of different ages seem to carry out their daily routines in a colorful, deliberately simplified outdoor setting. As with much of the artist’s work, the scene blends details he actually observed with imagined elements, creating a dreamlike atmosphere whose story is not easy to pin down. The painting’s intentionally challenging title adds to this effect, making us wonder whether the “you” refers to someone inside the picture or to us, the viewers.

CuratorialText: Motivations for travel throughout history have been almost as varied as the means of travel. Whether people traveled alone or in groups, on foot, on animals, in caravans, on ships, or using modern modes of transportation like planes, trains, and automobiles, art can capture how and why we move through the world.
persona3_adapted: Motivations for travel throughout history have been almost as varied as the means of getting from one place to another. Whether people journeyed alone or in groups, on foot, on animals, in caravans, on ships, or using modern forms of transportation like planes, trains, and automobiles, art has the power to capture both how and why we move through the world.

CuratorialText: Marco Polo (1254–1324) was a Venetian merchant who spent about seventeen years in China—from around 1274 to 1292—during the reign of the Mongol emperor Khubilai Khan (reigned 1260–94), founder of the Yuan dynasty. In 1276, Khubilai’s armies conquered the Chinese capital at Hangzhou with minimal destruction. Sometime after this, Marco Polo visited the city and its West Lake.
persona3_adapted: Marco Polo (1254–1324) was a merchant from Venice who lived in China for roughly seventeen years, from about 1274 to 1292. This was during the rule of Khubilai Khan (who reigned from 1260 to 1294), the Mongol emperor who founded the Yuan dynasty. In 1276, Khubilai’s armies took control of the Chinese capital, Hangzhou, causing very little damage. Some time after this event, Marco Polo traveled to the city and saw its famous West Lake.

CuratorialText: Travel is a common theme in traditional Chinese landscape painting. From ancient times, the emperors of China maintained a network of roads and waterways that unified their vast realm and facilitated the movement of people and goods. Owing to this imperial association, images of ordinary travelers on the empire’s roads and rivers connoted the benevolent rule of an enlightened sovereign and evoked an era of peace and prosperity. Rather than documenting the hardships or actual circumstances of any particular journey, images of anonymous characters traveling or working together capture some of the noteworthy sights and scenes a traveler might encounter along the way. Simultaneously, they promote the fundamental ideals of a harmonious society and a well-ordered state.
persona3_adapted: Travel is a frequent subject in traditional Chinese landscape painting. Since ancient times, China’s emperors built and maintained a system of roads and waterways that connected their vast empire, making it easier for people and goods to move around. Because of this connection to imperial rule, images of ordinary people traveling on the empire’s roads and rivers suggested the wise and fair leadership of an enlightened ruler, and brought to mind a time of peace and prosperity. Rather than showing the difficulties or real-life details of any specific journey, these paintings depict unnamed figures traveling or working together, capturing some of the memorable sights and scenes a traveler might come across along the way. At the same time, they promote the core ideals of a harmonious society and a well-ordered state.

CuratorialText: One of the most enduring motivations for travel is religious devotion. Nearly every major religion—Christianity, Judaism, Islam, Buddhism, Hinduism, Jainism, and Sikhism, among others—has a tradition of pilgrimage: intentional travel to a site of spiritual significance. Christian pilgrims traveled to the Holy Land to walk in the footsteps of Christ or visited more local destinations connected to the lives or relics of the saints. In Islam, pilgrimage (hajj) to Mecca is one of the principal duties of all believers. Other holy sites were also visited on a regular basis. Over many centuries, hundreds of Chinese, Japanese, and Korean pilgrims braved countless dangers to visit places where the Historical Buddha lived and taught. Pilgrims would sometimes bring gifts, called votive offerings, with them as a donation to the destination site.
persona3_adapted: One of the most lasting reasons people travel is religious devotion. Nearly every major religion—Christianity, Judaism, Islam, Buddhism, Hinduism, Jainism, and Sikhism, among others—has a tradition of pilgrimage: intentional travel to a place that holds spiritual meaning. Christian pilgrims traveled to the Holy Land to follow in the footsteps of Christ, or visited more local sites connected to the lives or remains of saints. In Islam, the pilgrimage (hajj) to Mecca is one of the essential duties for all believers. Other holy sites were also visited regularly. Over many centuries, hundreds of Chinese, Japanese, and Korean pilgrims braved countless dangers to visit places where the Historical Buddha lived and taught. Pilgrims sometimes brought gifts, known as votive offerings—objects given as a donation or expression of devotion—to leave at the destination.

CuratorialText: Trade was another common motivation for travel and occurred in many forms, but the most famous trade route in history was the Silk Road, the overland trade routes that linked the prosperous Tang empire (618–907) with Central, West, and South Asia. On the Silk Road, foreign merchants joined Buddhist missionaries, diplomatic envoys, translators, craftsmen, entertainers, and other skilled immigrants in a wealthy, worldly environment that offered a ready market for exotic imports, including silver and gold objects, delicate glassware, and even grape wine.
persona3_adapted: Trade was another common reason people traveled, and it took many different forms. However, the most famous trade route in history was the Silk Road—a network of land routes that connected the wealthy Tang empire (618–907) with Central, West, and South Asia. Along the Silk Road, foreign merchants traveled alongside Buddhist missionaries, government representatives, translators, craftsmen, entertainers, and other skilled immigrants. Together, they created a rich and worldly environment where there was a strong demand for exotic goods from distant lands. These included items made of silver and gold, delicate glassware, and even grape wine.

CuratorialText: In the twentieth century, travel offered a key means for diplomacy and cultural exchange. Alice Roosevelt Longworth (1884–1980), Theodore Roosevelt’s daughter, embarked on a tour of Asia accompanying William Howard Taft’s goodwill mission in 1905. This voyage is immortalized in a series of photographs but also through diplomatic gifts that dignitaries, such as the emperor of Japan, bestowed on Roosevelt.
persona3_adapted: In the twentieth century, travel became an important way for countries to build relationships and share culture. Alice Roosevelt Longworth (1884–1980), the daughter of President Theodore Roosevelt, joined a goodwill trip to Asia in 1905, traveling with William Howard Taft’s diplomatic mission. This journey is remembered not only through a series of photographs, but also through the official gifts given to Roosevelt by foreign leaders, including the emperor of Japan.

CuratorialText: Adventurers, archaeologists, and scholars have long been traversing the globe to conduct research and to explore foreign lands. In the early twentieth century, leisure travel among Europeans and North Americans also became increasingly common. Fascinated by the cultural heritage of Asia and the Middle East, tourists began to expand their horizons by venturing there.
persona3_adapted: Adventurers, archaeologists, and scholars have long traveled the world to conduct research and explore unfamiliar lands. By the early twentieth century, leisure travel had also become more common among Europeans and North Americans. Drawn to the cultural heritage of Asia and the Middle East, tourists began broadening their horizons by visiting these regions.

CuratorialText: Artists who traveled played a pivotal role in documenting and popularizing their visions of the distant places they visited. Their interactions with other artists and the things they experienced on their journeys contributed to the dispersal of different artistic styles and motifs.
persona3_adapted: Artists who traveled played a key role in recording and sharing their personal impressions of the faraway places they visited. The exchanges they had with other artists, along with what they encountered during their journeys, helped spread different artistic styles and recurring themes or patterns across regions.

CuratorialText: While travel has mostly positive connotations in popular culture today, it’s important to remember the darker sides of travel as well. Not all travel is undertaken by choice: people can be forced to travel as a result of political exile, as well as due to war, famine, disease, economic hardship, environmental disaster, and colonialism. Even when travel is voluntary, it can be dangerous, and this was particularly true in ages past. The physical act of traveling exposed people to potential injuries and diseases, the natural elements were unpredictable and threatened modes of transportation, and there was an inherent unpredictability to being on the road far from home. The recognition of these dangers shaped stories and cultural beliefs.
persona3_adapted: While travel is often seen as a positive experience in today's culture, it's also important to recognize its more difficult aspects. Not all travel is a choice: people may be forced to move due to political exile, war, famine, disease, economic hardship, environmental disaster, or colonialism. Even when travel is voluntary, it can be risky—especially in earlier times. The journey itself exposed people to potential injuries and illnesses, unpredictable weather that could endanger transportation, and the general uncertainty of being far from home. These dangers influenced the stories and beliefs that cultures developed around travel.

CuratorialText: Objects themselves have rich histories and often undertook journeys of their own, whether through trade, gift giving, or collecting. For conservators, the journey of an item is embedded within its material presence; marks, scars, repairs, and other interventions describe the actions of others who have contributed to its present state. The process of conservation involves the synthesis of material evidence and its placement in time, not just to understand an item’s past but to envision its future preservation. Anthropologists have described the trajectories of material items in terms of their lives, biographies, or itineraries, but such notions are intuitively familiar to conservators, whose actions are fundamentally additive to an item’s journey, both materially and through time.
persona3_adapted: Objects themselves have rich histories and often traveled from place to place—through trade, as gifts, or by being collected. For conservators (the professionals who care for and preserve objects), an item’s journey is written into its physical condition: marks, scratches, repairs, and other changes tell the story of people who have shaped what it looks like today. The work of conservation combines physical evidence with an understanding of when things happened, not only to learn about an object’s past but also to plan for its future care. Anthropologists have described these paths of objects as having lives, biographies, or itineraries, but such ideas feel natural to conservators, whose actions always add another chapter to an object’s journey—both in its material form and across time.

CuratorialText: Individual travelers carried things with them that were essential for the journey itself. Clothing and equipment suitable to traversing long distances sometimes set them apart from local residents, and travelers carried personal effects and accessories, such as small books, drinking flasks, and protective amulets, to make the journey easier.
persona3_adapted: Individual travelers brought along items that were necessary for the journey itself. The clothing and gear suited to covering long distances sometimes made them stand out from local people, and they carried personal belongings and small accessories—such as pocket-sized books, drinking flasks, and protective charms—to make the trip more manageable.

CuratorialText: Travelers brought objects acquired on their journeys home with them as well. Holy objects acquired by pilgrims allowed them to bring the blessings of their pilgrimage back from their destination. Images made during travel—whether sketches, paintings, or photographs—captured the experience of the journey for future reflection. Souvenirs purchased during a trip also offered travelers a way of commemorating a journey and gave them something tangible on which to reflect. The advent of commercial global travel networks in many cultures and contexts was accompanied by a flood of photographs, drawings, postcards, mementos, and other means of recording scientific and sentimental experiences abroad. Perhaps the most recognizable artifact of travel over the past 150 years is the picture postcard. These were collected as mementos or mailed to family and friends from abroad, accruing the additional prestige of a foreign stamp and postmark.
persona3_adapted: Travelers also brought back objects they had acquired during their journeys. Pilgrims, for example, collected sacred items that allowed them to carry the spiritual benefits of their pilgrimage home from the destination. Images created while traveling—whether sketches, paintings, or photographs—captured the experience of the journey, preserving it for later reflection. Souvenirs purchased along the way gave travelers a way to remember their trip and provided something physical to help them think back on it. As commercial global travel networks expanded across many cultures and time periods, there was a surge in photographs, drawings, postcards, mementos, and other ways of recording both scientific observations and personal experiences abroad. Perhaps the most familiar travel keepsake from the last 150 years is the picture postcard. These were collected as mementos or mailed to family and friends from other countries, gaining extra appeal from the foreign stamp and postmark they carried.

CuratorialText: Most travel we think of involves physically moving between places, but virtual travel (colloquially, “armchair travel”) that takes place in the mind spans time and cultures and is facilitated by books and works of art. Through focused contemplation of a travel account, a map, a cherished souvenir, or images of far-off places, memory and imagination combine to transport someone mentally and to simulate the experience of travel.
persona3_adapted: Most of the travel we're familiar with involves physically moving from one place to another. But there's another kind of travel—often called "armchair travel"—that happens entirely in the mind. This mental journey can cross both time and cultures, and it's made possible by things like books, maps, treasured souvenirs, or images of distant locations. When we focus deeply on a travel story, a map, a keepsake, or a picture of a faraway place, our memory and imagination work together to transport us mentally, creating an experience that feels like real travel.

CuratorialText: While it may not be the first thing we think of when looking at a work of art, environmental factors have shaped everything about it, from how it was made, to how it has been perceived and appreciated, to how it has been preserved for today’s audiences. Dive into the complex and interconnected relationships between art and the environment—from images of the natural world to recycling ancient jades—to see our collections in a whole new way.
persona3_adapted: When we look at a work of art, we might not immediately think about the environment. Yet environmental factors have influenced nearly every aspect of it—how it was created, how people have understood and valued it over time, and how it has been cared for so that we can see it today. This exhibition explores the many ways art and the environment are connected, from depictions of the natural world to the reuse of ancient jade objects. It invites you to see our collections from a fresh perspective.

CuratorialText: While environmental conditions differ from place to place, contact with nature has been a universal human experience. It’s therefore not surprising that natural motifs—plants, animals, water, the sky—adorn works of art across all cultures and are a consistent source of inspiration and meaning.
persona3_adapted: While environments vary from one place to another, the experience of nature is something all humans share. It's no wonder, then, that images of plants, animals, water, and the sky appear in art from cultures around the world, serving as a steady source of both inspiration and deeper meaning.

CuratorialText: Depictions of landscapes can serve as valuable documentation of specific places, but they can also evoke strong emotions. Images of the natural world can remind us of the beauty and power of nature, evoking feelings of tranquility, wonder, awe, and contemplation.
persona3_adapted: Landscape images do more than just record what a particular place looks like. They can also stir deep feelings. Pictures of nature can bring to mind both the beauty and the force of the natural world, prompting emotions like calm, curiosity, amazement, and thoughtful reflection.

CuratorialText: From the earliest Japanese poetry anthology, compiled in the eighth century, to the present day, the beauty of the Japanese landscape has been celebrated in literature and art. At first, landscapes were not an independent subject but were incorporated into the backgrounds of Buddhist paintings, seasonal subjects (shikinami-e), and paintings of famous scenic or sacred sites (meisho-e). Beginning around the thirteenth century, possibly through the influence of the introduction of new modes of ink painting from China and Korea, landscape became an independent subject in Japanese painting. Landscapes of Japan were most often, but not exclusively, painted in the full color mode, which effectively expressed the seasonal changes that are inextricably linked with natural imagery in Japanese poetry and art. Ink painting was usually chosen for varied and dramatic landscapes of China, which most Japanese artists understood through their study of imported paintings, and later, of painting manuals as well. Whether rendered in rich, deep colors or in subtle tones of ink, landscapes by Japanese artists are imbued with a sense of intimate understanding and appreciation of the transient beauty and spiritual significance of the natural world.
persona3_adapted: From the earliest collection of Japanese poetry, compiled in the eighth century, to the present day, the beauty of Japan's landscapes has been celebrated in both literature and art. At first, landscapes were not treated as a subject on their own. Instead, they appeared in the backgrounds of Buddhist paintings, in works showing the changing seasons, and in paintings of famous scenic or sacred places. Around the thirteenth century, possibly influenced by new ink-painting techniques brought from China and Korea, landscape became an independent subject in Japanese painting. Japanese landscapes were most often—though not always—painted in full color, a style that effectively captured the seasonal changes so closely tied to nature imagery in Japanese poetry and art. Ink painting, on the other hand, was usually chosen for varied and dramatic scenes of China, which most Japanese artists knew through studying imported paintings and, later, painting manuals. Whether rendered in rich, deep colors or in subtle shades of ink, Japanese landscapes convey a deep, personal appreciation for the fleeting beauty and spiritual meaning of the natural world.

CuratorialText: Travel to misty mountain valleys and foggy ocean beaches through the eyes of great Japanese artists from the sixteenth century on, accompanied by the sounds of the shakuhachi (bamboo flute), in this exploration of nature as an inspiration for Japanese art and music, guided by the virtuoso Kurahashi Yodo II and curator Frank Feltens.
persona3_adapted: Step into misty mountain valleys and foggy ocean beaches as seen through the eyes of great Japanese artists from the 1500s onward. In this exploration, you’ll discover how nature has inspired Japanese art and music, guided by the virtuoso performer Kurahashi Yodo II and curator Frank Feltens. The experience is accompanied by the sounds of the shakuhachi, a traditional bamboo flute.

CuratorialText: For Chinese scholar-officials, viewing landscape paintings not only provided aesthetic delight, but a landscape setting was often vested with meaning, which could range from providing a clue about the season of the painting to conveying more complex meanings. Seeing an image of figures in a remote landscape often referred to the key cultural and political concept of eremitism. The literati, or scholar class, believed in the Confucian notion that government service was a high moral duty. However, if the emperor was corrupt, or if a scholar was faced with serving a dynasty founded by usurping foreign rulers then, following the tradition of eremitism, a true gentleman refused government office. Instead, he became a mountain recluse, or at least assumed the demeanor of a hermit or rustic fisherman, to protest the corruption of moral order. Vested with meaning as a symbol of ethical reclusion, landscape motifs had poignant significance.
persona3_adapted:
For Chinese scholar-officials, viewing landscape paintings was not just about enjoying their beauty—the scenes themselves often carried deeper meanings. A landscape could hint at the season, or it could express more complex ideas. For example, when a painting showed people in a remote, mountainous setting, it often referenced a key cultural and political idea called *eremitism*—the practice of withdrawing from society as a form of moral protest.

The scholar class, known as the *literati*, followed the Confucian belief that serving the government was a high moral duty. However, if the emperor was corrupt, or if a scholar was asked to serve a dynasty founded by foreign invaders who had seized power, a true gentleman would refuse that role. Following the tradition of eremitism, he would instead become a mountain recluse—or at least take on the appearance of a hermit or a simple fisherman—to protest the breakdown of moral order. Because of this, landscape motifs in paintings were filled with deep emotional and ethical meaning, symbolizing a principled retreat from an unjust world.

CuratorialText: Careful observation of nature did not only translate to depictions of real places. Artists used their knowledge of the natural world to create imagined landscapes as well.
persona3_adapted: Careful observation of the natural world didn't just lead artists to paint real, recognizable locations. They also used their understanding of nature to invent entirely imaginary landscapes.

CuratorialText: A full appreciation of Chinese art includes embracing the category of “scholar’s rocks,” alternatively called “spirit stones.”  Scholar’s rocks consist of wondrously shaped and textured rocks, often of limestone, that are collected for placement in a designed garden or are brought indoors for display in a scholar’s study. The size can range from twelve feet or more to tiny enough to be enclosed in a fist. Philosophic and religious texts as well as poetry have a long history of singling out mountains and rocks for special consideration. A saying from the time of Confucius (551 BCE–479 BCE) notes, “The wise take joy in water; the humane take joy in mountains.” At the same time, Daoists pictured mountains as sanctuaries or paradises for immortal beings and used minerals in elixirs for infinite life. Placement of evocative rocks in gardens and homes helped turn a mundane space into a paradise, or at least into an environment conducive to cultivating personal longevity or to the pursuit of eternal life.
persona3_adapted: A full appreciation of Chinese art includes understanding a category known as "scholar's rocks," sometimes called "spirit stones." These are unusually shaped and textured rocks, often made of limestone, that people collect to place in a designed garden or to display indoors in a scholar's study. Their size can range from over twelve feet tall to small enough to fit in a closed hand. For a long time, philosophical and religious writings, as well as poetry, have paid special attention to mountains and rocks. A saying from the time of Confucius (551 BCE–479 BCE) notes, "The wise take joy in water; the humane take joy in mountains." Meanwhile, Daoists viewed mountains as safe havens or paradises for immortal beings and used minerals in potions meant to grant eternal life. Placing evocative rocks in gardens and homes helped turn an ordinary space into a paradise, or at least into an environment that supports personal longevity or the pursuit of eternal life.

CuratorialText: In Chinese tradition, the educated class delighted in rocks as tangible embodiments of the powers of creation and cosmic transformation, and they sometimes rhapsodized about them as eternally faithful “friends.” Gazing upon them could encourage imaginary travel deep into nature. Chinese scholars, poets, painters, and emperors all engaged in collecting rocks, and painters often studied the rocks they collected, treating them as both muse and model for their landscape paintings.
persona3_adapted: In Chinese tradition, educated people cherished rocks as physical symbols of the forces that create and transform the universe. They sometimes spoke of them with great emotion as eternally loyal "friends." Looking at these rocks could inspire an imaginary journey deep into nature. Chinese scholars, poets, painters, and emperors all collected rocks, and painters often studied the ones they owned, using them both as a source of inspiration and as a model for their landscape paintings.

CuratorialText: From intimate courtyards to monumental temple, tomb, and pleasure gardens, Asia has been central to the development of cultivated landscapes. The earliest known garden—the biblical Garden of Eden—may have been located in West Asia at the confluence of the Tigris and the Euphrates in present-day Iraq. The very word “paradise” is derived from the walled orchard gardens and hunting parks of ancient Iran, referred to as pardis. This type of enclosed garden became an integral part of Persian architectural landscape for centuries and also often included pavilions to provide shade and relief from the sun. According to written sources, the earliest gardens in China, dating to the Zhou period (ca. 1050–256 BCE), consisted of enclosed hunting grounds reserved for the royal elite. Chinese gardens were often characterized by carefully positioned rocks and pools intended to recreate microcosms of nature at large. In Japan, gardens followed a more naturalistic design and incorporated rolling hills and languid ponds to underline harmony between humans and their surroundings. In the arid landscapes of West and South Asia, one of the most common garden plans depended on a series of interconnected pools and axial watercourses.
persona3_adapted:
From small, private courtyards to grand temples, tombs, and pleasure gardens, Asia has played a central role in shaping how humans design and interact with cultivated landscapes. The earliest known garden—the biblical Garden of Eden—may have been located in West Asia, where the Tigris and Euphrates rivers meet in what is now Iraq. The word "paradise" itself comes from the walled orchard gardens and hunting parks of ancient Iran, which were called *pardis*. This style of enclosed garden became a key feature of Persian architecture and landscape design for centuries, often including pavilions that offered shade and relief from the sun.

According to written records, the earliest gardens in China, dating to the Zhou period (around 1050–256 BCE), were enclosed hunting grounds reserved for royalty and the elite. Chinese gardens often featured carefully placed rocks and pools, designed to create small, miniature versions of the natural world. In Japan, gardens followed a more natural-looking design, using rolling hills and calm ponds to emphasize a sense of harmony between people and their environment. In the dry landscapes of West and South Asia, one of the most common garden layouts relied on a series of connected pools and straight water channels.

CuratorialText: Throughout history, art has served as a reflection of and response to environmental change. Sometimes this change is deliberately brought about through human beings adapting to and altering their surrounding environment. Cultural landscapes—including the ways that humans have built on, altered, and imagined natural environments—play a significant role in how we understand the world we inhabit.
persona3_adapted: Throughout history, art has reflected and responded to changes in the natural world. Sometimes these changes are the result of human efforts to adapt to or reshape the environment around them. The ways people have built upon, transformed, and imagined the natural world—what experts call "cultural landscapes"—help shape how we understand the world we live in.

CuratorialText: Works of art can bear witness to the environmental changes that have taken place throughout recorded history. Depictions of animals from regions where those animals no longer exist, for example, can tell us about migrations or extinctions.
persona3_adapted: Works of art can serve as records of the environmental shifts that have occurred over the course of human history. For instance, images of animals from areas where those animals are no longer found can reveal information about their migrations or extinctions.

CuratorialText: Humans have always had an impact on the environments where they live, but the scale of that impact dramatically increased with industrialization. In the United States and Europe, factories and advances in transportation sprung up to create a range of consumer goods and ways to travel with new ease and speed. Largely coal powered, these machines spewed pollution that artists such as Whistler sought to visualize in paint.
persona3_adapted: Humans have always shaped the environments they inhabit, but the scale of that impact grew dramatically with industrialization. In the United States and Europe, factories and new transportation networks emerged, producing a wide range of consumer goods and enabling faster, easier travel. These machines were largely powered by coal, and the pollution they released became something artists like Whistler tried to capture and interpret in their paintings.

CuratorialText: Similarly, the infusion of Western technology into Japan from the beginning of the Meiji era (1868–1912) spurred widespread visual documentation of the new developments. Railroad transportation, telegraphic communication, gas-fueled lighting, steam-powered river and ocean vessels, and new materials and designs for bridges and buildings were crammed into the Japanese landscape over two decades.
persona3_adapted: Similarly, the introduction of Western technology into Japan from the start of the Meiji era (1868–1912) prompted a surge in visual records of these new developments. Over just two decades, the Japanese landscape became crowded with railroads, telegraph systems, gas lighting, steam-powered boats for rivers and oceans, and new materials and designs for bridges and buildings.

CuratorialText: Contemporary artists are increasingly exploring the impact of climate change on the natural world and human communities, whether their goal is to raise awareness about the urgent need to address climate change, to highlight the social and political issues that are bound up in environmental degradation, or to envision alternative futures and inspire hope and action.
persona3_adapted: Contemporary artists are increasingly looking at how climate change affects nature and people. Some aim to draw attention to the urgent need for action, while others focus on the social and political problems tied to environmental damage. Still others imagine different possible futures, hoping to inspire hope and motivate change.

CuratorialText: The creation of art itself impacts and is impacted by the environment. Stones and precious metals need to be mined. Wood needs to be harvested. Clay is extracted from the earth. Plants need to be gathered. Many works of art make use of animal products, such as parchment, feathers, horn, and ivory. Different production techniques require burning fuel sources for melting, firing, and glazing. The gathering of raw materials and their transformation into art objects is not without impact on the environment and has at times come at the expense of the exploitation of natural resources and people.
persona3_adapted: The process of making art both affects and is affected by the natural world. Stones and precious metals must be taken from mines. Wood has to be cut down. Clay is dug from the ground. Plants need to be collected. Many artworks use materials that come from animals, such as parchment (treated animal skin for writing or painting), feathers, horn, and ivory. Different methods of production require burning fuels to melt metals, fire clay, or apply glazes. Gathering these raw materials and turning them into art objects inevitably leaves a mark on the environment, and at times this has come at the cost of overusing natural resources and exploiting people.

CuratorialText: While recycling may seem like a very recent phenomenon, materials have long been reused in the creation of works of art, whether due to the scarcity of materials or to the value placed on an existing object. For example, since jade was such a prestigious medium and a valuable commodity in ancient China, broken or out-of-fashion articles were not discarded but were instead recycled to make new objects. This appears to be especially true in the late Shang dynasty (ca. 1250–1050 BCE). Archaeologists working at the late Shang capital of Anyang have found that, possibly due to the scarcity of resources at a time of high demand, earlier Stone Age (ca. 5000–1700 BCE) objects were being transformed into decorated jewelry and other items that held more interest and meaning to the Shang people. All three pendants shown here were made by cutting up a special kind of Neolithic square tube called a cong, which had lost its ritual significance by Shang times. It is noteworthy that the recycled cong used here represent different Stone Age cultures dispersed across north and south China.
persona3_adapted: While the idea of recycling might feel like a modern development, artists have actually been reusing materials for centuries—sometimes because new resources were hard to come by, and sometimes because an existing object already carried special meaning. In ancient China, for instance, jade was a highly valued and prestigious material. Rather than throwing away broken or outdated jade items, people would remake them into new objects. This practice seems to have been especially common during the late Shang dynasty (around 1250–1050 BCE). Archaeologists working at the Shang capital of Anyang have discovered that, likely due to a shortage of materials during a time of high demand, older objects from the Stone Age (roughly 5000–1700 BCE) were being carved into decorated jewelry and other items that held greater interest and significance for the Shang people. The three pendants shown here were all made by cutting up a type of Neolithic square tube known as a *cong*—a ritual object that had lost its original religious importance by the Shang period. Interestingly, the recycled *cong* used for these pendants come from different Stone Age cultures across northern and southern China.

CuratorialText: Environmental conditions can significantly impact the preservation of works of art. Factors such as temperature, humidity, light, and pollution affect the chemical and physical properties of materials in different ways, causing damage and degradation to objects over time. On a larger scale, major environmental changes such as temperature shifts and more destructive weather patterns can threaten heritage sites around the world. Conservators play a pivotal role in safeguarding the objects in the museum’s collections. Read about conservation and scientific research initiatives at the National Museum of Asian Art. The Smithsonian Institution acknowledges climate change as the existential threat of our time. Learn more about the Smithsonian’s sustainability initiatives.
persona3_adapted: The environment around us can have a major effect on how well artworks survive over time.
