Things like temperature, humidity, light, and pollution each interact with the materials in artworks in different ways, slowly causing them to break down or become damaged. On a broader scale, larger environmental changes—such as shifting temperatures and more extreme weather patterns—can also put historic sites around the world at risk. Specialists called conservators play a key role in protecting the objects in the museum’s collections. You can read more about conservation and scientific research efforts at the National Museum of Asian Art. The Smithsonian Institution views climate change as the most urgent threat of our time. To learn more, explore the Smithsonian’s sustainability initiatives.

CuratorialText: A color of vitality, passion, good fortune, and celebration, red has undeniable energy. It catches our eye and stirs our blood, forcing us to take notice. From glittering stones to ruddy earth, from insects to minerals, learn about various ways that people have made and understood red through examples drawn from across the museum’s collections.
persona3_adapted: Red is a color full of life, strong emotion, luck, and festivity. Its energy is impossible to ignore—it grabs our attention and quickens our pulse, making us stop and look. Whether it comes from sparkling gems, reddish soil, insects, or minerals, this exhibition explores the many ways people have created and interpreted red. Through objects chosen from across the museum’s collections, you’ll discover how this powerful color has been made and understood in different times and places.

CuratorialText: Several metal compounds (that include iron, mercury, or lead) form commonly used historic colored pigments. These compounds may be found, mined, or produced. Iron compounds in earth pigments can have different colors. Red iron earths are readily found or can be manufactured and have been discovered in many applications, including pottery and paints.
persona3_adapted: Several metal-based substances—those containing elements like iron, mercury, or lead—were historically used to create colored pigments. These substances could be found in nature, mined from the ground, or manufactured. For example, iron compounds found in earth pigments can produce a range of colors. Red iron earths, in particular, were either easily obtained from natural sources or artificially produced, and they have been used in many different applications, from pottery to paints.

CuratorialText: Similarly, mercury-based pigments are made from grinding the red mineral cinnabar. Synthetic versions have long been produced and are referred to as vermilion. This deep red pigment, though toxic, was used in burial ceremonies, incorporated in lacquers, and adorns paintings and documents.
persona3_adapted: Similarly, pigments made from mercury are created by grinding a red mineral called cinnabar. For centuries, people have also produced artificial versions, known as vermilion. This rich, deep red color—despite being toxic—was used in burial rituals, mixed into lacquers, and applied to paintings and manuscripts.

CuratorialText: While red lead has a naturally forming mineral equivalent (minimum), it was manufactured far more commonly for use as a pigment. In general, lead compounds were heated under conditions favorable to produce the desired colored material and minimizing the formation of others.
persona3_adapted: While red lead does occur naturally as a mineral (called minimum), it was much more often produced artificially for use as a coloring material. Typically, lead compounds were heated in carefully controlled conditions to create the intended color while limiting the formation of unwanted byproducts.

CuratorialText: Red copper oxide or cuprite is found as a degradation product on bronzes and forms by the reaction of the copper from the bronze and the atmosphere.
persona3_adapted: Red copper oxide, also known as cuprite, is a substance that forms on bronze objects as they deteriorate. It appears when the copper in the bronze reacts with the surrounding air.

CuratorialText: Plants and animals can also be sources of reds used in artworks. Madder root and safflower each may have their color extracted, purified, and prepared for use in objects such as textiles and prints. The conditions in which the dye or lake is prepared can change the final color. Scale insects (such as lac and cochineal) are another source. Such insects were harvested, with the red color extracted and prepared for use as a pigment or dye. When possible, determining the colorants used in artworks can shed light on historical trade routes due to the limited native habitat of these insects.
persona3_adapted: Plants and animals can also provide the reds used in artworks. For example, the roots of the madder plant and the petals of safflower can be processed to extract their color, which is then purified and made ready for use in items like textiles and prints. The way this dye—or "lake," a type of pigment made from dye—is prepared can affect the final shade. Another source comes from scale insects, such as those that produce lac and cochineal. These insects were collected, and their red color was extracted and turned into a pigment or dye. When possible, identifying the specific colorants in artworks can reveal information about historical trade routes, since these insects originally lived only in certain regions.

CuratorialText:
Dried cochineal insects. Image courtesy of Conservation and Scientific Research, National Museum of Asian Art, Smithsonian Institution
Red also occurred naturally in the form of precious and semi-precious stones, such as rubies, garnets, spinels, and carnelians. Prized for their color and rarity, they were valuable gifts and were often worn by elites or used to adorn luxury items.
persona3_adapted:
Dried cochineal insects. Image courtesy of Conservation and Scientific Research, National Museum of Asian Art, Smithsonian Institution

Red also appeared in nature as precious and semi-precious stones, including rubies, garnets, spinels, and carnelians. Valued for their vivid color and scarcity, these stones were given as important gifts and were frequently worn by the wealthy or used to decorate high-status objects.

CuratorialText: Rubrication, the practice of writing portions of a text in red ink, helped visually underscore certain information and became particularly common in European manuscripts after the seventh century. Red was sometimes used for the angular Kufic script in in copies of the Qur’an made between the eighth and eleventh centuries CE to indicate vocalization; it stood out next to black- or brown-inked calligraphy. Red was also commonly used in the paste for the inked seals that were added to finished works of Chinese painting or calligraphy; it contrasted against black ink writing and images. The seal might give the name of the artist or his studio, the collector, or other private individuals, and it occasionally expresses a favorite brief saying.
persona3_adapted: In many historical manuscripts, scribes used red ink to highlight important parts of the text—a practice known as rubrication. This technique became especially common in European books after the seventh century. In copies of the Qur’an made between the eighth and eleventh centuries, red was sometimes used for a specific angular style of writing called Kufic script, to show how words should be pronounced. The red stood out clearly against the black or brown ink used for the main calligraphy. Red was also commonly used in the paste for the inked seals that were added to finished Chinese paintings or calligraphy. These seals created a visual contrast with the black ink of the writing or images. A seal might include the name of the artist or their studio, the collector, or other private individuals, and occasionally it would express a favorite short saying.

CuratorialText: Love, passion, importance—red carries many positive associations today, and has throughout history. In ancient Egypt, pharaohs were depicted in red due to its association with royalty and passage through the afterlife. In China, red is auspicious—associated with life-generating energy (the sun, blood, and fire)—and is the color of celebrations and prosperity.
persona3_adapted: Red has long been linked to love, passion, and importance—associations that remain strong today. In ancient Egypt, pharaohs were shown wearing or surrounded by red because the color symbolized royalty and the journey into the afterlife. In China, red is considered lucky and is tied to life-giving forces like the sun, blood, and fire. It is also the color of celebrations and good fortune.

CuratorialText: Red is still associated with festivities and happiness in Chinese culture today. Learn more about Lunar New Year, when families decorate windows with red paper cuttings and adorn doors with couplets expressing auspicious wishes. Watch and learn how Chinese paper cuttings are made, from the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage.
persona3_adapted: Red continues to symbolize celebration and joy in Chinese culture today. Discover more about Lunar New Year, a time when families decorate their windows with red paper cuttings and hang couplets on doors—paired lines of poetry that express good wishes for the year ahead. Watch a demonstration of how Chinese paper cuttings are created, presented by the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage.

CuratorialText: Red can carry more negative associations as well, such as with warfare, rage, and destruction. The color of blood, there is a visceral quality to red that resonates uniquely with the human psyche. Haunted by the traces that the human body leaves behind, contemporary artist Chiharu Shiota’s 2015 installation “Over the Continents” amassed personal memories through an accumulation of nearly four hundred individual shoes. Collected by the artist, each shoe came with a note from the donor describing lost individuals and past moments. Almost four miles of red yarn wrapped around the shoes and attached to a single point in the museum’s Sackler pavilion, evoking the artist’s attention to the fundamental ties that connect humans to one another and the world.
persona3_adapted: Red can also carry more negative meanings, such as war, rage, and destruction. As the color of blood, it has a powerful, gut-level effect on the human mind that is unlike any other color. Haunted by the marks the human body leaves behind, contemporary artist Chiharu Shiota created a 2015 installation called “Over the Continents.” The work brought together personal memories through a collection of nearly four hundred individual shoes. The artist gathered each shoe, and each one came with a note from the person who donated it, describing lost individuals or past moments. Nearly four miles of red thread wrapped around the shoes and connected them to a single point in the museum’s Sackler pavilion. This arrangement highlights the artist’s focus on the fundamental bonds that link people to one another and to the world.

CuratorialText: Whether it’s in the form of growing plants, precious stones, or deliberately blended pigments, there’s a human impulse to surround ourselves with green in the art we create, the environments we inhabit, and the spaces we build. Green evokes fertile landscapes, growth and prosperity, and even health and wellness. Learn about how green has been made and understood across various cultures and traditions represented in the museum’s collections.
persona3_adapted: Whether it appears in the form of living plants, precious stones, or carefully mixed paints, there is a human drive to surround ourselves with the color green—in the art we make, the places we live, and the spaces we design. Green brings to mind fertile landscapes, growth, prosperity, and even health and well-being. Explore how this color has been created and interpreted across different cultures and traditions represented in the museum’s collections.

CuratorialText: While there aren’t many plant sources of green dyes in the art world, some Indian paintings use beetle wings to mimic shiny green emeralds. In very special works of art, emeralds could be carved to make unique objects, or were made into jewelry.
persona3_adapted: While green dyes from plants are relatively rare in historical art, certain Indian paintings achieve a shimmering, emerald-like green by using beetle wings. In particularly exceptional artworks, actual emeralds might be carved into one-of-a-kind objects or set into jewelry.

CuratorialText: The green patina on copper and bronze objects occurs as the copper corrodes. Patina occurs naturally when copper comes into contact with salty or acidic surroundings. See, for instance, how a range of corrosion products affects the surface color of the ancient Chinese bronze ritual vessel below: most of the surface is covered with a smooth, tin oxide-stained pale green with copper compounds, but there are also numerous scattered small patches of dull red cuprite, and the bright, powdery green seen here and there represents copper chloride. Explore this object in 3D.
persona3_adapted: The green coating you see on copper and bronze objects forms as the metal corrodes. This natural process happens when copper is exposed to salty or acidic environments. For example, look at the ancient Chinese bronze ritual vessel below: most of its surface has a smooth, pale green layer tinted by tin oxide and copper compounds. Scattered across it are small, dull red patches of a copper mineral called cuprite, while the bright, powdery green spots you see here and there are caused by copper chloride. You can explore this object in 3D.

CuratorialText: Copper corrosion products can be taken advantage of to create man-made copper green pigments; more commonly, however, the green mineral malachite can be ground to make paints. Malachite is often used in paintings in China.
persona3_adapted: Copper corrosion can be harnessed to produce artificial green pigments, but the more typical method involves grinding the green mineral malachite into paint. This mineral-based pigment appears frequently in Chinese painting traditions.

CuratorialText:
Fine-ground malachite. Image courtesy of Conservation and Scientific Research, National Museum of Asian Art, Smithsonian Institution
Copper, together with lead, is often the basis of green glazes in ceramics. Mixtures of copper with yellow particles of lead tin oxide or lead antimony oxide are also used for green enamels.
persona3_adapted:
Fine-ground malachite. Image courtesy of Conservation and Scientific Research, National Museum of Asian Art, Smithsonian Institution

Copper, often combined with lead, is a common ingredient in the green glazes found on ceramics. To create green enamels—a type of glassy coating—copper is also mixed with yellow particles, such as lead tin oxide or lead antimony oxide.

CuratorialText: “Celadon” refers to a large family of high-fired ceramics. Their glazes are colored by small percentages of iron oxide. When fired in an oxygen-deprived, or reducing, atmosphere, the glazes turn variable shades of bluish green, traditionally described with the French word céladon. The word is commonly believed to have been derived from a seventeenth-century French drama, L’Astrée, in which a shepherd named Céladon appears dressed in a gray-green robe. Some modern scholars prefer the term “green ware,” but the poetic French name is more likely to remind a reader that celadon ceramics can exhibit a broad range of colors, from sky blue to sea green.
persona3_adapted: "Celadon" is a term used for a broad category of ceramics that are fired at very high temperatures. Their glossy surface, or glaze, gets its color from small amounts of iron oxide. When these ceramics are fired in a kiln with very little oxygen—a process called reduction firing—the glaze turns into various shades of bluish green. This color is traditionally described by the French word *céladon*. The name is thought to come from a 17th-century French play, *L’Astrée*, where a shepherd named Céladon wears a gray-green robe. While some modern scholars prefer the more straightforward term "green ware," the poetic French name helps remind us that celadon ceramics can range widely in color, from sky blue to sea green.

CuratorialText: Jade was the first true luxury medium to emerge in ancient China. Although the term is used to refer to a range of hard, beautifully figured stones, most Chinese Neolithic, Bronze Age, and early imperial period examples have been scientifically identified as nephrite, geologically different from the bright, apple green jadeite used in much later times. Composed of interlocked, fibrous crystals and occurring as pebbles and boulders in a range of colors (due to iron, chromium, manganese, or similar materials fused in the rock), nephrite is an incredibly hard stone. It cannot be carved or even scratched by metal but instead must be ground into shape using quartz and other tough minerals. These qualities and its rarity inspired ancient Chinese people to value it very highly and to reserve its use for special purposes, such as for jewelry and ceremonial and ritual objects.
persona3_adapted: Jade was the first truly luxurious material to appear in ancient China. While the word "jade" is often used for a variety of hard, beautifully patterned stones, most examples from China’s Neolithic period, Bronze Age, and early imperial era have been scientifically identified as nephrite. This is a different mineral from the bright, apple-green jadeite that became popular much later. Nephrite is made up of tightly interlocked, fibrous crystals and is found as pebbles and boulders in many colors—these colors come from traces of iron, chromium, manganese, or similar elements fused within the rock. It is an extremely hard stone. You cannot carve or even scratch it with metal; instead, it must be ground into shape using quartz and other tough minerals. Because of these qualities and its rarity, ancient Chinese people valued nephrite very highly and reserved it for special uses, such as jewelry and objects used in ceremonies and rituals.

CuratorialText: According to his close friend Agnes Meyer, when Charles Lang Freer was ill near the end of his life, he often held a piece of jade in his hand, believing with an almost religious faith in the comforting and restorative powers of the stone. Freer collected ancient Chinese jades in large numbers, when most other Western collectors showed little interest. Ultimately, there were more than 500 pieces in his collection, spanning more than 5,000 years.
persona3_adapted: According to his close friend Agnes Meyer, near the end of his life, when Charles Lang Freer was ill, he often held a piece of jade in his hand. He believed—with an almost religious faith—that the stone had the power to comfort and restore him. Freer amassed a large collection of ancient Chinese jades at a time when most other Western collectors showed little interest in them. In the end, his collection included more than 500 pieces, spanning more than 5,000 years of history.

CuratorialText: Green is so prevalent in the flora of the natural world that the color easily conjures lush environments teeming with plants. Life, growth, and regeneration are all concepts that come to mind in the context of verdant landscapes. In Southeast Asia, during the ninth through the thirteenth century, constructing cities required carving out spaces from the verdant, tropical environment. The jungle had to be systematically cultivated; water had to be diverted into reservoirs, moats, and canals; and the landscape had to be carefully tended. When population centers shifted due to political, ecological, and religious changes, the tropical landscape readily encroached on the built environment, transforming once urban landscapes back into jungle. This environment informed Southeast Asian artists: on temple walls and sculptures, plants and flowers grow on thick stalks, vines curlicue, and sprays of foliage transform surfaces into lush landscapes.
persona3_adapted: Green is so common in the plants of the natural world that the color easily brings to mind rich environments full of vegetation. Life, growth, and renewal are all ideas that come to mind when we think of lush, green landscapes. In Southeast Asia, between the ninth and thirteenth centuries, building cities meant carving out spaces from the dense, tropical surroundings. The jungle had to be carefully managed; water had to be channeled into reservoirs, moats, and canals; and the landscape had to be thoughtfully maintained. When population centers shifted due to political, environmental, or religious changes, the tropical landscape quickly reclaimed the built areas, turning once-urban spaces back into jungle. This environment shaped how Southeast Asian artists worked: on temple walls and sculptures, plants and flowers grow on thick stems, vines curl in spirals, and sprays of leaves turn surfaces into rich, plant-filled scenes.

CuratorialText: Set in a garden, the image depicts a group of angels, referred to as huris or paris in Persian, entertaining their queen by playing music, dancing, and serving food and wine. The drawing's lively mood echoes other Persian representations of outdoor banquets or bazm (a form of celebration with wine and music) ceremonies, with the exception that this fine drawing features heavenly creatures--a favorite subject matter in sixteenth-and seventeenth-century Iran and Turkey--instead of the princely figures seen in illustrations to lyrical poetry by Hafiz and Assar.
persona3_adapted: Set in a garden, the image shows a group of angels—called *huris* or *paris* in Persian—entertaining their queen with music, dancing, and the serving of food and wine. The lively mood of the drawing is similar to other Persian scenes of outdoor banquets or *bazm* (a type of celebration that includes wine and music). However, in this case, the artwork features heavenly beings instead of the royal figures you might find in illustrations for the lyrical poetry of Hafiz and Assar. Such angelic subjects were especially popular in sixteenth- and seventeenth-century Iran and Turkey.

CuratorialText: Referred to as peri winged celestial creatures are among some of the most popular subjects in sixteenth-century Ottoman and Persian drawings and paintings. The exquisitely rendered composition typifies a distinct style of Ottoman drawing that combines sweeping, calligraphic lines with soft, feathery strokes, as is evident in the minute designs on the figure's robe, cup, and bottle. Light washes of color add a sense of volume and texture to the form of this heavenly being.
persona3_adapted: In sixteenth-century Ottoman and Persian art, winged celestial beings known as *peri* were among the most popular subjects in drawings and paintings. This beautifully detailed composition is a classic example of a distinct Ottoman drawing style, which blends sweeping, calligraphic lines with soft, feathery strokes. You can see this clearly in the tiny patterns on the figure's robe, cup, and bottle. Light washes of color give the heavenly being a sense of depth and texture.

CuratorialText: Four-part inro in form of flattened rectangle with rounded corners (probably derived from the coin called ryo). The inset rim of each of the three compartments fits into the base of the compartment above it or into the lid. The four components are secured by a blue-green silk cord passing through two holes in each of the elements, tied in a bow below and secured by the bead (ojime) above and atttached to the toggle (netsuke) at the end.
persona3_adapted: This small, four-part container (called an *inro*) is shaped like a flattened rectangle with rounded corners—a form that likely comes from a type of coin known as a *ryo*. Each of its three inner sections has a raised rim that fits snugly into the base of the piece above it or into the lid. A blue-green silk cord holds all four parts together, passing through two holes in each section. The cord is tied in a bow at the bottom, secured by a small bead (the *ojime*) above, and attached to a carved toggle (the *netsuke*) at the end.

CuratorialText: Box, small cylindrical, with cover of equal size with the box proper. Clay: hard, grayish. Glaze: pale greenish-gray celadon, with faint lilac tints. Decoration: inlaid in white and black under glaze: medallion of two flying cranes surrounded by geometric borders.
persona3_adapted: This small cylindrical box comes with a lid that matches it in size. It is made of hard, grayish clay. The surface is covered in a pale greenish-gray glaze, known as celadon, which has subtle hints of lilac. The decoration is created using an inlay technique, where white and black materials are set into the clay before the glaze is applied. The design features a central medallion showing two flying cranes, surrounded by borders made of geometric patterns.

CuratorialText: A straight-sided cup which angles inward about three-quarters of the way toward a flattened bottom. The walls of the cup are very thin and this type of ware has been referred to as egg-shell ware. The fabric is a pinkish beige color. The inside of the cup is unpainted. The details on the outside of the cup are painted in brownish-black and red on an upainted background. The same motif of a red lotus bud shape outlined in brownish-black and tied with ribbons of the same brownish-black color is repeated seven times around the cup. The ornament color is repeated seven times around the cup. The ornament extends from the edge of the cup to about the middle of the cup. Within the cup are remains of a black powdery substance.
persona3_adapted:
This cup has straight sides that gently angle inward about three-quarters of the way down to a flattened base. Its walls are exceptionally thin—so much so that this kind of pottery has sometimes been called "egg-shell ware." The material itself is a pinkish beige color.

The inside of the cup is left unpainted. On the outside, the decoration is painted in brownish-black and red against the bare surface. A repeating pattern appears seven times around the cup: a red lotus bud shape, outlined in brownish-black and tied with ribbons of the same dark color. This ornament runs from the rim down to about the middle of the cup.

Inside the cup, traces of a black, powdery substance remain.

CuratorialText: This is one of several paintings of shop fronts that Whistler completed while in St. Ives. Sympathetic critics singled it out for enthusiastic praise when Whistler exhibited it in London in 1884. One declared, it "is probably the most perfect little thing of its kind that was ever wrought by an artist who has learnt how to see….It is without detail, without apparent labour, without dramatic interest; but it is exquisite in colour, faultless in tone, and its well-considered mystery has, at least, the interest of suggestiveness."
persona3_adapted: This is one of several paintings of storefronts that Whistler completed while in St. Ives. Supportive critics singled it out for enthusiastic praise when Whistler exhibited it in London in 1884. One declared it "is probably the most perfect little thing of its kind that was ever wrought by an artist who has learnt how to see….It is without detail, without apparent labour, without dramatic interest; but it is exquisite in colour, faultless in tone, and its well-considered mystery has, at least, the interest of suggestiveness."

CuratorialText:
Plate with a flat rim and peacock in the center. Clay: buff-colored. Glaze: transparent over blue, green, red and black painted over a white paste. Decoration: Peacock amidst flowers in the center with a scroll of half-palmettes on the rim; eight blossoms on the exterior.
Greek letters on label: (^o^) and No. 48.
persona3_adapted: This plate has a flat rim and features a peacock at its center. It is made from buff-colored clay. The surface is covered with a transparent glaze, which sits over a white base layer painted in blue, green, red, and black. The central decoration shows a peacock surrounded by flowers, while the rim is adorned with a flowing pattern of half-palmettes—a leaf-like motif often used in decorative art. On the outside of the plate, there are eight blossoms. A small label on the piece includes Greek letters and the number 48.

CuratorialText: Molded Yue-ware vessels like this were made in large quantities, but their function is unclear. They may have held a candle, or perhaps scholars dipped their writing brushes in the water-filled interior. In the Jin dynasty (265-420 CE), mythical lions, with exaggeratedly curled manes, and winged chimeras were protective images; huge stone examples guarded important tombs.
persona3_adapted: Molded Yue-ware vessels—a type of early Chinese ceramic—were produced in large numbers, but their exact purpose remains uncertain. They might have been used to hold a candle, or perhaps scholars dipped their writing brushes into the water-filled interior. During the Jin dynasty (265–420 CE), mythical lions with dramatically curled manes and winged chimeras—creatures combining features of different animals—were seen as protective symbols. Large stone versions of these figures were placed to guard important tombs.

CuratorialText: A pair of blue and white molded porcelain beaker vases [F1992.47.1 and .2] decorated with freely drawn feather, foliate scroll and scattered flowerheads, each with a leaf or conch shell "emblem" mark on base. Part of a five-piece garniture set with three jars.
persona3_adapted: A pair of blue-and-white porcelain vases, shaped like tall, flared beakers, each decorated with loosely painted feather patterns, curling leaf-like scrolls, and scattered flower blossoms. On the base of each vase is a small mark shaped like a leaf or a conch shell—a type of maker's or workshop emblem. These vases were originally part of a five-piece decorative set, which also included three jars.

CuratorialText:
The Kiyotaki Waterfall is located along the route of the Tokaido, the great road passing through the eastern provinces of Japan and linking the city of Edo (now Tokyo), seat of the Tokugawa shogunate, with Kyoto, the capital and home of the emperor.
In this print from the series, Shokoku taki meguri (Famous Waterfalls in Various Provinces), the Kiyotaki Waterfall descends in branching rivulets over a sheer, rock face. In the foreground, pilgrims ascend from a teahouse toward a shrine dedicated to Kannon, a Buddhist deity associated with compassion. The remoteness and altitude of this scene is emphasized by the bands of clouds at the lower left.
persona3_adapted:
The Kiyotaki Waterfall lies along the route of the Tokaido, a major road that connected the eastern provinces of Japan. This road linked Edo (now Tokyo), the seat of the Tokugawa military government, with Kyoto, the imperial capital and home of the emperor.

This print is part of a series titled *Shokoku taki meguri*, or *Famous Waterfalls in Various Provinces*. Here, the Kiyotaki Waterfall cascades in branching streams down a steep, rocky cliff. In the foreground, pilgrims are shown climbing up from a teahouse toward a shrine dedicated to Kannon, a Buddhist figure known for compassion. The scene’s sense of remoteness and high altitude is reinforced by the bands of clouds visible in the lower left corner.

CuratorialText: Small dish with pleated rim. Clay: buff pottery. Glaze: transparent glaze with olive greenish tone (Ofuke glaze), large irregular cracks stained brown; glaze deeper green in tone where thick. Interior of footrim glazed; glazed wiped off outside of footrim and surrounding base. Decoration: pleating on narrow everted rim. The form of this dish with fluted rim distantly echoes the shape of Chinese Longquan-ware celadon dishes.
persona3_adapted: This small dish has a rim that is folded into a series of narrow, vertical ridges, like the edge of a pie crust. It is made from a light brown clay. The surface is covered in a transparent glaze that has an olive-green tint—a type known as Ofuke glaze. The glaze has developed large, irregular cracks that have been stained brown over time, and it appears a deeper green in the thicker areas. The inside of the footrim (the raised ring on the bottom that the dish rests on) is glazed, but the glaze has been wiped away from the outside of the footrim and the area around the base. The decoration is limited to the pleating on the narrow, outward-turned rim. The overall shape of this dish, with its fluted rim, is a distant echo of Chinese celadon dishes from the Longquan kilns.

CuratorialText:
The Khamsa, or Quintet, by Nizami ranks among the great masterpieces of Persian literature. The romance of Khosraw and Shirin, one of the five poems that constitute the Khamsa, narrates the adventures of the Sasanian king Khusraw Parviz and the Armenian princess Shirin. Attributed to the early fifteenth century, the Freer Khamsa is one of the earliest known illustrated copies of this work.
Khosraw at the Gate of Shirin's Castle is among the most poignantly expressive manuscript paintings of the fifteenth century. Displeased with Khosraw's behavior and his marriage to another woman, Shirin has withdrawn to her castle. Following the death of his queen, Khosraw repeatedly attempts to see Shirin, who finally yields to his request. Shielded by the royal umbrella and standing on precious brocades and carpets, the king gestures in supplication toward Shirin, who gazes down from a window in her castle. Although the text specifies that Khosraw visited Shirin at dawn in the dead of winter, the artist has situated the reconciliation in a lush garden setting softly illuminated by a waning moon. The elegant, mustachioed Khosraw in these paintings bears a striking resemblance to known depictions of the Timurid patron Baysunghur Mirza (1399-1434).
persona3_adapted:
The *Khamsa* (or *Quintet*) by the Persian poet Nizami is considered one of the great literary masterpieces of the Persian-speaking world. One of its five poems, *The Romance of Khosraw and Shirin*, tells the story of the Sasanian king Khusraw Parviz and the Armenian princess Shirin. The Freer *Khamsa*, believed to have been created in the early 1400s, is one of the earliest known copies of this work to include painted illustrations.

The painting *Khosraw at the Gate of Shirin's Castle* is one of the most emotionally powerful manuscript illustrations of the 1400s. In the story, Shirin has withdrawn to her castle because she is upset with Khosraw’s behavior and his marriage to another woman. After his queen dies, Khosraw tries repeatedly to see Shirin, and she eventually agrees to meet him. In the image, the king stands under a royal umbrella on expensive fabrics and carpets, gesturing humbly toward Shirin, who looks down from a castle window. Although the poem says Khosraw visited Shirin at dawn in the middle of winter, the artist has placed the scene in a lush garden, softly lit by a fading moon. The elegant, mustachioed Khosraw in these paintings closely resembles known portraits of the Timurid prince Baysunghur Mirza (1399–1434), who likely commissioned the work.

CuratorialText: Set in the London home of Whistler's brother-in-law Seymour Haden, this painting was originally titled The Morning Call. The woman reflected in the mirror at the left is Whistler's half sister Deborah Haden. The seated girl is his niece, Annie Haden. The standing woman, Miss Isabella Boott, was connected to the Haden family by marriage. Instead of trying to create a convincing illusion of three-dimensional space, Whistler adopted a color scheme and sharply angled perspective that emphasize the flatness of the picture plane.
persona3_adapted: Set in the London home of Whistler's brother-in-law, Seymour Haden, this painting was originally called *The Morning Call*. The woman reflected in the mirror on the left is Whistler's half-sister, Deborah Haden. The seated girl is his niece, Annie Haden. The standing woman, Miss Isabella Boott, was connected to the Haden family through marriage. Rather than trying to create a realistic sense of depth, Whistler used a carefully chosen color palette and a sharply tilted viewpoint to draw attention to the painting's flat surface.

CuratorialText: Popular tales and lively images played a role in transmitting beliefs and values to the Jain community. The Shripalarasa is a narrative that recounts the wondrous adventures of the Jain Prince Shripala, who travels to distant lands in search of trade and wealth. In this unusually large and vivacious painting, the artist represents the Prince's ship as a British vessel. The inscriptions scattered across the painting, which repetively identify parts of the ship, indicate a fascination with seafaring. The depiction of the Union Jack lends immediacy to a story that took place long before the British came to India.
persona3_adapted: Folk stories and lively pictures helped pass down beliefs and values within the Jain community. The *Shripalarasa* is a tale that follows the remarkable adventures of the Jain Prince Shripala, who journeys to far-off lands in search of trade and wealth. In this unusually large and energetic painting, the artist shows the Prince's ship as a British vessel. Labels scattered across the image repeatedly identify parts of the ship, revealing a fascination with seafaring. The presence of the Union Jack brings a sense of immediacy to a story set long before the British arrived in India.

CuratorialText: Funeral vase: tall, slender, on hollowed, spreading feet. Wood stand. Clay: soft, light-weight, gray-white. Glaze: thin, transparent green celadon; close brown crackle. Areas of stain and of over-baked wrinkled glaze. Decoration: hieratic figures and a dragon supporting the sun-disk - in applied relief, under glaze.
persona3_adapted: This tall, slender vase stands on a hollowed, flared base, which is set into a wooden stand. It is made from a soft, lightweight, gray-white clay. The surface is covered with a thin, transparent green glaze known as celadon, which has developed a fine pattern of brown cracks. Some areas are stained, and others show a wrinkled glaze from being over-fired. The decoration features formal, ceremonial figures and a dragon holding up a sun disk—these designs were pressed onto the surface in raised relief before the glaze was applied.

CuratorialText: The Tale of Shuten Doji has long been popular in Japan; the earliest-known illustrated version of this tale dates to the fourteenth century. Minamoto no Yorimitsu (948-1021), a historical figure of great martial prowess, was transformed in popular literature into a legendary hero who rescued the realm from demonic forces. Shuten Doji, a monster who lived southwest of Kyoto, terrorized the population, dining on locals and holding young maidens captive. With divine assistance and various means of subterfuge, Yorimitsu beheaded the ogre and returned the region to tranquility. Kyosai's interest in the narrative format and his careful connoisseurship of ancient works of art are evident in his paintings and sketchbooks. Riddled with macabre humor and images of the grotesque, his own paintings catered to the tastes of late Edo (1615-1868) and early Meiji (1868-1912) audiences.
persona3_adapted: The Tale of Shuten Doji has long been a popular story in Japan; the earliest known illustrated version dates back to the fourteenth century. Minamoto no Yorimitsu (948-1021), a real historical figure famous for his military skill, was transformed in popular literature into a legendary hero who saved the land from demonic forces. Shuten Doji, a monster living southwest of Kyoto, terrorized the population by eating locals and holding young women captive. With help from the gods and through various tricks and deceptions, Yorimitsu beheaded the ogre and restored peace to the region. Kyosai's interest in telling stories through pictures, as well as his careful study of older artworks, is clear in his paintings and sketchbooks. Filled with dark humor and disturbing images, his own paintings appealed to the tastes of audiences in the late Edo period (1615-1868) and early Meiji period (1868-1912).

CuratorialText: Hassam visited Oregon during the summer of 1908, completing this view of Portland's Chinatown after his return to New York. The subject matter is unusual for Hassam, but recalls a series of shop-front scenes painted by Whistler in the 1880s. Like Whistler, Hassam treated the street as a shallow stage set, emphasizing the geometrical patterns created by the play of variously colored rectangular shaped-bricks, signs, windows, doors, walls, sidewalk, alley, and the white diagonal of broken light splayed across the near foreground.
persona3_adapted: Hassam spent the summer of 1908 in Oregon, and painted this scene of Portland's Chinatown after he returned to New York. The subject is unusual for him, but it recalls a series of storefront paintings that Whistler made in the 1880s. Like Whistler, Hassam treated the street as if it were a shallow stage, drawing attention to the geometric patterns created by the arrangement of differently colored rectangular bricks, signs, windows, doors, walls, sidewalk, and alley. He also emphasized the white diagonal of broken light spreading across the front of the scene.

CuratorialText: Tall, blue and white glazed porcelain vase, with long neck, round bulbous body and "garlic"-shaped neck/mouth. Round ring base, slightly uneven. The body decorated with scrolling, flowering vines and animals.
persona3_adapted: This tall vase is made of porcelain, finished in a glossy blue-and-white glaze. It has a long neck, a rounded, bulbous body, and a mouth shaped like a garlic bulb. The base is a round ring, slightly uneven. Across the body, the decoration shows curling, flowering vines and animals.

CuratorialText: Legendary and theatrical references abound in the decoration of the cover of this lacquer box for an inkstone, a water dropper, and writing brushes. The Wisteria Maiden (Fuji Musume), whose silvery facial features have oxidized to bluish black, is a common subject of Otsu-e, a type of folk painting sold in the town of Otsu near Lake Biwa. In nineteenth-century kabuki, the Wisteria Maiden became the focus of a dance expressing unrequited love to the vocal and instrumental music of a nagauta (literally, "long song"). With her is a praying demon, another figure often seen in Otsu-e. The Thunder God rushes down from above, beating his ring of drums as the couple takes shelter from a rainstorm. The ornate pictorial decoration of this box was created by an artist who had mastered maki-e techniques, which employ powders and particles of gold and other material to create intricate designs.
persona3_adapted: Legendary and theatrical stories inspired the elaborate decoration on the lid of this lacquer box, which holds an inkstone, a water dropper, and writing brushes. The Wisteria Maiden (Fuji Musume)—whose silver facial features have darkened to a bluish black over time—is a common figure in Otsu-e, a type of folk painting sold in the town of Otsu near Lake Biwa. In nineteenth-century kabuki theater, the Wisteria Maiden became the central character in a dance about unrequited love, performed to the vocal and instrumental music of a nagauta (literally, "long song"). Beside her is a praying demon, another familiar figure in Otsu-e. Above them, the Thunder God rushes down from the sky, beating his ring of drums as the couple takes shelter from a rainstorm. The ornate pictorial decoration on this box was created by an artist who had mastered maki-e techniques, which use gold powders and particles of other materials to produce intricate designs.

CuratorialText: Bowl, conical, flaring; deep foot with four silica spur-marks underneath on base inside footrim. Six notches in rim. Clay: dense, sonant. Glaze: gray-green celadon; crackled in long lines. Decoration: molded and incised in paste under glaze: rosette in bottom; hibiscus flowers and foliage filling sides: single line incised below rim.
persona3_adapted:
This bowl has a conical shape that flares outward at the top, with a deep foot—the base that supports it. On the underside of the foot, inside the rim, you can see four small marks left by silica spurs, which were used to hold the bowl during firing. The rim itself has six small notches.

The clay body is dense and produces a clear, ringing sound when tapped. The glaze is a gray-green color, known as celadon, and it has developed a pattern of long, fine cracks across the surface. The decoration was created by molding and carving into the clay before the glaze was applied: a rosette (a rose-like design) sits in the center of the bowl’s interior, while hibiscus flowers and leaves fill the sides. A single incised line runs just below the rim.

CuratorialText: Depicts a hilly landscape, with a scraggly tree, and a Central Asian groom in a red jacket holding the bridle of a dappled horse with trappings and a saddle. Spurious inscription and one seal, no colophons or labels.
persona3_adapted: This painting shows a hilly landscape with a sparse, uneven tree. In the scene, a groom from Central Asia—wearing a red jacket—holds the bridle of a dappled horse. The horse is outfitted with decorative gear and a saddle. The work includes a false or added inscription and one seal, but it has no colophons or labels (that is, no explanatory notes or identifying text from the artist or later owners).

CuratorialText: A rectangular, off-white frit tile with an opaque white underglaze. Overglaze painted with yellow, green, turquoise, blue and manganese black/purple outlines depicting a bird perched on a fruit tree branch. Sides and back are unglazed.
persona3_adapted: This rectangular tile is made from a material called frit—a type of glass-based ceramic that was ground into a powder and then fired. Its surface is a soft off-white, achieved by applying an opaque white layer beneath the main decoration. On top of that layer, the artist painted a scene using vivid colors: yellow, green, turquoise, blue, and a dark purple-black for the outlines. The image shows a bird perched on the branch of a fruit tree. The sides and back of the tile were left unglazed, meaning they were not coated with the glassy finish that gives the front its sheen.

CuratorialText: Painting is one of a series of eight views of Tokyo by Ogata Gekko. This view of a racetrack focuses on the action in the foreground, where spectators are enthralled by the competition. Contained in modern wooden box with lid.
persona3_adapted: This painting is part of a series of eight scenes of Tokyo by the artist Ogata Gekko. In this particular view of a racetrack, the artist draws our attention to the action in the foreground, where the crowd is completely absorbed in the excitement of the race. The work is housed in a modern wooden box with a lid.

CuratorialText: Folio from a dispersed copy of Yusuf u Zulaykha by Jami; Persian in black nasta'liq script on gold-flecked paper; heading in red; recto: text: 2 columns, 14 lines; verso: text: 2 columns, 14 lines; one of a group of 13. Border: The recto is set in gold, green, and black rulings in a margin with birds, floral, and leaf scrolls. The verso is set in gold, green, and black rulings in a margin with lions, bulls, foxes, rabbits,and birds in a landscape.
persona3_adapted:
This page comes from a scattered copy of *Yusuf u Zulaykha*, a classic Persian poem by the writer Jami. The text is written in black ink in a flowing, elegant script called *nasta'liq*, on paper decorated with flecks of gold. The heading appears in red ink. On both the front and back of the page, the text is arranged in two columns, with fourteen lines per column. This page is one of a set of thirteen.

The front of the page is framed by borders in gold, green, and black, with a margin filled with birds, flowers, and curling leaves. The back of the page has similar gold, green, and black borders, but its margin shows a landscape scene with lions, bulls, foxes, rabbits, and birds.

CuratorialText: Squared, hollow cylinder of the type ts’ung [cong] 琮; narrow projecting collar at both ends; rich cream and tans; decoration: incised and in low relief, stylized masks and double bands of fine lines on corners. (Collar chipped.)
persona3_adapted: This object is a hollow, square-bodied cylinder with a narrow, projecting rim at both the top and bottom. It belongs to an ancient Chinese type known as a *cong* (pronounced "tsong"). The surface is a rich mix of cream and tan colors. The decoration is carved and lightly raised, featuring stylized mask-like faces and paired bands of fine lines along the corners. (Note: the rim is chipped.)

CuratorialText: One of a pair of blue and white vases, Kangxi period, each of trumpet shape form with flared foot, after a bronze Ku, each of the three sections with large loose lotus scroll, the central lobed section encircled by a diaper filled band.
persona3_adapted: One of a pair of blue-and-white vases from the Kangxi period (1661–1722). Each vase has a trumpet-shaped body and a flared foot, a form inspired by an ancient Chinese bronze vessel called a *gu*. The vase is divided into three sections, each decorated with large, flowing lotus scrolls. The central, lobed section is encircled by a band filled with a repeating diamond pattern, known as a diaper pattern.

CuratorialText: Pitcher, of a full-bodied pear form, on a high spreading foot; plaster restorations including handle and foot. Clay: soft, sandy, white. Glaze: turquoise green, partly disintegrated and having a silver iridescence.
persona3_adapted: This pitcher has a rounded, pear-like shape and stands on a tall, flared base. The handle and base have been restored with plaster. The clay is soft, sandy, and white. The glaze is a turquoise green, but it has partly worn away, and in some areas it shows a silvery, shimmering effect.

CuratorialText: Bowl, deep ovoidal; low foot with kiln-slag attachments. Clay: hard. Glaze: brilliant olive-gray, finely crackled. Decoration: molded in the paste in light relief, under glaze; single large floral motif (peony?) filling bottom, lower half of walls, difficult to decipher under glaze.
persona3_adapted: This bowl has a deep, egg-like shape and sits on a short base, which still has small bits of kiln material stuck to it from the firing process. The clay body is hard, and the glaze—a glossy olive-gray tone—shows a fine network of tiny cracks, a common effect in ceramics of this type. The decoration was created by pressing a design into the clay before glazing, resulting in a subtle, raised pattern. A single large floral motif, possibly a peony, fills the bottom and lower sides of the bowl, though the design is difficult to make out clearly beneath the glaze.

CuratorialText: Clay: dense, resonant, buff stoneware. Glaze: transparent, crackled, over white slip; unglazed area stained blackish-brown. Decoration: in cobalt blue, under glaze: linear patterns representing maple leaves and water or pampas grass; geometric bands on sides of lid and base. Square seal on underside of lid.
persona3_adapted:
This object is made of a dense, buff-colored stoneware clay that feels solid and produces a resonant sound when tapped. Its surface is covered with a transparent glaze that has developed a fine network of cracks, a natural effect that occurs over time. Beneath this glaze, the potter applied a white slip—a thin layer of liquid clay—to create a smooth, pale background. One area was left unglazed and has absorbed a dark, blackish-brown stain from use or firing.

The decoration is painted in cobalt blue, a deep blue pigment that was applied before the final glazing. The design consists of flowing, linear patterns that suggest maple leaves and either water or pampas grass. Geometric bands frame the sides of both the lid and the base. On the underside of the lid, a small square seal marks the maker or workshop.

CuratorialText: Tall blue and white glazed porcelain vase, with bulbous lower body, cylindrical center body with pinched "snake" handles and slightly outflaring rim. Porcelain body with dark blue cobalt underglaze with gray-blue-white glaze. Floral decorative patterns.
persona3_adapted: This tall porcelain vase features a striking blue-and-white glaze. Its form is composed of a rounded lower section, a straight central body, and a rim that flares slightly outward. Two narrow, pinched handles—often described as "snake" handles—curve from the neck to the shoulders. The decoration is painted in a deep cobalt blue beneath a translucent, grayish-blue-white glaze, and the surface is covered with floral patterns.

CuratorialText: Dish, low, with rounded sides; one chip on rim; hair-line crack. Clay: white porcelain. Glaze: clear, feldspathic; a few pinholes and impurity flecks. Decoration: painted in underglaze cobalt blue of deep tone, copying a traditional design of the Ch'eng-hua period. See F1951.10 and .11. Center inside, "three friends of winter" (pine, plum, and bamboo) with garden rock, and fungus, enclosed by double line band which is repeated at rim. Outside, following model, a scene of figures on a garden terrace with plants and trees.
persona3_adapted:
This dish has low, rounded sides, with a small chip on the rim and a hairline crack. It is made of white porcelain. The clear glaze, which contains feldspar (a type of mineral that helps create a glassy surface), shows a few tiny holes and specks of impurities. The decoration is painted in a deep blue pigment applied beneath the glaze, following a traditional design from the Ch'eng-hua period (an era in Chinese history known for its refined ceramics). For related examples, see F1951.10 and .11.

On the inside center, the design features the "three friends of winter"—pine, plum, and bamboo—alongside a garden rock and a fungus. These elements are enclosed by a double line, a pattern repeated at the rim. On the outside, following the original model, there is a scene of figures on a garden terrace, surrounded by plants and trees.

CuratorialText: Individual serving bowl (mukozuke [JPN]). Black lacquer rim. Red lacquer repairs. Clay: hard, light-fawn. Shiny brown on surface. Glaze: yellow and gray over blue-green; inside--brilliant brown and blue. Outside: compounded with iron and rice-straw ash. Inside: clear glaze.
persona3_adapted:
This small bowl, used for individual servings in Japanese dining, is called a *mukozuke*. Its rim is finished in black lacquer, and red lacquer has been used to repair cracks or chips—a technique that highlights rather than hides the object's history.

The bowl is made from a hard, light tan clay. Its surface has a glossy brown finish. The glaze—a glassy coating applied to the pottery—combines yellow and gray tones over a blue-green base. Inside the bowl, the glaze is a vivid mix of brown and blue.

On the outside, the glaze was created by mixing iron with ash from burned rice straw, a traditional method that gives the surface its distinctive texture and color. Inside, a clear glaze was used, allowing the clay body and the bright colors beneath to show through.

CuratorialText: White clay, pinkish buff where exposed. Decoration of grasses incised under clear brown wash; hatching incised under clear glaze (body indented in each area); and flower and grasses painted in black overglaze enamel. Kiln mark stamped on base inside foot rim. Thick iron wash over half, appearing dull yellowish brown; clear glaze on other half of body, appearing gray where it partially overlaps wash. Five spur marks (from clay wads) on foot rim.
persona3_adapted: This piece is made from white clay, which takes on a pinkish-buff color where the surface is left exposed. The decoration includes grasses scratched into the clay beneath a clear brown wash, as well as hatched lines—closely spaced parallel marks—carved under a clear glaze, with the body of the piece slightly indented in each area. Flowers and grasses are painted on top of the glaze in black enamel. A kiln mark is stamped on the base, inside the foot rim. Half of the piece is covered with a thick iron wash, which appears dull yellowish-brown; the other half has a clear glaze that looks gray where it partially overlaps the wash. On the foot rim, there are five spur marks—small scars left by clay supports used during firing.

CuratorialText: Bowl, small, ovoidal, on a bold, spreading foot. Cracked and repaired. Clay: soft, grayish-white. Glaze: inside, a deep aubergine; outside now covered with an iridescent patina. Decoration: painted in black under glaze.
persona3_adapted: This small, egg-shaped bowl sits on a wide, flared base. It has been cracked and repaired. The clay body is soft and grayish-white. The inside is glazed a deep eggplant purple, while the outside is now covered with a shimmering, rainbow-like surface layer. The decoration was painted in black before the glaze was applied.

CuratorialText: Mounted with F1937.39. Detached folio from a dispersed copy of Nuzhatnama-yi ala'i (Excellent book of counsel) by Shahmardan ibn Abi al-Khayr; text: Persian in black and red naskhi script; recto: illustration and text: Medicinal property of the mulberry tree, 2 lines, 3 paintings; verso: illustration and text: Pharmacological property of the olive tree, its leaves and fruits, 4 lines, 2 paintings; one of a group of three folios.
persona3_adapted: This folio, now separated from its original book, comes from a manuscript called the *Nuzhatnama-yi ‘Ala’i* (or *Excellent Book of Counsel*), written by Shahmardan ibn Abi al-Khayr. The text is in Persian, written in a clear, upright script known as *naskhi*, with black and red ink. On the front side of the page, there is an illustration and text describing the medicinal uses of the mulberry tree, along with two lines of writing and three small paintings. On the back side, the illustration and text explain the pharmacological properties of the olive tree—its leaves and fruits—with four lines of writing and two paintings. This page is one of three similar folios that once belonged to the same manuscript.

CuratorialText: Both Corvus, "The Raven," and Centaurus, "The Centaur," are constellations of the southern hemisphere. Corvus is composed of seven stars while Centaurus, whose forepart is made up of a man and hindpart of a horse, consists of 37. Al-Kazwini records that the last two stars of Centaurus are called "the two sworn upon," and according to tradition one of these passes very close to the path of Corvus. Some people, writes al-Kazwini, believe that the star belongs to "the Raven" while others insist that it is part of "The Centaur."
persona3_adapted: Both Corvus, "The Raven," and Centaurus, "The Centaur," are groups of stars visible in the southern half of the sky. Corvus is made up of seven stars, while Centaurus—which is imagined as a creature with a man's upper body and a horse's lower body—contains 37. The medieval scholar al-Kazwini notes that the last two stars of Centaurus are called "the two sworn upon." According to tradition, one of these stars passes very close to the path of Corvus. Al-Kazwini writes that some people believe this star belongs to "the Raven," while others insist it is part of "the Centaur."

CuratorialText: Bowl, large, deep, ovoidal; heavy foot; cracked. Clay: heavy, coarse, gray. Glaze: white, with tinges of green; crackled and discolored to a brownish tone. Chipped along the rim. Decoration: painted in blue and black under glaze.
persona3_adapted: This large, deep, egg-shaped bowl sits on a heavy base. It is made from thick, rough gray clay. The surface is covered in a white glaze—a glass-like coating—that has hints of green. Over time, the glaze has developed a network of fine cracks (a common effect called crackling) and has turned a brownish color in places. The rim is chipped. The bowl is decorated with blue and black paint applied before the glaze was fired, so the design is sealed beneath the glossy surface.

CuratorialText: Black-lacquered (shin nuri) cypress-wood utensil stand (shin daisu) with four black-lacquered posts and two shelves; four black-lacquered (shin nuri) cypress boards (shikiita) of various sizes for use with formal tea procedures.
persona3_adapted: This black-lacquered utensil stand is made from cypress wood and features four matching posts with two shelves. The term "black-lacquered" refers to a glossy, durable finish achieved by applying layers of lacquer—a natural resin—to the wood's surface. The stand also includes four cypress boards of different sizes, which are used in formal tea ceremonies to hold utensils or other items.

CuratorialText: Vessel in the shape of a Shang dynasty ceremonial "ku;" copper decorated with cloisonne enamels in light blue, dark blue, green, red, yellow, and white; inside the foot is an inscription of eight characters in relief giving a K'ang-hsi date. A separate flower holder fits inside the mouth. During the Qing dynasty (1644-1911), the display of ancient objects as well as contemporary ones made in an archaistic style was considered a sign of learning and refined taste. The shape and decoration of this vase, including the mask-like faces on the base, reproduce aspects of a Bronze Age ritual vessel called a gu.
persona3_adapted:
This vessel takes the form of a *gu*, a type of ritual wine container from China’s Bronze Age. It is made of copper and decorated with *cloisonné* enamel—a technique in which thin metal wires are used to create small compartments, or cells, that are then filled with colored enamel paste and fired. Here, the colors include light and dark blue, green, red, yellow, and white. Inside the foot of the vessel is an inscription of eight raised characters, which records a date from the reign of the Kangxi Emperor (1661–1722). A separate flower holder fits inside the mouth of the vase.

During the Qing dynasty (1644–1911), displaying ancient objects—as well as contemporary works made in an antique style—was seen as a mark of learning and sophisticated taste. The shape and decoration of this vase, including the mask-like faces on its base, deliberately echo features of a Bronze Age ritual vessel known as a *gu*.

CuratorialText: Polychrome. Clay: fine white porcelain. Glaze: transparent, high fired. Decoration: dragons amid clouds incised in the paste and covered with brilliant green enamel; six-character Hung-chih mark on base. (Pair with F1952.20).
persona3_adapted: This bowl is made of fine white porcelain clay, covered with a transparent, high-fired glaze. Its decoration—dragons among clouds—was carved directly into the clay surface before glazing, then coated with a vivid green enamel. The base bears a six-character mark identifying it as from the Hung-chih period. (This piece is part of a pair with F1952.20.)

CuratorialText: Bulb-bowl: circular, on three cloud-scroll feet. Two rows of bosses. Incised numeral 1 (one) and spur-marks on base. Clay: hard, fine, light-gray. Glaze: inside:olive, strong lavender-blue and lighter green-blue; outside: rose-purple, blue, gray and olive.
persona3_adapted: This bowl is circular in shape and rests on three feet that are carved to resemble curling clouds. Its surface is decorated with two rows of rounded, raised bumps. On the underside, the number "1" has been carved into the clay, and there are small marks left by the supports used during firing. The clay itself is hard, fine-grained, and light gray in color. The glaze—a glassy coating applied to the surface—varies in color depending on where you look: inside, it appears olive, a strong lavender-blue, and a lighter green-blue; outside, it shifts through shades of rose-purple, blue, gray, and olive.

CuratorialText:
The brilliant paintings on this pair of folding screens are regarded as masterpieces among only six surviving sets of screens by Sotatsu, a talented and innovative artist who headed a painting workshop known as Tawaraya. As a townsman, Sotatsu produced paintings such as fans for popular consumption. By the late 1620s, however, Sotatsu was painting for the imperial court, and his works survive in the collections of the Kyoto imperial palace. For his artistic merit, he was granted the honorary Buddhist ecclesiastical title Hokkyo (Bridge of the Law), which is included in his signature on these screens.
Matsushima (Pine Islands) is the name of a famous site (meisho) near Sendai, a city in Miyagi Prefecture in northeastern Japan. Its beauty inspired both poets and painters. In Sotatsu's screens the rock from which pine trees grow are rendered in brilliant mineral colors of green, blue, and brown, highlighted with gold. Waves in animated forms are delineated in alternating lines of ink and gold, producing a luminous effect. Clouds and embankments are rendered in particles of gold leaf accented with silver, which has darkened over time to a soft black tone.
Sotatsu's innovative composition creates a dynamic interplay among the land and cloud forms, the bending pines, and churning waves. His lifelong interest in pictorial composition left a lasting legacy. Later painters of the Rimpa school, such as Ogata Korin (1658-1716), repeated the Matsushima theme in their work. The Matsushima screens were probably painted in the early Kan'ei era (1624-44), a period of cultural efflorescence in Kyoto.
persona3_adapted:
The brilliant paintings on this pair of folding screens are considered masterpieces among only six surviving sets of screens by Sotatsu, a talented and innovative artist who led a painting workshop called Tawaraya. As a townsman—someone who lived in the city but was not part of the nobility—Sotatsu initially produced paintings for popular use, such as fans. By the late 1620s, however, he was painting for the imperial court, and his works survive in the collections of the Kyoto imperial palace. In recognition of his artistic merit, he was granted the honorary Buddhist title *Hokkyo* (meaning "Bridge of the Law"), which he included in his signature on these screens.

*Matsushima* (Pine Islands) is the name of a famous scenic site near Sendai, a city in Miyagi Prefecture in northeastern Japan. Its beauty inspired both poets and painters. In Sotatsu's screens, the rocks from which pine trees grow are rendered in vivid mineral colors—green, blue, and brown—highlighted with gold. Waves in lively, swirling forms are outlined with alternating lines of ink and gold, creating a glowing effect. Clouds and embankments are made with particles of gold leaf, accented with silver that has darkened over time to a soft black tone.

Sotatsu's inventive composition creates a dynamic interplay among the land and cloud forms, the bending pines, and the churning waves. His lifelong interest in pictorial composition left a lasting influence. Later painters of the Rimpa school, such as Ogata Korin (1658–1716), repeated the Matsushima theme in their own work. The Matsushima screens were likely painted in the early Kan'ei era (1624–44), a period of cultural flourishing in Kyoto.

CuratorialText:
The Tale of Shuten Doji has long been popular in Japan; the earliest-known illustrated version of this tale dates to the fourteenth century. Minamoto no Yorimitsu (948-1021), a historical figure of great martial prowess, was transformed in popular literature into a legendary hero who rescued the realm from demonic forces. Shuten Doji, a monster who lived southwest of Kyoto, terrorized the population, dining on locals and holding young maidens captive. With divine assistance and various means of subterfuge, Yorimitsu beheaded the ogre and returned the region to tranquility.
Kyosai's interest in the narrative format and his careful connoisseurship of ancient works of art are evident in his paintings and sketchbooks. Riddled with macabre humor and images of the grotesque, his own paintings catered to the tastes of late Edo (1615-1868) and early Meiji (1868-1912) audiences.
persona3_adapted:
The Tale of Shuten Doji has long been a popular story in Japan; the earliest known illustrated version dates back to the 1300s. Minamoto no Yorimitsu (948-1021), a real historical figure famous for his military skill, was turned in popular storytelling into a legendary hero who saved the land from demonic forces. Shuten Doji, a monster living southwest of Kyoto, terrorized the population by eating locals and holding young women captive. With help from the gods and various tricks, Yorimitsu cut off the ogre's head and brought peace back to the region.

Kyosai's interest in telling stories through images, along with his careful study of older artworks, is clear in his paintings and sketchbooks. Filled with dark humor and disturbing imagery, his own works appealed to the tastes of audiences in the late Edo period (1615–1868) and early Meiji period (1868–1912).

CuratorialText: Ceremonial sickle in four parts: three of bronze inlaid with turquoise, one (the blade) of jade decorated in linear relief with notched back and ground edge. No objects of this type have yet been scientifically excavated in China, so it is difficult to know the purpose of this extremely rare axe. It consists of four separate pieces: a tubular bronze handle, two caps with dragon motifs, and an unusual serrated jade blade that might not be original. Traces of wood inside the handle suggest it was lengthened with additional elements that are now missing.
persona3_adapted: This ceremonial object is made up of four separate pieces: three parts are bronze decorated with turquoise, and one—the blade—is jade, carved with a raised pattern and featuring a notched edge and a ground-down back. No examples of this type of object have ever been scientifically excavated in China, so its original purpose remains unclear. The object consists of a tubular bronze handle, two caps decorated with dragon designs, and an unusual jade blade with a serrated edge, which may not have originally belonged with the other pieces. Traces of wood found inside the handle suggest it was once extended with additional parts that are now lost.

CuratorialText: This is one of a pair of folding screens illustrating two of the fifty-four chapters in The Tale of Genji, a narrative composed in the early eleventh century by a lady of the court known as Murasaki Shikibu. This fictional biography of Prince Genji, whose life was filled with romance and tragedy, was a frequent subject of Japanese paintings. This screen represents chapter fifty-one, Ukifune. The lady Ukifune and her lover Niou, drift together in a boat on a wintry day, sheltered by a folding screen and temporarily isolated in their tragic romance. The painter Kano Naonobu chose monochromatic ink and thin color washes for a theme that was more commonly illustrated in lavish color and gold. The dreamlike effect of the mists and washes is poetically evocative of the mood of each episode. Naonobu was an official painter who served the Tokugawa shogun.
persona3_adapted:
This is one of a pair of folding screens that depict two of the fifty-four chapters in *The Tale of Genji*, a story written in the early eleventh century by a court lady known as Murasaki Shikibu. The tale is a fictional biography of Prince Genji, whose life was filled with romance and tragedy, and it became a popular subject for Japanese painters. This screen illustrates chapter fifty-one, titled *Ukifune*. In the scene, the lady Ukifune and her lover Niou drift together in a boat on a wintry day, sheltered by a folding screen and temporarily isolated in their tragic romance.

The painter, Kano Naonobu, chose to work in black ink and thin, diluted color washes—a restrained approach for a story that was more often shown in vivid colors and gold leaf. The dreamlike quality of the mist and the washes poetically captures the mood of each episode. Naonobu was an official painter who served the Tokugawa shogun, the military ruler of Japan at the time.

CuratorialText: Ornament of roughly rectangular shape; translucent mottled tan and cream color with areas of white film of incipient disintegration; blunt blade edge is indicated by lightly incised line and beveling; upper edge has irregular notches and a narrow projection; satin smooth surface. (One small chip). Acquired with a box, now lost.
persona3_adapted: This small, roughly rectangular ornament is made of a translucent stone with a mottled tan and cream color. In some areas, a white film has formed, which is an early sign of the material beginning to break down over time. The blunt edge of a blade is suggested by a lightly carved line and a sloping surface (a bevel). The top edge has irregular notches and a narrow projection. The surface is smooth, like satin. There is one small chip. The ornament originally came with a box, which is now lost.

CuratorialText: Jar, large, ovoidal, on a low, thick foot rim. Clay: soft, grayish. Glaze: variable aubergine, frosted with whitish areas of partial disintegration, with a slight iridescence. Earthy attachments. Decoration: impressed, incised and modelled in the paste.
persona3_adapted: This large, egg-shaped jar sits on a short, sturdy base. It is made of soft, grayish clay. The surface is covered in a glaze that shifts between shades of deep purple, with cloudy white patches where the glaze has partially worn away, giving it a faint, rainbow-like sheen. There are earthy-looking attachments on the surface. The decoration was created by pressing, carving, and shaping the clay itself before it was fired.

CuratorialText: Bowl: deep, ovoidal; low foot. Clay: hard, white. Glaze: pale green, mottled with blue-violet inside; gray-olive on rim and edge of flow; finely pitted.
persona3_adapted: This bowl has a deep, egg-like shape and sits on a short base. It is made of hard, white clay. The surface is covered with a glassy coating that is a pale green color, marked with patches of blue-violet on the inside. Around the rim and where the coating drips, the color shifts to a gray-olive. The surface has a fine, pitted texture.

CuratorialText: Chinese Han Glazed pottery " Hill-jar"; cylindrical vessel with cover; three bear-shaped feet; spur marks. Clay: reddish, sandy texture; fired medium. Glaze: lead silicate; deep green with areas of silvery iridescence, earth incrustations. Decoration: horizontal panels moulded in relief with animals a nd horsemen; two simulated mask and ring handles; cover represents mountains with animals in relief.
persona3_adapted: This jar, made in China during the Han dynasty, is a type of glazed pottery known as a "hill jar." It is a cylindrical container with a lid, resting on three feet shaped like bears. The reddish, sandy clay was fired at a medium temperature. The deep green glaze is made from lead and silica, and over time it has developed patches of silvery shimmer and earthy crusts from being buried. The decoration includes horizontal bands pressed into the clay, showing animals and horsemen in raised relief. Two handles are shaped to look like masks holding rings. The lid represents a mountain landscape, also decorated with raised animal figures.

CuratorialText: Dive into the artistic process of one of history’s most beloved and influential artists. A true titan of the Italian Renaissance, Raffaello di Giovanni Santi (1483–1520)—better known as Raphael—matched ambition with lyricism to create works with both intellectual heft and emotional depth, a necessary skill in the complex political landscape of Renaissance courts. In his short life of only 37 years, he achieved such profound success as a painter, designer, and architect that he was regarded as the pinnacle of artistic perfection for centuries after his death. Raphael: Sublime Poetry is the first comprehensive exhibition on Raphael in the United States, bringing together more than 170 of the artist’s greatest masterpieces and rarely seen treasures to illuminate the brilliance of Raphael’s extraordinary creativity. The son of a painter and poet, Raphael engaged with the foremost writers and thinkers of his age in Rome, displaying a poetic sensibility that captivated his peers and generations that followed. Follow the full breadth of his life and career, from his origins in Urbino to his rise in Florence, where he began to emerge as a peer of Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo, to his final, prolific decade at the papal court in Rome. To underscore the range of his genius, this presentation brings together important drawings, paintings, and tapestries from public and private collections across Europe and the United States, many of which have never been shown together. With particular attention to Raphael’s portrayal of women—from his use of nude female models for the first time in Western art to his tender depictions of the Madonna and Child—and recent scientific discoveries made using state-of-the-art technology, this exhibition offers a rare opportunity to experience the genius of an artist who helped shape the course of art history.
persona3_adapted:
Explore the creative process of one of history’s most beloved and influential artists. A towering figure of the Italian Renaissance, Raffaello di Giovanni Santi (1483–1520)—better known as Raphael—combined grand ambition with a graceful, poetic style to produce works that are both intellectually rich and emotionally powerful. This was a crucial skill in the complex political world of Renaissance courts. In his short life of just 37 years, he achieved such remarkable success as a painter, designer, and architect that for centuries after his death, he was seen as the ultimate standard of artistic excellence.

*Raphael: Sublime Poetry* is the first comprehensive exhibition of his work in the United States. It brings together more than 170 of his greatest masterpieces and rarely seen treasures to highlight the brilliance of his extraordinary imagination. The son of a painter and poet, Raphael connected with the leading writers and thinkers of his time in Rome, showing a poetic sensitivity that fascinated both his contemporaries and later generations.

Follow the full arc of his life and career—from his early years in Urbino, to his rise in Florence, where he began to rival Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo, and finally to his last, highly productive decade at the papal court in Rome. To emphasize the range of his talent, this exhibition brings together important drawings, paintings, and tapestries from public and private collections across Europe and the United States, many of which have never been displayed together before.

With special attention to Raphael’s portrayal of women—from his groundbreaking use of nude female models (the first time this was done in Western art) to his tender images of the Madonna and Child—and recent scientific discoveries made using advanced technology, this exhibition offers a rare chance to experience the genius of an artist who helped shape the course of art history.

CuratorialText: One of the greatest influencers of all time, Raphael (1483 –1520) was idolized for more than three centuries, in Europe and beyond, as the painter of supreme perfection and the role model for artists in training. During his short life, his contemporaries celebrated his enormous creative facility, and later his fame towered above that of other Italian Renaissance stars. Born Raffaello di Giovanni Santi, the ambitious young artist soon left behind his modest origins in the Marche region of central Italy in search of important patrons in Umbria and Tuscany. In Florence, then the capital of the European art world, he emerged as a peer to the elder Leonardo and Michelangelo.
