Raphael spent his final decade as the favorite artist of the popes in Rome, where he was praised as the “prince of painters.” A savvy businessman and impresario, he worked with well-organized teams of assistants and collaborators to propagate his inventions and style, spawning a new generation of noteworthy artists.
persona3_adapted: One of the most influential artists of all time, Raphael (1483–1520) was admired for more than three centuries, across Europe and beyond, as a painter of flawless beauty and a model for artists learning their craft. During his short life, his contemporaries celebrated his extraordinary creative talent, and later his reputation surpassed that of other great Italian Renaissance figures. Born Raffaello di Giovanni Santi, this ambitious young artist soon left his modest beginnings in the Marche region of central Italy to seek important supporters in Umbria and Tuscany. In Florence, then the center of the European art world, he became the equal of the older masters Leonardo and Michelangelo. Raphael spent his final ten years as the favorite artist of the popes in Rome, where he was hailed as the “prince of painters.” A shrewd businessman and organizer, he worked with well-coordinated teams of assistants and collaborators to spread his ideas and style, inspiring a new generation of notable artists.

CuratorialText: This first comprehensive U.S. exhibition on Raphael provides an immersive look at his meteoric career. Together, the drawings, paintings, prints, and tapestries on view present a portrait of his dashing artistic personality and astonishingly creative mind. This is also the story of an artist who was born to a poet-painter, became an intimate friend to literary figures, and tried his hand at composing sonnets. Raphael lived and worked in a culture accustomed to thinking of painting and poetry as intertwined sister arts. The elegance and dramatic force of his imagery still have the power to invoke the ancient dictum, much discussed in his time, that “painting is a mute poetry, and poetry is a blind painting.”
persona3_adapted: This first major U.S. exhibition devoted to Raphael offers an in-depth look at his remarkably swift rise to fame. The drawings, paintings, prints, and tapestries on display together reveal the personality of a dazzlingly inventive artist. The exhibition also tells the story of a man born to a poet-painter, who became a close friend of writers, and even tried his hand at writing sonnets. Raphael lived and worked in a culture that saw painting and poetry as closely connected “sister arts.” The grace and dramatic power of his images still bring to life an ancient idea, much debated in his time, that “painting is a silent poem, and poetry is a blind painting.”

CuratorialText: Raphael was born on April 6, 1483, in Urbino, a hill town set in a spectacular, rugged landscape in the Marche region, in east central Italy. The artist’s roots in Urbino, with its refined court life and unique intellectual heritage, played a defining role in his life and career. He proudly identified with his birthplace and signed his paintings “RAPHAEL VRBINAS” to the end of his days.
persona3_adapted: Raphael was born on April 6, 1483, in Urbino, a hill town set in a spectacular, rugged landscape in the Marche region of east central Italy. The artist’s upbringing in Urbino—known for its sophisticated court life and distinctive intellectual traditions—shaped his life and career in fundamental ways. He proudly identified with his birthplace and continued to sign his paintings “RAPHAEL VRBINAS” throughout his life.

CuratorialText: Raphael received his earliest training from his father, Giovanni Santi (ca. 1439–1494), a court painter and prolific poet. During Raphael’s childhood, Giovanni was busy composing an epic poem on Federico III da Montefeltro (1422–1482), the famous ruler who had brought peace, wealth, and fame to the Duchy of Urbino. A formidable general, scholar, and patron of art and architecture, Federico transformed his court into an internationally celebrated center for the arts and humanist learning. He founded a fabled library and rebuilt Urbino’s imposing Palazzo Ducale, whose rhythmic arcades and harmonic geometry embodied the humanist dedication to mathematics that distinguished the culture of Urbino. Those same ideals are evident in the vibrant architectural visions—enlivened with perspectival effects and classical references—created by Urbino painters.
persona3_adapted: Raphael’s first lessons in art came from his father, Giovanni Santi (about 1439–1494), who worked as a painter at court and was also a prolific poet. During Raphael’s childhood, Giovanni was busy writing a long epic poem about Federico III da Montefeltro (1422–1482), the famous ruler who had brought peace, wealth, and fame to the Duchy of Urbino. Federico was a formidable military leader, a scholar, and a generous supporter of art and architecture. He turned his court into an internationally celebrated center for the arts and for humanist learning—a movement that emphasized the study of classical texts and human potential. He founded a legendary library and rebuilt Urbino’s imposing Palazzo Ducale (the Duke’s Palace). The palace’s rhythmic rows of arches and balanced, harmonious design reflected the humanist belief in the importance of mathematics, a value that set Urbino’s culture apart. Those same ideals—vivid architectural scenes brought to life with perspective effects and references to classical antiquity—are clearly visible in the work of Urbino’s painters.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s father soon brought his precocious son to study with Pietro Perugino (1446/50–1523), an artist he likely knew through local projects. A brilliant, prolific painter and draftsman with active workshops in Perugia and Florence, Perugino relied heavily on collaboration with assistants to meet the demands of patrons all over Italy. Raphael coursed quickly through the stages of pupil and apprentice to become first a collaborator to Perugino and then an independent artist. Perugino’s elegant figures, breathtaking command of technique, and efficient methods for reproducing designs left their mark on the younger artist.
persona3_adapted: Raphael’s father soon brought his exceptionally talented young son to study with Pietro Perugino (1446/50–1523), an artist he likely knew from local projects. Perugino was a highly skilled and productive painter and designer, running busy studios in Perugia and Florence. To meet the demands of clients across Italy, he relied heavily on working with assistants. Raphael moved quickly through the stages of student and apprentice, first becoming a collaborator with Perugino and then an independent artist. Perugino’s graceful figures, stunning technical mastery, and efficient ways of reproducing his designs left a lasting influence on the younger artist.

CuratorialText: Ambitious, studious, and disciplined, the young Raphael first sought to establish himself as an independent professional in Città di Castello, a small, wealthy town near Perugia, where he painted notable church altarpieces. Confraternities, religious institutions for regular citizens devoted to works of charity, were also major sources of patronage for artists in Umbria and the Marche. Gathered here are paintings and related studies done for confraternities by Perugino and the young Raphael—including the first painting fully in Raphael’s hand, as confirmed by its recent conservation treatment.
persona3_adapted: Ambitious, hardworking, and disciplined, the young Raphael first tried to build his career as an independent artist in Città di Castello, a small, prosperous town near Perugia. There, he created notable religious paintings for church altars. Confraternities—religious groups made up of ordinary citizens dedicated to charitable work—were also important sources of support for artists in the regions of Umbria and the Marche. This exhibition brings together paintings and related preparatory drawings that Perugino and the young Raphael made for these confraternities. Among them is the first painting entirely by Raphael’s own hand, a fact confirmed by recent conservation work.

CuratorialText: From 1500 to 1507, the young Raphael endeavored to cultivate patrons by painting both monumental altarpieces and small-scale devotional works in the regions of the Marche, Umbria, and Tuscany. An iconic example is the large, multipart painting at the center of this gallery, made for nuns in Perugia and now known as the Colonna Altarpiece (after a later owner). The full ensemble is reunited here as a complete work for the first time since it was dismembered around 1663 and the individual paintings dispersed. Other notable projects include a mammoth altarpiece for the Baronci family chapel in Città di Castello, which was later destroyed in an earthquake and survives only in fragments and preliminary drawings, and a remarkable altarpiece for the Oddi family chapel in Perugia, represented here by studies and the painting that provided its base.
persona3_adapted: From 1500 to 1507, the young Raphael worked to win the support of wealthy sponsors by creating both large, impressive religious paintings for churches and smaller, more intimate works for private devotion. He did this across several regions of central Italy: the Marche, Umbria, and Tuscany. A key example is the large, multi-panel painting at the center of this gallery, originally made for nuns in Perugia and now known as the Colonna Altarpiece (named after a later owner). For the first time since the work was taken apart around 1663 and its individual panels scattered, the entire set is reunited here as a complete piece. Other major projects include a huge altarpiece for the Baronci family chapel in Città di Castello, later destroyed in an earthquake and now known only through fragments and early sketches; and a remarkable altarpiece for the Oddi family chapel in Perugia, shown here through preparatory studies and the painting that formed its base.

CuratorialText: Full-scale drawings for the Oddi Altarpiece reveal the workshop practices that Raphael had absorbed during his training and collaboration with Perugino. Dazzling studies on paper also show how he worked in a variety of media—black chalk, pen and ink, and metalpoint on prepared paper—often with a virtuosic command of technique. Drawings such as these likely served as his calling card and undoubtedly impressed his patrons.
persona3_adapted: Full-scale preparatory drawings for the Oddi Altarpiece show the studio methods that Raphael had learned while training and working alongside the artist Perugino. Brilliant sketches on paper also reveal how he worked with different materials—such as black chalk, pen and ink, and a technique called metalpoint on specially coated paper—often displaying exceptional skill. Drawings like these probably acted as his professional portfolio and certainly impressed those who commissioned his work.

CuratorialText: Raphael may have been drawn to Florence after hearing fellow painters rave about rival full-scale designs (cartoons) for spectacular murals there by Leonardo and Michelangelo. Related works in this gallery convey the electrifying novelty and arresting sculptural power of those unrealized projects. While in Florence between 1504 and 1508, Raphael also studied Michelangelo’s sculptures and Leonardo’s paintings and drawings, promptly and seamlessly adapting aspects of their styles and techniques in his work.
persona3_adapted: Raphael may have been drawn to Florence after hearing other painters praise the competing full-scale preparatory drawings (called cartoons) for spectacular wall paintings there by Leonardo and Michelangelo. Related works in this gallery capture the exciting newness and striking, sculpture-like impact of those projects, which were never completed. During his time in Florence, from 1504 to 1508, Raphael also studied Michelangelo’s sculptures and Leonardo’s paintings and drawings. He quickly and smoothly incorporated elements of their styles and techniques into his own work.

CuratorialText: Raphael also learned from his peers’ revolutionary approaches to inventing compositions. Leonardo’s theories about “sketching quickly,” in the fervor of artistic inspiration, transformed Raphael’s methods. Asserting that the effort to finish drawings and puzzle out details could dry up fresh ideas, Leonardo advised working out concepts in “rough sketches,” like a poet scrawling the first draft of a verse in messy handwriting with crossed-out words. Before Florence, Raphael had tended to develop compositions in a more artisanal way, figure by detailed figure, as he had learned from Perugino. Now, spontaneous sketching led him to more organic compositions pulsating with movement and vivid expression.
persona3_adapted: Raphael also learned from his contemporaries’ groundbreaking ways of creating compositions. Leonardo’s ideas about “sketching quickly,” in the heat of artistic inspiration, changed how Raphael worked. Leonardo believed that spending too much effort finishing drawings and working out small details could stifle fresh ideas. He advised developing concepts in “rough sketches,” much like a poet writing a first draft of a poem in messy handwriting with crossed-out words. Before his time in Florence, Raphael had tended to build his compositions in a more craft-based way, working figure by detailed figure, as he had learned from his teacher Perugino. Afterward, spontaneous sketching led him to create more fluid compositions, full of movement and vivid expression.

CuratorialText: The exposure to Leonardo’s and Michelangelo’s art infused Raphael’s compositions with a sense of space, sculptural monumentality, and expressive force. This was the result of an enormous amount of experimentation on paper. Sequences of drawings—from a sheet of sketches to studies of an arrangement based on three-dimensional clay or wax models—reveal Raphael’s increasingly disciplined approach to the design process. Working in orderly steps, for example, he gradually animated a pyramidal composition (favored by Leonardo), which could seem static, by building a dynamic interplay of gestures and gazes among the figures.
persona3_adapted: Raphael’s exposure to the work of Leonardo and Michelangelo gave his paintings a greater feeling of depth, a more solid and weighty presence in the figures, and a stronger emotional impact. This came from a huge amount of experimentation on paper. By looking at series of drawings—from quick sketches to studies based on small clay or wax models—we can see how Raphael developed a more organized and controlled way of working. For example, he took a pyramid-shaped arrangement of figures (a structure Leonardo often used) and, step by step, brought it to life. He added a lively exchange of gestures and glances among the figures, turning what could have been a still, balanced scene into something more active and engaging.

CuratorialText: In 1507 the twenty-four-year-old Raphael painted his most significant work before his move to Rome, an altarpiece of Christ’s entombment for the Baglioni family chapel in Perugia. The preliminary drawings show him in full command of his powers as a storyteller, and demonstrate how he constructed a dramatic composition with a climactic narrative. Through arduous study, aided by his knowledge of classical sculpture, the artist evolved a static arrangement of figures into an emotionally arresting action scene. Raphael had absorbed Leonardo’s principle that the physical gestures of the body express the “motions” of the mind and soul.
persona3_adapted: In 1507, the twenty-four-year-old Raphael painted his most important work before moving to Rome. It was an altarpiece—a painting placed above and behind an altar—showing the burial of Christ. The work was made for the Baglioni family chapel in the city of Perugia. His early sketches show him in full control of his skills as a storyteller, and reveal how he built a dramatic scene with a story that builds to a high point. Through intense study, and using what he knew about ancient Roman and Greek statues, the artist turned a still arrangement of figures into an emotionally powerful action scene. Raphael had taken on board Leonardo da Vinci’s idea that the physical movements of the body express the “movements” of the mind and soul.

CuratorialText: For centuries Raphael has been widely admired for Madonna and Child paintings that include a display of tenderness. From about 1300, Byzantine icons of the Eastern Orthodox faith had featured a form of the Madonna known as the Virgin Eleousa, from the Greek for “compassionate,” in which Mary embraces the Christ Child as he touches his cheek to hers. Disseminated widely, the imagery inspired numerous medieval and Renaissance artists, including Italian sculptors and painters. Raphael drew upon this model and thoroughly transformed it.
persona3_adapted: For centuries, Raphael has been widely admired for his paintings of the Madonna and Child—a mother and baby Jesus—that show a sense of tenderness. Around the year 1300, religious images from the Eastern Orthodox tradition, known as Byzantine icons, included a version of the Madonna called the Virgin Eleousa, a Greek word meaning “compassionate.” In this type of image, Mary holds the Christ Child close as he presses his cheek against hers. These images spread widely and inspired many artists in the Middle Ages and the Renaissance, including Italian sculptors and painters. Raphael took this traditional model and completely reimagined it.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s ability to capture a mood of vitality and innocent playfulness, especially while maintaining a superior command of anatomical realism, is striking in light of the historical realities of childbirth, infancy, and parenthood in his time. The rare account book displayed here records funerary expenses for Raphael’s mother, who died of childbirth-related complications in 1491, and for two of his siblings who died in infancy. Knowledge of gynecology and obstetrics was scarce, pregnancy and childbirth extremely dangerous, and infant mortality rates astronomically high. These facts, along with a desire among Renaissance artists to humanize religious subjects, all contributed to the appeal of the tender Madonna and Child as votive figures and subjects in art.
persona3_adapted: Raphael had a remarkable ability to capture a sense of liveliness and innocent playfulness in his work, all while demonstrating an exceptional grasp of realistic human anatomy. This is especially striking when we consider the harsh realities of childbirth, infancy, and parenthood during his era. The rare financial record displayed here lists the funeral expenses for Raphael’s mother, who died from complications related to childbirth in 1491, as well as for two of his siblings who died as infants. At the time, knowledge of women’s health and childbirth was very limited; pregnancy and giving birth were extremely dangerous, and the rate of infant death was devastatingly high. These difficult facts, combined with a desire among Renaissance artists to make religious subjects feel more human and relatable, all help explain why the tender image of the Madonna and Child became so popular—both as objects of devotion and as subjects for art.

CuratorialText: Despite his great ambitions, Raphael was largely thwarted in his attempts to break into the competitive art market in Florence. He found his most feasible source of patronage among the city’s wealthy merchants, who commissioned portraits and tender devotional paintings, mainly of the Madonna and Child. Raphael continued to produce works on this subject for private patrons well after his move to Rome in 1508, and many of his collaborators followed his example.
persona3_adapted: Despite his great ambitions, Raphael largely failed to break into the competitive art market in Florence. His most realistic source of support came from the city’s wealthy merchants, who hired him to paint portraits and gentle religious scenes, mostly of the Virgin Mary and the baby Jesus. Raphael kept creating works on this subject for private clients even after moving to Rome in 1508, and many of his assistants and fellow artists followed his lead.

CuratorialText: Raphael sought to bring the lauded ethereal physical beauty of the Madonna into radiant being. He was not immune to the convention, sung by poets both within and beyond his circle, that favored an elegant, aristocratic lady with dainty facial features and golden hair, but he also strove for long-established Christian ideals. Sermons, prayers, songs, and poetry inspired by early church writings had praised the Virgin’s beauty and grace as mirrors of her unique spiritual perfection. He infused his representations with a humanity and psychological presence revealed through gestures and reactions. Raphael’s paintings display an arresting command of light, color, space, and geometry, and his renderings of infants are more naturalistic in their proportions, poses, and expressions than those of many of his peers.
persona3_adapted: Raphael aimed to bring the Virgin Mary’s celebrated, almost otherworldly beauty vividly to life. He was influenced by the popular trend—celebrated by poets both in his own circle and beyond—that favored an elegant, aristocratic woman with delicate facial features and golden hair. But he also remained committed to long-standing Christian ideals. Writings from the early church, along with sermons, prayers, songs, and poetry, had praised the Virgin’s beauty and grace as reflections of her unique spiritual purity. Raphael added a sense of humanity and emotional depth to his portrayals, shown through the figures’ gestures and reactions. His paintings demonstrate a striking mastery of light, color, space, and geometric composition. And his depictions of infants are more lifelike in their proportions, poses, and expressions than those of many other artists of his time.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s portraits communicate a profound empathy and reflect years of exercising his hand in drawings to achieve an intimate, concerted contemplation of the sitter. The regal pose of Leonardo’s Mona Lisa, begun in 1503, offered a revolutionary model for the likeness of a luxuriously dressed person: a three-quarter view, head turned to the left, torso erect, and hands resting gracefully at the waist. The elegance of the poses here also suggests a striving for the cultivated manners of the Italian Renaissance courts and the ideals of beauty celebrated by famous poets. Raphael was an intimate friend of Baldassarre Castiglione (1478–1529), whose book on fashionable behavior at court promoted a model of comportment and grace that prized sprezzatura, a studied nonchalance or insouciance.
persona3_adapted: Raphael’s portraits convey a deep emotional understanding of the people he painted, a skill he developed through years of practicing drawing. This practice allowed him to create a focused, thoughtful study of each sitter. The dignified pose of Leonardo da Vinci’s *Mona Lisa*, begun in 1503, offered a groundbreaking way to portray a richly dressed person: a three-quarter view, with the head turned to the left, the body upright, and the hands resting gracefully at the waist. The elegance of the poses in Raphael’s work also suggests an effort to reflect the refined manners expected in Italian Renaissance courts, as well as the ideals of beauty celebrated by famous poets of the time. Raphael was a close friend of Baldassarre Castiglione (1478–1529), whose book on proper behavior at court promoted a model of poise and grace that valued *sprezzatura*—a studied, effortless nonchalance or casual elegance.

CuratorialText: Many Renaissance portraits of women were painted at the time of betrothal and marriage, a financial-dynastic arrangement in which romantic love had no place. Elegant fashion and jewelry were meant to impress the future husband’s family, as dowries and property were negotiated. Overt appraisals of women’s appearances by poets and artists also contributed to a culture in which young women could become innamorate—platonic beloveds or actual mistresses—of a male aristocracy.
persona3_adapted: Many Renaissance portraits of women were created around the time of their engagement or marriage, which was then a financial and family-based arrangement, not a matter of romantic love. The elegant clothing and jewelry in these paintings were intended to impress the groom’s family, as dowries and property were being negotiated. In addition, poets and artists openly praised women’s looks, contributing to a culture where young women could become *innamorate*—a term for either a platonic beloved or an actual mistress—of aristocratic men.

CuratorialText: At some point in 1508, Raphael arrived in Rome, where he would become the favorite court artist of Popes Julius II (r. 1503–13) and Leo X (r. 1513–21). An intriguing question is how exactly the twenty-five-year-old Raphael managed to supersede an earlier generation of painters working in the Vatican Palace and take control of the fresco decoration of the most important of the rooms, the Stanza della Segnatura. Surely artistic genius and personal savvy were key factors, but research shows that networks of political influence between Rome and his hometown of Urbino also paved the way, including support from his friend and countryman Donato Bramante (1444–1514), the pope’s favorite architect.
persona3_adapted: In 1508, Raphael arrived in Rome, where he would become the favorite artist of two popes: Julius II (who ruled from 1503 to 1513) and Leo X (1513–1521). A fascinating question is how this twenty-five-year-old managed to overtake an older generation of painters already working in the Vatican Palace and take charge of painting the frescoes—wall paintings made on wet plaster—in the most important room, the Stanza della Segnatura. His artistic brilliance and personal skill certainly played a major role, but research shows that political connections between Rome and his hometown of Urbino also helped clear the way. This included support from his friend and fellow Urbino native Donato Bramante (1444–1514), who was the pope’s favorite architect.

CuratorialText: Gathered here are Raphael’s studies for those Vatican frescoes: the School of Athens, with its gathering of philosophers, and the Disputa, representing Roman Catholic theology. These works show an artist in full command of the expressive potential of different drawing media. Rapidly sketched pen-and-ink sheets teem with the energy of ideas in the making, while studies in black chalk appear monumental despite their small size. In the next gallery, a digital video conveys the power of Raphael’s finished frescoes in four rooms of the Vatican Palace.
persona3_adapted: Here are Raphael’s sketches for his famous Vatican wall paintings: the *School of Athens*, which shows a gathering of philosophers, and the *Disputa*, which represents Roman Catholic theology. These works reveal an artist who fully controls the expressive possibilities of different drawing materials. Quickly drawn pen-and-ink pages are full of the energy of ideas as they take shape, while studies in black chalk feel grand and monumental, even though they are small. In the next room, a digital video shows the power of Raphael’s finished wall paintings in four rooms of the Vatican Palace.

CuratorialText: By about 1510 Raphael was collecting accolades as the most visible and prolific painter at the papal court in Rome; meanwhile, shut away in the Sistine Chapel, the secretive Michelangelo labored to paint his ceiling frescoes, which would deeply influence the younger artist, who snuck in to see them. Drawings here relate to projects that occupied Raphael as he concluded his first series of frescoes in the Vatican Palace and began the second, in the Stanza di Eliodoro, which communicate with greater monumentality and dramatic force. After Pope Julius II died in 1513 and Leo X ascended to the throne, some learned imagery vanished in favor of blunter papal messaging.
persona3_adapted: By around 1510, Raphael was earning widespread praise as the most famous and productive painter working for the pope in Rome. At the same time, the more private Michelangelo was working alone in the Sistine Chapel, painting the famous scenes on its ceiling. These works would later have a strong impact on Raphael, who secretly went in to see them. The drawings shown here are connected to projects Raphael worked on as he finished his first set of wall paintings in the Vatican Palace and began a second set in a room called the Stanza di Eliodoro. These later paintings show a greater sense of size, weight, and emotional intensity. After Pope Julius II died in 1513 and Leo X became pope, some of the more scholarly and symbolic imagery disappeared, replaced by more direct messages supporting the pope’s authority.

CuratorialText: Other drawings and prints illustrate Raphael’s collaboration with Marcantonio Raimondi (ca. 1480–1534), a Bolognese printmaker and close friend. In their fruitful partnership, beginning in 1510, they created engravings of tremendous technical skill and artistic power. This medium enabled the reproduction of imagery in multiples and ensured a long afterlife for Raphael’s compositions. The prints were copied by artists well into the late 1800s and repurposed in other media, such as the maiolica plates in a nearby case.
persona3_adapted: Other drawings and prints show how Raphael worked with Marcantonio Raimondi (around 1480–1534), a printmaker from Bologna and a close friend. In their productive partnership, which began in 1510, they made engravings—images carved into metal plates for printing—that displayed remarkable technical skill and artistic power. This method allowed Raphael’s designs to be reproduced in multiple copies, giving his compositions a long-lasting influence. Artists continued to copy these prints well into the late 1800s, and the designs were also reused in other forms, such as the painted ceramic plates displayed nearby.

CuratorialText: In the next gallery is a digital video showing Raphael’s monumental fresco cycles in four rooms of the Vatican Palace. The display provides a sense of scale and context for the many related drawings in this exhibition. Between 1508 and 1524, the frescoes were painted by Raphael and his workshop in the nearly square rooms now known as the Stanza della Segnatura, Stanza di Eliodoro, and Stanza dell’Incendio, and in the gargantuan oblong Sala di Costantino (completed by his assistants after his death in 1520).
persona3_adapted: In the next gallery, a digital video shows Raphael’s large-scale wall paintings in four rooms of the Vatican Palace. These paintings, known as frescoes, are created by applying pigment to wet plaster, which makes the image part of the wall itself. The video helps visitors understand the size and setting of these works, which is useful for appreciating the many related drawings in this exhibition. Between 1508 and 1524, Raphael and his assistants painted these frescoes in rooms that are nearly square in shape—now called the Stanza della Segnatura, Stanza di Eliodoro, and Stanza dell’Incendio—as well as in the enormous, rectangular Sala di Costantino, which was finished by his helpers after his death in 1520.

CuratorialText: The durable but difficult fresco technique requires expertise, the use of full-scale drawings (cartoons), and a willingness to anticipate challenges. Water-based colors applied to moist surface plaster (intonaco) dry and carbonate in a chemical process, permanently bonding the colors to the wall. Executed primarily by Raphael between 1508 and 1512, the frescoes in the Stanza della Segnatura are the most famous and include the School of Athens, the Disputa, the personifications of Justice, and the Parnassus. In the following three rooms Raphael worked with a greater involvement of collaborators who followed his designs closely.
persona3_adapted: The fresco technique is both long-lasting and demanding. It requires skill, the use of full-scale preparatory drawings (called cartoons), and the ability to plan ahead for potential problems. In this method, the artist applies water-based paint to wet plaster (known as intonaco). As the plaster dries, a chemical reaction called carbonation takes place, which permanently binds the colors to the wall. The frescoes in the Stanza della Segnatura—painted mainly by Raphael between 1508 and 1512—are his most famous works. They include the School of Athens, the Disputa, symbolic figures representing Justice, and the Parnassus. In the three rooms that follow, Raphael worked more closely with assistants, who followed his designs carefully.

CuratorialText: The paintings here represent the breathtaking visual eloquence of Raphael’s later years. New harmonies of color are transformed by a dark chiaroscuro reminiscent of Leonardo. Sculptural forms seem to project from dense compositions. Charged gazes, poses, and gestures communicate a sense of impending drama.
persona3_adapted: In these paintings, Raphael’s later style achieves a striking visual power and clarity. He creates new color harmonies, now deepened by a dramatic contrast between light and shadow—a technique that recalls Leonardo da Vinci’s work. The figures feel almost three-dimensional, as if they are pushing forward from the crowded scene. Their intense looks, postures, and gestures convey a sense of tension and approaching drama.

CuratorialText: Between 1514 and 1520, the demanding Pope Leo X rained project after project upon Raphael. He produced few paintings independently; using his drawings as a guide, skilled assistants painted most of the frescoes in the Vatican’s Stanza dell’Incendio. He was often disparaged for this practice by rivals, including his fierce enemy Michelangelo. But Raphael and his well-organized workshop completed an astonishing number of large-scale projects in his final six years. Executive help freed the artist to concentrate his creative energies on inventing new designs and exploring new forms. The involvement of workshop assistants in paintings and in certain types of drawings has inevitably led to debates about authorship. Labels in this exhibition attempt to indicate instances of their likely intervention, and a nearby display allows comparison of studies by Raphael and his fellow artists in an effort to characterize their individual styles.
persona3_adapted: Between 1514 and 1520, the demanding Pope Leo X assigned one project after another to Raphael. During this time, Raphael produced few paintings entirely on his own. Instead, skilled assistants painted most of the large wall paintings (called frescoes) in the Vatican’s Room of the Fire, following Raphael’s drawings as a guide. His rivals, including his fierce enemy Michelangelo, often criticized him for this practice. But Raphael and his well-organized workshop completed an astonishing number of large-scale projects in his final six years. Having help with the execution freed the artist to focus his creative energy on coming up with new designs and exploring new forms. Because assistants were involved in both paintings and certain types of drawings, experts often disagree about who actually made what. Labels in this exhibition try to point out where assistants likely contributed, and a nearby display lets you compare studies by Raphael and his fellow artists, helping to identify the unique style of each.

CuratorialText: The imperious Pope Leo X charged Raphael with the daunting task of designing a set of monumental tapestries to hang in the Sistine Chapel during special occasions—a project that put Raphael in direct competition with Michelangelo and his ceiling frescoes above. Starting in 1515, Raphael began drawing small preliminary studies. Aided by workshop assistants, he then painted in color gouaches on paper the enormous full-scale cartoons that would guide the weavers. Woven in Brussels using rich materials, the stratospherically expensive tapestries contributed to the bankruptcy of Leo’s papacy. With their inventive compositions, striking color harmonies, and monumental scale, they were soon the envy of monarchs across Europe. Kings commissioned second editions woven from Raphael’s cartoons, and the set owned by King Philip II of Spain (1527–1598) is represented here by three stunning examples.
persona3_adapted: Pope Leo X, a powerful and demanding patron, gave Raphael a formidable assignment: design a series of large-scale tapestries to hang in the Sistine Chapel during important ceremonies. This project placed Raphael in direct competition with Michelangelo, whose famous ceiling paintings were already in the same chapel. Starting in 1515, Raphael began by making small, rough sketches. With help from assistants in his workshop, he then created enormous, full-size drawings on paper, painted in colored gouache (a type of opaque watercolor). These full-scale drawings served as the exact guide for the weavers. The tapestries were woven in Brussels using luxurious materials, and their extremely high cost contributed to the financial collapse of Leo’s papacy. Thanks to their inventive designs, striking color combinations, and massive size, these tapestries soon became the envy of monarchs across Europe. Several kings ordered second editions made from Raphael’s original drawings. The set owned by King Philip II of Spain (1527–1598) is represented here by three stunning examples.

CuratorialText: As the tapestry designs progressed, the Raphael team largely took over the fresco work in the Vatican Palace, endeavoring to complete the Stanza dell’Incendio and assisting Raphael as he began to conceive decorations for the final space. Giulio Romano (1499?–1546) acted as his studio foreman and went on to enjoy a brilliant independent career.
persona3_adapted: As the designs for the tapestries moved forward, Raphael’s workshop team took on most of the fresco painting in the Vatican Palace. They worked to finish the Stanza dell’Incendio—a room whose name refers to a famous fire scene depicted there—and helped Raphael as he started planning decorations for the final chamber. Giulio Romano (around 1499–1546) served as the studio’s manager and later built a highly successful career on his own.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s last and largest altarpiece is the enormous Transfiguration, commissioned by Cardinal Giulio de’ Medici (the future Pope Clement VII) around 1517. Spurred by competition with artistic rivals, Raphael produced a work markedly more dramatic, ambitious, and complex than his earlier altarpieces. Although its execution was interrupted by his untimely death in 1520, the Transfiguration became Raphael’s artistic testament. The main event is Christ’s Transfiguration on Mount Tabor, during which he transformed before the stunned apostles Peter, James, and John to reveal his divinity.
persona3_adapted: Raphael’s final and largest altarpiece—a painted panel created for a church altar—is the monumental *Transfiguration*. It was commissioned around 1517 by Cardinal Giulio de’ Medici, who would later become Pope Clement VII. Driven by rivalry with other leading artists, Raphael made this work noticeably more dramatic, ambitious, and complex than his earlier altar pieces. Although his sudden death in 1520 interrupted its completion, the *Transfiguration* came to be seen as Raphael’s artistic legacy—a final statement of his vision. The central scene shows Christ’s Transfiguration on Mount Tabor, a moment when he was transformed before the astonished apostles Peter, James, and John, revealing his divine nature.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s studies for the Transfiguration, displayed in the niche at left, represent the culmination of his career as a draftsman. They are reunited here to show the range of his design process. Raphael used red chalk primarily when drawing nude models from life. He reserved black chalk for the full-scale studies of apostles’ heads and hands, a type of drawing called an “auxiliary cartoon” because the design starts from a dotted chalk outline transferred from another sheet.
persona3_adapted: Raphael’s preparatory drawings for the *Transfiguration*, shown in the alcove to the left, mark the high point of his skill as a designer. These works are brought together here to reveal the variety of methods he used to develop his composition. Raphael mainly used red chalk when sketching nude models from life. He saved black chalk for life-size studies of the apostles’ faces and hands—a type of drawing known as an “auxiliary cartoon,” meaning the design begins with a dotted chalk outline transferred from another sheet of paper.

CuratorialText: Raphael rarely interrupted his projects for Popes Julius II and Leo X to make room for other commissions. The notable exception was his powerful friend Agostino Chigi (1466–1520), whom he met in about 1510. Born in Siena, Chigi became the richest banker in Europe, and his legendary wealth financed the popes’ cultural and military enterprises. Raphael’s work for Chigi included fresco paintings, as well as designs for his remodeled stables and the multimedia ensembles of his two chapels.
persona3_adapted: Raphael seldom set aside his work for Popes Julius II and Leo X to take on other projects. A significant exception was his influential friend Agostino Chigi (1466–1520), whom he met around 1510. Born in Siena, Chigi became the wealthiest banker in Europe, and his legendary fortune funded the popes’ cultural and military ventures. Raphael’s work for Chigi included wall paintings (frescoes), as well as designs for his renovated stables and the combined artistic arrangements of his two chapels.

CuratorialText: Displayed here are a variety of Raphael’s preparatory drawings for Chigi’s projects. In chalk studies of nude figures, Michelangelo’s language of contorted poses and powerful muscularity is transformed with an effortless grace and the sensuality of living flesh. The drawings also allude to the legacy of Raphael’s approach to the human figure, which led to the style known as Mannerism after his death. Male and female forms here anticipate prototypical Mannerist poses—as with Raphael’s primeval personification of an earthquake in a Sistine Chapel tapestry design, which spawned the figural vocabulary that his former assistant Giulio Romano would exploit to spectacular effect in his later frescoes.
persona3_adapted: Displayed here are a variety of Raphael’s preparatory drawings for projects commissioned by the wealthy banker Chigi. In chalk studies of nude figures, we can see how Raphael took Michelangelo’s style—known for twisted poses and powerful, muscular bodies—and transformed it with an effortless grace and a sense of living, sensual flesh. These drawings also hint at the lasting influence of Raphael’s approach to the human figure, which after his death led to a style called Mannerism (a trend that emphasized elegance and exaggerated poses). The male and female forms here show early examples of typical Mannerist poses—as seen, for instance, in Raphael’s design for a tapestry in the Sistine Chapel, where he created a primal, symbolic figure representing an earthquake. This figure’s pose and shape became a kind of visual language that his former assistant, Giulio Romano, would later use to dramatic effect in his own wall paintings.

CuratorialText: Rome and its ancient monuments transformed Raphael’s art, and his work likewise changed the face of Rome. Following his arrival in the Eternal City in 1508, he threw himself into archaeological studies and made drawings of monuments that demonstrate a nearly scientific rigor. Pope Leo X tasked Raphael with creating an enormous drawing of ancient Rome, “making those members that are entirely ruined and are completely invisible correspond with those that are still standing and can be seen.” A letter to Leo, written on Raphael’s behalf, attests to the artist’s deep archaeological knowledge and technical skill, including a highly original method for using a magnetic compass in conducting surveys of ruins.
persona3_adapted: Rome and its ancient monuments had a profound impact on Raphael’s art, and in turn, his work reshaped the city of Rome itself. When he arrived in the Eternal City in 1508, he immersed himself in the study of archaeology, creating drawings of ancient structures that show an almost scientific level of detail. Pope Leo X asked Raphael to produce a large-scale drawing of ancient Rome, with the goal of “making those parts that are completely destroyed and now invisible match up with those that are still standing and can be seen.” A letter written on Raphael’s behalf to the Pope reveals the artist’s deep knowledge of archaeology and his technical skill, including an innovative method for using a magnetic compass to survey ruins.

CuratorialText: Around 1503 Pope Julius II had charged Bramante, Raphael’s mentor and close friend from Urbino, to design a new Saint Peter’s Basilica that would convey the majesty of the papacy in the aesthetic language of ancient Rome. Drawings and prints here illuminate Raphael’s career as an architect after he took over this massive project upon Bramante’s death. In 1517 Raphael bought the Palazzo Caprini, designed by Bramante and now destroyed but pictured in two works here, where he lived out his final years in Rome as the “prince of painters.”
persona3_adapted: Around 1503, Pope Julius II commissioned Bramante—Raphael’s mentor and a close friend from the city of Urbino—to design a new Saint Peter’s Basilica. The goal was to create a building that would express the power and importance of the papacy, using the visual style of ancient Rome. The drawings and prints in this section show how Raphael’s career as an architect developed after he took over this enormous project following Bramante’s death. In 1517, Raphael bought the Palazzo Caprini, a building designed by Bramante that no longer exists but is shown in two works here. He spent his final years in Rome living in this house, known in his time as the “prince of painters.”

CuratorialText: The Met acquired its first Chinese painting in 1902. Since then, the Museum has added more than two thousand works of painting and calligraphy, building one of the most comprehensive collections in the world. Spanning fifteen hundred years of cultural production and featuring a variety of genres, techniques, and styles, The Met's collection has become a key resource for the study of Chinese painting and calligraphy. This exhibition presents a rich selection of works from the collection arranged in a largely chronological display. The final gallery tells the story of Wen C. Fong (1930–2018), chairman of the Museum’s Department of Asian Art from 1971 to 2000, and his teacher Li Jian (1881–1956). As a young calligraphy prodigy in Shanghai in the 1940s, Fong studied brush arts with Li. The display centers on a group of fans, painted and written by Li, that were intended to transmit the canonical models of the classical tradition from teacher to student. Brought to the United States in 1949 and treasured by the Fong family since then, these fans are presented publicly for the first time.
