Brought to the United States in 1949 and treasured by the Fong family ever since, these fans are presented publicly for the first time.

CuratorialText: The hand-held fan was an unexpected muse for some of the most innovative artists in 19th-century Europe. Fans became hugely popular across many levels of society during this period, serving as functional and fashionable objects of adornment and communication. Well-known French Impressionists such as Edgar Degas and Camille Pissarro not only featured this feminine accessory in their work but also adopted it as an experimental format for their art. Fanmania investigates why avant-garde artists incorporated fans into their work and sheds light on themes of gender, courtship, consumerism, and appropriation. Artists were attracted to the semicircular form for myriad reasons, including fascination with fans from Asia and Spain, commercial ambition, and their interest in formal and technical innovation. Displaying more than 75 artworks from across The Met collection, this multimedia exhibition features painted and printed fans from Europe and Asia as well as artworks that depict women wielding fans to explore the phenomenon of “fanmania.”
persona3_adapted: The hand-held fan served as an unexpected source of inspiration for some of the most groundbreaking artists in 19th-century Europe. During this period, fans became extremely popular across all levels of society, functioning both as practical accessories and fashionable items for self-expression and nonverbal communication. Well-known French Impressionists like Edgar Degas and Camille Pissarro not only included this feminine object in their paintings but also used it as an experimental surface for their own art. This exhibition, titled *Fanmania*, explores why forward-thinking artists incorporated fans into their work, and examines themes such as gender roles, romantic rituals, consumer culture, and the borrowing of artistic styles from other cultures. Artists were drawn to the fan's semicircular shape for many reasons: fascination with fans from Asia and Spain, hopes for commercial success, and interest in exploring new forms and techniques. Featuring more than 75 artworks from across The Met collection, this multimedia exhibition includes painted and printed fans from Europe and Asia, as well as artworks showing women using fans, all to investigate the craze known as “fanmania.”

CuratorialText: During the nineteenth century, handheld fans achieved unprecedented popularity throughout European society. A newfound artistic appreciation for these objects also emerged, fueled by cultural crazes for all things Japanese and Spanish as well as by exhibitions and publications devoted to fan making and its history. “Almost all the great artists of the century have contributed . . . to the decoration of the best fans,” wrote the author Octave Uzanne in 1882. For the fourth Impressionist exhibition of 1879, Edgar Degas envisioned an entire gallery devoted to fans. Though the fan-shaped paintings his colleagues submitted were ultimately dispersed throughout the show, they nevertheless elicited strong responses from the public, including bewilderment, as illustrated below. Today, these works continue to raise questions—mainly, why did ambitious European artists adopt this accessory as a format for their work? Bringing together artworks from across The Met, Fanmania explores the reasons artists were drawn to this semicircular form, including its commercial potential, its fashionability, and the opportunities it offered for formal and technical innovation. Although avant-garde fans were primarily designed for display rather than use, they retain many of the associations of functional fans. Themes of gender, courtship, appropriation, and experimentation unfold in this examination of fans as objects of adornment, decoration, and communication in the nineteenth century.
persona3_adapted: During the 1800s, handheld fans became incredibly popular across European society. People also began to appreciate these objects as art, driven by widespread fascination with Japanese and Spanish culture, as well as by exhibitions and books about fan-making and its history. In 1882, the writer Octave Uzanne noted, “Almost all the great artists of the century have contributed . . . to the decoration of the best fans.” For the fourth Impressionist exhibition in 1879, the artist Edgar Degas planned an entire room dedicated to fans. Although the fan-shaped paintings his fellow artists submitted ended up scattered throughout the show, they still provoked strong reactions from the public—including confusion, as shown below. Today, these works continue to raise questions, mainly: why did ambitious European artists choose this accessory as a format for their art? Bringing together artworks from across The Met, *Fanmania* explores the reasons artists were drawn to this half-circle shape, including its potential to sell, its trendiness, and the ways it allowed for new approaches to form and technique. Even though avant-garde fans were mostly made for display rather than actual use, they still carry many of the meanings of functional fans. Themes of gender, romance, cultural borrowing, and artistic experimentation emerge in this look at fans as objects of decoration, fashion, and communication in the 1800s.

CuratorialText: Handheld folding fans are multimedia objects that involve many types of artistry and artisanal skill. This show focuses primarily on the role of painters and printmakers in the production of the fan’s semicircular leaf. In Europe, prior to 1700, most of these designs were unsigned. In the nineteenth century, major artists grew increasingly interested in making fans and began signing their work, as did preeminent fan-making houses, which evolved out of earlier guilds. Some of the precedents for these practices are on display here.
persona3_adapted: Handheld folding fans are objects that combine multiple forms of artistic and craft-based skill. This exhibition focuses mainly on the painters and printmakers who created the fan’s semicircular central panel. In Europe, before 1700, most of these designs were not signed. In the 1800s, major artists became more interested in making fans and began signing their work, as did the leading fan-making companies, which grew out of earlier trade organizations. Some examples of what came before these practices are shown here.

CuratorialText: Historically, European fans have been most closely associated with the eighteenth century, when their use became widespread. The examples here show the various roles fans played, including as tools for flirtation, amusement, education, propaganda, observation, and masquerade. Fan leaves communicated through their subjects and style, while users brought them to life through their gestures. Women were not only the consumers of fans but also among their producers, as fan making was considered an art form appropriate for female practitioners, whether amateur or professional.
persona3_adapted: Historically, European fans are most strongly linked to the eighteenth century, when their use became common. The examples here show the different roles fans played—as tools for flirtation, entertainment, learning, spreading ideas, watching others, and disguise. The painted surfaces of fans (called "leaves") communicated messages through their images and artistic style, while the people using them brought those messages to life through their hand movements. Women were not only the buyers of fans but also helped make them, since fan-making was seen as a suitable art form for women, whether they worked as amateurs or professionals.

CuratorialText: Handheld fans found perhaps their most prominent public display in the opera house. The atmosphere of spectatorship and performance—present both onstage and among the audience—heightened attention to fans in the hands of female theatergoers. The emphasis on seeing and being seen even gave rise to fan designs that incorporated opera glasses, or lorgnettes (from the French lorgner, “to ogle”), and small mirrors. A lorgnette fan is on view in the case nearby. Fan makers also developed a genre of fans devoted to the opera. These fans incorporated scores, lyrics, and homages to celebrated composers.
persona3_adapted: Handheld fans reached perhaps their most visible public role in the opera house. The sense of watching and being watched—both onstage and among the audience—made fans especially noticeable in the hands of female theatergoers. This focus on seeing and being seen even led to fan designs that included opera glasses (small binoculars used in theaters) or lorgnettes (from the French word *lorgner*, meaning “to ogle”), as well as small mirrors. A fan with a lorgnette is displayed in the nearby case. Fan makers also created a type of fan dedicated to the opera itself. These fans featured musical scores, lyrics, and tributes to famous composers.

CuratorialText: As theatergoing grew more popular later in the century, Impressionist artists seized on the novel subject of spectators in the theater loge. The fashionability and ubiquity of fans at the opera had become fully apparent by the early 1880s, as evidenced by the cartoon below.
persona3_adapted: As theatergoing became more popular later in the 1800s, Impressionist artists took up a new subject: people watching performances from theater boxes. By the early 1880s, it was clear that fans had become both fashionable and common at the opera, as the cartoon below shows.

CuratorialText: Movement is an essential aspect of the handheld fan. The nineteenth-century critic Charles Blanc wrote that the fan is “above all things . . . a means or motive of gracious movements, under pretext of agitating the air for the sake of coolness.” The expressive qualities of a fan’s motions are among the ways it can convey meaning and reveal the user’s personality, mood, or intentions. Fans can flutter, vibrate, waft, snap, open, shut, rise, and even fall (or be deliberately dropped) at a particularly opportune moment before a potential suitor.
persona3_adapted: Movement is central to how a handheld fan works. The nineteenth-century critic Charles Blanc described the fan as "above all things . . . a means or motive of gracious movements, under pretext of agitating the air for the sake of coolness." The way a fan moves can express a great deal—it can communicate meaning and reveal something about the user's personality, mood, or intentions. A fan might flutter, vibrate, waft, snap, open, shut, rise, or even fall (or be deliberately dropped) at just the right moment, perhaps in front of a potential suitor.

CuratorialText: Artists in the late nineteenth century understood the fan as a moving object and attempted to capture its dynamism in myriad ways. The fleeting and ephemeral nature of its movement surely made it an appealing subject for the Impressionists. Fan designs frequently incorporate metallic paints, gold leaf, feathers, spangles, and sequins, the visual impacts of which are heightened when in motion.
persona3_adapted: Artists in the late nineteenth century saw the fan as an object in motion and tried to capture its energy and movement in many different ways. The fan's brief and passing moments of motion likely made it an especially attractive subject for the Impressionists, a group of artists known for painting quick impressions of light and everyday life. Fan designs often include shiny metallic paints, thin sheets of gold, feathers, and small sparkling decorations, whose visual effects become more striking when the fan is moving.

CuratorialText: The term “Japonisme” was first used by French critics in the early 1870s to describe the passion for Japanese art and culture they observed among their contemporaries. Japanese goods became increasingly available in Europe after the United States forced Japan to open its ports to international trade in 1853–54. The 1867 and 1878 Universal Exhibitions in Paris were pivotal events in Europeans’ exposure to the arts of Japan. Many Impressionist artists and their friends collected such works, including fans, acquiring them from newly established specialist dealers and shops. By the late 1880s, Japanese fans were imported in the millions to France, where they were used as women’s accessories and decoration.
persona3_adapted: The term “Japonisme” was first used by French critics in the early 1870s to describe the widespread fascination with Japanese art and culture they noticed among their peers. Japanese products became more available in Europe after the United States pressured Japan to open its ports to international trade in 1853–54. Two major world’s fairs in Paris—the Universal Exhibitions of 1867 and 1878—were key moments in introducing Europeans to Japanese arts. Many Impressionist artists and their friends collected these items, such as fans, buying them from newly established specialty dealers and shops. By the late 1880s, millions of Japanese fans were being imported to France, where they were used both as fashion accessories for women and as decorative objects.

CuratorialText: European artists focused on the aesthetic value of these objects and borrowed from them liberally, often with little understanding of their original contexts. Today, some would call this cultural appropriation, though subtleties of interpretation varied. Certainly, the history of Japonisme is fraught, but the admiration for Japanese fans freed European artists to imagine new techniques and strategies for their art.
persona3_adapted: European artists were drawn to the beauty of these objects and freely took ideas from them, often without knowing much about their original cultural meanings. Today, some people would call this cultural appropriation, though opinions on the matter have always been complex. The story of Japonisme—the European fascination with Japanese art—is certainly complicated, but the appreciation for Japanese fans gave European artists the freedom to explore new methods and approaches in their own work.

CuratorialText: In the nineteenth century, Spain became a center of fan production at the same time that the country’s tourism industry emerged and its culture was increasingly exported throughout Europe. Reporting from his travels to the Iberian Peninsula in the early 1840s, the French writer Théophile Gautier claimed, “A woman without a fan is something I have not yet seen in this happy country.” The fan’s prominent role in Spanish flamenco dance is likely another reason it became inextricably linked with Spain in the nineteenth-century imagination, and remains so today. In Paris at mid-century, Spanish culture was in vogue, a phenomenon known as Hispagnolisme. Spanish-born empress Eugénie de Montijo, who married Napoleon III in 1853, exerted considerable influence on fashion and taste. Spanish dance troupes and musicians performed often in the capital, and avant-garde artists, such as Edouard Manet, took them up as subjects. For some artists who made fans in this period, it was Spain that they had foremost in mind. Manet and Degas both chose Spanish themes for their earliest fan designs.
persona3_adapted: In the nineteenth century, Spain became a major center for making fans, just as the country's tourism industry was growing and Spanish culture was being shared more widely across Europe. Writing about his travels to Spain in the early 1840s, the French author Théophile Gautier remarked, “A woman without a fan is something I have not yet seen in this happy country.” The fan's important role in Spanish flamenco dance likely also explains why it became so closely tied to Spain in the minds of nineteenth-century Europeans—a connection that continues today. In Paris around the 1850s, Spanish culture was very fashionable, a trend known as *Hispanolisme* (a fascination with all things Spanish). The Spanish-born empress Eugénie de Montijo, who married Napoleon III in 1853, had a strong influence on fashion and style. Spanish dance groups and musicians performed often in the French capital, and cutting-edge artists like Edouard Manet chose them as subjects for their work. For some artists who created fans during this period, Spain was the main inspiration. Both Manet and Degas selected Spanish themes for their earliest fan designs.

CuratorialText: The theme of the bourgeois woman with her fan in a domestic setting proliferated in art of the last quarter of the nineteenth century. In his 1882 book The Fan, the French writer Octave Uzanne described the role of fans to his female readers: “In all solitude, in every despair, it will remain your confidante.” Whether at home or on ventures out, the handheld fan served as a companion, trusted protector, and elegant accoutrement.
persona3_adapted: The image of a middle-class woman holding a fan in a home setting became increasingly common in art during the last quarter of the nineteenth century. In his 1882 book *The Fan*, the French writer Octave Uzanne described the fan’s role to his female readers: “In all solitude, in every despair, it will remain your confidante.” Whether at home or while out and about, the handheld fan acted as a companion, a trusted protector, and an elegant accessory.

CuratorialText: As fashion accessories, fans signaled facets of their user’s identity through their materials and stylistic form as well as in the way they were handled. “One wave of the Fan is sufficient by itself to distinguish between a princess and a countess, a marchioness and a plebeian,” remarked one female observer cited by Uzanne. A Japanese uchiwa fan in the hands of the Impressionist Berthe Morisot or a plumed fan held by one of Mary Cassatt’s sitters displayed their alignment with current taste and confirmed their social status.
persona3_adapted: As fashion accessories, fans communicated aspects of their owner’s identity—not only through the materials and design, but also through how they were held or moved. One female observer, quoted by the writer Uzanne, noted that “one wave of the Fan is sufficient by itself to distinguish between a princess and a countess, a marchioness and a plebeian.” For example, when the Impressionist painter Berthe Morisot carried a Japanese uchiwa fan (a flat, rigid type of fan), or when a woman in a painting by Mary Cassatt held a fan decorated with feathers, these choices showed that they followed current fashion and reinforced their place in society.

CuratorialText: Fans were among the first souvenirs, used since the seventeenth century to commemorate events and to remember visits to important sites. As travel increased in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, fans became popular as small, practical, and artistic objects that could serve as mementos. The Universal Exhibitions of the second half of the nineteenth century played a crucial role in promoting the French fan industry and exposing the European public to fans from other cultures. Souvenir fans were produced for each of these events, providing visitors with information and maps while also recording the remarkable buildings erected specifically for these occasions.
persona3_adapted: Fans were among the first souvenirs, used since the 1600s to mark special events and to remember visits to important places. As travel became more common in the 1700s and 1800s, fans grew popular as small, useful, and decorative objects that could serve as keepsakes. The World's Fairs of the late 1800s—large international exhibitions showcasing industry and culture—were especially important in boosting the French fan-making industry and introducing European audiences to fans from other cultures. Souvenir fans were made for each of these fairs, giving visitors information and maps while also capturing images of the remarkable buildings built specifically for these events.

CuratorialText: This monumental work was one of a series of carved architectural supports designed for the exterior courtyard of a Yoruba palace. It was commissioned by a king from the most renowned master sculptor in the history of Yoruba art, Olowe of Ise. The two-tiered composition embodies a formal dynamism that established Olowe's exceptional artistic reputation. Its principal personage, an equestrian warrior, is depicted frontally above a female caryatid with arms raised in three-quarter view. Through this contrast in their alignment, the figures at once reflect distinct attitudes while relating to each other fluidly. The compressed style of the upper half also contrasts with the greater degree of openness below. At the base two smaller figures radiate outward at oblique angles. In addition to the inventive dynamism of their overall design, works by Olowe are noted for the attention given to surface detail. The mounted warrior holds a spear and pistol in either hand, and his vest, saddle, and horse's headgear are articulated through deeply carved linear motifs. While innovative in its formal interpretation, the subject of this work is a classic emblem of regional leadership. In Yoruba art such equestrian figures identify their patrons with martial conquest achieved through physical might.
persona3_adapted:
This monumental work is one of a series of carved architectural supports originally made for the outdoor courtyard of a Yoruba palace. It was commissioned by a king from the most celebrated master sculptor in Yoruba art history, Olowe of Ise. The carving is divided into two levels, and its overall design creates a sense of controlled energy—a quality that established Olowe’s exceptional reputation.

The main figure is a warrior on horseback, shown facing forward. Beneath him is a female figure with her arms raised, depicted at a slight angle. By contrasting their poses—one rigidly frontal, the other turned—the artist suggests that the two figures have different attitudes, yet they still relate to one another smoothly. The upper half of the carving is more compact and tightly packed, while the lower half feels more open and spacious. At the base, two smaller figures lean outward at diagonal angles.

Beyond the inventive energy of the overall composition, Olowe’s works are also known for their careful attention to surface details. The mounted warrior holds a spear in one hand and a pistol in the other, and his vest, saddle, and the horse’s headgear are all decorated with deeply cut linear patterns. Although the carving’s style was innovative for its time, its subject is a classic symbol of regional leadership. In Yoruba art, images of horseback warriors identify their patrons with military conquest achieved through physical strength.

CuratorialText: This saltcellar is both an extraordinary example of skilled workmanship and an artifact that epitomizes a singularly important convergence of cultures. In the second half of the fifteenth century, Portuguese explorers and traders were impressed by the considerable talent of ivory carvers along the coast of West Africa. As a result, they were inspired to commission works of this kind for their patrons, which ingeniously combine both European and African aesthetics and forms. During this period, salt and pepper were costly commodities and elaborate receptacles were appropriate for their storage in princely homes. This work contains imagery relating to early Temne and Bullom worldviews. The four snakes, associated with mystical wealth, appear to confront four growling dogs. According to regional traditions, dogs are considered spiritually astute animals able to see spirits and ghosts that are invisible to humans. This depiction of the dogs, with teeth bared, hair bristling, and ears laid back, may relate to that ability. However, the level of animation in this scene could also derive from chivalric hunting scenes in European woodcuts, which were furnished to local African artists by their European patrons.
persona3_adapted:
This saltcellar is both a remarkable example of skilled craftsmanship and an object that represents a uniquely important meeting of cultures. In the second half of the fifteenth century, Portuguese explorers and traders were deeply impressed by the considerable talent of ivory carvers along the coast of West Africa. As a result, they commissioned works like this one for their patrons, pieces that cleverly blend both European and African visual styles and forms.

At the time, salt and pepper were expensive goods, so elaborate containers like this were fitting for storing them in wealthy households. The work includes imagery related to early Temne and Bullom worldviews. Four snakes, which symbolize spiritual wealth, appear to confront four growling dogs. According to local traditions, dogs are considered spiritually perceptive animals, able to see spirits and ghosts that remain invisible to humans. This depiction of the dogs—with teeth bared, fur bristling, and ears flattened—may relate to that ability. However, the sense of movement and energy in this scene could also come from European hunting scenes found in woodcuts, which were provided to local African artists by their European patrons.

CuratorialText: By the early nineteenth century, Chokwe chiefs in the savanna of present-day Democratic Republic of Congo and Angola had become active trading partners with merchants from Europe and the New World. Profiting from commerce in ivory, rubber, wax, and African slaves, they emerged as important regional rulers whose prestige and power was reflected in the art they commissioned for their courts at this time. This figure depicts a Chokwe chief, or mwanangana, and epitomizes the balance of power and refinement that is characteristic of Chokwe court art. His large, spreading hands, muscular shoulders, and aggressive posture epitomize the strength and vigor of a ruler. A bulging, domelike forehead and large eyes suggest mental acuity and keen eyesight. The sweeping curves and swelling volumes of the distinctive headdress, rendered here in wood, depict an actual Chokwe crown made of cloth-covered basketry. Horns of the tiny duiker antelope, which bear associations with hunting and the dangerous, mysterious practices of medicine and sorcery, are placed on the sides of the headdress. The chief holds a lamellophone, or "thumb piano," an instrument whose music often accompanies recitations of oral histories and the praise songs of important individuals. Instrument in hand, the chief is cast as a repository and guardian of historical and cultural knowledge. While depicting a nineteenth-century Chokwe chief, this sculpture also evokes the culture hero Cibinda Ilunga, a legendary hunter from the powerful Luba kingdom. Cibinda Ilunga is said to have introduced new principles of governance to the Chokwe peoples, and figures such as this one honor the productive union of these two cultures.
persona3_adapted:
By the early 1800s, Chokwe chiefs in the grasslands of what is now the Democratic Republic of Congo and Angola had become active trading partners with merchants from Europe and the Americas. They grew wealthy from trading ivory, rubber, wax, and enslaved people, and became powerful regional rulers. Their prestige and authority were reflected in the art they ordered for their royal courts during this period.

This sculpture shows a Chokwe chief, known as a *mwanangana*, and it perfectly captures the blend of power and elegance that defines Chokwe court art. His large, open hands, muscular shoulders, and assertive stance convey a ruler’s strength and energy. A bulging, dome-shaped forehead and large eyes suggest sharp intelligence and keen observation. The sweeping curves and rounded shapes of the distinctive headdress—carved here from wood—represent an actual Chokwe crown made of cloth-covered basketry. On the sides of the headdress are the horns of the tiny duiker antelope, which are linked to hunting and to the dangerous, mysterious practices of medicine and sorcery.

The chief holds a lamellophone, also called a “thumb piano.” The music from this instrument often accompanies the telling of oral histories and songs that praise important people. With this instrument in hand, the chief is shown as a keeper and protector of historical and cultural knowledge.

Although this sculpture depicts a nineteenth-century Chokwe chief, it also brings to mind the culture hero Cibinda Ilunga, a legendary hunter from the powerful Luba kingdom. According to tradition, Cibinda Ilunga introduced new ideas about leadership to the Chokwe people. Figures like this one honor the productive blending of these two cultures.

CuratorialText: The Dogon live in the elevated rocky heights of Mali's Bandiagara Escarpment. They rely on agriculture and manage to wrest subsistence crops from poor soil in an area that receives little rain. This monumental figure is one of the largest Dogon sculptures known. The region's dry climate has preserved Dogon works far longer than is usual for African wood sculpture, and radiocarbon testing of this work suggests that it may date as far back as the fifteenth century. Dogon sculpture is primarily concerned with the spirits responsible for the fertility of both land and people. These include a family's real and mythical ancestors, the souls of women who died in childbirth, and water spirits. While the exact functions and meanings of individual works often remain obscure, scholars agree that Dogon sculptures were created for shrines. The most distinctive subject rendered by Dogon sculptures is that of a single figure standing with raised arms. This posture has usually been interpreted as a gesture of prayer—an effort to link earth and heavens—and it has been suggested that it may represent an appeal for rain. This masterpiece of Dogon figurative art depicts a man with a well-modeled body in a naturalistic stance. The artist has carefully rendered the figure's musculature, anatomical detail, and body ornament. His stylized beard identifies him as an elder and an individual whose age and experience entitle him to participate in the most important religious, political, and social affairs of Dogon society. The figure wears wristlets, armlets, and anklets that indicate his status, as well as a belt and neck pendants resembling leather talismans that also suggest his spiritual importance.
persona3_adapted:
The Dogon people live in the rocky highlands of Mali's Bandiagara Escarpment, a region with poor soil and little rainfall where they rely on farming to grow enough food to survive. This large statue is one of the biggest known Dogon sculptures. Because the area's dry climate preserves wood much better than in most parts of Africa, scientists have been able to use radiocarbon dating to estimate that this work may have been created as early as the 1400s.

Dogon sculpture focuses on spirits that ensure the fertility of the land and of people. These include a family's real and mythical ancestors, the souls of women who died in childbirth, and water spirits. Although we often don't know the exact purpose or meaning of individual pieces, experts agree that Dogon sculptures were made for sacred spaces called shrines.

The most recognizable subject in Dogon sculpture is a single figure standing with arms raised. Scholars usually interpret this pose as a gesture of prayer—an attempt to connect the earth with the sky—and some suggest it may represent a plea for rain.

This masterpiece of Dogon figure carving shows a man with a realistically shaped body. The artist carefully depicted the figure's muscles, body details, and ornaments. His stylized beard marks him as an elder—someone whose age and experience give him the right to take part in the most important religious, political, and social matters of Dogon society. The figure wears bracelets on his wrists and arms, and anklets, which signal his high status. He also has a belt and neck pendants that look like leather charms, suggesting his spiritual importance.

CuratorialText: When works from Equatorial Africa in this refined style began to enter Western consciousness in the early twentieth century, they were a great enigma to art critics. Many speculated about the sources of their exotic aesthetic and even proposed possible Asian influence, though the art form was in fact indigenous to southern Gabon. Such masks were worn by virtuosic male performers of a stilt dance called "mukudj," which involved towering impressively while executing complex choreography and astonishing feats of acrobatics. The creator of a "mukudj" mask would attempt to capture the likeness of the most beautiful woman in his community. The subject of this particular idealized and stylized portrait was embellished in classic nineteenth-century fashion with a coiffure composed of a central lobe and two lateral tresses and with cicatrization motifs on the forehead and temples. Kaolin taken from riverbeds, which was associated with healing and with a spiritual, ancestral realm of existence, was applied to the surface of the face. By using this material, the artist both celebrated the beauty of a mortal woman and transformed her into a transcendent being.
persona3_adapted: When artworks from Equatorial Africa in this polished style first became known to Western audiences in the early 1900s, they puzzled art critics. Many wondered where their unusual beauty came from, and some even guessed that Asian art had influenced them—though in fact, this art form originated in southern Gabon. These masks were worn by highly skilled male dancers who performed a stilt dance called "mukudj." The dancers would tower above the crowd while carrying out complicated movements and amazing acrobatic feats. The artist who made a "mukudj" mask would try to capture the appearance of the most beautiful woman in his community. This particular portrait, which idealizes and stylizes its subject, was decorated in the classic style of the 1800s: a hairstyle with a central section and two side locks, plus scar patterns on the forehead and temples. A white clay called kaolin, taken from riverbeds, was applied to the face. Because kaolin was linked to healing and to a spiritual world of ancestors, the artist used it both to honor the beauty of a living woman and to turn her into a being that goes beyond ordinary life.

CuratorialText: A lü me was typically commissioned by a man of standing in honor of a favorite wife. The subject’s distinguishing traits, including her facial features, cicatrices, and braided coiffure, would be captured by the artist through direct observation. The unveiling of a completed figure was celebrated with lavish feasting sponsored by its patron. Named for their subjects and stored in special baskets, such portraits were subsequently displayed on special occasions. This example was carved by celebrated early 20th century carver, Zlan of Belewale, and his wife, Sonzlanwan. Zlan’s calling was revealed to him as an adolescent through the miraculous appearance of his signature tool, an adze, falling from the branches of an oil palm. The sculptor honed his skills on soft tuber roots and soapstone before his mastery of wood earned him the name Zlan (sometimes spelled Sra), meaning "creator" or "god." As the most sought-after Dan artist of the first half of the twentieth century, Zlan mentored scores of apprentices. Many of these trainees were sent by regional chiefs to acquire his expertise for their own communities. Through his efforts, the small Wè village of Belewale—where his father had also been a sculptor—became an important carving center. Zlan’s works, including masks, ladles, and figurative portraits, have been collected globally. Defying the convention that woodcarving was a man’s vocation, Sonzlanwon (literally, "a snail, if god agrees") collaborated with her husband on many of his greatest works. She accompanied him on commissions for important patrons—the intense concentration required to execute these was such that they often worked side by side in seclusion. Sonzlanwon is credited with blocking out Zlan’s works’ initial form, using wood felled by his apprentices. Following his refinement of the contours, she handled final detailing, including the braided raffia-palm coiffures added to figures and ladles. As Zlan aged, it is believed that Sonzlanwon’s role in their collaboration expanded. Documentation of her contributions derives from interviews conducted by German anthropologist Hans Himmelheber shortly before Zlan’s death, as well as from remembrances of surviving relatives and acquaintances.
persona3_adapted:
A *lü me* was a type of portrait sculpture, usually commissioned by a man of high status to honor a favorite wife. The artist would capture the woman’s distinguishing features—such as her facial characteristics, scar patterns, and braided hairstyle—by observing her directly. When the carving was finished, the person who paid for it would celebrate with a lavish feast. These portraits were named after the women they depicted, stored in special baskets, and brought out only for important occasions.

This particular example was carved by the celebrated early 20th-century artist Zlan of Belewale and his wife, Sonzlanwan. Zlan’s calling as a sculptor was revealed to him as a teenager when his signature tool—a type of carving axe called an adze—miraculously appeared, falling from the branches of an oil palm tree. He first practiced his craft on soft root vegetables and soapstone before mastering wood, eventually earning the name Zlan (sometimes spelled Sra), which means "creator" or "god." As the most sought-after artist of the Dan people in the first half of the twentieth century, Zlan trained many apprentices. Most were sent by regional chiefs who wanted him to pass on his skills to their own communities. Through his efforts, the small Wè village of Belewale—where his father had also been a sculptor—became an important center for carving. Zlan’s works, including masks, ladles, and portrait figures, are now collected around the world.

Defying the tradition that woodcarving was a man’s job, Sonzlanwon (whose name literally means "a snail, if god agrees") worked alongside her husband on many of his greatest pieces. She accompanied him on commissions for important patrons. The intense concentration required for these works was so great that the couple often worked side by side in seclusion. Sonzlanwon is credited with shaping the initial form of Zlan’s carvings, using wood that his apprentices had cut down. After Zlan refined the contours, she handled the final details, including the braided raffia-palm hairstyles added to figures and ladles. As Zlan grew older, it is believed that Sonzlanwon’s role in their partnership expanded. What we know of her contributions comes from interviews conducted by German anthropologist Hans Himmelheber shortly before Zlan’s death, as well as from the memories of surviving relatives and acquaintances.

CuratorialText: Central African power figures are among the ubiquitous genres identified with African art. Conceived to house specific mystical forces, they were collaborative creations of Kongo sculptors and ritual specialists. This example belongs to the most ambitious class of that tradition, attributed to the atelier of a master active along the coast of Congo and Angola at the end of the nineteenth century and identified with Mangaaka, the preeminent force of jurisprudence. That power was represented as a presiding authority and enforcing lord or king. Its crowning element is the distinctive mpu headdress worn by chiefs or priests. The figure's posture and gesture, leaning forward with hands placed akimbo on the hips, is the aggressive attitude of one who challenges fearlessly. Traces of a missing beard-a sign of seniority-survive in the form of nails along the contours of the chin. There are also vestiges of an abdominal cavity for medicinal matter that originally attracted the figure's defining force. The various metals embedded in the figure's expansive torso attest to its central role as witness and enforcer of affairs critical to its community. They document vows sealed, treaties signed, and efforts to eradicate evil. Ultimately, this work inspired reflection on the consequences of transgressing established codes of social conduct.
persona3_adapted: Central African power figures are among the most well-known types of art associated with the region. Designed to hold specific spiritual forces, they were created through the collaboration of Kongo sculptors and ritual specialists. This particular figure belongs to the most ambitious category of that tradition, attributed to the workshop of a master artist active along the coast of what is now Congo and Angola at the end of the nineteenth century. It is linked to Mangaaka, the supreme force of justice and law. That power was portrayed as a ruling authority and an enforcing lord or king. The figure’s crowning feature is the distinctive *mpu* headdress, a type of hat worn by chiefs or priests. Its posture and gesture—leaning forward with hands placed firmly on the hips—convey the aggressive stance of someone who challenges without fear. Traces of a missing beard, a sign of seniority, survive in the form of nails along the chin. There are also remnants of a cavity in the abdomen that originally held medicinal substances, which attracted the figure’s defining spiritual power. The various metals embedded across its broad chest show that this figure served as a witness and enforcer for important community matters. They mark vows that were sealed, treaties that were signed, and efforts to eliminate evil. Ultimately, this work encouraged reflection on the consequences of breaking established social rules.

CuratorialText: Throughout the twentieth century, members of Poro, a Senufo initiation association, wore small, finely carved face masks as insignia. The masks, known as kpeliye'e, feature delicate oval faces with geometric projections at the sides. Raised and incised scarification patterns ornament their smooth, glossy surfaces. Considered feminine, the masks honor deceased Senufo elders with their grace and beauty. They provide a complement to the aggressive Senufo helmet masks also sponsored by fraternal organizations in the region. The feathers and animal horns attached to this example are unusual, and may have reflected its owner's power to counteract negative forces in the community.
persona3_adapted: Throughout the twentieth century, members of Poro—a Senufo society that initiates young people into adulthood—wore small, carefully carved face masks as badges of their membership. These masks, called *kpeliye'e*, have delicate oval faces with geometric shapes projecting from the sides. Raised and carved patterns, representing scarification (a traditional form of body marking), decorate their smooth, shiny surfaces. Considered feminine, the masks honor deceased Senufo elders through their grace and beauty. They serve as a counterpart to the more aggressive Senufo helmet masks, also sponsored by men's societies in the region. The feathers and animal horns attached to this particular mask are unusual, and may have reflected its owner's power to ward off harmful forces in the community.

CuratorialText: This cross was created in the northern Highlands, near the Red Sea, the birthplace of Ethiopia's earliest kingdom and of Christianity in Africa. A small corpus of Ethiopian Christian works from before the seventeenth century has survived. This exceptional creation, in which a highly unusual interplay of materials affords rich tonal contrasts, is a tour de force. The solidity of the carved-wood structure is permeated by finely applied metal inlays. The complexity of this work, with each side elaborately distinct, is unrivaled. It has been attributed to Ezra (ca. 1460-1522), a member of the Stephanite monastic order. The Stephanites came to be recognized as the foremost carvers of crosses and illuminators of manuscripts. During a period of the order's exile in Egypt, Ezra is reputed to have enhanced his training as a craftsman and learned the technique of marquetry. It has been suggested that he may have produced this work at the Gunda Gunde monastery, renowned for its manuscripts. In the Ethiopian church, the wooden cross is perceived as having been sanctified by Christ's blood, which conferred upon it infinite power to heal and to bless. Foliate and organic interlace designs, branching from the foot of the cross, are interpreted as symbolizing the Tree of Paradise, and thus a vibrant life-giving force. Commissioned by Ethiopian royalty, such works were presented to important monasteries to be carried in liturgical processions.
persona3_adapted:
This cross was made in the northern Highlands, near the Red Sea—the birthplace of Ethiopia’s earliest kingdom and of Christianity in Africa. Only a small number of Ethiopian Christian works from before the 1600s have survived. This exceptional piece stands out for its striking use of materials, which creates rich contrasts in light and dark tones. The solid carved-wood structure is enhanced by finely applied metal inlays. The work is unusually complex, with each side elaborately different from the other—a feature that has no equal among similar objects.

It has been attributed to Ezra (about 1460–1522), a member of the Stephanite monastic order. The Stephanites became known as the finest carvers of crosses and illuminators of manuscripts. During a period when the order was exiled in Egypt, Ezra is said to have improved his skills as a craftsman and learned the technique of marquetry—a method of inlaying pieces of wood or other materials into a surface to form decorative patterns. Some scholars suggest he may have created this cross at the Gunda Gunde monastery, famous for its manuscripts.

In the Ethiopian church, the wooden cross is believed to have been sanctified by Christ’s blood, which gave it infinite power to heal and to bless. The leaf-like and intertwined designs that branch from the foot of the cross are understood to symbolize the Tree of Paradise, and therefore a vibrant, life-giving force. Commissioned by Ethiopian royalty, such works were given to important monasteries to be carried in religious processions.

CuratorialText: This illuminated manuscript of the Four Gospels was created at a monastic center in northern Ethiopia. Twenty full-page paintings depict scenes from the life of Christ and four portraits of the evangelists introduce the respective Gospel texts. The New Testament was translated from Greek into Geez, the classical language of Ethiopia, in the sixth century. Both this text and its pictorial format draw upon Byzantine prototypes, which were transformed into a local idiom of expression. Stylistically consistent, the paintings reflect the hands of two distinct artists. The color scheme consists of red, yellow, green, and blue. A stylized uniformity is reflected in the abbreviated definition of facial features and the bold linear articulation of the human form in black and red. Figures' heads are depicted frontally, their bodies often in profile. Bodies are treated as columnar masses encased in textiles composed of striated fields juxtaposed against one another. This work is evidence of sub-Saharan Africa's historically complex interrelationships with Arabia, Egypt, and the eastern Mediterranean. Early complex societies in the Highlands developed primarily through local processes beginning in the late second millennium B.C.E. Those societies attracted trade from across the Red Sea, Mediterranean, and Indian Ocean, which spurred migrations, religious exchanges, and greater concentrations of wealth. By the early first millennium C.E., the Kingdom of Aksum emerged as a powerful civilization that, at its height, extended from Ethiopia's Highlands in the South, to the Eastern Desert in the North, to southern Arabia in the East. Emperor Ezana (r. ca. 320s-360 C.E.) was the first monarch to convert to Christianity, and adopted it as the state religion. Subsequent kingdoms, including the Zagwe Dynasty (ca. 1137-1270 C.E.) and the Ethiopian Empire (1270-1974 C.E.) continued to embrace Christianity. Monasteries were founded as centers of learning responsible for disseminating knowledge and consolidating the power and influence of the ruling elite. In the Ethiopian Orthodox Church, the text of the Gospels was considered the most important holy writing; the miniatures at the beginning of this manuscript were intended to be viewed during liturgical processions. Such works were frequently presented to churches by distinguished patrons; they reflected both the prestige of royal benefactors and the erudition of the monastic scriptoria in which they were created. Recent research suggests that a member of Ethiopia's ruling elite may have commissioned this manuscript at Dabra Hayg Estifanos monastery for presentation to his or her favored church or monastery. Brief notations indicate that the church in question was dedicated to the Archangel Michael.
persona3_adapted:
This illuminated manuscript of the Four Gospels was made at a monastery in northern Ethiopia. It contains twenty full-page paintings showing scenes from the life of Christ, along with four portraits of the evangelists—the writers of the Gospels—placed at the beginning of each Gospel text. The New Testament was translated from Greek into Geez, the classical language of Ethiopia, in the sixth century. Both the text and the style of the illustrations draw on models from the Byzantine Empire (the eastern Roman world), but these were reworked into a distinctly Ethiopian visual language.

The paintings are consistent in style, though they show the work of two different artists. The colors used are red, yellow, green, and blue. The figures have a stylized, uniform look: facial features are simplified, and the human form is outlined boldly in black and red. Heads are shown facing forward, while bodies are often turned to the side. Bodies are treated like solid, column-like shapes wrapped in clothing made up of striped sections placed next to each other.

This manuscript is evidence of the historically complex connections between sub-Saharan Africa and regions such as Arabia, Egypt, and the eastern Mediterranean. Early complex societies in the Ethiopian Highlands developed largely through local processes beginning in the late second millennium B.C.E. These societies attracted trade from across the Red Sea, the Mediterranean, and the Indian Ocean, which encouraged migration, religious exchange, and the growth of wealth. By the early first millennium C.E., the Kingdom of Aksum had become a powerful civilization. At its height, it stretched from the Highlands of Ethiopia in the south to the Eastern Desert in the north, and as far as southern Arabia in the east.

Emperor Ezana (who ruled around the 320s–360s C.E.) was the first Ethiopian monarch to convert to Christianity, and he made it the state religion. Later kingdoms, including the Zagwe Dynasty (about 1137–1270 C.E.) and the Ethiopian Empire (1270–1974 C.E.), continued to embrace Christianity. Monasteries were established as centers of learning, responsible for spreading knowledge and strengthening the power and influence of the ruling elite. In the Ethiopian Orthodox Church, the text of the Gospels was considered the most important holy writing. The paintings at the beginning of this manuscript were meant to be viewed during religious processions.

Such works were often given to churches by important patrons. They reflected both the prestige of royal donors and the learning of the monastic scriptoria (writing rooms) where they were made. Recent research suggests that a member of Ethiopia’s ruling elite may have commissioned this manuscript at Dabra Hayg Estifanos monastery, to be presented to a church or monastery dedicated to the Archangel Michael. Brief notations in the manuscript confirm this dedication.

CuratorialText: Phillips pursued a long and successful career over fifty years, taking to the road as an itinerant artist painting portraits of clusters of friends, relatives, and neighbors in New York State and New England. His stock in trade was the plain likeness, devoid of shadows or modeling. Although he depended on repetitious formulas to work efficiently as he traveled the countryside, he was nevertheless able to individualize his sitters imaginatively. In "Mrs. Mayer and Daughter," Phillips’s masterful design abilities are evident in the cleanly contained forms of the mother and child, enriched by the use of brilliant, saturated color and careful detail.
persona3_adapted: Phillips enjoyed a long and successful career that spanned more than fifty years. He traveled from place to place as an itinerant artist, painting portraits of groups of friends, relatives, and neighbors in New York State and New England. His specialty was the plain likeness—a straightforward, realistic portrait without shadows or the illusion of three-dimensional form. Although he relied on repeated patterns and techniques to work efficiently as he moved through the countryside, he was still able to give each person he painted a unique and imaginative character. In *Mrs. Mayer and Daughter*, Phillips’s skillful sense of composition is clear in the neatly contained shapes of the mother and child, made richer by the use of bright, intense colors and careful attention to detail.

CuratorialText: Madame Pierre Gautreau (the Louisiana-born Virginie Amélie Avegno; 1859–1915) was known in Paris for her artful appearance. Sargent hoped to enhance his reputation by painting and exhibiting her portrait. Working without a commission but with his sitter’s complicity, he emphasized her daring personal style, showing the right strap of her gown slipping from her shoulder. At the Salon of 1884, the portrait received more ridicule than praise. Sargent repainted the shoulder strap and kept the work for over thirty years. When, eventually, he sold it to the Metropolitan, he commented, “I suppose it is the best thing I have done,” but asked that the Museum disguise the sitter’s name.
persona3_adapted: Madame Pierre Gautreau—born Virginie Amélie Avegno in Louisiana, 1859–1915—was famous in Paris for her carefully cultivated look. The artist John Singer Sargent hoped to boost his own career by painting and publicly showing her portrait. He worked without a formal request from a client, but with his subject’s cooperation. In the painting, he highlighted her bold sense of style by showing the right strap of her gown slipping off her shoulder. When the portrait was displayed at the official Paris art exhibition in 1884, it received more mockery than praise. Sargent later repainted the shoulder strap and kept the painting for more than thirty years. When he finally sold it to the Metropolitan Museum, he remarked, “I suppose it is the best thing I have done,” but asked the museum to hide the sitter’s name.

CuratorialText: Alexander, who lived in Paris during the 1890s, achieved international success with his figure paintings of gracefully posed women in elegant interiors. In this striking composition, the model’s provocative facial expression and supple curves speak to the contemporary French taste for sensuous images of women. The bold composition also suggests the undulating linear rhythms of the Art Nouveau style, examples of which were featured at the Paris Exposition. Alexander, known as the "the painter of the flowing line," was celebrated at home and abroad for these works of sinuous grace and lyricism.
persona3_adapted: Alexander, who lived in Paris in the 1890s, became famous worldwide for his paintings of women—gracefully posed in elegant rooms. In this striking work, the model’s bold facial expression and soft, curving body reflect the French taste at the time for sensual images of women. The daring arrangement of the picture also hints at the flowing, wave-like patterns of the Art Nouveau style, which was shown at the Paris Exposition. Alexander, known as "the painter of the flowing line," was celebrated both in his home country and abroad for these works of smooth, graceful beauty and poetic feeling.

CuratorialText: In early 1859, Bierstadt accompanied a government survey expedition, headed by Frederick W. Lander, to the Nebraska Territory. By summer, the party had reached the Wind River Range of the Rocky Mountains in present-day Wyoming. Painted in New York after Bierstadt’s return, this work advertised the landscape as a frontier destined to be claimed by White settlers, according to the doctrine of Manifest Destiny. This belief that Americans were the divinely ordained "masters" of the continent systematically ignored—with dire consequences—the presence of Indigenous populations, such as the Shoshone peoples depicted in the foreground.
persona3_adapted: In early 1859, Bierstadt joined a government-led survey expedition under Frederick W. Lander, traveling to what was then the Nebraska Territory. By summer, the group had reached the Wind River Range of the Rocky Mountains in present-day Wyoming. After returning to New York, Bierstadt painted this scene. The artwork presents the landscape as a frontier that, according to the idea of Manifest Destiny, was meant to be taken over by white settlers. This belief held that Americans were divinely chosen to control the continent—a view that deliberately ignored, with severe consequences, the presence of Native peoples, including the Shoshone people shown in the foreground.

CuratorialText: In the summer of 1845, Bingham returned to his St. Louis home from a winter stay in central Missouri, bringing with him several paintings and sketches. This was one of those works that he later sent to New York’s American Art-Union, a subscription-based organization that promoted American art nationally through exhibitions and the distribution of popular prints. Titled by the artist "French Trader & Half breed Son", the Art-Union changed it to the more generic and less controversial "Fur Traders Descending the Missouri". Bingham, who began his career as a portraitist, produced this distinctive genre painting with little precedent in his oeuvre. The tranquil scene, with its luminous atmosphere, idealized the American frontier for the benefit of an Eastern audience.
persona3_adapted: In the summer of 1845, Bingham returned to his home in St. Louis after spending the winter in central Missouri, bringing with him several paintings and sketches. This was one of the works he later sent to New York’s American Art-Union, a membership-based organization that promoted American art across the country through exhibitions and the distribution of popular prints. The artist originally titled the piece "French Trader & Half breed Son," but the Art-Union changed it to the more neutral and less provocative "Fur Traders Descending the Missouri." Bingham began his career painting portraits, but this work is a distinctive example of a type of painting that depicts everyday life—known as genre painting—and it had little precedent in his body of work. The calm scene, with its glowing light, presents an idealized view of the American frontier, crafted to appeal to audiences in the eastern United States.

CuratorialText: Fascinated by the winding Connecticut River, Cole made the U-shaped bend known as the “Oxbow” the subject of this painting. Beyond the tempestuous storm clouds and blasted tree lies a patchwork of farmland, sunlight illuminating private properties full of crops, hay bales, and sheep. Cole’s composition emphasizes the encroachment of Euro-American settlement into a supposed untamed “wilderness.” At the lower center, the artist depicted himself pausing from a sketch and gazing at the viewer. “I think that [Cole] is calling out the fact that our way of developing—our way of improving the land or making it profitable—was pushing nature out, Indigenous people out.” Xiye Bastida, climate activist, Audioguide 4026
persona3_adapted: Fascinated by the winding Connecticut River, Cole made the U-shaped bend known as the “Oxbow” the focus of this painting. Beyond the violent storm clouds and broken tree lies a patchwork of farmland, where sunlight brightens private properties filled with crops, hay bales, and sheep. Cole’s arrangement of the scene highlights the spread of Euro-American settlement into a landscape that was falsely seen as untouched “wilderness.” At the lower center, the artist painted himself pausing from a sketch and looking directly at the viewer. “I think that [Cole] is pointing out that our way of developing—our way of improving the land or making it profitable—was pushing nature out, Indigenous people out.” Xiye Bastida, climate activist, Audioguide 4026

CuratorialText: Returning to Philadelphia from Europe in 1870, Eakins began a series of representations of the sport of sculling, a subject with which he is uniquely identified. This is the first major work in that series of paintings and watercolors, and the one that launched his professional career. It is believed to commemorate the victory of Max Schmitt (1843–1900), a school friend, attorney, and skilled amateur rower, in an important race on the Schuylkill River in October 1870. Also an avid rower, Eakins depicted himself pulling the oars of a scull in the middle distance.
persona3_adapted: Returning to Philadelphia from Europe in 1870, Eakins began a series of artworks focused on the sport of sculling—a type of rowing where each person uses two oars—a subject that has become closely tied to his name. This painting is the first major piece in that series, which includes both paintings and watercolors, and it was the work that launched his professional career. It is believed to commemorate the victory of Max Schmitt (1843–1900), a school friend, lawyer, and skilled amateur rower, in an important race on the Schuylkill River in October 1870. Eakins, himself an enthusiastic rower, painted his own image rowing a scull in the middle distance of the scene.

CuratorialText: A man and his dog sit perched on the shore of Narragansett Bay, Rhode Island, sunlight at their backs as they witness darkening clouds and a thin bolt of lightning warning of the tempest to come. Heade vividly captures the anticipation—in the words of a critic of his day, the “ominous hush”—that prevailed under a blackening sky and the eerily illuminated terrain. This is one of the earliest works in a small series of coastal storm subjects painted by Heade, based on a sketch of an approaching storm he witnessed from Prudence Island around 1858.
persona3_adapted: A man and his dog sit on the shore of Narragansett Bay, Rhode Island, with the sun behind them as they watch dark clouds gather and a thin bolt of lightning flash—a warning of the coming storm. Heade captures the feeling of anticipation perfectly. As one critic of his time put it, there is an “ominous hush” in the scene, a tense stillness beneath the blackening sky and the strangely lit landscape. This is one of the earliest works in a small group of coastal storm paintings by Heade. He based it on a sketch of an approaching storm he saw from Prudence Island around 1858.

CuratorialText: Threatened by sharks and a waterspout, a Black man faces his demise aboard a damaged boat. The painting represents the culmination of Homer’s interest in depicting conflict between humans and nature. Sugarcane, the Caribbean commodity central to the economy of empire, and transatlantic slavery are linked by the titular Gulf Stream current. Homer interwove geopolitical themes into an epic saga that foregrounds human struggle—personified by a stoic survivor—against the relentless power of nature and history.
persona3_adapted: Threatened by sharks and a waterspout—a tornado-like column of water at sea—a Black man faces his death on a damaged boat. This painting marks the peak of Homer’s focus on showing the struggle between people and nature. Sugarcane, a Caribbean crop that was essential to the economy of colonial empires, and the history of transatlantic slavery are connected through the painting’s title, *The Gulf Stream*. Homer wove political and global themes into a dramatic story that puts human struggle at the center—embodied by a calm, enduring survivor—against the overwhelming forces of nature and history.

CuratorialText: Cassatt and her family spent the summer of 1880 at Marly-le-Roi, outside of Paris, where the artist focused on depicting gardens and domestic life. Here, she has portrayed her older sister, Lydia, fashionably if incongruously overdressed and insulated by a walled garden landscape. She is absorbed in the sort of old-fashioned textile handwork that was increasingly prized by the affluent in these years of mechanized labor. Although Cassatt’s experiments with plein-air (outdoor) painting are few, in this dynamic composition she effectively captured the dazzling sunlight associated with her fellow French Impressionists.
persona3_adapted: Cassatt and her family spent the summer of 1880 in Marly-le-Roi, a town just outside Paris, where the artist focused on painting gardens and scenes of everyday home life. In this work, she shows her older sister, Lydia, dressed in fashionable but oddly heavy clothing, as if she is too formally dressed for her surroundings. She is enclosed by a walled garden, absorbed in a type of traditional needlework—handmade textile crafts that had become increasingly valued by wealthy people during a time when machines were taking over much of the labor. Although Cassatt rarely painted outdoors, in this lively and energetic composition she successfully captured the bright, intense sunlight that was a hallmark of her fellow French Impressionists.

CuratorialText: Spencer portrayed the unvarnished reality of male-female relationships in this painting, which chronicles the travails of early married life. Here, the unseen young wife is not in full command of her kitchen, having sent her young husband out to procure the groceries—a task for which he is evidently not qualified. Using her own husband as a model, Spencer depicted the man stumbling down the street and attempting to hold on to the items in his overflowing basket, attracting gazes from at least one onlooker and suffering a public humiliation. Spencer depicted in humorous terms a real social anxiety of the time: that of setting up a household and running it efficiently.
persona3_adapted: Spencer captured the raw, unpolished dynamics of male-female relationships in this painting, which tells the story of the struggles of early married life. Here, the young wife—whom we cannot see—is not fully in control of her kitchen, having sent her new husband out to buy the groceries, a task he is clearly not suited for. Using her own husband as a model, Spencer shows the man stumbling down the street, trying to keep hold of the items in his overflowing basket. He draws the attention of at least one onlooker and endures a public embarrassment. With humor, Spencer depicts a real social worry of the time: the challenge of setting up a household and running it smoothly.

CuratorialText: This sculpture is one of the most spectacular examples of ceramic figures created by Tolita-Tumaco artists. It depicts an elderly person, recognizable by facial wrinkles, baggy skin beneath the eyes, the few remaining teeth, and chin stubble. Its flattened head reveals cranial modification during childhood, a cultural practice that could have signaled ethnic identity or rank in antiquity. The gender of this effigy is not clear. Marked nipples, as in this figure’s chest, were more common in female than male representations in Tolita-Tumaco and Jama-Coaque ceramic traditions of coastal Ecuador. This statue, however, lacks the long skirts that are typically seen in female images. The artist(s) would have taken several days to complete the sculpture due to the complexity of the body and the weight of the moist clay. This figure was crafted using the gray clay paste that characterizes Tolita-Tumaco ceramics. The torso was shaped most likely using clay slabs that were gradually added until completing the final form. The limbs and the head were made separately and attached to the torso before drying and firing. A one-side mold was used for the face while the wrinkles and other details were incised when the clay paste was still soft. Today’s monochromatic and matte appearance of these sculptures is the result of wear. Apparently, many of these objects had a smooth and polished finish and were decorated with post-fired paints (see Valdez 1992:136; MMA 1991.436.7a, b), in a similar fashion to Jama-Coaque sculptures (MMA 1980.34.38a, MMA 1980.34.39). A careful examination of the lower back shows that this figure may have been seated on a stool (compare with MMA 1991.436.7a, b). Elaborate stone or wood seats were important symbols of authority in the ancient Americas, from Ecuador to Mesoamerica, including the Amazonian and the Caribbean areas (McEwan and Looper 2021). The relatively high rank or importance of this individual is also suggested by the collar and bracelets modeled in clay. Adornments, probably made of gold, originally hung from holes on its nose, ears, and nipples (see MMA 1979.206.1204 and 1979.206.1136). Tolita-Tumaco potters are known for their realistic renderings of humans, such as the examples in the collections of Casa de Alabado in Quito (Cruz Cevallos 1992:225) and Museo Chileno de Arte Precolombino in Santiago (MCHAP 0348). Some of their figures are depicted leaning forward, adopting an animated pose, with flexed legs, arched torsos, and faces staring ahead. Elders were a popular theme, and several examples can be found in the collections of the Museo del Banco Central de Ecuador in Quito (No. 12-53-69, LT-2-16-69, LT-3-11-70) and in Guayaquil (GA-1-2268-82, GA-5-1631-80). A common interpretation is that these elder figures were representations of religious specialists, or ancient "shamans" who had the responsibility to mediate relations between people and other beings in nature. The marked spine in the back may be revealing of more than a very lean body, as it bears a resemblance to iguanas (compare with a Bahia example of iguanas: MMA 1988.117.7). With dry and wrinkled skin, the comparison of Tolita-Tumaco revered elders with those reptiles could have been a way to evoke the human-animal connection that is a key element in shamanistic rituals. Tolita-Tumaco was a civilization that flourished on the tropical Pacific coast of northern Ecuador and southern Colombia. In a landscape shaped by the rivers, with swampy mangroves, and hills covered in dense forests, these communities settled in large villages organized around plazas and several massive earthen mounds, known as tolas, that were the residences and burials of the elite. Remains of these human and animal sculptures have been found in settlements and cemeteries, but their original use remains unknown. The Tolita-Tumaco tradition started around 600 BCE, but its distinctive style matured between 200 BCE and 400 CE. During these centuries, these communities produced one of the most refined metalwork traditions in the ancient Americas and established relationships with other communities on the Pacific coast, including those in Northern Peru.
persona3_adapted:
This sculpture is one of the most striking examples of ceramic figures made by artists from the Tolita-Tumaco culture. It shows an elderly person, recognizable by facial wrinkles, sagging skin under the eyes, a few remaining teeth, and stubble on the chin. The figure’s flattened head indicates that its skull was intentionally shaped during childhood—a cultural practice that may have signaled ethnic identity or social status in ancient times.

The gender of this figure is not clear. Marked nipples, like those on this figure’s chest, were more common in female than male representations in the ceramic traditions of coastal Ecuador’s Tolita-Tumaco and Jama-Coaque cultures. However, this statue lacks the long skirts typically seen in female images. The artist or artists would have taken several days to complete the sculpture because of the complexity of the body and the weight of the moist clay.

This figure was made from gray clay paste, a material that defines Tolita-Tumaco ceramics. The torso was likely shaped using slabs of clay that were gradually added until the final form was complete. The limbs and head were made separately and attached to the torso before the piece was dried and fired in a kiln. A one-sided mold was used for the face, while the wrinkles and other details were carved into the clay while it was still soft.

The sculpture’s current dull, single-color appearance is the result of wear over time. Originally, many of these objects had a smooth, polished finish and were decorated with paints applied after firing, similar to sculptures from the Jama-Coaque culture. A close look at the lower back suggests that this figure may have been seated on a stool. Elaborate stone or wooden seats were important symbols of authority in the ancient Americas, from Ecuador to Mesoamerica, including the Amazon and Caribbean regions.

The relatively high rank or importance of this individual is also suggested by the collar and bracelets modeled in clay. Adornments, probably made of gold, originally hung from holes in its nose, ears, and nipples. Tolita-Tumaco potters are known for their realistic portrayals of humans. Some of their figures are shown leaning forward in an animated pose, with bent legs, arched torsos, and faces staring straight ahead.

Elderly figures were a popular theme, and several examples can be found in museum collections. A common interpretation is that these elder figures represented religious specialists, or ancient shamans, who were responsible for mediating relationships between people and other beings in nature. The prominent spine on the figure’s back may suggest more than just a very lean body—it bears a resemblance to iguanas. With dry, wrinkled skin, comparing revered elders to these reptiles could have been a way to evoke the human-animal connection that is a key element in shamanistic rituals.

The Tolita-Tumaco civilization flourished on the tropical Pacific coast of northern Ecuador and southern Colombia. In a landscape shaped by rivers, with swampy mangroves and hills covered in dense forests, these communities settled in large villages organized around plazas and several massive earthen mounds, known as tolas. These mounds served as residences and burial sites for the elite. Remains of human and animal sculptures like this one have been found in settlements and cemeteries, but their original use remains unknown.

The Tolita-Tumaco tradition began around 600 BCE, but its distinctive style matured between 200 BCE and 400 CE. During these centuries, these communities produced one of the most refined metalworking traditions in the ancient Americas and established relationships with other communities along the Pacific coast, including those in northern Peru.

CuratorialText: This mask, made of hammered sheet gold alloy and covered in red pigment, once adorned the body of a deceased ruler on Peru’s north coast. Powerful dynasties arose in this region between the eighth and the fourteenth centuries CE and amassed great riches in gold and silver before they were conquered by the Inca Empire in the late fifteenth century. The lords of these dynasties were the patrons of vast workshops where finely crafted ornaments and ceremonial vessels were created (see 1991.419.62; 66.196.27). At death, the lords were buried deep in monumental mud-brick platform mounds along with large numbers of objects of precious metal, shell, and cloth. In addition to beakers, disks, and other ornaments, the burials included large masks made of sheet gold. As many as five masks were placed into one burial: one attached to the head of the textile-wrapped body, and the other four stacked at the feet of the deceased. This mask was made of an alloy of 74 percent gold, 20 percent silver, and 6 percent copper which was then hammered into a sheet and shaped into the form of a face. Interestingly, cinnabar, a red mineral pigment, covers much of the cheeks and forehead of this mask, obscuring the gold surface. The cinnabar may emulate the patterning of face paint worn by individuals of importance: according to early colonial accounts a courtier was charged with the task of maintaining such paints. Faint traces of the textile wrappings that once enveloped the mummy bundle can be detected in impressions on the red paint. Masks in museum collections are often missing such surface embellishment (see 1979.206.556), as the pigment would have been removed in modern times to highlight the golden substrate. The eyes of this mask have thin, skewer-like projections emerging from the pupils, perhaps suggesting powerful or even piercing vision. In other masks these projections include small beads of amber and emerald, which have led scholars to interpret the projections as tears. On the ears and ear ornaments can be seen restored examples of the silver-surfaced overlays (now corroded green) that would have also been originally present attached to the mask. Further surface additions include danglers on the U-shaped nose ornament. Such spangles would have caught the light of the bright sun and conveyed a sense of movement and life as a mummy bundle was conveyed to its final resting place. Masks such as these, with their characteristic ovoid eyes terminating in a point, are associated with the Lambayeque culture, named for a region near the modern city of Chiclayo on Peru’s north coast. This polity, also known as Sicán, erected great monumental centers such as Batán Grande, Chornancap, and other sites. Recent excavations at Chornancap by Carlos Wester and his team have revealed that such masks were part of the burial regalia of high-status women as well as men. According to myths on the north coast, gold was particularly associated with male rulers, silver with noble women, and copper with commoners. A mask from Chornancap, found in the tomb of an importance priestess, was made of silver. The pointed ovoid eyes on this mask and others, sometimes referred to as winged eyes, have been identified as defining features of a being known as the Sicán Deity. According to Izumi Shimada and colleagues, an individual interred with such a mask would have been thought to take on aspects of the Sicán Deity’s power, and would have been transformed into a venerated ancestor upon death.
