These figures are unusual because the Seasons are typically shown as women, but here they appear as strong young men.

Around these five central figures are other figures and objects associated with the worship of Dionysos, all carved at a smaller scale. On the rounded ends of the sarcophagus are two more groups of large figures, similarly mixed with smaller ones. On the left end, Mother Earth is shown reclining on the ground, accompanied by a satyr (a half-human, half-goat creature from myth) and a youth carrying fruit. On the right end, a bearded male figure—likely meant to represent a river god—reclines in front of two winged youths, who may stand for two additional Seasons.

This sarcophagus is a superb example of Roman funerary art, displaying the full skill of the workshop where it was carved. The marble comes from a quarry in the eastern Mediterranean and was probably shipped to Rome, where it was worked. Only a very wealthy and powerful person could have commissioned and purchased such a piece, and it was likely made for a member of one of Rome’s old aristocratic families.

The subjects—the triumph of Dionysos and the Seasons—probably did not have any special personal meaning for the deceased. In fact, it is clear that the design was copied from a sculptor’s pattern book. Another sarcophagus, now in the Hessisches Landesmuseum in Kassel, Germany, has the same composition of Dionysos flanked by the four Seasons, though the style and carving of the figures are quite different.

On the Badminton sarcophagus, the figures are carved in high relief, which gives the crowded scene many areas of light and shadow, allowing the eye to move easily from one figure to another. We must also imagine that certain details were highlighted with color and even gold leaf, making the whole composition a visual masterpiece. Very few Roman sarcophagi of this quality have survived. Although the Badminton sarcophagus is missing its lid, the fact that it was discovered in the early eighteenth century and soon after installed at Badminton Hall means it has been preserved almost intact—only a few small extremities are now missing.

CuratorialText:
On the neck, obverse, battle of centaurs and Lapiths; reverse, youths and women
Around the body, Amazonomachy (battle between Greeks and Amazons). The ancient Greeks almost never depicted contemporary or historical events in art. Thus, while literary works of the fifth century B.C. make clear that the Greeks understood the magnitude of their victory in the Persian Wars, there was no concern among artists to illustrate major events or personalities. Instead, their preference was for grand mythological battles between Greeks and eastern adversaries, notably Amazons. The Amazons were a mythical race of warrior women whose homeland lay far to the east and north. The most celebrated Amazonomachies in Athens during the first half of the fifth century were large-scale wall paintings that decorated the Theseion and the Stoa Poikile (the Painted Portico). Their influence was considerable and underlies the representation here and on the adjacent krater, 06.286.86.
persona3_adapted:
On the neck of the vase, one side shows a battle between centaurs (half-human, half-horse creatures) and Lapiths (a mythical Greek tribe); the other side shows young men and women. Around the body of the vase is a scene of an Amazonomachy—a battle between Greeks and Amazons.

The ancient Greeks almost never depicted real, historical events in their art. Even though writings from the fifth century B.C. show that the Greeks understood how important their victory in the Persian Wars was, artists did not try to illustrate major events or famous people from that conflict. Instead, they preferred to show grand, mythical battles between Greeks and eastern enemies, especially the Amazons. The Amazons were a legendary race of warrior women whose homeland was thought to be far to the east and north.

In Athens, during the first half of the fifth century B.C., the most famous Amazonomachy scenes were large wall paintings that decorated two important buildings: the Theseion (a temple dedicated to the hero Theseus) and the Stoa Poikile (the "Painted Portico," a public building with famous murals). These paintings had a major influence on later art, and their style and subject matter can be seen in the scene on this vase, as well as on the nearby krater (a large mixing bowl for wine), numbered 06.286.86.

CuratorialText: Approximately 230 paintings in the collection of the Rijksmuseum are still lifes. As early as the 16th century, Flemish painters specialized in this genre. These are arranged displays featuring, for example, shells, books, food and drink, tableware, skulls, or dead animals, but also flowers, insects, and sometimes a person in the background. Life literally stands still for a moment. Separate specializations soon emerged within the genre. For instance, Rachel Ruysch primarily painted flowers, while Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda enjoyed creating arrangements with food and eating and drinking utensils. Another variant, the vanitas still life, which often depicts a skull, refers to the transience of life and the material world.
persona3_adapted: Approximately 230 paintings in the Rijksmuseum’s collection are still lifes. This type of painting—showing arranged objects like shells, books, food, drink, tableware, skulls, or dead animals, as well as flowers, insects, and sometimes a person in the background—first became a specialty of Flemish painters in the 1500s. In these works, life literally pauses for a moment. Soon, artists began focusing on particular subjects within this category. For example, Rachel Ruysch mainly painted flowers, while Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda specialized in arrangements of food, drinking vessels, and tableware. Another type, the vanitas still life, often includes a skull and is meant to remind viewers of the fleeting nature of life and the material world.

CuratorialText: Orange above! With the coronation of King William I (1772-1843), the Netherlands became a monarchy for the first time in 1815. This first king is the son of the last Stadtholder, William V, who had fled to England years earlier. King William I has canals and roads constructed in the Netherlands and invests in trade, shipping, and industry. However, with his sights firmly set on the economy, William I pays less attention to other affairs of state. Discontent in the Southern Netherlands grows. The population rises in revolt, and in 1831, the secession of Belgium becomes a reality. The king resists, but attempts to regain the territory come to nothing. After years of struggle, William I recognizes independent Belgium in 1839. Disappointed, he abdicates the throne a year later, and his son, William II, takes over.
persona3_adapted: Orange above! In 1815, the Netherlands became a monarchy for the first time when King William I (1772-1843) was crowned. This first king was the son of the last Stadtholder—a historical title for a leader or governor—William V, who had fled to England years earlier. King William I focused on building canals and roads across the Netherlands and invested in trade, shipping, and industry. However, because his attention was so firmly fixed on the economy, he paid less attention to other matters of state. Discontent grew in the Southern Netherlands, and the population rose in revolt. In 1831, Belgium broke away and became an independent country. The king resisted and tried to regain the territory, but his efforts failed. After years of struggle, William I finally recognized an independent Belgium in 1839. Disappointed by this outcome, he gave up the throne a year later, and his son, William II, took over.

CuratorialText: The extensive costume collection of the Rijksmuseum, the oldest in the Netherlands, tells a wealth of stories. From women's and men's to children's clothing: these pieces reveal stories about the wearer and their taste, the methods of the makers, and local and global influences. Many garments are custom-made for a special occasion, while others showcase the technological advancements of ready-to-wear fashion. From the Frisian branch of the Orange dynasty in the 17th century to Yves Saint Laurent: the collection features highlights of Dutch and international fashion. The collection is a remarkable reflection of fashion developments throughout the centuries.
persona3_adapted: The Rijksmuseum’s extensive costume collection—the oldest in the Netherlands—holds a rich variety of stories. It spans women’s, men’s, and children’s clothing, each piece revealing details about the wearer’s personal taste, the skills of the makers, and the local and global influences that shaped it. Some garments were made to order for special events, while others highlight the technological advances that made ready-to-wear clothing possible. From the Frisian branch of the Orange dynasty in the 1600s to the work of Yves Saint Laurent, the collection includes standout examples of both Dutch and international fashion. Together, these pieces offer a remarkable view of how fashion has evolved over the centuries.

CuratorialText: At the end of the 19th century, a new art style emerges. It is a reaction by artists who rebelled against the historicism prevailing at the time, which harked back to the styles of previous centuries. The new art, aptly named Art Nouveau, is characterized by natural forms, such as flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. The emphasis lies on flowing lines, geometric shapes, and asymmetrical compositions. With Paris, Brussels, and Munich as centers, Art Nouveau spread as an international movement in Western Europe and the United States. During this period, which lasted from approximately 1890 until the beginning of the First World War in 1914, Art Nouveau became visible in all fields of the arts.
persona3_adapted: At the end of the 19th century, a new art style emerged. It was a reaction by artists who rejected the dominant trend of their time—an approach called historicism, which simply means looking backward and imitating the styles of earlier centuries. This fresh style, fittingly named Art Nouveau (French for "new art"), drew inspiration from natural forms like flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. Its key features include flowing, curving lines, geometric shapes, and arrangements that are intentionally unbalanced or asymmetrical. Centered in cities such as Paris, Brussels, and Munich, Art Nouveau spread as an international movement across Western Europe and the United States. During this period, lasting from about 1890 until the start of World War I in 1914, Art Nouveau appeared in every branch of the arts—from architecture and furniture to painting and jewelry.

CuratorialText: Hendrick Goltzius (1558-1617) was born in Germany. Trained as a draftsman, engraver, and glass painter, he also developed into a renowned painter. Goltzius followed his teacher Dirck Volkertsz. Coornhert to Haarlem. There, together with Karel van Mander and Cornelis Cornelisz. van Haarlem, he introduced the Mannerism of Bartholomäus Spranger to the Netherlands. The style is recognizable by muscular bodies, twisted poses, and small heads in intricate compositions. During a trip through Italy in 1590-91, Goltzius was inspired by the work of the Italian masters. Back in the Netherlands, he left Mannerism behind and opted for a more classicist style with classical proportions in his work. His technically complex engravings, in particular, were admired throughout Europe. Goltzius's oeuvre provides a fine reflection of the developments in Dutch painting around 1600.
persona3_adapted: Hendrick Goltzius (1558-1617) was born in Germany. Trained as a draftsman, engraver, and glass painter, he also became a well-known painter. Goltzius followed his teacher Dirck Volkertsz. Coornhert to Haarlem. There, together with Karel van Mander and Cornelis Cornelisz. van Haarlem, he introduced a style called Mannerism—brought from Bartholomäus Spranger—to the Netherlands. This style is easy to spot: it features muscular bodies, twisted poses, and small heads, all arranged in complex compositions. During a trip through Italy in 1590-91, Goltzius was inspired by the work of Italian masters. When he returned to the Netherlands, he left Mannerism behind and adopted a more classicist approach, using balanced proportions in his work. His technically intricate engravings, in particular, were admired across Europe. Goltzius's body of work offers a clear reflection of the developments in Dutch painting around 1600.

CuratorialText: Rembrandt van Rijn (1606-1669) decided at a young age that he wanted to become a painter. He first apprenticed with Jacob van Swanenburgh in his hometown of Leiden and subsequently continued his training with Pieter Lastman in Amsterdam. Rembrandt allowed himself to be unconstrained by rules and experimented freely with daring compositions, paint handling, and rendering of textures. He enhanced the drama in his depictions with strong light-dark contrasts. His early works are painted quite precisely; later, his brushstrokes became increasingly loose. He regularly received important commissions, including his most famous and largest painting, The Night Watch. Instead of a static portrait, Rembrandt painted a lively moment in which all the figures are depicted in action. In addition to this masterpiece, the Rijksmuseum holds 24 other paintings, 64 drawings, and approximately 1,300 prints by Rembrandt in its collection.
persona3_adapted: Rembrandt van Rijn (1606-1669) decided at a young age that he wanted to become a painter. He first trained with Jacob van Swanenburgh in his hometown of Leiden, then continued his studies with Pieter Lastman in Amsterdam. Rembrandt refused to be limited by established conventions, freely experimenting with bold arrangements of figures, the way he applied paint to the canvas, and how he captured the look and feel of different surfaces. He heightened the sense of drama in his scenes by using strong contrasts between light and shadow. His early works are painted with careful detail; later, his brushstrokes became much looser and more expressive. He regularly received major commissions, including his most famous and largest painting, *The Night Watch*. Rather than creating a still, formal group portrait, Rembrandt chose to show a lively, dynamic moment in which all the figures are caught in motion. In addition to this masterpiece, the Rijksmuseum holds 24 other paintings, 64 drawings, and about 1,300 prints by Rembrandt in its collection.

CuratorialText: Although Johannes Vermeer (1632-1675) is now world-famous, little is known about him. It is clear, however, that the native of Delft spent his days as an art dealer, father of a large family, and, naturally, a painter. His early work includes a Biblical and a mythological scene. Other early works reveal influences from the Utrecht Caravaggists. Vermeer primarily paints interiors and tronies (portrait-like studies of people). His most famous works are the intimate genre pieces, snapshots of everyday life: a lady reading a letter, a woman engaged in a household chore. All the scenes share a serene calm, which sometimes even feels somewhat mysterious. Vermeer is admired for his exceptional use of light, powerful colors, and incredible precision. During his lifetime, he rose to a certain fame and commanded high prices for his paintings. Afterward, Vermeer fell into obscurity and was not rediscovered until the mid-19th century.
persona3_adapted: Although Johannes Vermeer (1632–1675) is now world-famous, surprisingly little is known about his life. What is clear is that he was born in Delft, worked as an art dealer, raised a large family, and, of course, painted. His earliest works include a scene from the Bible and one from classical mythology. Other early paintings show the influence of a group of artists known as the Utrecht Caravaggists—followers of the Italian painter Caravaggio, who used dramatic contrasts of light and shadow. Vermeer mostly painted indoor scenes and what are called *tronies*: studies of people that look like portraits but are not meant to depict specific individuals. His most famous works are intimate scenes of everyday life—a woman reading a letter, another busy with a household task. These are often described as "genre" paintings, meaning they show ordinary people in domestic settings. All of these scenes share a quiet calm, which can sometimes feel a bit mysterious. Vermeer is especially admired for his masterful handling of light, his rich use of color, and his extraordinary attention to detail. During his lifetime, he achieved a certain level of fame and could charge high prices for his paintings. After his death, however, his work was largely forgotten, and it was not until the mid-19th century that he was rediscovered and celebrated once again.

CuratorialText: Rachel Ruysch (1664-1750) is one of the most successful artists of her time. Her flower still lifes are very detailed. Roses, poppies, carnations, and irises: all almost indistinguishable from the real thing. Her work bears witness to a vast knowledge of nature. Ruysch acquires this knowledge at the Amsterdam Botanical Garden, where her father, Frederik Ruysch, teaches as a professor. She also studies his collection of prepared flowers and insects. She learns the tricks of the trade from still-life painter Willem van Aelst. In 1701, Ruysch becomes the first woman to join the Hague artists' society Pictura. From 1708 to 1716, she works as court painter for the German Elector John William of the Palatinate, a particularly honorable position. He is by no means the only admirer of her work. Ruysch's flower still lifes are collected by enthusiasts far beyond the national borders.
persona3_adapted: Rachel Ruysch (1664–1750) was one of the most accomplished artists of her era. Her paintings of flower arrangements are extraordinarily detailed. Roses, poppies, carnations, and irises—each bloom looks almost exactly like the real thing. Her work reveals a deep understanding of the natural world. Ruysch gained this knowledge at the Amsterdam Botanical Garden, where her father, Frederik Ruysch, was a professor. She also studied his collection of preserved flowers and insects. She learned the techniques of still-life painting from the artist Willem van Aelst. In 1701, Ruysch became the first woman to join Pictura, an artists' society based in The Hague. From 1708 to 1716, she worked as a court painter for the German Elector John William of the Palatinate—a particularly prestigious role. He was far from the only admirer of her work. Collectors across Europe eagerly acquired Ruysch's flower still lifes, and her reputation extended well beyond her home country.

CuratorialText: Thérèse Schwartze (1851-1918) was born into the artistic family of painter Johann Georg Schwartze and grew into a talented portrait painter, draftsman, and printmaker. Trained in her father's studio and after art education in Munich and Paris, she develops into a true talent. When her father dies prematurely, the 23-year-old Schwartze becomes the new breadwinner of the family. Her style is powerful, with rapid brushstrokes and large areas of color, which make her portraits full of character yet flattering. Schwartze's work is well received, and she becomes increasingly famous both at home and abroad. Around the year 1900, Schwartze is the most successful portraitist in the Netherlands, and she portrays members of the Royal Family. She works quickly and creates hundreds of portraits in pastel and oil paint during her career, of which the Rijksmuseum holds a number of artist portraits in its collection.
persona3_adapted: Thérèse Schwartze (1851-1918) was born into an artistic family—her father, Johann Georg Schwartze, was a painter—and she grew up to become a highly skilled portraitist, as well as a draftsman and printmaker. After training in her father’s studio and studying art in Munich and Paris, she developed into a true talent. When her father died unexpectedly, the 23-year-old Schwartze became the family’s main earner. Her painting style is bold and energetic, marked by quick brushstrokes and broad areas of color. This approach gives her portraits a strong sense of personality while still being flattering to her subjects. Schwartze’s work was well received, and she grew increasingly famous both in the Netherlands and abroad. By around 1900, she was the most successful portrait painter in the country, and she painted members of the Royal Family. She worked quickly, producing hundreds of portraits in pastel and oil paint over the course of her career. The Rijksmuseum now holds a number of her portraits of artists in its collection.

CuratorialText: Anyone walking through Amsterdam towards the end of the 19th century has a good chance of encountering George Hendrik Breitner (1857-1923). For days on end, the painter roams through his beloved Amsterdam with a sketchbook. He calls himself 'the painter of the people' and primarily draws and paints 'ordinary' city dwellers. Breitner also uses his camera to capture life. He subsequently applies his typical photographic style, in which figures partially fall outside the frame, to his paintings as well. This style, combined with thick brushstrokes and smudges, is not understood by everyone. Journalists are critical of his work, but enthusiasts love it. To this day, Breitner remains one of the most famous Impressionists of his time.
persona3_adapted:
If you were walking through Amsterdam in the late 1800s, you would have had a good chance of running into George Hendrik Breitner (1857–1923). Day after day, the artist wandered through his beloved city with a sketchbook in hand. He called himself "the painter of the people" and focused on drawing and painting everyday city dwellers—ordinary people going about their lives.

Breitner also used a camera to capture scenes of city life. He then brought a photographer’s way of seeing into his paintings, often framing his subjects so that parts of them were cut off by the edge of the picture. This style, combined with thick brushstrokes and smudges of paint, wasn’t always well received. Journalists criticized his work, but others loved it. To this day, Breitner remains one of the most famous Impressionists—artists who aimed to capture fleeting moments and the effects of light—of his time.

CuratorialText: He drew extensively and received his first painting lessons from Anton Mauve, a cousin by marriage and a renowned painter. In his early years, Van Gogh primarily drew and painted many peasant scenes in dark colors. After a brief period of study in Antwerp, Van Gogh left for Paris, where he was influenced by Impressionism and Pointillism. He developed a style characterized by short brushstrokes and bright, unmixed colors applied side by side. In 1888, he moved to sunny Southern France. Van Gogh struggled with mental health issues, for which he was hospitalized. However, this did not prevent him from continuing to paint. It was only after his death, in 1890, that his work reached the general public and Van Gogh became world-famous.
persona3_adapted: He drew frequently and received his first painting lessons from Anton Mauve, a relative by marriage and a well-known artist. In his early years, Van Gogh mostly drew and painted scenes of peasant life, using dark colors. After a short period of study in Antwerp, he moved to Paris, where he was influenced by two major art movements: Impressionism, which focused on capturing the effects of light and everyday scenes, and Pointillism, a technique that uses small dots of pure color placed next to each other to create an image. He developed a style marked by short brushstrokes and bright, unmixed colors applied side by side. In 1888, he moved to sunny southern France. Van Gogh struggled with mental health issues and was hospitalized at times, but this did not stop him from continuing to paint. It was only after his death in 1890 that his work reached the general public and he became world-famous.

CuratorialText: Born into a Utrecht family of silversmiths, the career path of Adam van Vianen (1568/69-1627) was determined early on. Around 1590, Van Vianen must have completed his master's examination, as he worked independently from that moment on and joined the blacksmiths' guild. His imaginative objects tell a story through lobe-like ornaments and forms. In 1614, Van Vianen received a commission from the Amsterdam guild to create a jug in memory of his own brother Paulus, who was also a silversmith and had passed away shortly before. The jug became a tribute to his brother's work and creativity. In this jug, the ornament plays the leading role for the first time.
persona3_adapted: Born into a family of silversmiths in Utrecht, Adam van Vianen (1568/69–1627) had his future career largely set from an early age. Around 1590, he likely passed his master’s examination, which allowed him to work independently and join the local blacksmiths’ guild. The objects he created are highly imaginative, telling a story through their curved, lobe-like shapes and decorative forms. In 1614, the Amsterdam guild commissioned Van Vianen to make a jug in memory of his own brother Paulus, also a silversmith, who had died shortly before. The jug became a tribute to his brother’s skill and artistic vision. In this piece, the decoration itself takes center stage for the first time, rather than simply serving as an addition to the object’s function.

Goltzius followed his teacher Dirck Volkertsz. Coornhert to Haarlem. There, together with Karel van Mander and Cornelis Cornelisz. van Haarlem, he introduced a style called Mannerism—brought from Bartholomäus Spranger—to the Netherlands. CuratorialText: Visual artist and photographer Viviane Sassen (1972) visited the village of Pikin Slee, deep in the rainforest of Suriname, in 2012. Here, people live without running water, electricity, roads, or the internet. But however remote the village may be, the influence of the modern world is clearly noticeable. For instance, this community also possesses mobile phones and plastic household items. The photo series that Sassen created in Pikin Slee captures the beauty of everyday life in abstract compositions. Largely in black and white, but with the colorful splashes characteristic of Sassen's earlier work. In 2013, the Pikin Slee series was added to the collection of the Rijksmuseum as a modern addition to the collection of photographs of Suriname, which until then had been primarily historical in character.
persona3_adapted: Visual artist and photographer Viviane Sassen (born 1972) visited the village of Pikin Slee, deep in the rainforest of Suriname, in 2012. The community lives without running water, electricity, roads, or the internet. Yet despite its remoteness, the influence of the modern world is clearly visible—residents, for instance, own mobile phones and plastic household items. The photo series Sassen created in Pikin Slee captures the beauty of everyday life through compositions that are not realistic but instead emphasize shape, pattern, and form. Most of the images are in black and white, but they include the bursts of color that have become a signature of Sassen's earlier work. In 2013, the Pikin Slee series entered the Rijksmuseum's collection, adding a modern perspective to its photographs of Suriname, which had previously been mostly historical in nature.

CuratorialText: William of Orange (1533–1584) was an ambitious nobleman who rose to become the leader of the Revolt and was revered as the 'father of the fatherland'. At the age of 11, William inherited the French principality of Orange and received the title 'Prince of Orange'. He led a turbulent life with an impressive career. Philip II, the son of Charles V, appointed William as Stadtholder of Holland, Zeeland, and Utrecht, the most important Dutch provinces. The two men clashed. Philip strived for an empire with central authority and one religion: Roman Catholicism. William, on the other hand, stood for freedom of religion and conscience for everyone. This struggle cost him his life on July 10, 1584, from a gunshot.
persona3_adapted: William of Orange (1533–1584) was an ambitious nobleman who rose to become the leader of the Dutch Revolt and was honored as the 'father of the fatherland.' At age 11, he inherited the French principality of Orange, earning the title 'Prince of Orange.' His life was turbulent, marked by an impressive career. Philip II, the son of Charles V, appointed William as Stadtholder—a kind of governor—of Holland, Zeeland, and Utrecht, the most important Dutch provinces. The two men clashed. Philip sought to build an empire with centralized authority and a single religion: Roman Catholicism. William, in contrast, championed freedom of religion and conscience for all. This struggle ultimately cost him his life on July 10, 1584, when he was shot.

CuratorialText: Who is that girl in the painting by Simon Maris, anyway? Little is known about her. However, it is clear that around the year 1906, she modeled for Simon Maris in his studio at Keizersgracht 498. She was about 12 years old at the time. This is evident from photographs Maris took of the young lady in his studio. Documents have been preserved in Simon Maris's archive in which he refers to her as 'Isabella'. Some other paintings Maris made of the girl were also given this name. Nevertheless, it is not certain that this young lady was really named 'Isabella'. It is possible that Maris invented this name for her. Or perhaps she used a pseudonym herself, as models often did at that time.
persona3_adapted: Who is that girl in the painting by Simon Maris, anyway? We know very little about her. What we do know is that around 1906, she posed for Maris in his studio at Keizersgracht 498. At the time, she was about 12 years old. We know this from photographs Maris took of her in that same studio. In Maris’s personal archive, there are documents where he refers to her as “Isabella.” Several other paintings he made of her also carry this name. Still, it’s not certain that “Isabella” was her real name. Maris might have invented it. Or perhaps she used a false name herself—a common practice among models in that era.

CuratorialText: Amalia of Solms (1602-1675) played a crucial role in increasing the prestige of the House of Orange and the international positioning of the Netherlands. She serves as a sounding board and advisor to her husband, Stadtholder Frederik Hendrik. When he falls ill, she assumes some of his duties and charts a course that heralds the end of the Eighty Years' War. Three years after her husband's death, her son passes away, marking the beginning of the first stadtholderless era. To safeguard the future of the House of Orange, Van Solms exerts national and international influence. She uses art as a political tool. Her collection of paintings, furniture, and Asian objects competes with other European courts. In doing so, she firmly establishes the Netherlands, with the House of Orange at its head, as a European center of art and trade.
persona3_adapted: Amalia of Solms (1602–1675) was a key figure in raising the status of the House of Orange and strengthening the Netherlands’ position on the world stage. She acted as a trusted advisor and sounding board for her husband, Stadtholder Frederik Hendrik—a title roughly equivalent to a military and political leader of the Dutch Republic. When he fell ill, she took on some of his responsibilities and helped steer a course that led to the end of the Eighty Years' War. Three years after her husband’s death, her son also died, ushering in a period known as the first stadtholderless era, when no single leader held that role. To protect the future of the House of Orange, Amalia used her influence both within the Netherlands and abroad. She deliberately employed art as a political tool. Her collection of paintings, furniture, and objects from Asia was designed to rival those of other European royal courts. In this way, she helped firmly establish the Netherlands—with the House of Orange at its center—as a major European hub for both art and trade.

CuratorialText: Between 1650 and 1672, there was no Stadtholder. The most powerful citizens governed the country. As Grand Pensionary of Holland, Johan de Witt rose to become the most influential politician. He cunningly plays England and France, the Republic's enemies, against each other. Nevertheless, these countries, together with the prince-bishoprics of Münster and Cologne, attack the Netherlands in 1672. His brother, Cornelis de Witt, oversees the military activities as a deputy of the States General. As the foreign troops gain ground, the call for the return of the Stadtholder grows ever louder. Cornelis is arrested on suspicion of conspiracy against the Prince of Orange. When Johan visits him in prison, an incited mob drags the brothers out and kills them. Long after their deaths, both are remembered as champions of 'true freedom'.
persona3_adapted: Between 1650 and 1672, the Netherlands had no Stadtholder—a position similar to a governor or head of state. Instead, the country was run by its most powerful citizens. During this period, Johan de Witt, serving as Grand Pensionary (the highest political office in the province of Holland), became the nation's most influential leader. He skillfully played the Republic's enemies, England and France, against each other. Despite his efforts, in 1672, these countries, along with the prince-bishoprics of Münster and Cologne, attacked the Netherlands. Johan's brother, Cornelis de Witt, oversaw military operations as a representative of the States General, the Dutch governing body. As foreign forces advanced, public pressure grew for the return of the Stadtholder. Cornelis was arrested on suspicion of plotting against the Prince of Orange. When Johan visited him in prison, an angry mob dragged both brothers out and killed them. Long after their deaths, they are remembered as defenders of what was called "true freedom"—a vision of government without a single powerful ruler.

CuratorialText: At the beginning of the 19th century, Hortense de Beauharnais (1783-1837) and Louis Napoleon became the first Queen and King of Holland, appointed by Emperor Napoleon Bonaparte. The royal couple had three sons together, including the future Emperor Napoleon III, but it was an unhappy marriage. Queen Hortense was often present at festivities and official occasions and, partly due to her visits to local businesses, became increasingly popular with the people. That was until her husband, displeased with her popularity, forced her into the background. After Napoleon's defeat, she settled in Switzerland without her husband. At her impressive Arenenberg estate, De Beauharnais, herself a talented musician and composer, organized cultural gatherings that were talked about for a long time afterwards.
persona3_adapted: At the start of the 19th century, Hortense de Beauharnais (1783-1837) and Louis Napoleon became the first Queen and King of Holland, a title granted by Emperor Napoleon Bonaparte. The royal couple had three sons together—one of whom would later become Emperor Napoleon III—but their marriage was an unhappy one. Queen Hortense frequently appeared at celebrations and official events, and in part because she visited local businesses, she grew increasingly popular among the people. That changed when her husband, displeased by her popularity, pushed her out of the public eye. After Napoleon’s defeat, she settled in Switzerland without her husband. At her impressive estate in Arenenberg, Hortense—herself a talented musician and composer—hosted cultural gatherings that people talked about for a long time afterward.

CuratorialText: On July 13, 1885, the new Rijksmuseum officially opened its doors after a construction period of nearly ten years. The building was designed by the architect Pierre Cuypers (1827–1921), a prominent member of the Catholic Church. On the exterior of the Rijksmuseum, Cuypers depicted himself, just as the master builders did in the decoration of cathedrals during the Middle Ages. Over the years, many original decorations in the museum have disappeared. During the last renovation, however, Cuypers' work was restored to its former glory. The Rijksmuseum was not Cuypers' only showpiece. In 1889, 'his' Central Station in Amsterdam was unveiled. In addition to these famous projects, Cuypers focused primarily on churches and monuments.
persona3_adapted: On July 13, 1885, the new Rijksmuseum officially opened its doors after nearly ten years of construction. The building was designed by the architect Pierre Cuypers (1827–1921), a prominent member of the Catholic Church. On the museum’s exterior, Cuypers included a depiction of himself, following a tradition used by master builders in the Middle Ages, who often placed their own images in the decorations of cathedrals. Over time, many of the museum’s original decorative features were lost. However, during the most recent renovation, Cuypers’ original work was carefully restored to its former splendor. The Rijksmuseum was not Cuypers’ only major achievement. In 1889, his design for Amsterdam’s Central Station was unveiled. Beyond these famous projects, Cuypers focused mainly on churches and other monuments.

CuratorialText: Willem Drees (1886-1988) made a career in The Hague as a member of the Social Democratic Workers' Party, which would later become the PvdA. The politician climbed a long ladder of various positions: municipal councilor, alderman, Member of Parliament. In the first cabinet elected after the war, Drees was appointed Minister of Social Affairs. With his Drees Emergency Act, a provision for fixed benefits, he helped the elderly out of financial distress. Drees was popular and became Prime Minister in 1948. It was his task to effectively handle the reconstruction of the Netherlands. Drees proved to be the right man in the right place. With a sense of solidarity, the Netherlands was rebuilt. Under Drees's leadership, until 1958, the foundation of the modern Dutch welfare state was laid. To this day, the statesman is appreciated for his efforts for the Netherlands.
persona3_adapted: Willem Drees (1886-1988) built his political career in The Hague as a member of the Social Democratic Workers' Party, which later became the PvdA (the Dutch Labour Party). He rose through a series of roles: first as a municipal councilor, then as an alderman, and later as a member of Parliament. In the first government elected after World War II, Drees was appointed Minister of Social Affairs. There, he introduced the Drees Emergency Act, a program that provided fixed financial benefits to help elderly people in need. Drees was widely popular and became Prime Minister in 1948. His main task was to oversee the reconstruction of the Netherlands after the war. He proved to be exactly the right person for the job. Through a spirit of collective responsibility, the country was rebuilt. Under Drees's leadership, which lasted until 1958, the foundations of the modern Dutch welfare state were established. To this day, he is remembered and respected for his contributions to the Netherlands.

CuratorialText: At the end of the 16th century, there was a preference in Italy for Mannerism, a style full of complex compositions and figures in intricate, twisted poses. Around 1600, the Italian Baroque artist Michelangelo Merisi da Caravaggio broke radically with this art form. His figures are actually very realistic. Wrinkled faces, unkempt hair, dirty feet: gods and saints are depicted as very ordinary people. Caravaggio has a preference for strong light-dark contrasts, dramatic effects, and compositions with a clear structure. His method spreads rapidly throughout Europe. Especially in Utrecht, around 1620, many followers, known as Caravaggists, can be found. Artists such as Gerard van Honthorst, Hendrick ter Brugghen, and Dirck van Baburen travel to Rome to study Caravaggio's style. And although he never visited Italy, Rembrandt was also inspired by Caravaggio's lighting effects.
persona3_adapted: In the late 1500s, Italian artists favored a style called Mannerism, which featured elaborate compositions and figures in complex, twisting poses. Around 1600, the Italian painter Michelangelo Merisi da Caravaggio broke sharply from this tradition. His figures are strikingly realistic: gods and saints appear with wrinkled faces, messy hair, and dirty feet—just like ordinary people. Caravaggio was drawn to strong contrasts between light and shadow, dramatic effects, and clear, well-organized compositions. His approach quickly spread across Europe. In particular, around 1620, many artists in Utrecht—known as Caravaggists—adopted his style. Painters like Gerard van Honthorst, Hendrick ter Brugghen, and Dirck van Baburen traveled to Rome to study Caravaggio’s methods. And although Rembrandt never visited Italy, he too was influenced by Caravaggio’s dramatic use of light.

CuratorialText: The 15th and 16th centuries are broadly regarded as the period of the Renaissance, a term that literally means 'rebirth'. In Europe, the arts, literature, and sciences flourish due to a new appreciation of the classical antiquity of the Romans and Greeks. Artists and architects seek ideal proportions. They study Roman architecture and adopt formal languages ​​such as round arches, columns, and pediments. The visual arts increasingly focus on a realistic representation of nature and man, using perspective and anatomical studies. Alongside religious themes, secular themes and mythological representations become popular. The Renaissance spreads from Italy throughout Europe, thanks to the rise of printmaking and printing in the mid-15th century.
persona3_adapted: The 15th and 16th centuries are widely known as the Renaissance, a word that literally means "rebirth." During this period, the arts, literature, and sciences thrived across Europe, driven by a renewed interest in the classical cultures of ancient Greece and Rome. Artists and architects sought to create works based on ideal proportions, studying Roman buildings and adopting their distinctive features, such as rounded arches, columns, and triangular roof structures called pediments. In painting and sculpture, there was a growing emphasis on realistic depictions of nature and the human body, achieved through techniques like perspective—which creates the illusion of depth—and careful study of human anatomy. While religious subjects remained important, non-religious themes and stories from classical mythology also became popular. The Renaissance began in Italy and spread throughout Europe, aided by the rise of printmaking and the printing press in the mid-1400s.

CuratorialText: The period between the First and Second World Wars is dominated by Art Deco, an international art movement in which tradition and industrial progress go hand in hand. Emerging from Art Nouveau, Art Deco also draws inspiration from stylized plant and floral motifs as well as linear, geometric designs. Additionally, artists look to folk art and art forms from Africa, East Asia, and Central America, a source of inspiration that is further fueled by archaeological finds. The term Art Deco is derived from the 1925 World's Fair in Paris, the Exposition Internationale des Arts Décoratifs et Industriels Modernes.
persona3_adapted: The period between the First and Second World Wars is defined by Art Deco, a global design movement that blends tradition with industrial progress. Building on the earlier Art Nouveau style, Art Deco takes inspiration from both stylized plant and flower patterns and clean, geometric shapes. Artists also look to folk traditions and art forms from Africa, East Asia, and Central America—a source of inspiration further fueled by new archaeological discoveries. The name "Art Deco" comes from the 1925 World's Fair in Paris, officially called the *Exposition Internationale des Arts Décoratifs et Industriels Modernes*.

CuratorialText: From around 1150, increasingly tall churches rose in and around Paris, with Notre-Dame as the most famous example. It is seen as the beginning of Gothic architecture, a style that spread rapidly throughout the rest of Europe and would continue until shortly after 1500. Church walls became airier thanks to tall pointed arch windows fitted with colorful stained glass; the vaults rested on slender columns and were strikingly ribbed, and on the exterior, the masonry was supported by slender flying buttresses with pinnacles. Under the influence of the Gothic style, sculptors also gave their human, animal, and plant figures a stylized appearance with elegant poses, calligraphic drapery, and graceful gestures. Meanwhile, the forms of Gothic architecture also seeped into other art forms, for example, painting, illuminated manuscripts, metalwork, and furniture. The Rijksmuseum possesses numerous ecclesiastical objects in this style, such as painted altars, silver monstrances, wooden prayer beads, and a small nativity scene.
persona3_adapted: From around 1150, increasingly tall churches rose in and around Paris, with Notre-Dame as the most famous example. This is seen as the beginning of Gothic architecture, a style that spread rapidly throughout the rest of Europe and continued until shortly after 1500. Church walls became lighter and more open, thanks to tall, pointed-arch windows fitted with colorful stained glass. The ceilings rested on slender columns and were strikingly ribbed—meaning the stonework formed visible, arching lines across the vault. On the outside, the masonry was supported by slender flying buttresses (external stone arches) topped with pointed pinnacles. Under the influence of the Gothic style, sculptors also gave their human, animal, and plant figures a stylized appearance, with elegant poses, flowing, decorative drapery, and graceful gestures. Meanwhile, the forms of Gothic architecture also spread into other art forms, such as painting, illuminated manuscripts (hand-decorated books), metalwork, and furniture. The Rijksmuseum possesses many religious objects in this style, including painted altars, silver monstrances (holders for displaying the consecrated host), wooden prayer beads, and a small nativity scene.

CuratorialText: The term Impressionism, which means snapshot, was initially used in the early 1870s as a negative label for the work of a group of Parisian painters. Claude Monet, Edgar Degas, and Berthe Morisot depict reality as seen through the eyes of the artist. The French Impressionists are inspired by the realistic style of the French Barbizon School and the work of the slightly older Dutch artist Johan Barthold Jongkind. They work with bright colors and short, loose brushstrokes. They choose their themes from modern life. Due to the sketchy approach, it sometimes appears as if their paintings were created quickly and spontaneously. This often leads critics to say that the artworks are unfinished. From the late 1880s onwards, appreciation for French Impressionism also grows in the Netherlands.
persona3_adapted: The term *Impressionism*—which literally means "snapshot"—was first used in the early 1870s as a dismissive label for the work of a group of Parisian painters. Artists like Claude Monet, Edgar Degas, and Berthe Morisot aimed to capture reality as it appeared through the artist's own eyes. These French Impressionists drew inspiration from the realistic style of the earlier French Barbizon School (a group of painters who focused on natural landscapes) and from the work of the slightly older Dutch artist Johan Barthold Jongkind. They used bright colors and applied paint in short, loose strokes, which gave their paintings a sense of movement and immediacy. Their subjects were drawn from modern, everyday life. Because their technique often looked sketchy or unfinished, the paintings could appear as if they had been made quickly and spontaneously. This led many critics at the time to claim the works were incomplete. From the late 1880s onward, however, French Impressionism also began to gain appreciation in the Netherlands.

CuratorialText: At the end of the 19th century, a new art style emerges. It is a reaction by artists who rebelled against the historicism prevailing at the time, which harked back to the styles of previous centuries. The new art, aptly named Art Nouveau, is characterized by natural forms, such as flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. The emphasis lies on flowing lines, geometric shapes, and asymmetrical compositions. With Paris, Brussels, and Munich as centers, Art Nouveau spread as an international movement in Western Europe and the United States. During this period, which lasted from approximately 1890 until the beginning of the First World War in 1914, Art Nouveau became visible in all fields of the arts.
persona3_adapted: At the end of the 19th century, a new artistic style emerged. It was a reaction by artists who rejected the dominant trend of their time—an approach called historicism, which simply means looking backward and imitating the styles of earlier centuries. This fresh style, fittingly named Art Nouveau (French for "new art"), drew inspiration from natural forms like flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. Its key features included flowing, curving lines, geometric shapes, and compositions that were deliberately unbalanced or asymmetrical. With major creative hubs in Paris, Brussels, and Munich, Art Nouveau spread as an international movement across Western Europe and the United States. During this period, which lasted from roughly 1890 until the outbreak of World War I in 1914, Art Nouveau made its mark in every field of the arts—from architecture and furniture to painting, jewelry, and graphic design.

CuratorialText: Rembrandt is not only a successful painter, but also exceptionally talented as a draftsman and etcher. The Rijksmuseum collection contains approximately 1,300 prints by his hand, including sometimes multiple copies and different states of a single print. The subjects of his etchings range from biblical and mythological scenes to everyday scenes, landscapes, and (self-)portraits. Rembrandt's development in technique, style, and artistic approach can be clearly seen in these works. His loose drawing style and the high-contrast rendering of light and shadow are unique. The choice to depict the most emotional moments is also remarkable for his time. It makes his work human and relatable. Rembrandt has thus had a major influence on artists of his own time and for a long time thereafter.
persona3_adapted: Rembrandt was not only a highly successful painter, but also an exceptionally skilled draftsman and printmaker. The Rijksmuseum holds around 1,300 prints made by his hand, including multiple copies and different versions—or "states"—of the same print. His subjects range from biblical and mythological stories to everyday scenes, landscapes, and portraits (including self-portraits). These works clearly show how Rembrandt’s technique, style, and artistic approach evolved over time. His loose, sketch-like drawing style and the dramatic contrast between light and dark are unique. He also made the striking choice to focus on the most emotionally charged moments, which was unusual for his era. This makes his work feel deeply human and relatable. As a result, Rembrandt had a major influence on artists of his own time and for many years afterward.

CuratorialText: Under the patronage of Augustus the Strong, Elector of Saxony, alchemist Johann Friedrich Böttger succeeded in becoming the first in Europe to produce porcelain in 1708. Not long afterwards, the first European porcelain factory was established in Meissen. The factory played an important role in the dream of August the Strong: a Japanese Palace, in which he wanted to exhibit his collection of Asian and Meissen porcelain. Since the end of the 16th century, porcelain, also known as 'white gold', had been imported from Asia and collected by the wealthy members of European courts and the elite. The fact that porcelain was now also being produced in Europe is one of the most important moments in the history of the European applied arts. The Rijksmuseum holds one of the most important collections of Meissen porcelain, including pieces from the factory's early production from the collection of Margarethe and Franz Oppenheimer.
persona3_adapted: Under the patronage of Augustus the Strong, the Elector of Saxony, the alchemist Johann Friedrich Böttger became the first person in Europe to successfully produce porcelain in 1708. Shortly after, the first European porcelain factory was established in Meissen. This factory played a key role in Augustus the Strong’s ambitious vision: a Japanese Palace where he planned to display his collection of both Asian and Meissen porcelain. Since the late 1500s, porcelain—often called 'white gold'—had been imported from Asia and collected by wealthy European court members and the elite. The fact that porcelain could now also be made in Europe marks a turning point in the history of European decorative arts. The Rijksmuseum holds one of the most important collections of Meissen porcelain, including early pieces from the factory, originally part of Margarethe and Franz Oppenheimer’s collection.

CuratorialText: The Rijksmuseum has been collecting Asian ceramics since its founding in 1885. The largest part of this is Chinese (approx. 2,300 pieces) and Japanese (approx. 600), plus another 50 pieces from other Asian countries. After all, the Dutch have been importing porcelain from Asia since the founding of the Dutch East India Company (VOC, 1602). From the 17th century onwards, thin-fired porcelain with Chinese blue decorations became immensely popular. Consequently, Chinese and Japanese porcelain play an important role in Dutch interiors. Since 1952, the collection of the Royal Society of Friends of Asian Art (KVVAK) has also been housed in the Rijksmuseum, comprising pieces that are part of art production in Asia: tea ceramics from Japan, Song bowls and Tang funerary sculptures from China, and ceramics from Southeast Asia and Korea.
persona3_adapted: The Rijksmuseum has been collecting Asian ceramics since it opened in 1885. The largest part of this collection consists of Chinese pieces (about 2,300) and Japanese pieces (about 600), along with another 50 from other Asian countries. This makes sense historically: the Dutch have been importing porcelain from Asia ever since the Dutch East India Company (VOC) was founded in 1602. Starting in the 17th century, thin, finely fired porcelain decorated with blue Chinese patterns became extremely popular. As a result, Chinese and Japanese porcelain came to play a significant role in Dutch home interiors. Since 1952, the museum has also housed the collection of the Royal Society of Friends of Asian Art (KVVAK). This collection includes objects that represent different traditions of Asian ceramic art: tea ceramics from Japan, bowls from the Song dynasty and sculptures made for tombs from the Tang dynasty in China, as well as ceramics from Southeast Asia and Korea.

CuratorialText: From 1610 onwards, around one hundred thousand pieces of Chinese porcelain were imported into the Netherlands annually. To withstand the Chinese competition, Dutch potters began to refine their products. The earthenware becomes thinner, whiter, and shinier. Blue paintings are applied to a base of white tin glaze, a color scheme strongly associated with Asian porcelain. This earthenware is called 'plateel' or 'faience', but in the 17th and 18th centuries it acquires the name 'Hollants porceleyn'. From 1650 onwards, Delft becomes the center of earthenware production, hence the eventual name 'Delft earthenware'. During the same period, trade in Chinese porcelain comes to a virtual standstill due to the civil war in China (1644-1684). This gives Delft earthenware the opportunity to flourish and gain popularity among the European elite.
persona3_adapted: Between 1610 and the mid-1600s, the Netherlands imported roughly one hundred thousand pieces of Chinese porcelain every year. To compete with this influx, Dutch potters began improving their own ceramics. Their earthenware became thinner, whiter, and shinier. They painted blue designs onto a white, glass-like coating made from tin—a color scheme that strongly echoed the look of Asian porcelain. This type of pottery was known as *plateel* or *faience*, but by the 1600s and 1700s, people started calling it "Hollants porceleyn" (Dutch porcelain). After 1650, the city of Delft became the main center for this kind of earthenware, which is why it eventually came to be called "Delftware." Around the same time, trade in Chinese porcelain nearly stopped because of a civil war in China (1644–1684). This pause gave Delftware a chance to thrive and become highly popular among Europe’s wealthy and powerful classes.

CuratorialText: The bronze collection of the Rijksmuseum contains a collection of small sculptures from the Renaissance, primarily from Italy. A number of these originated in the late 15th century in the university city of Padua. These are small bronze utilitarian objects inspired by classical antiquity: candlesticks, perfume burners, oil lamps, and inkwells, all decorated with mythological figures. Many small workshops cast such luxury utensils in series, but unique sculptures and objects were also made in bronze. A good example is the large candlestick that Verrocchio designed in 1469 for Palazzo Vecchio, the city hall of Florence. Bronze casting in the style of classical antiquity also flourished in other Italian cities, such as Rome and Venice. In the 16th and 17th centuries, these figurines found their way into the studies of humanist scholars, art cabinets of wealthy citizens, and Kunstkammern of princes and nobility.
persona3_adapted: The Rijksmuseum’s collection of bronze works includes small sculptures from the Renaissance, most of them from Italy. Some of these objects were created in the late 1400s in the university city of Padua. They are small, everyday items made of bronze, inspired by the art and design of ancient Greece and Rome: candlesticks, perfume burners, oil lamps, and inkwells, all decorated with figures from mythology. Many small workshops produced these luxury items in sets, but unique sculptures and objects were also made in bronze. A notable example is the large candlestick designed in 1469 by Verrocchio for the Palazzo Vecchio, Florence’s city hall. The technique of casting bronze in the style of ancient Greece and Rome also flourished in other Italian cities, such as Rome and Venice. During the 1500s and 1600s, these small figures found their way into the private studies of humanist scholars, the art collections of wealthy citizens, and the curiosity cabinets of princes and nobility.

CuratorialText: Originally, artists in seascapes primarily captured historical events at sea, so that posterity could relive the heroic deeds. 'War correspondent' and artist Willem van de Velde I created eyewitness paintings of famous Dutch naval battles. His son Willem was a master at capturing calm or, conversely, stormy seas. In the 18th and 19th centuries, Ludolf Bakhuyzen and Wijnand Nuijen opted for a dramatized depiction of ships in distress. Bakhuyzen even went out to sea to study weather effects up close. Thanks to painters such as Andreas Schelfhout, Louis Meijer, and father and son Schotel, Dutch art abroad became almost synonymous with the seascape. For the artists of the Hague School, the effect of light and cloudy skies played a major role, and they paid more attention to the harsh life of fishermen.
persona3_adapted: Originally, artists who painted the sea focused mainly on recording important naval battles, so that future generations could experience those heroic moments. Willem van de Velde I, who worked like a modern war correspondent, created firsthand paintings of famous Dutch naval conflicts. His son, Willem van de Velde II, was especially skilled at depicting either perfectly calm waters or, on the other hand, violent storms. In the 1700s and 1800s, artists like Ludolf Bakhuyzen and Wijnand Nuijen chose to portray ships in danger in a more dramatic, emotionally charged way. Bakhuyzen even went out onto the sea to study weather effects up close. Thanks to painters such as Andreas Schelfhout, Louis Meijer, and the father-and-son team Schotel, Dutch art abroad became almost synonymous with seascapes. For the artists of the Hague School—a group known for their realistic, atmospheric style—the way light played across the sky and the shifting cloud patterns were central concerns. They also paid more attention to the harsh, difficult lives of fishermen.

CuratorialText: In the 17th century, the marksmen of the civic guard were regularly immortalized in group portraits. At that time, the powerful cities themselves were responsible for security. The militia ensured the maintenance of order and security. Every adult man up to the age of 60 is expected to join, although he must be able to purchase his own weaponry. The group portraits are a costly undertaking, as each marksman pays for his own portrait. The earliest marksman portraits date from the 16th century and are rather static. In the course of the 17th century, painters opted for a somewhat looser composition, with Rembrandt's Night Watch and the lively Banquet of the Marksmen by Bartholomeus van der Helst as highlights. Not only marksmen's guilds commission group portraits; other urban, provincial, and national organizations and individuals also commission them.
persona3_adapted: In the 17th century, members of the civic guard—a kind of local militia—were regularly commemorated in group portraits. At that time, powerful cities were responsible for their own security, and the militia helped maintain order and safety. Every adult man up to the age of 60 was expected to join, though he had to buy his own weapons. These group portraits were expensive projects, since each guard member paid for his own likeness to be included. The earliest such portraits date from the 1500s and are quite stiff and formal. Over the course of the 1600s, painters began to arrange their subjects more naturally and dynamically. Two famous examples are Rembrandt's *The Night Watch* and Bartholomeus van der Helst's lively *Banquet of the Marksmen*. It wasn't just the marksmen's guilds that commissioned these group portraits; other city, regional, and national organizations—as well as private individuals—also ordered them.

CuratorialText: Approximately 230 paintings in the collection of the Rijksmuseum are still lifes. As early as the 16th century, Flemish painters specialized in this genre. These are arranged displays featuring, for example, shells, books, food and drink, tableware, skulls, or dead animals, but also flowers, insects, and sometimes a person in the background. Life literally stands still for a moment. Separate specializations soon emerged within the genre. For instance, Rachel Ruysch primarily painted flowers, while Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda enjoyed creating arrangements with food and eating and drinking utensils. Another variant, the vanitas still life, which often depicts a skull, refers to the transience of life and the material world.
persona3_adapted: Approximately 230 paintings in the Rijksmuseum’s collection are still lifes. This type of painting—showing arranged objects—first became a specialty of Flemish artists in the 1500s. In a still life, the artist composes a scene featuring things like shells, books, food and drink, tableware, skulls, or dead animals, as well as flowers, insects, and sometimes a person in the background. Life, in a sense, literally stands still for a moment. Soon, artists began to focus on particular subjects within this category. For example, Rachel Ruysch mainly painted flowers, while Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda were known for arranging food, drinking vessels, and table settings. Another type, the vanitas still life—which often includes a skull—serves as a reminder of life’s shortness and the emptiness of material possessions.

CuratorialText: The Rijksmuseum has an enormous collection of portraits from the late Middle Ages to the present day. In addition to their artistic value, these more than 1,700 portraits give the past a face. They describe what people looked like: what clothing and jewelry they wore, what their hairstyles were like, and sometimes what the interiors looked like at the time. There are also significant differences, as each century emphasizes different aspects. In the Renaissance, portraits seem to become increasingly realistic, whereas artists from the 20th and 21st centuries move away from that realism. Throughout the centuries, governments, organizations, and individuals have commissioned portraits for all kinds of occasions: to confirm profession or status, for a wedding, or as a memorial after someone's death. The Rijksmuseum collection also contains many self-portraits by famous painters such as Jan Steen, Rembrandt, and Van Gogh.
persona3_adapted: The Rijksmuseum holds an extensive collection of portraits spanning from the late Middle Ages to the present day. Beyond their artistic merit, these more than 1,700 portraits bring the past to life by showing us what people actually looked like: the clothing and jewelry they wore, their hairstyles, and sometimes the interiors of their homes. Yet the portraits also reveal striking differences across time, as each century emphasizes different qualities. During the Renaissance, for example, portraits become increasingly realistic—meaning they aim to capture people as they truly appeared—while artists from the 20th and 21st centuries deliberately move away from that kind of lifelike representation. Over the centuries, governments, organizations, and individuals have commissioned portraits for many reasons: to confirm a person’s profession or social standing, to celebrate a wedding, or to serve as a memorial after someone’s death. The Rijksmuseum collection also includes many self-portraits by famous painters such as Jan Steen, Rembrandt, and Van Gogh.

CuratorialText: Genre paintings are paintings depicting scenes from everyday life. They are lively scenes that often take place in kitchens, living rooms, inns, and markets. They capture people in their natural environment: children romping, a family huddled together in the kitchen, or a merchant hawking his wares. The first genre paintings emerged in 16th-century Flemish painting, but in the Netherlands, genre art did not flourish until a century later. The works of Johannes Vermeer, Jan Steen, and Judith Leyster became famous. They often concealed a mischievous content or, conversely, a moral message in their work. The term 'genre paintings' did not emerge until the 19th century, when the popularity of genre art reached a peak. The Rijksmuseum collection also includes works by 19th-century painters such as Jozef Israëls, known for his lively scenes of farmers and fishermen.
persona3_adapted: Genre paintings show scenes from everyday life. These are lively images, often set in kitchens, living rooms, inns, or markets. They capture people in their natural surroundings: children playing, a family gathered together in the kitchen, or a merchant selling his goods. The first such paintings appeared in 16th-century Flemish art, but in the Netherlands, this type of work didn’t truly flourish until a century later. Artists like Johannes Vermeer, Jan Steen, and Judith Leyster became well known for it. Their works often hid a playful or mischievous meaning—or, on the other hand, carried a moral lesson. The term "genre paintings" itself wasn’t used until the 19th century, when this style of art reached the height of its popularity. The Rijksmuseum collection also includes works by 19th-century painters such as Jozef Israëls, who is known for his lively scenes of farmers and fishermen.

CuratorialText: A history piece depicts a memorable historical story, which often originates from a written source. This can be a story from the Bible or mythology, but also events from history. Multiple figures are often visible in these narrative pieces. For instance, around 1530, Jan Jansz. Mostaert depicted a scene from the conquest of America, while in 1824, Jan Willem Pieneman captured The Battle of Waterloo , the largest painting in the Rijksmuseum. In the 17th century, painters also chose Roman scenes as a metaphor for the political situation in the Netherlands. For instance, the Batavian revolt against the Romans, depicted by Ferdinand Bol in the painting Claudius Civilis, refers to the struggle against the Spaniards.
persona3_adapted: A history painting tells a memorable story from the past, often based on a written source. That source could be a story from the Bible or mythology, but it might also come from actual historical events. These narrative works usually include several figures. For example, around 1530, Jan Jansz. Mostaert created a scene from the conquest of America, while in 1824, Jan Willem Pieneman painted *The Battle of Waterloo*—the largest painting in the Rijksmuseum. In the 17th century, artists also used Roman scenes as a way to comment on the political situation in the Netherlands. For instance, Ferdinand Bol’s painting *Claudius Civilis* shows the Batavian revolt against the Romans, which was meant to refer to the Dutch struggle against the Spanish.

CuratorialText: “We, the people of Indonesia, hereby declare the independence of Indonesia.” By uttering these words, the nationalist leader Sukarno declared his country, Indonesia, independent on August 17, 1945. Former colonizer the Netherlands refuses to recognize that independence and attempts to reoccupy Indonesia. In defense of its independence, Indonesia wages war against the Netherlands on its own soil. From the beginning, Indonesia seeks and receives support from the international community. Under international pressure, the Netherlands is forced to recognize Indonesia's independence in 1949. In 1950, Indonesia becomes a member state of the United Nations, and the international confirmation of independence becomes a reality.
persona3_adapted: On August 17, 1945, the nationalist leader Sukarno declared Indonesia’s independence with the words: “We, the people of Indonesia, hereby declare the independence of Indonesia.” However, the Netherlands, the country that had previously colonized Indonesia, refused to accept this declaration and attempted to reclaim control. To defend its newly declared independence, Indonesia fought a war against the Netherlands on its own land. From the very beginning, Indonesia sought and received support from other countries around the world. Faced with growing international pressure, the Netherlands was eventually forced to recognize Indonesia’s independence in 1949. The following year, in 1950, Indonesia became a member of the United Nations, marking the final international confirmation of its independence.

CuratorialText: Water is crucial for the development of the Netherlands as a trading nation. The economy grows thanks to the raw materials that enter via rivers and the world's oceans. Consider bulk goods such as grain and timber from the Baltic region, wool from England, wine from France, and raw salt from the Iberian Peninsula. Using these raw materials, the Dutch produce items such as beer, cloth (woolen fabric), fine salt (for the herring trade), weapons, and ships. Many of these products are subsequently exported, often again by water. Cities are important links in this international trade. In the 15th and 16th centuries, Antwerp is the largest port city of the Netherlands. After the (re)conquest by the Spaniards during the Dutch Revolt, Holland, and especially Amsterdam, takes over the role as a staple market. International trade leads to the emergence of a financial market, significant employment, and strong urban growth.
persona3_adapted:
Water played a vital role in the Netherlands' rise as a trading nation. The economy grew thanks to raw materials brought in by rivers and oceans from around the world. These included bulk goods like grain and timber from the Baltic region, wool from England, wine from France, and raw salt from the Iberian Peninsula. The Dutch used these materials to produce items such as beer, cloth (a type of woolen fabric), fine salt (used in the herring trade), weapons, and ships. Many of these finished goods were then exported, often again by water.

Cities were key links in this international trade network. In the 15th and 16th centuries, Antwerp was the largest port city in the Netherlands. However, after the Spanish recaptured the city during the Dutch Revolt, Holland—and especially Amsterdam—took over as the main trading hub, or "staple market." This international trade led to the growth of a financial market, created many jobs, and fueled rapid urban expansion.

CuratorialText: What is money actually worth? Why does a small piece of metal worth a few euro cents suddenly have a much higher value due to the '2 euro' imprint? Whoever holds the right to mint coins chooses the appearance, the amount of precious metal, and the value. Through trade contacts, gold and silver coins spread across the world for centuries. Coins with little gold or silver circulated primarily locally, because once abroad, the metal value was of paramount importance. The Amsterdam Exchange Bank was for a long time a key hub for the trade in precious metals. As early as the Eighty Years' War, a large portion of the silver that Spain obtained from Spanish America ended up in Amsterdam. Until well into the 18th century, veritable silver fleets sailed from Spain to the Republic. From Amsterdam, money traveled the world, such as to Asia via the VOC.
persona3_adapted: What gives money its value? How does a small piece of metal worth just a few euro cents suddenly become far more valuable simply because it is stamped with the words '2 euro'? The authority to mint coins determines their design, the amount of precious metal they contain, and their assigned worth. For centuries, gold and silver coins spread across the globe through trade. Coins with only a small amount of gold or silver tended to circulate mainly within local regions, because once they traveled abroad, the actual metal content became the key measure of value. The Amsterdam Exchange Bank long served as a major center for the trade in precious metals. As early as the Eighty Years' War, a large portion of the silver that Spain obtained from its colonies in the Americas ended up in Amsterdam. Until well into the 1700s, regular fleets of silver ships sailed from Spain to the Dutch Republic. From Amsterdam, money traveled onward across the world—for example, to Asia through the Dutch East India Company (VOC).

CuratorialText: William of Orange is regarded as the 'Father of the Fatherland' because of his leading role in the struggle for independence. The House of Orange owes its princely title to the insignificant French city-state of Orange on the Rhône, yet manages to carve out a place for itself among the great European royal houses through its wealth and prestige. The wives of the stadtholders play an important role in these networks. Nevertheless, it seems to be the end for the House of Orange at the end of the 18th century: Stadtholder William V flees to England, and the Batavian Republic is proclaimed. But in 1813, his eldest son returns and becomes King William I. From that moment on, the Netherlands is a 'constitutional monarchy,' and a member of the House of Orange-Nassau has always sat on the throne.
persona3_adapted: William of Orange is known as the 'Father of the Fatherland' because he led the fight for Dutch independence. The House of Orange gets its princely title from a small French city-state called Orange, located along the Rhône River. Despite this modest origin, the family managed to earn a place among Europe’s great royal dynasties through its wealth and influence. The wives of the stadtholders—a Dutch term for a kind of governor or leader—played an important role in building and maintaining these connections. However, by the end of the 18th century, the House of Orange seemed to be finished: Stadtholder William V fled to England, and the Batavian Republic was declared. But in 1813, his eldest son returned and became King William I. From that point on, the Netherlands became a constitutional monarchy—a system where the monarch’s powers are limited by law—and a member of the House of Orange-Nassau has always held the throne.

CuratorialText: Whose object was it before it came to the museum? The lives, relationships, and circumstances of the owners tell something about the significance of the object. Provenance research reveals stories of pre-war owners and reminds us of the people who were persecuted during the Holocaust. Even if objects were not looted, their provenance provides insight into their history. The Rijksmuseum collection primarily tells the stories of collectors from the period between 1933 and 1945. Research into the collection can therefore not possibly provide a complete picture of all persecuted owners. After all, many everyday objects did not end up in museums or are more difficult to identify. Nevertheless, the objects in the Rijksmuseum remind us of the diversity of pre-war Europe. Provenance research shows that the people persecuted by the Nazis actively contributed to Dutch culture and history. Objects in museums are still the silent witnesses of this today.
persona3_adapted: Whose object was it before it came to the museum? The lives, relationships, and circumstances of the owners tell us something about why the object mattered. Research into an object's history—called provenance—reveals the stories of its pre-war owners and reminds us of the people who were persecuted during the Holocaust. Even when objects were not stolen, their history still offers insight into their past. The Rijksmuseum collection mainly tells the stories of collectors from the period between 1933 and 1945. Because of this, research into the collection cannot give a complete picture of all persecuted owners. Many everyday objects never ended up in museums, or are harder to identify. Still, the objects in the Rijksmuseum remind us of the diversity of pre-war Europe. Provenance research shows that the people persecuted by the Nazis actively contributed to Dutch culture and history. Objects in museums remain the silent witnesses of this today.

CuratorialText: Before the Europeans arrive, centuries-old trade routes already exist around the Indian Ocean. The Dutch East India Company (VOC) forcibly expands these networks and exploits areas such as the Moluccas and Sri Lanka for spices. Batavia (Jakarta) and the Cape of Good Hope become important centers for sugar, coffee, and rice. In the Atlantic region, Dutch merchants trade in products such as sugar and coffee, which leads to the establishment of the Dutch West India Company (WIC). Over time, slave labor and the slave trade take place at all trading posts. Even for a craftsman in the Netherlands, there is money to be made from colonial trade, for example as a supplier of products for the slave trade or for daily life in the colonies. The transport of the products to Europe and their further processing also generates revenue. In this way, colonial trade stimulates the national economy.
