persona3_adapted: Before European arrival, trade routes around the Indian Ocean had already existed for centuries. The Dutch East India Company (VOC) aggressively expanded these networks and took control of areas like the Moluccas (a group of islands in present-day Indonesia) and Sri Lanka to obtain spices. Cities such as Batavia (now Jakarta) and the Cape of Good Hope (in modern-day South Africa) became major hubs for sugar, coffee, and rice. In the Atlantic region, Dutch merchants traded goods like sugar and coffee, which led to the founding of the Dutch West India Company (WIC). Over time, enslaved labor and the slave trade became common at all of these trading posts. Even craftsmen back in the Netherlands could profit from colonial trade—for example, by supplying goods used in the slave trade or items needed for daily life in the colonies. Transporting these products to Europe and processing them further also generated income. In these ways, colonial trade helped stimulate the Dutch national economy.

CuratorialText: The Netherlands was a colonial power for more than 350 years and established trading posts and colonies worldwide in the 17th century, including in Indonesia, South Africa, Sri Lanka, South America, and the Caribbean. Dutch colonial settlement often took place through the conclusion of trade contracts, but also through military force. The Rijksmuseum possesses many (art) objects that were brought to the Netherlands during and after this period, ranging from war booty to items acquired through trade. It is important to be open about the history of this collection and to reflect on the role of the Netherlands in the colonial past. Through research and collaboration with countries of origin, the Rijksmuseum aims to provide a nuanced and diverse picture of this shared past.
persona3_adapted: The Netherlands was a colonial power for more than 350 years. During the 1600s, it set up trading posts and colonies around the world, including in Indonesia, South Africa, Sri Lanka, South America, and the Caribbean. Dutch colonial expansion often happened through trade agreements, but also through military force. The Rijksmuseum holds many art and everyday objects that were brought to the Netherlands during and after this period—some taken as war booty, others acquired through trade. It is important to be open about the history of this collection and to reflect on the Netherlands' role in its colonial past. Through research and partnerships with the countries where these objects originated, the Rijksmuseum aims to present a more complete and balanced view of this shared history.

CuratorialText: For 750 years, Amsterdam has been a city that grows, flourishes, and buzzes. Residents from all corners of the world have made the city what it is today. The first inhabitants of Amsterdam were farmers and fishermen, living along the River Amstel. In the late Middle Ages, Amsterdam grew into a small port city amidst the peatlands. Partly due to the establishment of the trading companies VOC and WIC, the city transformed rapidly into a global metropolis with more than 200,000 inhabitants during the 17th century, where almost every Amsterdammer was directly or indirectly involved in colonial trade. From rope makers to cartographers, from notaries to confectioners. To manage the population growth, a plan was drawn up to expand the city. It was the beginning of the famous canal belt.
persona3_adapted: For 750 years, Amsterdam has been a city that grows, thrives, and hums with activity. People from all over the world have shaped it into the place it is today. The earliest residents were farmers and fishermen who lived along the River Amstel. During the late Middle Ages, Amsterdam developed into a small port town set in the peatlands. In part because of the founding of the VOC and WIC—two major Dutch trading companies—the city rapidly transformed into a global hub, home to more than 200,000 people by the 1600s. At that time, nearly every Amsterdammer was directly or indirectly involved in colonial trade, from rope makers and mapmakers to notaries and candy makers. To manage the growing population, a plan was drawn up to expand the city. This marked the beginning of what would become Amsterdam’s famous ring of canals.

CuratorialText: The tulip originates from Turkey and was introduced to Europe in the late 16th century. The flower proved to thrive particularly well in the sandy soil of the Dutch coastal region. In the 17th century, traders of tulips and other exotic flowers began publishing 'Florilegia', books with detailed illustrations serving as catalogues of their wares. Enthusiasts of unusual flowers and plants could also turn to painters and draftsmen. Flower still lifes became a popular genre in art. Real flowers wilt, but paintings could serve as durable substitutes. Moreover, these flower still lifes sometimes cost even less than the flowers depicted on the canvas.
persona3_adapted: The tulip originally came from Turkey and was brought to Europe in the late 1500s. It grew especially well in the sandy soil of the Dutch coastal region. In the 1600s, traders who sold tulips and other unusual flowers began publishing books called 'Florilegia'—these were illustrated catalogues that showed detailed pictures of the flowers they had for sale. People who loved rare flowers and plants could also hire painters or sketch artists. Paintings of flowers in vases, known as flower still lifes, became a popular type of artwork. Real flowers eventually die, but paintings could last much longer as a permanent record. Interestingly, these flower paintings sometimes cost even less than the actual flowers shown in them.

CuratorialText: Throughout history, the role of pets changes significantly, from useful working animals to beloved companions. It was not until between 500 and 1500 that noblemen began keeping dogs for their company. In paintings from the 17th century, these four-legged creatures often appear in that role. For a long time, cats were primarily used as mouse exterminators and had a bad reputation due to their medieval association with witchcraft. It was not until the Victorian era that people accepted cats as pets. A beautiful pedigree cat, such as a British Shorthair or the Persian Longhair, became a status symbol for wealthy citizens. From the 19th century onwards, dogs and cats, and later birds and fish, became everyday pets for all social classes. Artist Henriëtte Ronner-Knip (1821–1909) specialized in painting cats, which brought her fame. She was regularly commissioned to immortalize the beloved pets of aristocratic and bourgeois families.
persona3_adapted: Throughout history, the role of pets has shifted significantly, from useful working animals to beloved companions. It wasn't until the period between 500 and 1500 that noblemen began keeping dogs primarily for their company. In paintings from the 1600s, these four-legged animals often appear in that role. For a long time, cats were mainly used to catch mice and had a bad reputation because of their medieval association with witchcraft. It wasn't until the Victorian era—roughly the 1800s—that people began to accept cats as pets. A beautiful purebred cat, such as a British Shorthair or the Persian Longhair, became a status symbol for wealthy citizens. From the 1800s onward, dogs and cats, and later birds and fish, became everyday pets for people of all social classes. The artist Henriëtte Ronner-Knip (1821–1909) specialized in painting cats, which brought her fame. She was regularly hired to create lasting portraits of the beloved pets of aristocratic and upper-middle-class families.

CuratorialText: Sculpture. From the Middle Ages to the present day, in every shape and size and encompassing a wide range of materials. This expanding online catalog presents a comprehensive overview of the latest scholarly insights into the Rijksmuseum's vast and highly diverse collection of European sculpture. This online catalog includes every work of European sculpture in the Rijksmuseum's art collection. The most recently published catalog of sculpture dates from 1973. Prompting a thorough update of this valuable, but by now outdated reference work was the ever-growing body of art historical research and the long list of acquisitions made in recent decades.   The Rijksmuseum (and its precursors: the Royal Cabinet of Rareities and later the Dutch Museum of History and Art) began acquiring sculpture on a systematic basis only in the last quarter of the nineteenth century. Additions to the collection prior to this time are few in number. One early example is the Dutch government's purchase ofArtus Quellinus's magnificent marble bust portrait of Andries de Graeff in 1817. Initially, the museum's sculpture acquisition policy was primarily oriented to works of national interest. It was not until the twentieth century that this focus gradually began to shift, largely thanks to the efforts of Adriaan Pit (museum director from 1898 to 1917) and Jaap Leeuwenberg (curator of sculpture from 1948 to 1969 and compiler of the 1973 catalogue), who sought to actively acquire international works. As a result, sculptures of non-Netherlandish origin today make up approximately half of the collection. This international scope received an additional impulse when, in 1952, the Dutch government charged the Rijksmuseum with the care of Italian and German works formerly in the possession of Fritz Mannheimer and recovered from Germany after the Second World War. This enhancement of the collection has since served to stimulate the museum's policy of acquiring international works.
persona3_adapted:
**Sculpture at the Rijksmuseum: A Living Collection**

From the Middle Ages to today, sculpture has taken countless forms and sizes, made from an enormous variety of materials. This growing online catalog offers a broad, up-to-date look at what scholars now know about the Rijksmuseum's vast and remarkably diverse collection of European sculpture. It includes every European sculpture in the museum's art collection.

The last printed catalog of the museum's sculptures was published in 1973. That valuable reference work has since become outdated, mainly because art-historical research has expanded greatly and the museum has added many new works over the past few decades. This online catalog is a thorough update.

The Rijksmuseum (and its earlier versions—the Royal Cabinet of Curiosities and later the Dutch Museum of History and Art) only began collecting sculpture in a planned, systematic way during the last quarter of the nineteenth century. Before that, the museum acquired very few sculptures. One early exception was the Dutch government's purchase in 1817 of a magnificent marble bust of Andries de Graeff, carved by the artist Artus Quellinus.

At first, the museum focused its sculpture collecting on works that were important to Dutch national history and culture. It wasn't until the twentieth century that this focus gradually shifted, largely thanks to two key figures: Adriaan Pit (museum director from 1898 to 1917) and Jaap Leeuwenberg (curator of sculpture from 1948 to 1969, who also compiled the 1973 catalog). Both actively sought to acquire sculptures from outside the Netherlands. As a result, about half of the collection today comes from other European countries.

This international direction received another major boost in 1952, when the Dutch government asked the Rijksmuseum to take care of Italian and German sculptures that had once belonged to Fritz Mannheimer. These works had been recovered from Germany after World War II. This addition encouraged the museum to continue its policy of acquiring international sculptures.

CuratorialText:
The Rijksmuseum holds a world-class collection of Japanese works on paper: woodblock prints, illustrated books and drawings. Among the almost 4000 prints, more than 800 are exclusive, privately published surimono. This online catalog features 203 surimono donated to the museum between 1991 and 1999 by the collector JHW Goslings (1943-2011). The entries in this catalog are taken from Matthi Forrer's 2013 collection catalog Surimono in the Rijksmuseum Amsterdam.
Poetry and image combined
“Surimono (literally 'printed things') constitute one of the most delicate genres in Japanese printmaking. This genre fascinates because it combines poetry and image and because it presents a pictorial puzzle, which provides the viewer with a particular insight into the intellectual and literary world of the late 18th- and early 19th-century Edo (today's Tokyo). Major artists such asKatsushika Hokusai,Utagawa Kunisada,Toyota HokkeiandYashima Gakutei, to name but a few, provided imagery to accompany the poetic exploits of poetry club members.
persona3_adapted:
The Rijksmuseum holds a world-class collection of Japanese works on paper: woodblock prints, illustrated books, and drawings. Among its nearly 4,000 prints, more than 800 are exclusive, privately published *surimono*—a special type of print made for private occasions. This online catalog features 203 *surimono* donated to the museum between 1991 and 1999 by the collector JHW Goslings (1943-2011). The entries in this catalog are taken from Matthi Forrer's 2013 collection catalog *Surimono in the Rijksmuseum Amsterdam*.

**Poetry and Image Combined**

*Surimono* (literally "printed things") represent one of the most delicate forms of Japanese printmaking. This genre fascinates because it brings together poetry and image, and because it presents a visual puzzle. Solving that puzzle offers the viewer a unique window into the intellectual and literary world of late 18th- and early 19th-century Edo (modern-day Tokyo). Major artists such as Katsushika Hokusai, Utagawa Kunisada, Toyota Hokkei, and Yashima Gakutei—to name just a few—created images to accompany the poetic achievements of poetry club members.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Maria Trip (1619-83), sister of Jacobus Trip and later the wife of Balthasar Coymans. Standing, half-length, facing left. Her left arm rests on a banister, holding a fan.
persona3_adapted: Portrait of Maria Trip (1619–83), the sister of Jacobus Trip, who later married Balthasar Coymans. She is shown standing, from the waist up, facing left. Her left arm rests on a banister as she holds a fan.

CuratorialText: He sits with outstretched wings on a cloud placed on a round plinth, holding his right index finger to the mouth of his mischievously smiling face, while reaching with his left hand for an arrow in the quiver lying beside him. His left leg is drawn up, and the foot of his right leg, positioned diagonally in front of him, extends over the round plinth. Beside him lies a flowering rose branch. The quiver filled with arrows displays, among other things, fluting, a wreath of laurel leaves, and godrons. The pedestal, a short column, has a profiled base decorated with a wreath of laurel leaves, a shaft with curved fluting containing culots transitioning into a smooth upper section interrupted by a profiled edge, and furthermore, a profiled, overhanging cover plate decorated with egg-and-dart molding, which is rotatable by means of two brass knobs.
persona3_adapted: He sits on a cloud resting atop a round base, his wings spread wide. With a mischievous smile, he presses his right index finger to his lips, while reaching with his left hand toward an arrow in the quiver beside him. His left leg is drawn up, and his right leg extends diagonally forward, its foot hanging over the edge of the round base. Next to him lies a flowering rose branch. The quiver, filled with arrows, is decorated with vertical grooves, a wreath of laurel leaves, and a pattern of repeating oval shapes. The pedestal—a short column—has a base shaped with layered contours, also adorned with a laurel wreath. Its shaft features curved grooves that end in leaf-shaped ornaments, then transitions into a smooth upper section interrupted by a raised band. Above this sits a projecting top plate decorated with a repeating pattern of egg-shaped forms and darts, which can be rotated using two brass knobs.

CuratorialText: Indoor room with a mother removing lice from her child's hair, known as 'Mother's Duty'. Interior with mother and child sitting in front of a box bed on the right. A high chair on the right; on the left, a dog sits on the tiled floor and looks at the open window in the room behind. Above the door is a small painting of a landscape.
persona3_adapted: A quiet indoor scene shows a mother carefully removing lice from her child's hair—a task once known as "Mother's Duty." The two sit in front of a box bed on the right side of the room. Nearby stands a high chair, while on the left, a dog rests on the tiled floor, gazing toward an open window in the room behind. Above the door hangs a small landscape painting.

CuratorialText: This portrait of Alida Assink presents her in her finest attire, depicted from head to toe. That is quite exceptional, as the painter Kruseman typically reserved such a full-length portrait exclusively for royalty. Assink was 23 years old when this likeness was commissioned by her guardian, a wealthy landowner. The rural setting with hunting dog and garden vase harks back to the English portrait style, which Kruseman admired.
persona3_adapted: This portrait of Alida Assink shows her in her finest clothing, from head to toe. That is quite unusual, because the painter Kruseman usually painted full-length portraits only for royalty. Assink was 23 years old when this likeness was commissioned by her guardian, a wealthy landowner. The countryside setting, with a hunting dog and a garden vase, draws on the English portrait style—a tradition Kruseman admired.

CuratorialText: The Dordrecht artist Aert Schouman was no scientist, but he was deeply interested in true-to-life depictions of the bird realm. His keen powers of observation are demonstrated by the way in which this red-billed toucan grasps the branch, namely with two, rather than three toes like other species. The landscape airily painted in transparent shades of green and blue evokes the bird’s native regions in Guyana and North Brazil.
persona3_adapted: The Dordrecht artist Aert Schouman was not a scientist, but he had a strong interest in painting birds as they appear in real life. His careful observation is clear in the way this red-billed toucan grips the branch—using two toes instead of three, as most other birds do. The landscape, painted in light, see-through shades of green and blue, suggests the bird’s natural home in Guyana and northern Brazil.

CuratorialText: Still life with roses. Also included are a butterfly and two snails.
persona3_adapted: A still life arrangement of roses, accompanied by a butterfly and two snails.

CuratorialText: Here the Duke of Wellington is receiving the message that Prussian forces are coming to his aid. Wellington, commander of the Anglo-Dutch troops, is the central figure in this group portrait of the major players at Waterloo. Lying wounded on a stretcher in the left foreground is the Dutch Crown Prince, later King William II. Originally intended for Wellington, the painting remained in the Netherlands thanks to William I who bought the picture for his son.
persona3_adapted: Here the Duke of Wellington is receiving the news that Prussian forces are coming to his aid. Wellington, who commanded the Anglo-Dutch troops, is the central figure in this group portrait of the key leaders at the Battle of Waterloo. In the lower left corner, lying wounded on a stretcher, is the Dutch Crown Prince—later King William II. Although the painting was originally meant for Wellington, it stayed in the Netherlands because King William I bought it for his son.

CuratorialText: Of the noblemen who rebelled against Phillip II’s Spanish rule of the Netherlands, William of Orange rose to become the great leader of the Dutch Revolt. He defended his own interests, strove for greater independence and advocated freedom of religion. In 1580 Phillip II put a price on his head, and he was assassinated four years later.
persona3_adapted: Of the noblemen who rebelled against Philip II's Spanish rule of the Netherlands, William of Orange emerged as the key leader of the Dutch Revolt. He fought to protect his own interests, pushed for greater independence, and championed religious freedom. In 1580, Philip II placed a bounty on his head, and William was assassinated four years later.

CuratorialText: This still life invites close examination of all the details and elicits marvel at the richness of God’s creation: the colourful flowers, the exotic shells, but certainly also the beautiful insects. At the same time, decay is spreading. Fruit shows the first rotten spots, leaves are eaten away by caterpillars and snails and ants drag a dead fly. The painting is thus also about impermanence.
persona3_adapted: This still life painting rewards careful looking. It invites us to marvel at the richness of the natural world: the colorful flowers, the exotic shells, and the beautiful insects. But signs of decay are also present. Fruit shows the first rotten spots, leaves are being eaten by caterpillars, and snails and ants drag a dead fly. In this way, the painting also reflects on the theme of impermanence—the idea that all living things eventually fade and pass away.

CuratorialText: Presented here are two squirrel monkeys from Central America, two white sulphur-crested cockatoos from Australia, a grey parrot from Africa and a purple-naped lory – on a chain at the lower left – from Indonesia. Hondecoeter combined these creatures and several other splendid birds in this painting, which was destined for Het Loo, the palace of William III. It hung above the door of the king’s private apartment.
persona3_adapted: Here are two squirrel monkeys from Central America, two white sulphur-crested cockatoos from Australia, a grey parrot from Africa, and a purple-naped lory—shown on a chain at the lower left—from Indonesia. In this painting, Hondecoeter brought together these animals and several other magnificent birds. The work was made for Het Loo, the palace of King William III, and it hung above the door of the king’s private apartment.

CuratorialText: This is a panoramic, sun-drenched river landscape – somewhat hazy in the distance – with sketchily drawn clouds. Two Dutch officers halt at the river, and water their horses next to a herder and his resting cattle. This idyllic scene does not unfold in Italy, but along the Waal between Nijmegen and Cleves, where Cuyp made landscape drawings that he took back to his workshop in Dordrecht.
persona3_adapted: This is a wide, sunlit view of a river landscape, with the distance appearing somewhat hazy and the clouds loosely sketched in. Two Dutch officers stop by the river to water their horses, next to a herder and his resting cattle. Despite its Italianate feel, this peaceful scene is not set in Italy, but along the Waal River between Nijmegen and Cleves. The artist, Cuyp, made drawings of this landscape and brought them back to his studio in Dordrecht to create this painting.

CuratorialText: Ice entertainment near a village. Winter village scene with many figures skating, playing, and bowling on the ice. On the left, two people have fallen through the ice. In the distance, a drawbridge and a windmill.
persona3_adapted: On a frozen river near a village, a lively winter scene unfolds. Many people are skating, playing games, and bowling on the ice. To the left, two figures have broken through the ice and fallen into the water. In the background, a drawbridge and a windmill can be seen, adding a sense of everyday life and landscape to the scene.

CuratorialText: This dolls’ house is exceptionally realistic. All the contents have been made of authentic materials, and the proportions are exactly correct. The fine cabinet, of tortoiseshell decorated with pewter inlays, was made by a cabinetmaker from France, who worked in Amsterdam for several years. Petronella Oortman was married to the Amsterdam merchant Johannes Brandt.
persona3_adapted: This dolls’ house is remarkably lifelike. Every object inside is made from real materials, and the scale is perfectly accurate. The elegant cabinet, crafted from tortoiseshell and decorated with pewter inlays, was built by a French cabinetmaker who spent several years working in Amsterdam. Petronella Oortman was married to Johannes Brandt, a merchant from Amsterdam.

CuratorialText: This scene is painted on a copper panel. Its smooth surface was well suited to meticulous and detailed finish. Van Kessel made dozens of such little ‘coppers’ of insects and other animals, which he would arrange in a larger composition. They may have also served as decorative panels for a small cabinet of curiosities.
persona3_adapted: This scene is painted on a copper panel. The metal’s smooth surface allowed the artist to achieve an exceptionally fine and detailed finish. Van Kessel created dozens of these small copper panels featuring insects and other animals, which he would then arrange into a larger composition. It is also possible that these pieces were used as decorative panels for a small cabinet of curiosities—a private collection of unusual or fascinating objects.

CuratorialText: 18 June 1648: a banquet is taking place at the Amsterdam crossbowmen’s guild. The occasion was the signing of the Treaty of Münster, which marked an end to the war with Spain. The captains of the civic guard company shake hands as a sign of peace, and the drinking horn is passed around. The poem on the drum proclaims the joy of Amsterdam’s armed militia that their weapons can henceforth be laid to rest.
persona3_adapted: 18 June 1648: a banquet is underway at Amsterdam’s crossbowmen’s guild. The event celebrated the signing of the Treaty of Münster, which ended the war with Spain. The captains of the city’s civic guard—a local militia—shake hands as a gesture of peace, while the drinking horn is passed around. A poem inscribed on the drum announces the militia’s joy that their weapons can now be put aside.

CuratorialText: The Fishing for Souls: an allegory of the jealousy of the various religions during the Twelve Years' Truce (1609-1621) between the Dutch Republic and Spain. In a river landscape, large groups of people are situated on both banks of the river; several rowboats are on the water. On the horizon, a rainbow. On the left side are the Protestants and their supporters: the Princes of Orange, Maurice and Frederick Henry, Frederick V of the Palatinate, James I, King of England, and the youthful Louis XIII, King of France, with his mother, Maria de' Medici. In the boat in the foreground sit the Protestant fishermen; their nets are marked with Fides, Spes, and Charitas. On the right side stand the Catholics and their supporters: Archdukes Albert and Isabella, General Ambrosio Spinola, and the Pope carried by cardinals. In the small Catholic boat, priests are fishing under the leadership of a bishop.
persona3_adapted:
**Fishing for Souls: An Allegory of Religious Rivalry During the Twelve Years' Truce (1609–1621)**

This painting is an allegory—a symbolic story—about the intense competition between different Christian groups during a temporary peace between the Dutch Republic and Spain. The scene unfolds across a river landscape, with large crowds gathered on both banks and several rowboats on the water. A rainbow stretches across the horizon.

On the left side are the Protestants and their allies. These include the Princes of Orange, Maurice and Frederick Henry; Frederick V of the Palatinate; James I, King of England; and the young Louis XIII, King of France, with his mother, Maria de' Medici. In the boat closest to us, Protestant fishermen are at work. Their nets are marked with the Latin words *Fides*, *Spes*, and *Charitas*—meaning Faith, Hope, and Charity, three central Christian virtues.

On the right side stand the Catholics and their supporters: Archdukes Albert and Isabella, General Ambrosio Spinola, and the Pope, who is being carried by cardinals. In the smaller Catholic boat, priests are fishing under the direction of a bishop.

The painting uses the act of fishing as a metaphor for each side's efforts to win followers for their faith. The river itself represents the divide between the two groups, and the rainbow may suggest hope or divine promise—though the scene is charged with tension, not harmony.

CuratorialText: Wedding portrait of Isaac Abrahamsz Massa and Beatrix van der Laen, married in Haarlem on April 25, 1622. The couple is seated full-length on a raised platform under a tree. On the left, a thistle; on the right, ivy. On the right in the background, elegant couples are walking in a garden with a fountain at a country house.
persona3_adapted: Wedding portrait of Isaac Abrahamsz Massa and Beatrix van der Laen, who were married in Haarlem on April 25, 1622. The couple is shown seated at full length on a raised platform beneath a tree. To the left, a thistle plant appears; to the right, ivy. In the background on the right, well-dressed couples stroll through a garden featuring a fountain, set beside a country house.

CuratorialText: Figure of painted porcelain. The figure depicts a parrot (macaw) sitting on a tree stump with leaves. The parrot has a blue-scaled neck and head and a white body. The tail and the tips of the wings are painted blue, yellow, and white. A branch with cherries lies beneath the right leg. The figure is unmarked.
persona3_adapted: This small porcelain figure shows a parrot—specifically a macaw—perched on a tree stump with leaves. The bird’s head and neck are covered in blue, scale-like markings, while its body is white. The tips of its wings and its tail are painted in blue, yellow, and white. A branch with cherries rests beneath its right leg. The piece carries no maker’s mark or signature.

CuratorialText: Formal long-sleeved kimono for an unmarried young woman (furisode), decorated all over with more than two hundred flying cranes. Black silk crepe (chirimen) with yuzen decoration in white, red, and grey tones, with gold foil detailing. Red silk lining. Single bamboo crest (mon).
persona3_adapted: This formal long-sleeved kimono, known as a *furisode*, was worn by an unmarried young woman. Its entire surface is decorated with over two hundred cranes in flight, a symbol of longevity and good fortune. The kimono is made of black silk crepe, a fabric with a slightly crinkled texture, and features a traditional Japanese dyeing technique called *yuzen*, which allows for detailed, hand-painted patterns. The design uses white, red, and grey tones, with accents of gold foil for added elegance. The inside is lined with red silk. A single bamboo crest, or *mon*, marks the garment as belonging to a specific family.

CuratorialText: Still life with flowers. A bouquet of flowers in a metal vase standing in a niche. The bouquet contains roses, irises, poppies, a wheat stalk, tulips, and other flowers. A snail, a caterpillar, and a beetle crawl among the flowers and over the niche. Pendant of SK-A-337.
persona3_adapted: A still life painting of flowers. A bouquet of roses, irises, poppies, tulips, and a stalk of wheat rises from a metal vase set into a wall niche. Crawling among the blossoms and across the niche are a snail, a caterpillar, and a beetle. This work is the companion piece to another painting in the museum's collection (SK-A-337).

CuratorialText: Mary Magdalene sitting under a tree with the ointment jar on her lap. The saint is seated in a landscape with mountains and rugged rock formations.
persona3_adapted: Mary Magdalene is shown sitting beneath a tree, with a jar of ointment resting on her lap. She is positioned in a landscape that includes mountains and rough, uneven rock formations.

CuratorialText: Portrait of the twenty-year-old Marten Soolmans standing full-length on a checkered tile floor in an undefined space. He is dressed in an expensive black suit with a lace collar, bows, cuffs, and garters, wears a black hat and shoes with rosettes. In his left hand, he holds a glove.
persona3_adapted: A full-length portrait of twenty-year-old Marten Soolmans, standing on a checkered tile floor in a space that is not clearly defined. He wears a costly black suit, complete with a lace collar, decorative bows, cuffs, and garters, along with a black hat and shoes adorned with rosettes. In his left hand, he holds a single glove.

CuratorialText: Portrait of 23-year-old Oopjen Coppit standing full-length on a tiled floor in an otherwise undefined space. She is dressed in an expensive black dress, lace collar and cuffs, and a black veil. She wears precious jewelry. In her right hand, she holds a fan of black ostrich feathers.
persona3_adapted: Oopjen Coppit, a 23-year-old woman, is shown standing at full height on a tiled floor, with no other background or setting to define the space around her. She wears a costly black dress, a lace collar and cuffs, and a black veil, all of which signal her wealth and social status. She also wears valuable jewelry, and in her right hand, she holds a fan made from black ostrich feathers.

CuratorialText: Still life with a golden ceremonial goblet, a silver holder on which stands a Chinese dish, an orange, a peeled lemon, grapes, peaches, a lobster, a plate of oysters and a roll.
persona3_adapted: A still life arrangement featuring a golden ceremonial goblet, a silver stand holding a Chinese dish, an orange, a peeled lemon, grapes, peaches, a lobster, a plate of oysters, and a bread roll.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Marie Fargues, the artist's wife, in Turkish costume. Full-length, seated on a divan. Her head resting on her right hand. On the divan lie a book and a basket containing a mirror, comb, and bonnet. On the floor are an Oriental rug and a small vase with carnations
persona3_adapted: This portrait shows Marie Fargues, the artist’s wife, dressed in a Turkish costume. She is seated on a low sofa, resting her head on her right hand. On the sofa beside her are a book and a basket holding a mirror, a comb, and a bonnet. On the floor, we see an Oriental rug and a small vase filled with carnations.

CuratorialText: A cheerful violinist holding a birch flask. A laughing man leans out of a window covered by an Oriental rug. In his right hand, he holds up a birch flask filled with wine; in his left hand, a violin with a bow. On his head, a feathered hat.
persona3_adapted: A cheerful violinist holds a birch flask—a type of wooden bottle—while a laughing man leans out of a window draped with an Oriental rug. In his right hand, he raises a birch flask filled with wine; in his left, he holds a violin and bow. He wears a feathered hat on his head.

CuratorialText: View of Olinda in Dutch Brazil. On the left, on a hill, a group of people by the ruins of the church. In the foreground, tropical plants, fruits, and animals (armadillo, monkey, sloth, anteater, lizard).
persona3_adapted: This scene captures the Brazilian town of Olinda during the period of Dutch occupation. To the left, atop a hill, a small group of people gathers near the remains of a church. In the foreground, the artist has arranged a vivid assortment of tropical plants, fruits, and animals—including an armadillo, monkey, sloth, anteater, and lizard—likely to emphasize the region's exotic natural abundance for European viewers.

CuratorialText: Winter landscape. View of a wide, snow-covered lane leading past a forest. In the foreground, three boys with sleds. Further back, a wagon drawn by two horses stands on the road in front of a farm.
persona3_adapted: A winter scene unfolds before us: a broad, snow-covered road stretches through the landscape, passing by a forest. In the foreground, three boys stand with sleds, while further along, a horse-drawn wagon waits on the road in front of a farmhouse.

CuratorialText: Italian landscape with draftsman. A road through the landscape with a bridge over a mountain stream. On the road, a man with a mule. In the foreground, some seated figures.
persona3_adapted: A scene of the Italian countryside, featuring a draftsman—an artist sketching the view. A road winds through the landscape, crossing a bridge over a stream that flows down from the mountains. On the road, a man leads a mule. In the foreground, a few figures are seated, perhaps resting or taking in the scenery.

CuratorialText: Still life with turkey pie. Table with a white damask tablecloth on which stand pewter plates with a pie, oysters, lemon, salt and pepper, and olives (?). Also a pewter pitcher, roemer, knife, nuts, bread rolls, a nautilus cup, and a Wan Li bowl with fruit. In the background stands a large pie on which a turkey has been placed.
persona3_adapted: A still life scene featuring a turkey pie. A table is covered with a white damask tablecloth, upon which rest pewter plates holding a pie, oysters, lemon, salt and pepper, and what appear to be olives. Also present are a pewter pitcher, a type of wine glass known as a roemer, a knife, nuts, bread rolls, a cup made from a nautilus shell, and a bowl from the Chinese Wan Li period, filled with fruit. In the background, a large pie is displayed, topped with a turkey.

CuratorialText: The stone bridge. Landscape with a stone bridge over a river or canal. On the water are two small boats with figures. Various people are walking on the road, a wagon on the left. In the middle, a hay barn between trees, a church tower in the distance on the right.
persona3_adapted: A stone bridge spans a river or canal in this landscape scene. Two small boats carrying figures drift on the water, while several people walk along the road. To the left, a wagon is visible. In the center, a hay barn sits nestled between trees, and in the distance on the right, a church tower rises.

CuratorialText: A stone bust of Flora in an ornamental cartouche surrounded by a festoon of various types of flowers, including tulips, roses, delphinium, foxglove, hollyhocks, carnations, and ivy.
persona3_adapted: A carved stone portrait of Flora, the Roman goddess of flowers, is set within an ornate, decorative frame. This frame is encircled by a garland made up of many different kinds of flowers, such as tulips, roses, delphinium, foxglove, hollyhocks, carnations, and ivy.

CuratorialText: The overturned bouquet. A cat, playing with a mousetrap, knocks over a vase containing a bouquet of flowers. In the bouquet: tulips, irises, poppies, and carnations. Flying among the flowers are butterflies, a spider, a dragonfly, and snails.
persona3_adapted: A vase filled with a bouquet of flowers has been knocked over—tulips, irises, poppies, and carnations spill out. The cause? A cat, distracted by playing with a mousetrap. Among the scattered blooms, butterflies, a spider, a dragonfly, and even snails are caught in motion, as if the scene itself has come alive with small, unexpected details.

CuratorialText: The Fall of Man. Adam and Eve standing before the Tree of Knowledge. A snake offers Eve an apple. Surrounding the figures are various animals: monkey, cat, dog, slugs, hedgehog, frog, fox, and owl. In the background on the left, Adam and Eve are warned by God not to eat from the tree.
persona3_adapted: Adam and Eve stand before the Tree of Knowledge in this depiction of the Fall of Man. A snake offers Eve an apple, while a variety of animals—including a monkey, cat, dog, slugs, hedgehog, frog, fox, and owl—surround the pair. In the background on the left, God warns Adam and Eve not to eat from the tree.

CuratorialText: View of an orchard with plum blossoms, seen through the branches of a plum tree in the foreground; picnicking visitors in the background; part of the entrance gate on the left in the foreground; against a bright red sky.
persona3_adapted: Against a vivid red sky, a plum tree in the foreground frames a view of an orchard in bloom. Through its branches, we see visitors enjoying a picnic in the distance, while part of the entrance gate appears on the left, closer to us. The scene captures a moment of leisure in a blossoming landscape, with the bright sky adding a dramatic, almost theatrical quality to the everyday activity.

CuratorialText: The painting is executed in a pointillist technique. The sitter is positioned on the right, facing the viewer, while studying a book in the light of a kerosene lamp. She is dressed in a pink gown, known as reform clothing, and has her hair simply styled in an updo. The kerosene lamp is partially visible on the table on the left, placed next to a small vase of roses on a small table covered with a floral tablecloth. Flowers in a large vase in the background are visually incorporated into the pattern of the decorative wallpaper.
persona3_adapted: The painting uses a technique called pointillism, where the artist builds the image from many tiny dots of color rather than brushstrokes. The woman sits on the right side of the canvas, facing us as she reads a book by the light of a kerosene lamp. She wears a pink gown—a style known as reform clothing, which was part of a movement for more practical and comfortable women's fashion—and her hair is simply pinned up. The lamp is partly visible on the table to the left, next to a small vase of roses on a table covered with a floral cloth. In the background, flowers in a large vase blend into the pattern of the decorative wallpaper, so that the real and painted flowers seem to merge.

CuratorialText: Kitten game. A small cat plays with dominoes on a table. There is also an ashtray with a cigar on the table.
persona3_adapted: A small cat is shown playing with dominoes on a table, while an ashtray holding a cigar rests nearby. The scene captures a quiet, everyday moment, inviting the viewer to notice the playful contrast between the kitten's lively curiosity and the still, adult objects around it.

CuratorialText: Interior in which a family celebrates the Feast of Saint Nicholas. In the center, a girl holding a John the Baptist doll and a bucket of candy in her arms. On the left, a crying boy who has received a rod in his shoe. Next to the mother with outstretched arms stands a laughing boy with a club. On the floor is a basket of cakes and rolls; by the fireplace, several young people are singing a Sinterklaas song. In the background, an older man and woman.
persona3_adapted: In this interior scene, a family celebrates the Feast of Saint Nicholas, a Dutch holiday similar to Christmas. At the center, a young girl holds a doll representing John the Baptist and a bucket of candy. To the left, a boy cries because he has received a rod—a traditional punishment—in his shoe instead of a gift. Nearby, the mother stands with her arms outstretched, while a laughing boy beside her carries a club. On the floor rests a basket of cakes and rolls; by the fireplace, several young people sing a Sinterklaas song. In the background, an older man and woman look on.

CuratorialText: Jeremiah mourning over the destruction of Jerusalem. The old prophet sits on the rocky slope of a mountain, his head resting on his left hand. On a stone beside him lie a golden bowl, a pitcher, and books, sanctuaries from the destroyed temple. Below to the left, the burning buildings of the city and soldiers can be seen.
persona3_adapted: Jeremiah mourns the destruction of Jerusalem. The elderly prophet sits on a rocky mountainside, his head resting on his left hand. Beside him on a stone lie a golden bowl, a pitcher, and books—objects saved from the ruined temple. Below to the left, the burning buildings of the city and soldiers are visible.

CuratorialText: Still life with fruit, oysters, and a porcelain bowl. On a stone table, a silver plate with oysters, fruit, a porcelain bowl, an inverted roemer, a glowing wick, and a bunch of grapes on a wooden box. Also snails and insects.
persona3_adapted: On a stone table sits a silver plate filled with oysters, alongside fruit, a porcelain bowl, an upside-down wine glass (called a roemer), a glowing wick, and a bunch of grapes resting on a wooden box. The scene also includes snails and insects, inviting the viewer to consider the contrast between abundance and decay—a common theme in still-life paintings of this era.

CuratorialText: Painting titled 'View at the Geestbrug'. River landscape with the Trekvliet near Rijswijk, with De Binckhorst Castle in the distance on the left and the Laakmolen on the right. A woman with children stands on the road to the farm, and boats are sailing on the water.
persona3_adapted: A painting titled *View at the Geestbrug* shows a river landscape along the Trekvliet near Rijswijk. In the distance, you can see De Binckhorst Castle on the left and the Laakmolen windmill on the right. On the road leading to a farm, a woman stands with children, while boats glide across the water.

CuratorialText: The well-stocked kitchen, with Jesus with Martha and Mary in the background. In the kitchen, two maidservants are busy preparing food. One is plucking a chicken, the other is skewering a chicken. To the left, others are sitting by the hearth. In the foreground, all kinds of game and poultry, vegetables, and fruits are laid out. Among these are also various glasses, jugs, pitchers, and plates.
persona3_adapted: In a well-stocked kitchen, a biblical scene unfolds in the background: Jesus with Martha and Mary. Two maidservants are busy preparing food—one plucking a chicken, the other skewering it. To the left, others sit by the hearth. In the foreground, an abundant display of game, poultry, vegetables, and fruits is laid out, alongside various glasses, jugs, pitchers, and plates.

CuratorialText: Still life with flowers and fruit. On a plinth stands a stone vase with flowers, fruit, and vines. On the plinth is a glass, and lying are a pineapple, peaches, grapes, and a rose. On the wall hangs a painting of a woman with a flower basket handing a child a flower wreath.
persona3_adapted: On a stone pedestal sits a vase filled with an arrangement of flowers, fruit, and vines. Resting on the pedestal is a glass, while a pineapple, peaches, grapes, and a single rose lie nearby. Hanging on the wall behind them is a painting that shows a woman with a flower basket, reaching out to hand a flower wreath to a child.

CuratorialText: Six warships lie on a vast, calm sea. The sails are being hoisted, the anchors weighed. The stern of the ship on the right in the foreground is decorated with the coat of arms of Amsterdam, supported by two standing lions. This likely depicts part of a war fleet preparing for departure. A sloop with dignified figures, probably from the Admiralty, sails past the ships to the sound of trumpets and salutes, while a little further away the last provisions and crew are still being taken on board. The enormous accuracy in the rendering of details—rigging, sails, and cannons—is striking.
persona3_adapted: Six warships rest on a vast, calm sea. Crews are raising the sails and lifting the anchors. The back end of the ship on the right, in the front of the scene, displays Amsterdam's coat of arms—a design featuring two standing lions. This scene likely shows part of a war fleet getting ready to leave. A small boat carrying important-looking figures, probably officials from the Dutch navy (the Admiralty), glides past the ships as trumpets sound and cannons fire in salute. Meanwhile, a bit farther away, the last supplies and crew members are still being brought on board. What stands out is the incredible precision in how every detail is shown—the ropes, sails, and cannons are all rendered with remarkable accuracy.

CuratorialText: Still life with a hammered bronze vase containing a bouquet of flowers: tulips, roses, anemones, and marigolds. On the tabletop lie a dead frog, a cluster of berries, and a caterpillar.
persona3_adapted: A still life arrangement shows a hammered bronze vase holding a bouquet of flowers—tulips, roses, anemones, and marigolds. Resting on the tabletop are a dead frog, a cluster of berries, and a caterpillar.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Giuliano da Sangallo (c. 1445-1516). Architect, son of Francesco Giamberti da Sangallo. Bust facing right, with a landscape in the background. A compass and pen lie on the plinth at the bottom of the image. Part, with SK-C-1367, of a diptych.
persona3_adapted:
**Portrait of Giuliano da Sangallo (c. 1445–1516)**  
This portrait shows Giuliano da Sangallo, an architect and the son of Francesco Giamberti da Sangallo. He is depicted from the chest up, facing to the right, with a landscape visible behind him. On the ledge at the bottom of the image rest a compass and a pen—tools that identify his profession as a designer and builder. This work forms one half of a two-panel painting, or diptych, paired with another portrait (SK-C-1367).

CuratorialText: An old woman, probably Rembrandt's mother, Neeltgen Willemsdr van Zuydtbroeck (died 1640), presumably depicted as the prophetess Anna. A seated old woman, reading a large book lying open on her lap.
persona3_adapted: An elderly woman—likely Rembrandt's mother, Neeltgen Willemsdr van Zuydtbroeck (who died in 1640)—is shown here, probably in the role of the prophetess Anna from the Bible. She sits reading a large book that lies open on her lap.

CuratorialText: Allegorical musical group. On the left, a man in Oriental clothing and a turban plays a viola da gamba. Next to him stands a young man with a harp. A young woman sits beside a small table with an open songbook on her lap and sings. Behind her, an old woman with a cloth over her head looks on. In the foreground lie musical instruments (lute, violin) and stacks of books. On the wall is a painting of Lot's Flight from Sodom.
persona3_adapted: This musical scene is more than just a performance—it carries a hidden, symbolic meaning. On the left, a man dressed in Eastern-style clothing and a turban plays a viola da gamba, an early string instrument played between the legs. Beside him stands a young man holding a harp. A young woman sits at a small table with an open songbook on her lap, singing. Behind her, an older woman with a cloth covering her head watches the scene. In the foreground, musical instruments like a lute and a violin lie scattered alongside stacks of books. On the wall hangs a painting showing Lot's Flight from Sodom, a biblical story of escape and destruction.

CuratorialText: Venus and Adonis. The half-naked Venus and Cupid try to prevent Adonis from going hunting. Two white doves in the lower right. Adonis's quiver hangs from the tree. Adonis has two hunting dogs with him.
persona3_adapted: Venus and Adonis. The partially undressed Venus, along with Cupid, attempts to hold Adonis back from heading out to hunt. In the lower right corner, two white doves appear. Adonis’s quiver—the container for his arrows—is hanging from a tree, and he is accompanied by two hunting dogs.

CuratorialText: The Cannon Shot. A warship in calm with slack sails fires a cannon shot. Two longboats on either side, in the distance another warship with furled sails. Pendant to SK-A-1848.
persona3_adapted: The Cannon Shot depicts a warship at rest, its sails hanging loosely in calm weather, as it fires a single cannon blast. On either side of the main vessel, two longboats float nearby, while another warship with its sails tightly furled appears in the distance. This painting is a companion piece to another work in the collection, SK-A-1848, meaning the two were designed to be viewed together as a pair.

CuratorialText: The word Sufi often conjures up images of Turkish whirling dervishes, turning gracefully round themselves in a mystical ceremony. But outside the world of Islamic mysticism, not much is known of the beliefs and practices in which such ceremonies are rooted, or about the communities that embrace them.
persona3_adapted: The word "Sufi" often brings to mind images of Turkish whirling dervishes, spinning gracefully in a mystical ceremony. However, beyond this association with Islamic mysticism, little is widely understood about the beliefs and practices that underlie such rituals, or about the communities that follow them.

CuratorialText: This display will explore Sufi life and art through a selection of objects that represent Sufi communities in the Middle East, North and Sub-Saharan Africa, and northern India. This mystical movement, which first appeared in the Islamic world during the eighth century AD, has been defined as a pathway to a personal experience with God. Among the British Museum objects on display will be Islamic artefacts imbued with Sufi connotations. Some, such as alms bowls, are linked overtly to dervishes, while others have a more subtle connection, such as calligraphic works, miniature paintings and wall tiles. Sufis come from all walks of life, crossing the boundaries of class, ethnicity and regionality. The display will explore such themes as mystical love, poverty and asceticism, as well as Sufi influence upon the wider societies they belong to.
persona3_adapted: This display explores Sufi life and art through a selection of objects from Sufi communities in the Middle East, North and Sub-Saharan Africa, and northern India. Sufism is a mystical movement within Islam that first emerged in the eighth century AD. It can be understood as a spiritual path toward a personal, direct experience of God. The objects on display, drawn from the British Museum’s collection, are Islamic artifacts that carry Sufi meanings. Some, like alms bowls, are clearly linked to dervishes—members of Sufi groups known for their vows of poverty and devotion. Others have a more subtle connection, including calligraphic works, miniature paintings, and wall tiles. Sufis come from all walks of life, crossing boundaries of class, ethnicity, and region. The display explores themes such as mystical love, poverty, and asceticism (the practice of self-denial for spiritual reasons), as well as the influence Sufism has had on the wider societies in which it exists.

CuratorialText: Hawaiʻi: a kingdom crossing oceans is a dazzling celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. The exhibition brings together remarkable objects – from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved deities, to powerful shark-toothed weapons and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu travelled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this ill-fated royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture through an exploration of the complex and enduring relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations. Building on years of collaboration with Native Hawaiian artists, practitioners and scholars, the show centres on indigenous knowledge, shining new light on exceptional objects and extraordinary stories. Shaped together with Hawaiian knowledge-bearers, the exhibition showcases seldom-seen international loans alongside the remarkable collections at the British Museum – one of the largest in the world outside of Hawaiʻi.
persona3_adapted: Hawaiʻi: a kingdom crossing oceans is a dazzling celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. The exhibition brings together remarkable objects—from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved figures of gods, to powerful weapons edged with shark teeth and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu traveled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this tragic royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture by exploring the complex and lasting relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations. Built on years of collaboration with Native Hawaiian artists, practitioners, and scholars, the show centers on indigenous knowledge, offering fresh perspectives on exceptional objects and extraordinary stories. Shaped together with Hawaiian knowledge-bearers, the exhibition features rarely-seen international loans alongside the remarkable collections at the British Museum—one of the largest in the world outside of Hawaiʻi.

CuratorialText: Explore prints by Scottish poet, writer, artist and gardener Ian Hamilton Finlay (1925–2006) on the theme of revolution and its relationship to contemporary society. Finlay became known from the late 1950s for his poetry but in the 1960s his output expanded to include the production of prints and three-dimensional works of art in stone, metal, neon and glass, which he produced in collaboration with various craftspeople. He also became a dedicated gardener, developing a conceptual garden at his remote home on the edge of the Pentland Hills, southwest of Edinburgh, which incorporated many of his works of art. He named his garden Little Sparta after the ancient Greek city, a militaristic rival to Athens, reflecting his deep interest in classical history.
persona3_adapted: Explore prints by Scottish poet, writer, artist and gardener Ian Hamilton Finlay (1925–2006) on the theme of revolution and its relationship to contemporary society. Finlay first gained attention in the late 1950s for his poetry, but in the 1960s his work expanded to include prints and three-dimensional pieces made from materials like stone, metal, neon and glass—all created in collaboration with various skilled craftspeople. He also became a dedicated gardener, developing a garden that was itself a work of art at his remote home on the edge of the Pentland Hills, southwest of Edinburgh. This garden incorporated many of his artworks. He named it Little Sparta after the ancient Greek city, a militaristic rival to Athens, reflecting his deep interest in classical history.

CuratorialText: In the 1980s, Finlay's interest in the French Revolution began to dominate his work. He was fascinated by revolutions and ruptures, and how high ideals could easily lead to violence and corruption. The 12 prints in this display, made between 1984 and 1992, form part of Finlay's engagement with the French Revolution as a subject. They are from a group of 26 prints donated in 2025 by Prof Stephen Bann, an art historian who has written extensively on Finlay and who corresponded with the artist for many years.
persona3_adapted: In the 1980s, Finlay’s work became increasingly focused on the French Revolution. He was drawn to the idea of revolutions and sudden breaks with the past, and to how noble ideals could easily lead to violence and corruption. The 12 prints in this display, made between 1984 and 1992, are part of Finlay’s exploration of the French Revolution as a theme. They come from a larger set of 26 prints donated in 2025 by Professor Stephen Bann, an art historian who has written extensively about Finlay and corresponded with him over many years.

CuratorialText: In a career that lasted from the 1760s to the 1830s, Pierre Alexandre Wille (1748–1837) experienced some of the most turbulent events in French history. A pupil of Jean Baptiste Greuze (1725–1805), Wille established himself by imitating the genre scenes that won Greuze renown at the Salons of the 1760s, showing an inclination for delicacy that suited the tastes of the ancien régime.
persona3_adapted: In a career that spanned the 1760s to the 1830s, Pierre Alexandre Wille (1748–1837) lived through some of the most dramatic upheavals in French history. Trained by the well-known painter Jean Baptiste Greuze (1725–1805), Wille first gained recognition by creating scenes of everyday life—a style known as "genre scenes"—that had made Greuze famous at the official art exhibitions called the Salons in the 1760s. Wille’s work showed a preference for gentle, refined details, which matched the fashionable tastes of France’s old ruling order, the ancien régime.

CuratorialText: Yet Wille the man embraced the Revolution, even joining the republican army, and he busied himself chronicling the personalities of the day. Ultimately, however, the Revolution proved more of a challenge than an opportunity. The younger Wille had embraced a variety of styles but, in later life, he found it difficult to adapt to the more austere taste of Napoleonic France. By the time he died, he had long been reduced to poverty. During his lifetime and since, Wille was renowned more for his drawings than his paintings. The British Museum has been acquiring his work since the 19th century, and its collection has recently doubled in size following a bequest from the dealer and philanthropist Colin Clark (1935–2020). This gift included some of the finest finished drawings by Wille; one of the most powerful is the portrait which claims to show the 'Angel of Assassination' Charlotte Corday, made in her prison cell days or even hours before her execution. This celebration of Clark's bequest illustrates Wille's world through his drawings and those of his peers, revealing him to have been a talented draughtsman whose works offer a fascinating insight into French society before, during and after the Revolution.
persona3_adapted: Yet Wille the man embraced the Revolution, even joining the republican army, and he busied himself recording the notable figures of his time. Ultimately, however, the Revolution proved more of a challenge than an opportunity. The younger Wille had experimented with a variety of artistic styles but, in later life, he found it difficult to adapt to the more restrained tastes of Napoleonic France. By the time he died, he had long been reduced to poverty. During his lifetime and since, Wille was more famous for his drawings than his paintings. The British Museum has been acquiring his work since the 19th century, and its collection has recently doubled in size following a gift from the dealer and philanthropist Colin Clark (1935–2020). This gift included some of the finest completed drawings by Wille; one of the most striking is the portrait that claims to show the 'Angel of Assassination' Charlotte Corday, made in her prison cell days or even hours before her execution. This celebration of Clark's gift showcases Wille's world through his drawings and those of his peers, revealing him to have been a talented artist whose works offer a fascinating window into French society before, during, and after the Revolution.

CuratorialText: Made in England between 1340 and 1405, the Asante Ewer later travelled to West Africa. In the 1880s it was photographed in a courtyard of the royal palace of the Asantehene, king of the Asante people, in Kumasi (in present-day Ghana). During the Anglo-Asante War of 1896 it was looted by British forces and subsequently purchased by the British Museum. A second ewer, also taken in 1896 and shown in the same photograph, was presented to the Prince of Wales's Own (West Yorkshire) Regiment. This display reunites these two jugs. A major new research project into the Asante Ewer – one of the finest examples of late medieval English bronze casting – is helping to further trace the story of its journey to Ghana, and its status and significance in West Africa. Its location in the centre of the palace courtyard, in what appears to be a shrine, embedded in the roots of sacred trees, suggests it was an object of significant cultural or ritual value. Remarkable for surviving the long-distance journey from Europe to West Africa and back, the Asante Ewer has had vastly different lives, from a luxury English jug to sacred West African vessel, to colonial loot taken by British troops.
persona3_adapted:
Made in England between 1340 and 1405, the Asante Ewer later traveled to West Africa. In the 1880s, it was photographed in a courtyard of the royal palace of the Asantehene—the king of the Asante people—in Kumasi, in what is now Ghana. During the Anglo-Asante War of 1896, British forces took it as loot, and it was later purchased by the British Museum. A second jug, also taken in 1896 and visible in the same photograph, was given to the Prince of Wales's Own (West Yorkshire) Regiment. This display brings these two jugs back together.

A major new research project into the Asante Ewer—one of the finest surviving examples of late medieval English metalwork made by pouring molten bronze into a mold—is helping to trace more of its journey to Ghana, as well as its meaning and importance in West Africa. Its placement in the center of the palace courtyard, inside what appears to be a shrine and embedded in the roots of sacred trees, suggests it held significant cultural or ritual value. Remarkably, it survived the long journey from Europe to West Africa and back again. The Asante Ewer has led vastly different lives: a luxury English jug, a sacred West African vessel, and finally, colonial loot taken by British soldiers.

CuratorialText: This display forms part of a three-year research project, The age of copper, ivory and gold, supported by The British Academy and Wolfson Foundation, exploring the connections between Western Europe and West Africa during the Middle Ages and Early Modern period. It is the result of close collaboration with colleagues at the Manhyia Palace Museum in Kumasi, York Army Museum and many others across Ghana, the US and the UK.
persona3_adapted: This display is part of a three-year research project called *The Age of Copper, Ivory and Gold*, funded by The British Academy and Wolfson Foundation. The project investigates the connections between Western Europe and West Africa during the Middle Ages and the Early Modern period—roughly from the 5th to the 18th century. It was developed in close partnership with colleagues at the Manhyia Palace Museum in Kumasi, the York Army Museum, and many other institutions across Ghana, the United States, and the United Kingdom.

CuratorialText: In the 1400s and 1500s, the Low Countries were a powerhouse of artistic innovation. This was a transformative period for Netherlandish drawings, as they evolved from preparatory studies to works of art in their own right.
persona3_adapted: In the 1400s and 1500s, the region now known as the Low Countries (roughly modern-day Belgium, Netherlands, and Luxembourg) was a major center of artistic creativity. During this time, drawings in this area underwent a significant shift: they were no longer just rough sketches made in preparation for paintings or other finished works, but began to be valued as independent pieces of art in their own right.

CuratorialText: Featuring around 120 works by artists including Rogier van der Weyden, Lucas van Leyden, Pieter Bruegel the Elder and Hendrick Goltzius, this exhibition charts the development of drawing in the Low Countries (present-day Belgium, Luxembourg and the Netherlands) before 1600. The exceptional breadth and quality of the British Museum's holdings provides a unique opportunity to present a comprehensive account of drawing from this region.  Early Netherlandish drawings are extremely rare, as they were made as functional objects in the workshop and were not typically retained. This was unlike Italy or the German-speaking lands, where the taste for collecting drawings in the 1500s ensured the survival of a higher number of works from this period. Drawings were central to the design and production of works of art in different media, including tapestries, paintings, stained glass and prints – and surviving examples shed light on the creativity and collaboration of Netherlandish artists.
persona3_adapted: Featuring around 120 works by artists such as Rogier van der Weyden, Lucas van Leyden, Pieter Bruegel the Elder, and Hendrick Goltzius, this exhibition traces how drawing developed in the Low Countries (present-day Belgium, Luxembourg, and the Netherlands) before 1600. The British Museum’s collection is unusually broad and high in quality, offering a rare chance to present a thorough overview of drawing from this region. Early Netherlandish drawings are extremely scarce because they were created as practical tools within artists’ workshops and were not usually kept for posterity. This contrasts with Italy and German-speaking regions, where a growing interest in collecting drawings during the 1500s helped preserve many more works from that time. Drawings played a central role in designing and producing art in various forms—such as tapestries, paintings, stained glass, and prints—and the surviving examples reveal the creativity and collaborative spirit of Netherlandish artists.

CuratorialText: Based on a research project and supported by a grant from the International Music and Art Foundation, the exhibition highlights new findings that have come from combining curatorial and conservation expertise along with scientific analysis. New research into drawings previously only connected to Rogier van der Weyden and his circle, for instance, reveals they originate directly from Rogier's workshop and must have been drawn by his pupils as part of their training.
persona3_adapted: Based on a research project and supported by a grant from the International Music and Art Foundation, the exhibition highlights new discoveries made by combining curatorial and conservation expertise with scientific analysis. For example, new research into drawings once thought only to be connected to the artist Rogier van der Weyden and his circle—meaning his close associates or followers—now shows that these works actually come directly from his workshop. This means they were likely drawn by his students as part of their training.

CuratorialText: Be among the first to view designs for a permanent memorial to Queen Elizabeth II in central London.
persona3_adapted: Be among the first to see the proposed designs for a permanent memorial to Queen Elizabeth II, planned for a location in central London.

CuratorialText: Featuring a 10ft bronze statue of Elizabeth as a young Queen, on a 14ft plinth, the memorial in St James' Park will commemorate the late monarch and her lifelong commitment to public service. The memorial is expected to be completed in 2028. Designed by acclaimed sculptor Martin Jennings, to be sculpted in bronze, the statue will show Her Majesty in an early stage of her reign, dressed in the robes of the Order of the Garter, using a famous painting by Pietro Annigoni as inspiration.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

The memorial in St James' Park will feature a 10-foot bronze statue of Queen Elizabeth as a young monarch, standing atop a 14-foot base. Designed by the celebrated sculptor Martin Jennings, the statue is expected to be completed by 2028. It will depict the Queen early in her reign, wearing the ceremonial robes of the Order of the Garter—a prestigious British knighthood order. Jennings drew inspiration from a well-known portrait of the Queen painted by the Italian artist Pietro Annigoni. The memorial is intended to honor the late Queen and her lifelong dedication to public service.

CuratorialText: Mr Jennings will also sculpt a statue of Prince Philip, the country's longest-serving consort, at a similar age wearing a uniform of Admiral-of-the-Fleet. His statue will be located close to Queen Elizabeth's, in recognition of the support he gave her during her 70-year reign. Other elements of the memorial have been designed by Foster + Partners in collaboration with Michel Desvigne Paysagiste. The British Museum display features maquettes of the statues and a scale model of the wider memorial park. The King and Queen, other Members of the Royal Family and the Prime Minister viewed the designs at the Museum on 21 April to mark the 100th anniversary of Queen Elizabeth II's birth. The permanent memorial will also feature: a new cast-glass bridge inspired by Queen Mary's Fringe Tiara, which was worn by Queen Elizabeth on her wedding day; a bust of Her Majesty in her later years by sculptor Karen Newman; and The Commonwealth Wind Sculpture, a new abstract work by Yinka Shonibare.
persona3_adapted: Mr. Jennings will also create a statue of Prince Philip, the country's longest-serving royal spouse, shown at a similar age and dressed in the uniform of an Admiral of the Fleet. This statue will be placed near Queen Elizabeth's, to acknowledge the support he gave her during her 70-year reign. Other parts of the memorial have been designed by Foster + Partners in collaboration with Michel Desvigne Paysagiste. The display at the British Museum includes small-scale models of the statues and a miniature version of the entire memorial park. The King and Queen, other members of the Royal Family, and the Prime Minister viewed the designs at the Museum on 21 April, marking the 100th anniversary of Queen Elizabeth II's birth. The permanent memorial will also include: a new bridge made of cast glass, inspired by Queen Mary's Fringe Tiara (the headpiece Queen Elizabeth wore on her wedding day); a sculpture of Her Majesty in her later years by artist Karen Newman; and The Commonwealth Wind Sculpture, a new abstract piece by Yinka Shonibare.

CuratorialText: The design also includes gardens dedicated to the Commonwealth and to the nations of the United Kingdom, creating spaces for relaxation and reflection. The memorial has been developed in consultation with the Queen Elizabeth Memorial Committee following a design competition held last year. As well as the memorial park, recommendations made by the Queen Elizabeth Memorial Committee include a new charitable trust for regenerating community assets across the UK, and an online Digital Memorial(Opens in new window) to allow people to submit their own memories of the late Queen.
persona3_adapted: The design also includes gardens honoring the Commonwealth and the nations of the United Kingdom, offering areas for both relaxation and quiet thought. The memorial was created with input from the Queen Elizabeth Memorial Committee, following a design competition held last year. In addition to the memorial park, the committee has recommended establishing a new charitable trust to help revitalize community spaces across the UK, as well as an online Digital Memorial—accessible through a new window—where people can share their own memories of the late Queen.

CuratorialText:
Discover how people lived and died in ancient Egypt and explore their hopes and aspirations for the afterlife. 
From human remains, painted coffins, amulets, shabtis and tomb models of daily life to spectacular wall paintings and papyri; investigate what tombs, human remains and burial goods tell us about the lives and afterlives of ancient Egyptian people. The tour will explore the preparation of the deceased for burial, including mummification, the furnishing of the tomb for an eternal restful existence, and the use of magic to guide and protect people on their perilous journey to the afterlife. It will also show how funerary remains can tell us about the lives of the ancient Egyptians. This tour visits displays that include human remains. The British Museum is committed to curating human remains with care, respect and dignity. Find out more about our principles governing the holding, display and study of human remains.
persona3_adapted:
Discover how people lived and died in ancient Egypt, and learn about their beliefs and dreams for what happens after death. 

Through human remains, painted coffins, protective charms, small servant figurines called *shabtis*, and miniature models of everyday life—alongside stunning wall paintings and ancient scrolls made from papyrus—this tour explores what tombs, bodies, and burial objects reveal about the lives and afterlives of ancient Egyptians. 

You’ll see how the dead were prepared for burial, including the process of mummification (preserving the body for the next world), how tombs were furnished for an eternal, peaceful existence, and how magical practices were used to guide and protect people on their dangerous journey to the afterlife. The tour also shows how these burial remains can tell us about daily life in ancient Egypt. 

Please note that this tour includes displays of human remains. The British Museum is committed to treating these remains with care, respect, and dignity. You can learn more about our principles for holding, displaying, and studying human remains.

CuratorialText:
Discover the fascinating history of ancient Egypt and explore one of the most iconic spaces in the Museum before it opens to the public.
In the Egyptian sculpture gallery (Room 4), you can see impressive statues of kings and gods, monumental tomb architecture and ancient tomb reliefs spanning 3,000 year. These include the imposing colossal bust of Ramesses II and the Gayer-Anderson Cat. Learn about the importance of large-scale sculpture in ancient Egyptian temples, tombs and much more in this special guided tour.
persona3_adapted:
Discover the fascinating history of ancient Egypt and explore one of the Museum's most iconic spaces before it opens to the public.

In the Egyptian sculpture gallery (Room 4), you'll see impressive statues of kings and gods, massive tomb structures, and carved stone panels from ancient tombs—all spanning 3,000 years. Highlights include the towering bust of Ramesses II and the famous Gayer-Anderson Cat. This special guided tour will explain why large-scale sculpture played such a vital role in ancient Egyptian temples and tombs, and much more.

CuratorialText: Founded in 1753, the British Museum opened its doors to visitors in 1759. The Museum tells the story of human cultural achievement through a collection of collections. This room celebrates some of the collectors who, in different ways, have shaped the Museum over four centuries, along with individuals and organisations who continue to shape its future. The adjoining galleries also explore aspects of collecting. Room 1: Enlightenment, tells the story of how, in the early Museum, objects and knowledge were gathered and classified. Room 2a: The Waddesdon Bequest displays the collection of Renaissance and Baroque masterpieces left to the British Museum by Baron Ferdinand Rothschild MP at his death in 1898.
persona3_adapted: Founded in 1753, the British Museum first opened to the public in 1759. It tells the story of human cultural achievement through a collection of collections—that is, a gathering of objects assembled by different people over time. This room highlights some of the collectors who, in various ways, have shaped the Museum over four centuries, as well as the individuals and organizations who continue to influence its future. The adjoining galleries also explore different aspects of collecting. Room 1, called "Enlightenment," explains how, in the Museum’s early years, objects and knowledge were gathered and organized into categories. Room 2a, "The Waddesdon Bequest," displays the collection of masterpieces from the Renaissance and Baroque periods—roughly the 14th to 18th centuries—left to the British Museum by Baron Ferdinand Rothschild, a member of Parliament, upon his death in 1898.

CuratorialText: The British Museum has successfully raised £3.5 million, which means the Tudor Heart Pendant will remain on public display for generations to come.  In December 2019 a metal detectorist in Warwickshire found a spectacular Tudor Heart jewel associated with Henry VIII and his first wife Katherine of Aragon in the ground after centuries of rest. The Tudor Heart Pendant is unlike any object in the British Museum collection or elsewhere in the UK. This highly accomplished jewel with enamelled motifs is attached to a 75-link gold chain by a suspension clasp in the form of a hand emerging from a cloud.
