Few objects survive to tell the story of Henry's life with the devoutly Catholic Katherine, a consequence of the annulment of their marriage in 1533. Their union was the longest of all of Henry's, lasting 24 years it decisively shaped the first three decades of his reign.
persona3_adapted: The British Museum has successfully raised £3.5 million, ensuring that the Tudor Heart Pendant will remain on public display for generations to come. In December 2019, a metal detectorist in Warwickshire discovered a remarkable Tudor-era heart-shaped jewel, buried for centuries, that is linked to Henry VIII and his first wife, Katherine of Aragon. This pendant is unlike any other object in the British Museum’s collection—or anywhere else in the UK. It is a highly skilled piece of craftsmanship, featuring designs created using a technique called enameling (where colored glass powder is fused onto metal). The jewel is attached to a 75-link gold chain by a clasp shaped like a hand emerging from a cloud. Very few objects survive that tell the story of Henry’s life with the devoutly Catholic Katherine—a consequence of the annulment of their marriage in 1533. Their union was the longest of all Henry’s marriages, lasting 24 years, and it decisively shaped the first three decades of his reign.

CuratorialText: In the 18th century, the collections were divided into 'Natural and Artificial Rarities' – objects found in nature or made by people. Only a few rooms in the early Museum had manmade objects but case after case was filled with natural specimens from Sloane's collection and his library which included the 265 volumes of his herbarium. In 1735, the Swedish botanist Carl Linnaeus revolutionised the way plants, animals and other objects from the natural world were named and classified. His pupil, Daniel Solander, was a curator in the Museum and applied the system to Sloane's collections and to the natural history specimens Solander himself collected with Sir Joseph Banks on the first Cook voyage.  Many of these original specimens are included in the exhibition, as well as a number of fossils. By 1880 there were so many natural history specimens that they needed a museum of their own – the Natural History Museum in South Kensington.
persona3_adapted: In the 18th century, the collections were grouped into two categories: 'Natural and Artificial Rarities'—items found in nature or made by humans. Only a few rooms in the early Museum contained manmade objects, but case after case was filled with natural specimens from Sloane's collection, along with his library, which included the 265 volumes of his herbarium (a systematic collection of dried plants). In 1735, the Swedish botanist Carl Linnaeus transformed how plants, animals, and other natural objects were named and organized. His student, Daniel Solander, worked as a curator at the Museum and applied this system to Sloane's collections, as well as to the natural history specimens Solander himself gathered with Sir Joseph Banks during the first Cook voyage. Many of these original specimens are featured in the exhibition, alongside a number of fossils. By 1880, there were so many natural history specimens that they required their own museum—the Natural History Museum in South Kensington.

CuratorialText: Antiquaries collected books, manuscripts, drawings and artefacts from the past, and travelled the country studying ruins in order to learn more about Britain's Roman past and its earlier history. It was fairly easy to learn about medieval times because many of these objects had survived, including religious artefacts, armour, books and buildings. But antiquaries also began to survey and map Roman and earlier sites, such as Stonehenge, in a more scientific and systematic way and to learn more about the earliest Britons.  This knowledge, combined with the new study of rock strata, led archaeologists to question the accepted date of the world's creation – 4004 BC – calculated from readings of the Bible.
persona3_adapted: Antiquaries were early scholars who collected books, manuscripts, drawings, and objects from the past. They traveled across the country studying ruins to learn more about Britain’s Roman period and its earlier history. Learning about medieval times was fairly easy because many objects from that era had survived, including religious items, armor, books, and buildings. However, antiquaries also began to survey and map Roman and even older sites—such as Stonehenge—in a more scientific and organized way. This allowed them to learn more about the earliest inhabitants of Britain. This growing knowledge, combined with the emerging study of rock layers (known as rock strata), led archaeologists to question the widely accepted date of the world’s creation—4004 BC—which had been calculated from readings of the Bible.

CuratorialText: In 1824, Charles Townley's famous collection of Greek and Roman sculptures, acquired on his Grand Tours in Italy, came to the British Museum. They joined the collections of prints and drawings, gems, coins, bronzes, vases and other classical antiquities from fellow connoisseurs such as Sir William Hamilton and Richard Payne Knight. These men studied each other's collections to learn about the 'progress' of art, from 'primitive' beginnings in early civilisations to what they considered to be the height of artistic achievement – the sculpture and architecture of classical Greece. This became the standard against which to measure all art and led, in late 18th-century Britain, to a classical revival in architecture and the decorative arts.
persona3_adapted: In 1824, Charles Townley’s notable collection of Greek and Roman sculptures—acquired during his educational travels through Italy—arrived at the British Museum. There, it joined the collections of prints, drawings, gems, coins, bronze objects, vases, and other ancient artifacts from fellow enthusiasts like Sir William Hamilton and Richard Payne Knight. These men studied one another’s collections to understand what they saw as the “progress” of art: a journey from what they considered the crude or simple beginnings of early civilizations to what they believed was the peak of artistic achievement—the sculpture and architecture of classical Greece. This Greek ideal became the standard by which all other art was judged, and in late 18th-century Britain, it sparked a revival of classical styles in both architecture and decorative arts.

CuratorialText: Everything gathered in the 18th century had to be classified and organised so that it could be more easily understood and used.  King George III's library was presented to the nation by George IV and was housed in the room that is now the Enlightenment Gallery (his library is now in the British Library). The coins and medals collection was among the first part of the library to arrive at the Museum and they were arranged by country and ruler to help bring past history alive. George III was also keenly interested in the navy, navigation and scientific discoveries and collected instruments and working models. Sir William Hamilton arranged his vases found in the area around Naples according to the styles of painting, attempting to establish a chronology for them. He later published them in beautifully illustrated folios. In the early part of the century, Sir Hans Sloane filled his cabinets with curious 'artificial' (manmade) objects from around the world, including those collected from enslaved peoples in Jamaica. These objects were catalogued and displayed in many different ways – by type, material, date or purpose.
persona3_adapted: Everything collected in the 1700s had to be sorted and organized so it could be better understood and used. King George III’s library was given to the nation by his son, George IV, and was kept in the room that now serves as the Enlightenment Gallery (the library itself is now housed in the British Library). The collection of coins and medals was among the first parts of the library to arrive at the Museum, and these items were arranged by country and ruler to help bring past history to life. George III was also deeply interested in the navy, navigation, and scientific discoveries, and he gathered instruments and working models. Sir William Hamilton organized the ancient vases he found near Naples according to their painting styles, trying to establish a timeline for them. He later published these vases in beautifully illustrated large-format books. In the early part of the century, Sir Hans Sloane filled his cabinets with curious manmade objects from around the world, including items collected from enslaved peoples in Jamaica. These objects were cataloged and displayed in many different ways—by type, material, date, or purpose.

CuratorialText: The Enlightenment led to a fevered interest in deciphering the mysterious forms of Egyptian hieroglyphs and the cuneiform inscriptions found in lands described in the Bible as well as early forms of Sanskrit in India. Scholars 'cracked the codes' in the 19th century, opening up thousands of years of history. At the same time, antiquaries deciphered runes and other early forms of writing, revealing more of our own history. This was a period when significant numbers of antiquities, acquired from collectors, diplomats and through excavation, entered the British Museum.
persona3_adapted: The Enlightenment sparked an intense curiosity about unlocking the meanings behind Egyptian hieroglyphs—the picture-based writing of ancient Egypt—as well as the wedge-shaped cuneiform inscriptions found in lands mentioned in the Bible, and early forms of Sanskrit in India. In the 19th century, scholars successfully "cracked the codes" of these ancient scripts, revealing thousands of years of previously unknown history. At the same time, antiquaries—historians who study ancient objects—deciphered runes and other early writing systems, uncovering more about our own past. This era also saw a large number of ancient artifacts, acquired from collectors, diplomats, and through archaeological digs, entering the British Museum.

CuratorialText: Christian mistrust of superstition and idols, rituals and magic led to ignorance about other religions. Enlightenment scholars attempted to dispel this by studying the ancient and modern religions they encountered. They researched rituals and cults in different cultures, collected religious artefacts, grouped gods into families and examined their attributes and functions. They wanted to find similarities that might shed light on the origins of the world's religions. Much of this activity took place within the context of colonialism.
persona3_adapted: Christian distrust of superstition, idols, rituals, and magic caused widespread ignorance about other religions. Scholars during the Enlightenment period tried to correct this by studying both ancient and modern religions they came across. They investigated rituals and religious practices in different cultures, collected religious objects, organized gods into groups, and examined their characteristics and roles. Their goal was to find similarities that might help explain where the world's religions came from. Much of this work happened within the context of colonialism.

CuratorialText: In this era of colonialism and global trade, travellers, traders, scientists, explorers and diplomats created collections of ethnographic objects, including ceremonial and everyday items, thought representative of customs and cultures of people around the globe. They collected artefacts and published illustrated accounts about the lives and cultures of people around the world, from a European perspective. Alongside Sir Hans Sloane's collection, the British Museum also houses objects from the voyages of Captain James Cook and Sir Joseph Banks to Australia, New Zealand, Tahiti and other Pacific islands. A significant number of objects were acquired through employees of the East India and Royal Africa Companies, whose wealth was made possible through the use of enslaved labour and the exploitation of people and resources.
persona3_adapted: In this era of colonialism and global trade, travelers, traders, scientists, explorers, and diplomats gathered collections of objects from different cultures—both items used in ceremonies and in daily life. These objects were thought to represent the customs and ways of life of people around the world. The collectors also published illustrated accounts describing the lives and cultures of these peoples, but always from a European point of view. Alongside Sir Hans Sloane's collection, the British Museum holds objects from the voyages of Captain James Cook and Sir Joseph Banks to Australia, New Zealand, Tahiti, and other Pacific islands. A significant number of these objects were acquired through employees of the East India Company and the Royal Africa Company—companies whose wealth came from the use of enslaved labor and the exploitation of people and natural resources.

CuratorialText: During his leadership, from 336–323 BC, Alexander the Great conquered the Greek city-states, the Persian Empire and founded more than 70 cities creating an empire that stretched across three continents and covered about two million square miles.  The Hellenistic period dates from the death of Alexander the Great in 323 BC to the defeat of Cleopatra VII and Mark Antony at Actium in 31 BC. After his death, Alexander's empire was divided up into kingdoms, ruled by his generals. The major dynasties sponsored major architectural programmes and were great patrons of the arts and culture. Objects on display in Room 22 include a colossal carved column drum from the Temple of Artemis at Ephesos (one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World), the Demeter from Knidos in south-west Turkey and Greek bronzes. Displays of smaller objects show the skill of ancient goldsmiths, metalworkers, potters and coroplasts (makers of clay or terracotta figurines) and gem cutters.
persona3_adapted: During his rule, from 336 to 323 BC, Alexander the Great conquered the Greek city-states and the Persian Empire, and founded more than 70 cities. In doing so, he created an empire that stretched across three continents and covered about two million square miles. The period that followed is known as the Hellenistic era, which began with Alexander’s death in 323 BC and ended with the defeat of Cleopatra VII and Mark Antony at the Battle of Actium in 31 BC. After Alexander died, his empire was divided into kingdoms ruled by his former generals. The major ruling families of these kingdoms sponsored large-scale building projects and were generous supporters of the arts and culture. Objects on display in Room 22 include a massive carved column drum from the Temple of Artemis at Ephesos—one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World—alongside the Demeter statue from Knidos in southwestern Turkey, and Greek bronze works. Smaller displays highlight the skill of ancient goldsmiths, metalworkers, potters, and makers of clay or terracotta figurines, as well as gem cutters.

CuratorialText: The exhibition is a dazzling celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. It brings together remarkable objects – from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved deities, to powerful shark-toothed weapons and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu travelled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this ill-fated royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture through an exploration of the complex and enduring relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations.
persona3_adapted: This exhibition celebrates the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. It brings together remarkable objects—from feathered cloaks once worn by chiefs and finely carved figures of gods, to powerful weapons edged with shark teeth and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu traveled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition marks over 200 years since this tragic royal visit and explores Hawaiʻi's history and culture by examining the complex and lasting relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations.

CuratorialText: The exhibition is a dazzling celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. It brings together remarkable objects – from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved deities, to powerful shark-toothed weapons and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu travelled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this ill-fated royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture through an exploration of the complex and enduring relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations.
persona3_adapted:
This exhibition celebrates the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi, bringing together remarkable objects that tell a powerful story. You’ll see feathered cloaks once worn by chiefs, finely carved figures of gods, weapons edged with shark teeth, and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists.

The exhibition marks over 200 years since a pivotal moment: the 1824 journey of the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu across the ocean to England. That royal visit would end in tragedy, but it also marked a turning point in Hawaiʻi’s history. By exploring the complex and lasting relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations, the exhibition sheds light on Hawaiian culture and the events that shaped it.

CuratorialText: This fashion show presents contemporary clothing of the Moana-Nui-a-Kiwa (the great ocean of Kiwa/the Pacific Ocean), which has been facilitated in the UK by Interisland Collective and curated by Lyall Hakaraia. The collection has been conceived and made by Māori designer Lyall Hakaraia in collaboration with venerated practitioners from across the Pacific. These practictioners are tohunga (experts) who are building and continue to grow Pacific cultural knowledge. They consider themselves as guides and strive to be 'good ancestors' through their daily practices, powerful intentions and vision. They are building resilient futures for mokopuna (grandchildren) and generations to come. The show will act as a focal point for the Pacific diaspora community as they activate a VĀ (portal) to their ancestors. The show is an offering to those ancestors that remain at the British Museum and are embodied through Pacific collections. It aims to 'warm' them in slumber. The collective will sing, chant and offer prayers to them during the show with Aroha (love) from across the Pacific.
persona3_adapted:
This fashion show presents contemporary clothing from Moana-Nui-a-Kiwa—the great ocean of Kiwa, known in English as the Pacific Ocean. The event has been organized in the UK by Interisland Collective and curated by Lyall Hakaraia. The collection was conceived and created by Māori designer Lyall Hakaraia in collaboration with highly respected practitioners from across the Pacific. These practitioners are *tohunga* (experts) who are building and continuing to grow Pacific cultural knowledge. They see themselves as guides and strive to be 'good ancestors' through their daily practices, powerful intentions, and vision. Their work aims to build resilient futures for *mokopuna* (grandchildren) and generations to come.

The show will serve as a gathering point for the Pacific diaspora community as they activate a *VĀ*—a portal or sacred space—connecting them to their ancestors. It is an offering to those ancestors who remain at the British Museum, embodied in the museum's Pacific collections. The performance seeks to 'warm' them in their slumber. During the show, the collective will sing, chant, and offer prayers to these ancestors with *Aroha* (love) from across the Pacific.

CuratorialText: One half of the gallery presents the histories of China from 5000 BC to the present: from iconic Ming dynasty blue-and-white porcelain to delicate handscrolls, from magnificent Tang dynasty tomb figurines to modern works of art. The displays feature the richness of art and material culture in China, including painting, prints, jade, bronze, lacquer and ceramics.
persona3_adapted: One half of the gallery traces China’s history from 5000 BC to the present, showcasing a wide range of objects: from the famous blue-and-white porcelain of the Ming dynasty to delicate handscrolls (long, rolled paintings meant to be viewed section by section), from impressive Tang dynasty tomb figurines to modern artworks. The displays highlight the richness of Chinese art and material culture, including painting, prints, jade, bronze, lacquer, and ceramics.

CuratorialText: The other half of the gallery presents South Asia's many histories chronologically and by region, from early human occupation to the present. Highlights include seals from the Indus civilisation, superb south Indian sculptures of Shiva and one of the finest statues of the goddess Tara from Sri Lanka. Sophisticated paintings and objects from the courts of the Mughal emperors can be seen alongside 20th-century paintings, including by the Nobel laureate Rabindranath Tagore.
persona3_adapted: The other half of the gallery arranges South Asia’s many histories in chronological order and by region, covering the period from early human settlement to the present day. Among the standout pieces are engraved seals from the Indus Valley civilization, finely crafted stone sculptures of the Hindu god Shiva from southern India, and one of the finest statues of the goddess Tara ever produced in Sri Lanka. Highly refined paintings and decorative objects from the courts of the Mughal emperors are displayed alongside 20th-century paintings, including works by Rabindranath Tagore, who won the Nobel Prize in Literature.

CuratorialText: China is one of the world's oldest civilisations. Today it covers a vast territory the size of Europe and is home to a quarter of the world's population. China has produced a highly distinctive culture with beautifully crafted objects made on an industrial scale from the earliest times. Room 33 examines the past 7,000 years of China's history, exploring the themes of writing systems, rituals, beliefs, war, international trade and more. Room 33 also explores South Asia's history, from 1.5 million years ago to the present day. Trade, exchange, migration and political power are some of the themes examined. South Asia spans India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Nepal, the Tibetan plateau and Sri Lanka. The region is home to numerous ancient and modern languages, such as Sanskrit, Tamil, Hindi and Urdu. The Buddhist, Hindu, Jain, Sikh and Islamic faiths have inspired magnificent architecture, sculpture, painting, literature and music.
persona3_adapted: China is one of the world’s oldest civilizations. Today, it covers a vast area about the size of Europe and is home to a quarter of the world’s population. From very early times, China developed a highly distinctive culture, producing beautifully crafted objects on a large, almost industrial scale. Room 33 looks back over 7,000 years of Chinese history, exploring topics such as writing systems, rituals, beliefs, warfare, and international trade. The gallery also examines the history of South Asia, from 1.5 million years ago to the present day, focusing on themes like trade, exchange, migration, and political power. South Asia includes India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Nepal, the Tibetan plateau, and Sri Lanka. This region is home to many ancient and modern languages, including Sanskrit, Tamil, Hindi, and Urdu. Religions such as Buddhism, Hinduism, Jainism, Sikhism, and Islam have inspired magnificent works of architecture, sculpture, painting, literature, and music.

CuratorialText:
Africa's rich cultural heritage and histories are represented at the Museum both in Africa (Room 25), The Sainsbury Galleries, and across the galleries. Join our volunteer-led tours, developed in collaboration with community members, to explore their personal selection of objects ranging from the ancient world to the present day. Each guide will deliver their own personal tour exploring one of the following themes:
Out of Africa: stories of migration, materials and mythology
The Igbo worldview: artefacts from the past in the British Museum
African metal artefacts: tradition, technique and trade
Ancient objects from African kingdoms
persona3_adapted:
Africa’s rich cultural heritage and histories are represented at the Museum in several locations, including Africa (Room 25), The Sainsbury Galleries, and other galleries throughout the building. Join our volunteer-led tours, created in partnership with community members, to explore a personal selection of objects that span from ancient times to the present day. Each guide will offer their own unique tour, focusing on one of the following themes:

- **Out of Africa**: stories of migration, materials, and mythology  
- **The Igbo worldview**: historical objects from the Igbo culture at the British Museum  
- **African metal artefacts**: tradition, technique, and trade in metalworking  
- **Ancient objects from African kingdoms**

CuratorialText:
Step into spring and the month of May in this creative play session for children aged five and under.
Meet May Queens, Green Men and woodland creatures in this child-led, open-ended creative session. Stop and smell some floral scents, draw and scribble with a range of art materials and make crowns and masks to wear for spring festivities! Working at your child's pace, together we'll explore textures, colours and scents through a range of materials.
persona3_adapted:
Step into spring and the month of May in this creative play session for children aged five and under.

Meet May Queens, Green Men, and woodland creatures in this child-led, open-ended creative session. Pause to enjoy floral scents, draw and scribble with a variety of art materials, and make crowns and masks to wear for spring celebrations. Moving at your child's pace, together we'll explore textures, colors, and scents through different materials.

CuratorialText: Sennacherib was the king of Assyria from 704–681 BC and was famous for his building projects. The rooms and courtyards of his Neo-Assyrian Southwest Palace at Nineveh were decorated with a series of detailed carved stone panels. Many of these stone panels are on display in Room 9. The panels depict a variety of scenes, including the transport of huge sculptures of human-headed winged bulls (lamassu) that weigh up to 30 tons and were intended for the main entrances to the palace. These illustrations provide an insight into ancient quarrying and transport techniques, as well as Sennacherib's keen interest in his building projects. Other panels on display depict the king's military campaigns.
persona3_adapted: Sennacherib ruled Assyria from 704 to 681 BC and was well known for his ambitious construction projects. The rooms and courtyards of his Southwest Palace at Nineveh—part of the later Assyrian period—were decorated with detailed stone panels carved with scenes. Many of these panels are now on display in Room 9. They show a variety of subjects, including the transport of enormous sculptures of human-headed winged bulls, known as *lamassu*. Each of these figures weighed up to 30 tons and was intended to stand at the palace’s main entrances. These images offer a glimpse into ancient methods of quarrying and moving heavy stone, as well as Sennacherib’s personal involvement in his building projects. Other panels in the room depict the king’s military campaigns.

CuratorialText: Split across three rooms, these galleries display life during this period in Assyrian culture. On display are lion hunting reliefs, the siege of the city of Lachish and scenes from inside the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad.  Visitors to Room 10a are greeted by a pair of colossal human-headed winged bulls. Intended as a supernatural guardians, these gigantic figures guarded the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad, and were intended to deter evil of all kinds from entering.
persona3_adapted: Spread across three rooms, these galleries show what life was like during this period in Assyrian culture. You'll see stone carvings (called reliefs) depicting lion hunts, the siege of the city of Lachish, and scenes from inside the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad. As you enter Room 10a, you're greeted by a pair of enormous human-headed winged bulls. These massive figures were meant to serve as supernatural guardians, protecting King Sargon II's palace at Khorsabad and keeping all forms of evil from entering.

CuratorialText: In ancient Assyria, hunting lions was considered the sport of kings, symbolic of the ruling monarch's duty to protect and fight for his people. The sculpted reliefs in Room 10a illustrate the sporting exploits of the last great Assyrian king, Ashurbanipal (668–631 BC) and were created for his palace at Nineveh (in modern-day northern Iraq). The hunt scenes, full of tension and realism, rank among the finest achievements of Assyrian Art. They depict the release of the lions, the ensuing chase and subsequent killing.
persona3_adapted: In ancient Assyria, hunting lions was seen as the sport of kings—a powerful symbol of the ruler's duty to protect and fight for his people. The stone carvings on display in Room 10a show the hunting achievements of the last great Assyrian king, Ashurbanipal (who ruled from 668 to 631 BC). These carvings were made for his palace in Nineveh, located in what is now northern Iraq. The hunting scenes are full of tension and lifelike detail, and they are considered some of the finest examples of Assyrian art. They show the lions being released, the chase that follows, and finally the killing.

CuratorialText: Lachish was one of the chief cities of the kingdom of Judah in the southern Levant and in 701 BC it was captured by the Assyrian King Sennacherib (704–681 BC). The siege followed the refusal of Lachish to pay tribute to the Assyrian Empire (based in modern northern Iraq) and is mentioned in the Bible. Many of the relief sculptures on display in Room 10b depict the capture of the city, alongside a selection of items and weaponry used in the siege. A 'prism' inscribed with an Assyrian account of the campaign is also on show.
persona3_adapted: Lachish was one of the major cities of the kingdom of Judah, located in the southern part of the ancient Near East (roughly modern-day Israel and Palestine). In 701 BC, it was conquered by the Assyrian king Sennacherib (who ruled from 704 to 681 BC). The siege began after Lachish refused to pay tribute—essentially a forced payment or tax—to the Assyrian Empire, which was based in what is now northern Iraq. This event is also mentioned in the Bible. Many of the carved stone panels, or reliefs, on display in Room 10b show scenes from the city's capture, along with a selection of objects and weapons used during the siege. Also on view is a clay prism inscribed with the Assyrian version of the campaign.

CuratorialText: The city and palace at Khorsabad (in modern northern Iraq), was built for the Assyrian King Sargon II (721–705 BC). The palace entrances were originally dominated by pairs of colossal human-headed winged bulls, which were intended as guardians, accompanied by protective spirits with magical powers. Two of these impressive statues now stand in Room 10c, along with carvings depicting the king and crown prince, royal courtiers and hunting scenes. Inscriptions on display in the gallery come from a similar winged bull from the palace of Sennacherib (704–681 BC) at nearby Nineveh and were badly burnt when the city was destroyed in 612 BC.
persona3_adapted: The city and palace at Khorsabad, located in what is now northern Iraq, were built for the Assyrian King Sargon II, who ruled from 721 to 705 BC. The entrances to the palace were originally flanked by pairs of enormous statues—human-headed winged bulls. These figures were meant to serve as guardians, accompanied by protective spirits believed to possess magical powers. Two of these impressive statues are now displayed in Room 10c, alongside carvings that show the king, the crown prince, royal courtiers, and scenes of hunting. Inscriptions on view in the gallery come from a similar winged bull that once stood in the palace of Sennacherib (who ruled from 704 to 681 BC) at nearby Nineveh. These inscriptions were badly burned when the city was destroyed in 612 BC.

CuratorialText: Rooms 57–59 present the material culture of the region from the Neolithic farmers of the eighth millennium BC to the fall of the Neo-Babylonian Empire in 539 BC, within the context of major historical events. Objects on display illustrate the continuity of the Canaanite culture of the southern Levant throughout this period. They highlight the indigenous origins of both the Israelites and the Phoenicians. The display compares this culture with that of the people of central inland Syria, the Amorites and the Aramaeans.
persona3_adapted: Rooms 57–59 showcase the everyday and ceremonial objects made and used by people in this region, from the early farming communities of the eighth millennium BC up to the collapse of the Neo-Babylonian Empire in 539 BC. The items on display are arranged to show how the Canaanite culture of the southern Levant—an area roughly covering modern-day Israel, Palestine, Jordan, and Lebanon—remained remarkably consistent over thousands of years. These artifacts also reveal that both the Israelites and the Phoenicians had their roots in this local Canaanite tradition. The exhibition sets this culture alongside that of the Amorites and Aramaeans, peoples who lived in the inland areas of central Syria, allowing visitors to compare their ways of life.

CuratorialText: Ancient Anatolia and Urartu form an important land link between Europe and Asia. They lie where the modern Republic of Turkey, Armenia, Georgia and north-west Iran are located today.  Examples of Early Bronze Age craftsmanship on display include silver bull and cup. Business archives of Middle Bronze Age merchants illustrate trading between central Anatolia and Mesopotamia (modern Iraq). Delicate gold jewellery and figurines date from the Hittite period. Iron Age objects from Urartu, include winged bulls and griffins used to decorate furniture.
persona3_adapted: Ancient Anatolia and the kingdom of Urartu served as a vital land bridge connecting Europe and Asia, in what is now modern-day Turkey, Armenia, Georgia, and northwestern Iran. Among the objects on display from the Early Bronze Age are a silver bull and a cup, showcasing the skilled metalwork of that era. Records from Middle Bronze Age merchants—essentially ancient business archives—reveal the trade networks that linked central Anatolia with Mesopotamia, present-day Iraq. From the Hittite period come delicate gold jewelry and small human or animal figures. Objects from the Iron Age kingdom of Urartu include winged bulls and griffins, mythical creatures that were used as decorative elements on furniture.

CuratorialText: Reaching back more than 2,000 years, this exhibition explored the origins of Hindu, Jain and Buddhist sacred art in the ancient and powerful nature spirits of India, and the spread of this art beyond the subcontinent.  One of the first major exhibitions in the world to look at the early devotional art of India from a multi-faith, contemporary and global perspective, it highlighted the inspiration behind now-familiar depictions of the deities and enlightened teachers of these world religions – and how they were shared across the Indian Ocean to Southeast Asia and along the Silk Roads to East Asia.
persona3_adapted: Reaching back more than 2,000 years, this exhibition explored the origins of Hindu, Jain, and Buddhist sacred art in the ancient and powerful nature spirits of India, and how this art spread beyond the subcontinent. It was one of the first major exhibitions worldwide to examine early devotional art from India through a multi-faith, contemporary, and global lens. The show highlighted the inspiration behind now-familiar images of the deities and enlightened teachers of these world religions—and how these images were shared across the Indian Ocean to Southeast Asia and along the Silk Roads to East Asia.

CuratorialText: Colourful, multi-sensory and atmospheric, this exhibition was developed in collaboration with an advisory community panel of practising Hindus, Buddhists and Jains. These living religious traditions and their sacred art are now integral to the daily lives of almost two billion people around the world including in the UK. Key loans from our community partners helped to tell this contemporary story.
persona3_adapted: Vivid, immersive, and rich in sensory detail, this exhibition was created in partnership with an advisory panel made up of practicing Hindus, Buddhists, and Jains from the community. These living faith traditions—and the sacred art they inspire—are now a central part of daily life for nearly two billion people worldwide, including here in the UK. Important objects lent by our community partners helped bring this modern-day story to life.

CuratorialText: The exhibition showcased more than 180 objects – including sculptures, paintings, drawings and manuscripts – from the South Asian collection at the British Museum as well as generous loans from national and international partners. It highlighted provenance, examining the stories, from creation to acquisition by museums, of every object in the show.
persona3_adapted: The exhibition featured over 180 items—such as sculptures, paintings, drawings, and handwritten documents—drawn from the British Museum's South Asian collection, along with generous loans from museums and institutions in the UK and abroad. A key focus was on provenance, meaning the full history of each object, from its original creation to how it came to be owned by museums.

CuratorialText: From the symbolic footprints which preceded portrayals of the Buddha in human form to the cosmic serpents incorporated into Hindu art and the nature spirits who attend Jain enlightened teachers, this compelling exhibition told the ancient stories behind these living traditions.
persona3_adapted: From the symbolic footprints that once stood in for images of the Buddha—before artists began showing him in human form—to the cosmic serpents woven into Hindu art, and the nature spirits that accompany Jain enlightened teachers, this compelling exhibition revealed the ancient stories behind these still-living traditions.

CuratorialText: The first exhibition on Hiroshige to be held at the British Museum, and the first on the artist in London for more than a quarter of a century, this was a visually stunning portrait of a country about to change forever. Born during an unsettled time in Japan's history, Utagawa Hiroshige (1797–1858) went on to become one of the country's most talented, prolific and popular artists. As Japan confronted the encroaching outside world, Hiroshige's calm artistic vision connected with – and reassured – people at every level of society. From fashionable figures and energetic city views to remote landscapes and impressions of the natural world, Hiroshige captured many aspects of life in the Japan of his time. Stunning bird-and-flower prints reveal his poetic feeling for nature while his evocative landscapes reflected the growing interest in travel across Japan. Hiroshige portrayed his world sometimes as it was, but often the way he imagined it could be.
persona3_adapted: The first exhibition on Hiroshige at the British Museum—and the first in London in over twenty-five years—offered a visually striking portrait of a country on the verge of profound change. Born during a turbulent period in Japan’s history, Utagawa Hiroshige (1797–1858) became one of the nation’s most gifted, productive, and beloved artists. As Japan faced increasing pressure from the outside world, Hiroshige’s calm artistic vision connected with—and offered reassurance to—people across all levels of society. From fashionable figures and lively city scenes to remote landscapes and impressions of the natural world, he captured many aspects of life in the Japan of his time. Stunning prints of birds and flowers reveal his poetic sensitivity to nature, while his evocative landscapes reflect a growing fascination with travel across Japan. Hiroshige portrayed his world sometimes as it was, but often as he imagined it could be.

CuratorialText: Possessed of remarkable technical skills, both as a colourist and draftsman, Hiroshige had a sympathetic regard for people from all walks of life. Unlike most other print designers of his day, he came from a samurai family, but crossed social boundaries to devote himself to depicting popular customs. His work was affordable, too – along with celebrated landscape prints, he also designed hundreds of hand-held, disposable fans that were available to all.
persona3_adapted: A master of both color and line, Hiroshige possessed remarkable technical skill and a genuine warmth toward people from every level of society. Unlike most other print designers of his time—who came from common backgrounds—he was born into a samurai family, yet he crossed class boundaries to focus on depicting everyday customs and popular life. His work was also affordable: in addition to his famous landscape prints, he designed hundreds of hand-held, disposable fans that almost anyone could buy.

CuratorialText: The exhibition featured prints, drawings, illustrated books and paintings from the British Museum collection, as well as a significant gift and loan of prints from Alan Medaugh, a major US collector of Hiroshige's work, and other important loans. As well as exploring Hiroshige's incredible body of work, this show considered his global legacy, which spans from Japan's Edo period (1615–1868) through to Vincent van Gogh and contemporary artists such as Julian Opie.
persona3_adapted: The exhibition includes prints, drawings, illustrated books, and paintings from the British Museum’s collection, along with a major gift and loan of prints from Alan Medaugh, a prominent American collector of Hiroshige’s work, as well as other important loans. Beyond showcasing Hiroshige’s remarkable range of work, the exhibition examines his lasting influence around the world—from Japan’s Edo period (1615–1868) to artists like Vincent van Gogh and contemporary figures such as Julian Opie.

CuratorialText: A stone figure of 'Hara-Pārvatī' (Śiva seated with Pārvatī on his knee). Also known as 'Umamahesvaramurti'. Accompanied by a lion and a bull (Nandi) and surrounded by many flying and standing attendants (musicians, garland-bearers), there is also a row of ritual equipment: two conch-shell trumpets on stands, a lamp and a bell.
persona3_adapted: A stone sculpture depicts the Hindu deities Śiva and Pārvatī, with Śiva seated and Pārvatī resting on his knee. This type of image is also known as 'Umamahesvaramurti.' The couple is accompanied by a lion and a bull (Nandi), and surrounded by numerous attendants, some flying and others standing, who are shown as musicians or carrying garlands. Below them, a row of ritual objects appears: two conch-shell trumpets placed on stands, a lamp, and a bell.

CuratorialText: Pair of bride's slippers (tarkasin) made of red-dyed leather embroidered in silk thread and decorated with metal studs. Tongues cut in the shape of the khamsa ('five') or 'Hand of Fatima'.
persona3_adapted: This pair of bride’s slippers, known as *tarkasin*, is made from leather dyed red and embroidered with silk thread, with metal studs for added decoration. The tongues of the slippers are cut in the shape of the *khamsa* (meaning “five” in Arabic), also called the “Hand of Fatima”—a symbol widely used for protection and good fortune.

CuratorialText: Head and upper body of pink/grey granite monumental statue of Ramses II (one of a pair placed before the door of the Ramesseum) wearing nemes head-cloth and circlet of uraei (about half now lost), the sculptor has exploited the bichrome nature of the stone to emphasise the division between body and face; the dorsal pillar is inscribed with vertical registers of hieroglyphs - giving the name and titles of the king and part of a dedication to Amun-Ra; in 1817 it was noted that there were traces of colour upon the statue and it may have, therefore, been painted red in antiquity.
persona3_adapted: Head and upper body of a monumental statue of Ramses II, carved from pink and grey granite. This statue was originally one of a pair placed before the entrance of the Ramesseum, the king's memorial temple. The figure wears the *nemes*—the striped headcloth often associated with Egyptian royalty—and a circlet of *uraei* (sacred cobras), though about half of this crown is now missing. The sculptor made clever use of the stone's two-tone coloring to create a clear visual separation between the body and the face. On the pillar running down the statue's back, vertical columns of hieroglyphs record the king's name and titles, along with part of a dedication to the god Amun-Ra. In 1817, observers noted traces of color on the statue, suggesting it may originally have been painted red in ancient times.

CuratorialText: Head from monumental red granite statue of Amenhotep III(?).
persona3_adapted: Head from a large-scale statue of Amenhotep III(?), carved from red granite.

CuratorialText: Base and lid of the anthropoid wooden inner coffin of Hornedjitef, son of Nekhthorheb, who held a large number of priestly offices. Polychrome painted and gilded face, wig, collar and pectoral, winged scarab across breast, body inscribed with vertical registers of painted hieroglyphs, flanked by deities, the rest of the surface is unelaborated. Interior of lid decorated with a figure of Nut, surrounded by astronomical images of decan stars and constellations. Interior of base also decorated.
persona3_adapted: This is the base and lid of the inner wooden coffin of Hornedjitef, son of Nekhthorheb. Hornedjitef held many important roles as a priest. The coffin is painted in multiple colors, with the face, wig, collar, and chest piece covered in gold leaf. Across the chest is a winged scarab beetle, a symbol of rebirth. The body of the coffin is covered with vertical columns of painted hieroglyphs, flanked by gods and goddesses. The rest of the surface is left plain. The inside of the lid shows the sky goddess Nut, surrounded by images of stars and constellations used by ancient Egyptians to mark time. The inside of the base is also decorated.

CuratorialText: Brass (heavily leaded zinc-brass) head cast using the lost wax (cire perdue) technique. The head is a little under life size and is made in a naturalistic style. It has a headdress, suggesting a crown, of complex construction. The main tubular section of the crown runs around the head in a three-layer composition. The upper layer has a band of four horizontal rectangles representing flat, discoidal beads surmounted by a red-painted tubular bead and a tassel. The central layer has a row of vertical rectangles representing tubular beads with tassels. The lower layer has a row of rosettes painted in red. The main tubular section of the crown also has a projecting arc around the forehead comprising small tubular beads edged with a row of red-painted feathers. At the back of the crown is a neck cover. The central part of the cover has eighteen vertical elements incised to indicate plaitwork with traces of black paint. The bottom and sides have a row of red-painted rosettes. A crest rises above the central crown at the front. It comprises a conical roundel with central boss surrounded by seven concentric rings, probably representing beads. A plaitwork element rises behind the roundel, it terminates in a pointed ovoid tip; both elements bear traces of black paint. The face is slightly elongated and has vertical incised markings (striations). Two lines of holes run between the lobes of the ears, one going through the angle of the neck and the jaw, the second crossing the jaw below the lower lip. A double line of holes runs across the upper lip. The almond-shaped eyes are small in proportion to the head and the eyebrow-ridge is sharply defined. The lips are not striated. There are grooves round the front of the neck representing skin creases. There are holes pierced through the neck at the front and at both sides. There is a large irregular hole on the right proper side of the jaw.
persona3_adapted:
This head was cast from a type of brass that contains a high amount of lead and zinc, using a method known as lost-wax casting. In this technique, a model is first made in wax, then covered in clay or another heat-resistant material. When heated, the wax melts and drains away, leaving a hollow mold into which molten metal is poured.

Slightly smaller than life-size, the head is rendered in a naturalistic style, meaning it aims to look lifelike and true to human proportions. It wears an elaborate headdress that suggests a crown. The crown’s main band wraps around the head in three stacked layers. The top layer features a row of four horizontal rectangles that represent flat, disc-shaped beads, each topped with a red-painted tubular bead and a tassel. The middle layer has a row of vertical rectangles representing tubular beads with tassels. The bottom layer shows a row of rosettes painted in red.

Across the forehead, the crown projects forward in an arc made of small tubular beads, edged with a row of red-painted feathers. At the back, a neck cover extends downward. The center of this cover has eighteen vertical lines carved into it to suggest braided or woven work, with traces of black paint still visible. The bottom and sides of the neck cover are decorated with a row of red-painted rosettes.

At the front of the crown, a crest rises upward. It consists of a cone-shaped roundel with a central knob, surrounded by seven concentric rings—likely representing beads. Behind this roundel, a braided element rises and ends in a pointed, egg-shaped tip. Both the roundel and the braided element still show traces of black paint.

The face is slightly elongated and marked with vertical incised lines, or striations. Two lines of small holes run between the earlobes: one line passes through the angle of the neck and jaw, and the other crosses the jaw below the lower lip. A double line of holes runs across the upper lip. The eyes are almond-shaped and small compared to the size of the head, and the brow ridge is sharply defined. The lips are not marked with striations. Grooves around the front of the neck suggest skin creases. There are holes pierced through the front and both sides of the neck. On the right side of the jaw, there is a large, irregular hole.

CuratorialText: Goddess. A standing figure of a female deity (Tārā) solid cast in bronze and gilded. The figure has a high tubular coiffure (jatamukuta) held in place by a medallion flanked by makara-s (water creatures). The medallion was probably set with stones, as were the eyes. The earlobes are elongated and the lower body dressed in a tight-fitting cloth knotted at the hips. The upper body is uncovered. The proper right hand is in varadamudrā (gesture of giving), the left hand in katakahastamudrā (the hand pose wherein the tips of the fingers are applied to the thumb so as to form a ring, as if to hold a flower). The two middle fingers of the right hand are missing as are toes from both feet.
persona3_adapted:
**Goddess.**  
This is a standing figure of a female deity, known as Tārā, made from solid cast bronze and covered in gold. Her hair is styled into a tall, tube-like shape, held in place by a decorative medallion. On either side of the medallion are mythical water creatures called *makara-s*. The medallion was likely set with precious stones, as were the eyes of the figure. Her earlobes are stretched long, and her lower body is wrapped in a tight cloth, knotted at the hips. Her upper body is bare. Her right hand is held in a specific gesture known as *varadamudrā*, which symbolizes giving or blessing. Her left hand forms *katakahastamudrā*, a pose where the tips of the fingers meet the thumb to create a ring, as if holding a flower. Two middle fingers of the right hand are missing, as are some toes from both feet.

CuratorialText:
The Virgin and child: cartoon; the Virgin half-length, Christ standing with his arms around his mother
Black chalk and/or charcoal, touches of white chalk, pricked and indented for transfer, on two conjoined sheets; framed
persona3_adapted:
The Virgin and Child: a preparatory drawing; the Virgin is shown half-length, with the Christ child standing and wrapping his arms around his mother.

This artwork was created using black chalk and/or charcoal, with small touches of white chalk. The surface has been pricked and indented with tiny holes and lines—techniques used to transfer the design onto another surface, such as a canvas or panel. The drawing is made on two sheets of paper joined together, and it is presented in a frame.

CuratorialText: Marble torso of Nike, originally winged, wearing a sleeveless chiton; probably part of an akroterion.
persona3_adapted: Marble torso of Nike, the Greek goddess of victory, originally depicted with wings and wearing a sleeveless garment called a chiton. This sculpture was likely part of an akroterion—an ornamental figure placed at the peak or corners of a temple roof.

CuratorialText:
Fragment of a marble corner palmetto with bird foot.
The small fragment originates from the standing, rectangular snapped off corner palmetto above a volute acroterion. One both sides of the middle leave, which protrudes from the corner, three lightly curved and hollow shaped leaves are visible and whose edges are accompanied by a bar. The round endings were emphasised on the outer side by tubercle. Darts appear between the leaves. On the left side of the fragment remains of a curved ovolo moulding of the volute is preserved. The upper and back side show a small pentagonal block with a 45^ (degrees) outstanding, discs-like tunnel. Leaves with a convex back site seem to grow out of the tunnel. On the block and on both sides of the tunnels two claws are visible with bird legs on each the edges
On the darts on the left side traces of red paint was found on the uppermost and the third. Traces of red and blue paint.
The light crystalline marble is fairly sanded on the surface, however, the underside is fractured.
persona3_adapted:
Fragment of a marble corner ornament with a bird's foot.

This small piece comes from a standing, rectangular corner ornament that was broken off from a larger decorative element above a spiral-shaped rooftop sculpture. On both sides of the central leaf, which sticks out from the corner, you can see three gently curved and hollowed-out leaves. Each leaf is outlined by a raised line. The rounded tips of the leaves were emphasized on the outer side by small bumps. Arrow-shaped decorations appear between the leaves. On the left side of the fragment, part of a curved, egg-shaped molding from the spiral is preserved. The top and back of the piece show a small, five-sided block with a disk-shaped tunnel projecting at a 45-degree angle. Leaves with a curved back seem to grow out of this tunnel. On the block and on both sides of the tunnel, two claws are visible, each with bird legs along the edges.

On the arrow-shaped decorations on the left side, traces of red paint were found on the topmost and third ones. There are also traces of red and blue paint.

The light, crystalline marble is fairly smooth on the surface, but the underside is broken and rough.

CuratorialText: Chess piece; walrus ivory; king: bearded; wearing floriated crown, tunic, mantle and brooch; seated upon high-backed chair holding sheathed sword; chair back: two horizontal compartments separated by zigzag band; upper filled with interlaced arcading of round arches; lower filled with symmetrical foliate ornaments; chair sides decorated with interlaced patterns.
persona3_adapted: This chess piece, carved from walrus ivory, depicts a king. He has a beard and wears a crown decorated with floral patterns, a tunic, a cloak, and a brooch. Seated in a high-backed chair, he holds a sheathed sword. The back of the chair is divided into two horizontal sections separated by a zigzag band. The upper section is filled with a pattern of interlocking rounded arches, while the lower section features symmetrical leaf-like ornaments. The sides of the chair are decorated with interwoven patterns.

CuratorialText: Egyptian terracotta model palmette acroterion. The upper corner broken from a miniature altar, decorated on each side with a half-palmette. The altar was made in a multiple mould, probably of two pieces, but perhaps more. Traces of a matt red slip survive, inside and out and signs of burning within. Brown Nile silt with a light-brown core, abundant fine gold mica and some white inclusions.
persona3_adapted: This small decorative object is an Egyptian terracotta ornament shaped like a stylized fan of leaves, known as a palmette. It was originally attached to the upper corner of a miniature altar, which has since broken off. Both visible sides of the ornament are decorated with a half-palmette design. The altar itself was made using a multi-part mold, likely consisting of two pieces, though possibly more. Traces of a matte red coating, called a slip, survive on both the interior and exterior surfaces. There are also signs of burning inside the altar. The object is made from brown Nile silt clay, which has a light-brown core and contains abundant fine gold mica as well as some white inclusions.

CuratorialText: Indigo resist-dyed cloth (adire eleko); on factory-made cotton; two panels machine sewn tgether. Each panel is divided into four by seven squares. Hand-painted patterns include two squares with the pillars of Mapo Hall (in Ibadan) between which are four pestles (or spoons).
persona3_adapted: This cloth is a type of indigo-dyed fabric known as *adire eleko*, made using a resist-dyeing technique. It is sewn together from two machine-made cotton panels. Each panel is divided into a grid of four rows and seven columns. The patterns were painted by hand and include two squares featuring the pillars of Mapo Hall, a historic building in Ibadan, Nigeria. Between these pillars are four shapes that resemble pestles or spoons.

CuratorialText:
Ticket addressed to A. W. Franks to see Falero's painting 'Nightmare' at the Gainsborough Gallery, with image of a creature behind the ticket, with a bat's wing, claw and bone rib cage. 1888
Wood-engraving and letterpress
persona3_adapted:
Ticket addressed to A. W. Franks for viewing Falero's painting 'Nightmare' at the Gainsborough Gallery. The ticket features an image of a creature with a bat's wing, claw, and skeletal rib cage. 1888
Wood engraving (a technique where the design is carved into a block of wood and then printed) and letterpress (a method of printing using movable type)

CuratorialText:
Ticket to see Frith's painting 'The Railway Station', with central image of a steam train arriving at the station with figures; empty space at the top to add name. 1865
Lithograph
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This is a ticket to see William Powell Frith's painting *The Railway Station*. The artwork shows a steam train pulling into a station, surrounded by a crowd of people. The ticket itself is a lithograph—a type of print made by drawing on stone or metal with a greasy substance, then pressing ink onto paper. It was created in 1865. Notice the empty space at the top, where a visitor's name could be added.

CuratorialText:
Paper admission ticket. (whole)
Photographs of, left to right, Klimt painting, the Leopold Museum and Shiele painting; inscription in black above: multicolour print on white background.
Inscription, in black print on white background.
persona3_adapted:
Admission ticket made of paper.  
On the left side, there are photographs of a painting by Gustav Klimt, the Leopold Museum building, and a painting by Egon Schiele. Above these images, the text is printed in black on a white background, using multiple colors in the print itself.  
Below, additional text appears in black print on a white background.

CuratorialText:
Paper admission ticket. (whole)
Photograph, view of the Basilica Cistern in Istanbul showing columns and arched roof; inscription to right in black: multicolour print on pink and white background.
Unidentified historical painting showing forces gathered, tree to left: multicolour print on white background.
persona3_adapted:
Paper admission ticket. (Full ticket shown)

A photograph of the Basilica Cistern in Istanbul, capturing its rows of columns and arched ceiling. To the right, black text is printed against a pink and white background.

An unidentified historical painting, showing a gathering of military forces with a tree on the left side. This image is a multicolored print on a white background.

CuratorialText:
View of the interior of a tent with visitors gathered before Copley's huge history painting (Guildhall Art Gallery), elaborately framed with pillars and swags, including two young men at left flirting with young women, and two older men at right seated with papers, figures at a desk behind at left; facsimile of the admission ticket to the exhibition, from an engraving by Bartolozzi. c.1850s-60s
Photomechincal reproduction
persona3_adapted: This scene shows the inside of a tent where visitors are gathered in front of a massive history painting by Copley (now in the Guildhall Art Gallery). The painting is framed in an elaborate style, with pillars and decorative fabric drapes. On the left, two young men are flirting with young women, while on the right, two older men sit with papers. Behind them on the left, figures work at a desk. The image also includes a copy of the exhibition's admission ticket, based on an engraving by Bartolozzi. This reproduction dates from around the 1850s–60s and was made using a photomechanical process—a method that transfers photographs onto a printing surface.

CuratorialText:
Admission ticket to a performance organised by the Guild of Literature and Art, with figure representing the painter Richard Wilson at left, taking a painting to a pawn shop, and at right figure representing Daniel Defoe, with a manuscript of 'Robinson Crusoe' in his pocket, leaving the office of the publisher Edmund Curll with a sad expression on his face, met by his worried wife and daughter; books and pallette with paintbrushes lying in foreground; after Ward. 1851
Mixed method, including etching, aquatint and engraving, on chine collé
persona3_adapted:
This admission ticket was created for an event put on by the Guild of Literature and Art. On the left, we see a figure representing the painter Richard Wilson, shown bringing a painting to a pawn shop. On the right, the writer Daniel Defoe—with a manuscript of *Robinson Crusoe* in his pocket—is leaving the office of the publisher Edmund Curll. His worried wife and daughter meet him as he walks away with a sad expression. Books and an artist’s palette with paintbrushes lie in the foreground. The image was made after an original design by Ward, in 1851.

The print uses a combination of techniques: etching, aquatint, and engraving. It is printed on *chine collé*, a method where a thin sheet of paper is bonded to a stronger backing sheet.

CuratorialText:
Admission ticket to a performance organised by the Guild of Literature and Art, with figure representing the painter Richard Wilson at left, taking a painting to a pawn shop, and at right figure representing Daniel Defoe, with a manuscript of 'Robinson Crusoe' in his pocket, leaving the office of the publisher Edmund Curll with a sad expression on his face, met by his worried wife and daughter; books and pallette with paintbrushes lying in foreground; after Ward. 1851
Mixed method, including etching, aquatint and engraving, on chine collé
persona3_adapted:
This admission ticket was created for a performance organized by the Guild of Literature and Art. On the left side, the figure represents the painter Richard Wilson, shown bringing a painting to a pawn shop. On the right, the figure represents the author Daniel Defoe, with a manuscript of *Robinson Crusoe* in his pocket. He is shown leaving the office of the publisher Edmund Curll with a sad expression, while his worried wife and daughter meet him. Books and a painter’s palette with brushes lie in the foreground. The image was made after an earlier work by the artist Ward, in 1851.

The print was created using a combination of techniques: etching (where lines are cut into a metal plate with acid), aquatint (which creates soft, wash-like tones), and engraving (where lines are cut directly into the plate). It was printed on a type of paper called *chine collé*, where a thin, delicate sheet is layered onto a stronger backing paper.

CuratorialText:
Admission ticket to a performance organised by the Guild of Literature and Art, with figure representing the painter Richard Wilson at left, taking a painting to a pawn shop, and at right figure representing Daniel Defoe, with a manuscript of 'Robinson Crusoe' in his pocket, leaving the office of the publisher Edmund Curll with a sad expression on his face, met by his worried wife and daughter; books and pallette with paintbrushes lying in foreground; after Ward. 1851
Mixed method, including etching, aquatint and engraving, on chine collé
persona3_adapted:
This admission ticket was created for an event held by the Guild of Literature and Art. On the left side, the ticket shows the painter Richard Wilson bringing a painting to a pawn shop. On the right, the writer Daniel Defoe is shown leaving the office of his publisher, Edmund Curll, with a sad expression. Defoe has a manuscript of *Robinson Crusoe* in his pocket, and his worried wife and daughter meet him outside. Books and an artist’s palette with paintbrushes lie in the foreground. The image was made after an earlier work by the artist Ward, in 1851.

The print was created using a combination of techniques: etching (where lines are cut into a metal plate with acid), aquatint (which creates soft, wash-like tones), and engraving (where lines are cut directly into the plate). It was printed on *chine collé*, a method where a thin, delicate sheet of paper is attached to a stronger backing paper during printing.

CuratorialText:
Ticket to a special exhibition at the South Kensington Museum, with signature by James Beck; am emblem on the top left with the letters VR and a crown above. 1862
Woodcut and letterpress
persona3_adapted: Ticket to a special exhibition at the South Kensington Museum, signed by James Beck. In the top left corner, there is an emblem featuring the letters "VR" with a crown above it. 1862. This ticket was made using a woodcut (a design carved into a block of wood and then printed) combined with letterpress (a method of printing using movable type).

CuratorialText:
Advertisement for "Aspinall's Enamel"; Princess Alexandra, full length, seated to right, painting a stool with Aspinall's Enamel; illustration to "The Queen, the Lady's Newspaper" (3 December, 1887).
Wood engraving
persona3_adapted: This advertisement for "Aspinall's Enamel" shows Princess Alexandra seated, facing to the right, as she paints a stool with the product. The image was created as an illustration for the publication *The Queen, the Lady's Newspaper* on December 3, 1887. It is a wood engraving—a print made by carving the design into a block of wood, inking the raised surface, and pressing it onto paper.

CuratorialText: Large glazed and framed oil painting on canvas depicting a swirling abstract composition dominated by crimson on a dark background. The central bright design has areas of thickly applied paint while in the black background the contracting of the paint surface has produced a textured effect.
persona3_adapted: A large oil painting on canvas, framed and covered with glass, shows a swirling abstract design. The main area of the composition is dominated by a vivid crimson color against a dark background. In the bright central section, the paint has been applied in thick layers, creating a raised, textured surface. In the surrounding black areas, the paint has shrunk as it dried, producing a cracked or wrinkled effect.

CuratorialText: Painting. Oil painting on canvas. The adoration of the Śālagrāma is shown in the middle of the picture. To the left, is Balakṛṣṇa holding the butter ball. On the right, a female figure holds a parasol over a central throne which suggests an aniconic reverence to Viṣṇu, also suggested by the large conch positioned centrally in the foreground. Other ritual objects typically used in 'puja' can be seen too: a copper water-pourer and a bell.
persona3_adapted: In this oil painting on canvas, the central scene depicts the worship of the Śālagrāma—a sacred stone considered a form of the god Vishnu. On the left, we see Balakrishna, the child form of Krishna, holding a ball of butter. To the right, a female figure holds a parasol over a central throne, a way of showing reverence to Vishnu without depicting him directly. This indirect form of worship is also suggested by the large conch shell placed prominently in the foreground. Other ritual objects commonly used in Hindu worship, or *puja*, are also visible, including a copper water-pourer and a bell.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Lady Layard, wife of Henry Layard; oil painting on canvas in a carved and gilded wood frame. Lady Layard is seated on the balcony of Ca Capello, that Layards' house in Venice, overlooking the Grand Canal. She wears a closely-fitting belted red jacket and a black skirt with an enormously long train; she holds a fan in her right hand and leans on the balcony balustrade. Open French doors set with glass panels give onto an interior with potted plants; in the distance is a view across the Grand Canal.
persona3_adapted: Portrait of Lady Layard, wife of Henry Layard; an oil painting on canvas set in a carved and gilded wooden frame. Lady Layard is shown seated on the balcony of Ca' Capello, the Layards' home in Venice, looking out over the Grand Canal. She wears a fitted red jacket with a belt and a black skirt that trails into an extremely long train. In her right hand, she holds a fan, and she leans against the balcony railing. Behind her, open French doors with glass panels reveal an interior space filled with potted plants. In the distance, a view stretches across the Grand Canal.

CuratorialText: Painting in mixed media (?oil pencil) of a bearded head and shoulders of a male figure. This is one from the series 'Telingana Faces'. The figure wears a square amulet around his neck. Signed and dated by the artist. Mounted
persona3_adapted: A mixed-media painting—likely using oil pencil—depicts the head and shoulders of a bearded male figure. It belongs to a series titled *Telingana Faces*. The figure wears a square amulet around his neck. The work is signed and dated by the artist, and it is mounted for display.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Voltaire (1694-1778), T.Q.L., standing, body nearly in profile to r., head turned half r., and eyes looking full, wearing dark green coat trimmed with gold lace, and holding book in his l. hand.
persona3_adapted: This portrait shows Voltaire (1694–1778) standing, with his body turned almost completely to the right. His head is angled slightly toward the viewer, and his eyes meet ours directly. He wears a dark green coat trimmed with gold lace and holds a book in his left hand.

CuratorialText:
Painting; portrait of Se-Tan-Si-Tan, also known as Yellow Hawk a Dakota Sans Arc Chief, sitting. Wearing three feathers in his hair, ear and neck -ornaments, decorated shirt with feathers on upper sleeves and blanket wrap; holding a Pipe-tomahawk.
Oil on canvas
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This oil painting is a portrait of Se-Tan-Si-Tan, also known as Yellow Hawk, a chief of the Dakota Sans Arc people. He is shown seated, wearing three feathers in his hair, along with ear and neck ornaments. His shirt is decorated, with feathers adorning the upper sleeves, and he is wrapped in a blanket. In his hands, he holds a pipe-tomahawk—a combined ceremonial pipe and weapon.

The work is painted in oil on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Portrait of Johann Gutenberg (c. 1398-1468), H.L., turning half-r., the eyes looking full, wearing a cap, holding a wood block with the letters of the alphabet A-I.
Oil painting on canvas
persona3_adapted:
**Portrait of Johann Gutenberg (c. 1398–1468)**  
The subject is shown from the waist up, his body turned slightly to the right, while his eyes meet the viewer’s gaze directly. He wears a cap and holds a wooden block engraved with the letters A through I—a reference to his invention of movable type, which revolutionized printing in Europe.  
Oil on canvas

CuratorialText:
Portrait of Isabella Clara Eugenia of Austria (1566-1633), Infanta of Spain, half-length turned slightly to left, wearing white dress trimmed with red rosettes etc., large ruff, and jewelled headdress; after Frans Pourbus II
Oil painting on canvas
persona3_adapted: Portrait of Isabella Clara Eugenia of Austria (1566-1633), a Spanish princess, shown from the waist up and turned slightly to the left. She wears a white dress trimmed with red rosettes, a large ruffled collar, and a jeweled headdress. This painting is a copy after the original work by Frans Pourbus II, executed in oil on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Portrait of Augustus II ('The Strong') (1670-1733), King of Poland and Elector of Saxony, three-quarter length, standing, body turned half-right, head half-left, wearing armour and ermine-trimmed cloak, and holding sceptre.
Oil painting on canvas
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This painting shows Augustus II, known as "the Strong," who was both King of Poland and Elector of Saxony (a German prince with the right to vote for the Holy Roman Emperor). He lived from 1670 to 1733. In the portrait, he is shown from about the knees up, standing with his body turned slightly to the right and his head turned slightly to the left. He wears armor and a cloak trimmed with ermine—the white winter fur of a stoat, traditionally associated with royalty. He holds a scepter, a ceremonial staff that symbolizes his authority. The work is an oil painting on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Portrait said to be of Mary Tudor (1516-58) as Princess; Head and shoulders, 3/4 face, eyes looking full. Wearing ruff, earring, embroidered dress, ?chain, headdress.
Oil painting on canvas
persona3_adapted: This portrait is believed to depict Mary Tudor (1516–1558) as a princess. It shows her from the chest up, with her body turned slightly to the side while her eyes meet the viewer’s gaze directly. She wears a ruff—a stiff, pleated collar fashionable at the time—along with an earring, an elaborately embroidered dress, what appears to be a chain, and a headdress. The work is an oil painting on canvas.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Jacques (aka Jean, John, James) Rousseau (1630-93), Oval, head and shoulders turned half-r., eyes looking full, wearing dark coat and lace cravat. Oil on canvas.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This oval portrait shows Jacques Rousseau (also known as Jean, John, or James), who lived from 1630 to 1693. The painting captures him from the chest up, with his body turned slightly to the right, while his eyes meet the viewer's directly. He wears a dark coat and a lace cravat—a type of decorative neckcloth popular at the time. The work is executed in oil on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Portrait of Dr Robert G W Anderson. Head, profile, facing to sitter's right
Oil painting on panel. The subject is looking out of a window in the artist's studio at Talfourd Road, Peckham. Superimposed on the window is a section of the Sloane astrolabe. The tie is a version of the Great Court ties sold at the British Museum around the end of 2000, to mark the opening of the Great Court.
persona3_adapted: Portrait of Dr. Robert G. W. Anderson. The painting shows his head in profile, facing to the right. It is an oil painting on a wooden panel. The subject is looking out of a window in the artist's studio on Talfourd Road in Peckham. Overlaid on the window is a section of the Sloane astrolabe—an old scientific instrument used for measuring the positions of stars and planets. The tie he wears is a version of the Great Court ties that were sold at the British Museum around the end of the year 2000, to celebrate the opening of the museum's Great Court.

CuratorialText: Oil painting on canvas stretched and glued to back of stained wooden frame. Painting shows scene of an imaginary beach side village with huts and trees in the foreground, ocean and sky with full moon behind. Painting is signed.
persona3_adapted: Oil painting on canvas, which has been stretched and attached to the back of a stained wooden frame. The scene depicts an imaginary beachside village, with huts and trees in the foreground, the ocean and sky in the background, and a full moon visible behind them. The painting is signed.

CuratorialText: Oil painting; commercial sign-board made of plywood, used to advertise barbers' shop hairstyles. Six individual heads are depicted in profile, each numbered and displaying a different haircut. The heads face in different directions and are painted in bold black outlines on a white background; each head has a blue collar.
persona3_adapted: Oil painting on a plywood signboard, originally used to advertise hairstyles at a barbershop. It shows six individual heads in profile, each numbered and featuring a different haircut. The heads face in various directions and are painted with strong black outlines against a white background; each head includes a blue collar.

CuratorialText: A large oil painting on canvas depicting the slaying of the dragon by St George who is the central figure of the painting, mounted on a white horse and carrying a circular shield in one hand, a spear in the other. The dragon, with striped skin and small spotted wings, lies on its back beneath the horse's hooves, together with a little blue vanquished devil, his face in profile. To the right of the painting Biruwit is depicted in the branches of a tree; to the left stand a group of soldiers armed with spears and shields.
persona3_adapted: A large oil painting on canvas shows Saint George killing a dragon. George is the main figure in the painting, riding a white horse. He holds a round shield in one hand and a spear in the other. The dragon has striped skin and small spotted wings. It lies on its back under the horse's hooves. Next to the dragon is a small blue defeated devil, shown from the side. On the right side of the painting, a figure named Biruwit appears in the branches of a tree. On the left, a group of soldiers stand holding spears and shields.

CuratorialText: Oil painting; wooden frame painted brown; matte of white mesh; at centre is miniature oil painting on white background with brown matte around it; painting shows a matador waving a red cape in front of a bull.
