Written on back: "El Paso del Desden: 1.8 cm x 2 cm ozeo (?) s/ P. Ingres Fabr. Guillermo Calaveo Lazaro 1 217 Oct de 1985"
persona3_adapted: This small oil painting is set within a wooden frame painted brown. A white mesh matte surrounds the central image, which is a miniature oil painting on a white background with a brown border. The scene depicts a matador waving a red cape in front of a bull. On the back of the piece, an inscription reads: "El Paso del Desden: 1.8 cm x 2 cm ozeo (?) s/ P. Ingres Fabr. Guillermo Calaveo Lazaro 1 217 Oct de 1985."

CuratorialText:
Painting; pouches made from the skin of small animals; used for carrying tobacco, smoking barks and weeds. plate 13.
Oil on cardboard
persona3_adapted: This small painting on cardboard shows pouches sewn from the skin of small animals. These pouches were used to carry tobacco, as well as smoking barks and weeds. The work is labeled as plate 13, suggesting it was part of a series or study.

CuratorialText:
Painting; three Javanese men; wearing head-dress, shirt and sarong. Man at the front has a kris or sword, secured to his back with a waist wrap. House and trees are in the background.
oil on canvas
persona3_adapted: Three Javanese men are depicted in this oil painting on canvas. They wear traditional head coverings, shirts, and sarongs—a type of long cloth wrapped around the waist. The man in the foreground carries a *kris*, a distinctive Indonesian dagger or sword, secured to his back with a cloth wrapped around his waist. In the background, a house and trees are visible, setting the scene in a rural or domestic environment.

CuratorialText:
Painting; full length portrait a Javanese woman; wearing head, neck, arm ornament and designed costume. People, trees and two thatched buildings are in the background.
oil on canvas
persona3_adapted: This woman from Java is shown in a full-length portrait, wearing an intricately designed costume along with ornaments on her head, neck, and arms. In the background, we can see people, trees, and two thatched buildings. The work is painted in oil on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Painting; full length portrait of a Javanese man holding a spear; wearing a helmet, striped shirt and leggings and sarong with waist wrap. He carries a kris either side of his waist wrap and a sword hanging on his left side. Trees and buildings, one with a large flag are in the background.
oil on canvas
persona3_adapted: A full-length portrait of a Javanese man, standing with a spear in hand. He wears a helmet, a striped shirt and leggings, and a sarong—a traditional wrapped garment—secured with a waist wrap. Tucked into either side of the wrap are two *kris*, a type of ceremonial dagger with a distinctive wavy blade, and a sword hangs at his left side. In the background, trees and buildings are visible, including one structure flying a large flag. This work is painted in oil on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Painting; portrait of Wunagisa, also known as Moses Keokuk, Jr; was principal Chief of the Sacs & Fox Nation; wearing headdress with feathers, ear-rings, necklaces, one made of bear claws, shirt and a decorated blanket wrap; holding a Pipe-tomahawk and fan made of feathers.
Oil on canvas
persona3_adapted: This oil painting is a portrait of Wunagisa, also known as Moses Keokuk, Jr., who served as the principal chief of the Sac and Fox Nation. He is depicted wearing a feathered headdress, earrings, and several necklaces—one of which is made from bear claws. His clothing includes a shirt and a decorated blanket wrap. In his hands, he holds a pipe-tomahawk (a ceremonial weapon that also functioned as a smoking pipe) and a fan made of feathers.

CuratorialText:
Painting; from a collection of seventeen. An Aboriginal Australian woman from Spring Creek, near Port Fairy, Victoria; wearing head-band and possum skin cloak.
Oil on canvas.
persona3_adapted: A painting from a set of seventeen. It shows an Aboriginal Australian woman from Spring Creek, near Port Fairy, in Victoria. She is wearing a headband and a cloak made from possum skin. The work is painted in oil on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Painting; portrait of a Native North American Chief, name unknown; wearing two feathers in his hair, neck ornament, decorated frill shirt, blanket wrap and holding a Pipe-tomahawk and tobacco pouch.
Oil on canvas
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This oil painting on canvas is a portrait of a Native North American chief, though his name is not known. The artist has depicted him wearing two feathers in his hair, a neck ornament, a shirt with a decorated frill, and a blanket wrapped around him. In his hands, he holds a Pipe-tomahawk—a ceremonial object that combines a pipe for smoking with a small axe—and a tobacco pouch. The portrait captures both the dignity of the sitter and the cultural significance of the items he carries, offering a glimpse into the traditions and status of a leader from that time.

CuratorialText:
Painting; side view of a Javanese soldier holding a kris and circular shield; wearing head-dress, top garment and serong, with a kris-sheath secure to his back with his waist wrap. People, trees and the top of a building are in the background.
oil on canvas
persona3_adapted: A painting shows a Javanese soldier from the side, holding a traditional dagger called a *kris* and a circular shield. He wears a headdress, an upper garment, and a *serong*—a type of cloth draped over the body. A sheath for the *kris* is secured to his back by his waist wrap. In the background, there are people, trees, and the top of a building. The work is painted in oil on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Painting; five decorated pipe bowls featuring men, bears, and a woman; below is a decorated tomahawk pipe. plate 8.
Oil on cardboard
persona3_adapted: Five decorated pipe bowls, each carved with images of men, bears, and a woman, are arranged above a similarly decorated tomahawk pipe. This work is painted in oil on cardboard.

CuratorialText:
Framed painting; portrait of Sina Kavaliku, seated on the ground, wearing a western-style dress, her hair in plaits and a flower in her hair. Tonga. 1951.
Oil on canvas.
persona3_adapted: A framed oil painting on canvas from 1951 depicts a woman named Sina Kavaliku seated on the ground. She wears a Western-style dress, with her hair braided and a flower tucked into it. The work comes from Tonga.

CuratorialText: Pottery Protocorinthian alabastron decorated with a lion and two griffins; wheel-made; depressed-ovoid body on a rounded base; small mouth with a sloping rim; pierced lug handle at the top; made of fine buff clay and slip, with painted decoration which has fired black; also added purple paint on the animal bodies; details of the figures are incised. The main frieze shows two griffins separated by stylised tree-like motif; a lion occupies the space between the two mythical beasts; filling motifs in the field. Above the main scene is a frieze of stylised floral designs; below simpler motifs composed of dots separated by S-shaped strokes. Simple rays on the vase, and tongues on the lip.
persona3_adapted:
This small pottery container, known as an alabastron, was made in ancient Corinth using a potter's wheel. It has a rounded, egg-shaped body, a narrow mouth with a sloping rim, and a small pierced handle at the top. The vessel is made from fine, light-colored clay, coated with a thin liquid clay called slip, and then painted with a black-fired decoration. Additional purple paint was applied to the bodies of the animals, and the details of the figures were carved into the surface.

The main band of decoration shows two griffins—mythical creatures with the body of a lion and the head of an eagle—separated by a stylized, tree-like design. A lion fills the space between these two mythical beasts, and smaller decorative motifs fill the background. Above this main scene is a band of stylized floral patterns, while below are simpler designs made of dots separated by S-shaped strokes. The vase also features simple ray-like lines, and the lip is decorated with tongue-shaped patterns.

CuratorialText: IWM holds approximately 11 million photographs of modern conflict from the First World War to present day.
persona3_adapted: The IWM’s collection includes around 11 million photographs documenting modern conflict, from the First World War up to the present day.

CuratorialText: The collection covers the activities of British and Commonwealth forces and international perspectives. Photographs from official, press, and private perspectives such as soldiers, nurses and civilians who participated in or witnessed conflict reveal the human impact of war. The collection includes notable photographers such as Olive Edis, Bill Brandt and Tim Hetherington.
persona3_adapted: The collection documents the experiences of British and Commonwealth forces, as well as offering viewpoints from around the world. Photographs taken by official military sources, the press, and private individuals—including soldiers, nurses, and civilians who took part in or observed conflict—reveal the human toll of war. Among the photographers featured are notable figures such as Olive Edis, Bill Brandt, and Tim Hetherington.

CuratorialText: The photographs can be viewed alongside original documents which help us understand how and why these photographs were taken.
persona3_adapted: The photographs are displayed together with original documents that offer insight into the circumstances and motivations behind their creation.

CuratorialText:
Uniforms, Flags, Badges and Insignia
An important and international collection of flags and uniforms, the latter representing all of the armed services and institutions of not only Great Britain and the Commonwealth, but Allies as well as former enemies along with their associated badges and insignia.
persona3_adapted:
Uniforms, Flags, Badges, and Insignia  
This significant international collection includes flags and uniforms from a wide range of military and institutional sources. The uniforms represent not only the armed services and official organizations of Great Britain and the Commonwealth, but also those of Allied nations and former enemies. Each uniform is accompanied by its associated badges and insignia—the emblems, patches, or symbols that indicate rank, unit, or affiliation.

CuratorialText:
Mementoes and Miscellanea
A diverse collection containing objects of personal significance, including escape and evasion items, prisoner of war and civilian internee items, trench art, battlefield souvenirs, commemorative objects, foodstuffs, currency, tobacciana, toys and games, Kindertransport and Holocaust related items, home-front and peace-time items.
persona3_adapted:
**Mementoes and Miscellanea**  
This varied collection brings together objects that held deep personal meaning for those who owned them. It includes items used to escape or avoid capture, belongings from prisoners of war and civilians held in internment camps, and "trench art"—objects crafted by soldiers from war materials like shell casings. Also featured are souvenirs gathered from battlefields, objects made to commemorate events, preserved foodstuffs, currency, items related to tobacco use, toys and games, objects tied to the Kindertransport (the rescue of children from Nazi-occupied Europe) and the Holocaust, as well as items from life on the home front and peacetime.

CuratorialText:
Orders, Medals and Decorations
An important collection comprising orders, medal and decorations issued to service personnel and civilians.
persona3_adapted:
Orders, Medals, and Decorations  
This significant collection brings together orders, medals, and decorations awarded to both military personnel and civilians.

CuratorialText:
Equipment
An extensive collection of equipment used in twentieth and twenty-first-century warfare, including radios, cipher machines, radars, cameras, medical equipment and even pigeon baskets.
persona3_adapted:
Equipment  
A wide-ranging collection of tools and devices used in warfare from the 1900s to the present day. This includes radios, code-making and code-breaking machines, radar systems, cameras, medical supplies, and even baskets designed to carry pigeons.

CuratorialText:
Weapons and Ammunition
A major reference collection that includes firearms, edged weapons, trench clubs or concussive weapons, ordnance and munitions.
persona3_adapted:
**Weapons and Ammunition**  
This is a key reference collection, bringing together firearms, bladed weapons, trench clubs (used for blunt-force impact), and various types of artillery and ammunition.

CuratorialText:
Vehicles, Aircraft and Ships
An internationally renowned collection including models, battle tanks, maritime equipment and aircraft components. Most of the collection of artillery and large military equipment is on show at IWM London or IWM Duxford.
persona3_adapted:
**Vehicles, Aircraft and Ships**  
This internationally recognized collection brings together models, battle tanks, maritime equipment, and aircraft components. Most of the artillery and large military equipment is on display at IWM London or IWM Duxford.

CuratorialText: IWM’s Sound Collection is home to over 35,000 recordings relating to conflicts since 1914.  It is the largest oral history archive of its kind in the world, with more than 60,000 hours of material and interviews in over 15 different languages.  Beyond interviews it also includes speeches, sound effects, radio broadcasts and music. The collection covers the wartime experiences of ordinary men and women- from soldiers and activists, to factory workers and journalists. It also includes notable figures such as Field Marshal Bernard Montgomery and Vera Lynn.  Our unique collection of oral history interviews has formed the basis for significant academic research as well as featuring heavily in bestselling books, films, TV and radio broadcasts and podcasts. Many thousands of our audio recordings can be listened to via the IWM website, which is regularly updated with new additions.
persona3_adapted: IWM’s Sound Collection holds more than 35,000 recordings connected to conflicts since 1914. It is the largest archive of its kind in the world, containing over 60,000 hours of material and interviews in more than 15 languages. In addition to interviews, the collection includes speeches, sound effects, radio broadcasts, and music. It captures the wartime experiences of ordinary people—soldiers and activists, factory workers and journalists—as well as notable figures like Field Marshal Bernard Montgomery and Vera Lynn. This unique set of recorded oral histories has supported major academic research and has been widely used in bestselling books, films, TV and radio programs, and podcasts. Thousands of these audio recordings are available to listen to on the IWM website, which is regularly updated with new material.

CuratorialText:
IWM has managed a Film Archive for over a century. From its very beginnings as an institution IWM understood the importance of film as primary source material and its extensive collection illustrates the causes, course and consequences of conflict from the First World War to the present day. A remarkable resource for researchers and film-makers
Our constantly growing collection now extends to over 25,000 hours of moving images, representing a wide and diverse range of material from public and service information films, documentaries and unedited combat film, through to official newsreels and amateur films. Two substantial collections from NATO and the United Nations in the former Yugoslavia are also held. An ongoing film digitisation programme provides free online access, with newly-digitised media added as soon as it becomes available.
persona3_adapted:
IWM has been managing a Film Archive for over a hundred years. From the very start, the institution recognized that film serves as a vital primary source—a direct, firsthand record of events. This extensive collection shows the causes, course, and consequences of conflict, from the First World War to the present day. It is a remarkable resource for researchers and filmmakers alike.

Our constantly growing collection now includes more than 25,000 hours of moving images. It covers a wide and diverse range of material: public and service information films, documentaries, unedited combat footage, official newsreels, and amateur films. We also hold two substantial collections from NATO and the United Nations in the former Yugoslavia. An ongoing film digitization program provides free online access, with newly digitized media added as soon as it becomes available.

CuratorialText:
Private Papers
IWM holds over 20,000 individual collections of private papers, principally comprising unpublished diaries, letters and memoirs written primarily by British and Commonwealth servicemen and women since 1914 and by civilians mainly during the two world wars. We also hold many important documents from victims and survivors of the Holocaust.
persona3_adapted:
Private Papers

The IWM houses more than 20,000 personal collections of private papers. These are mostly unpublished diaries, letters, and memoirs written by British and Commonwealth service members since 1914, as well as by civilians, primarily during the two world wars. The archive also includes many significant documents from victims and survivors of the Holocaust.

CuratorialText:
Foreign and Related Documents
We also look after a large collection of foreign documents, including the official British records of the major war crimes trials conducted at Nuremberg and Tokyo, together with related documents from the Second World War which mainly concern the German and Japanese war efforts.
persona3_adapted:
Foreign and Related Documents

Our collection also includes a large number of documents from outside the United Kingdom. Among these are the official British records of the major war crimes trials held in Nuremberg and Tokyo, as well as related materials from the Second World War. These documents primarily focus on the German and Japanese war efforts.

CuratorialText: This exceptional collection is one of the most important representations of twentieth century British art in the world. It includes many great works of art from the British government war art schemes of the First and Second World Wars, which employed the greatest artists of their day, including leaders of the avant garde. These included Paul Nash, C R W Nevinson, John Singer Sargent and Sir William Orpen.  IWM also holds the unique War Artists Archive, which reveals the day-to-day running of the war art schemes and gives fascinating glimpses of the artists' experiences.
persona3_adapted: This exceptional collection is one of the most significant displays of twentieth-century British art anywhere in the world. It includes many outstanding works created for British government programs during the First and Second World Wars, which commissioned the leading artists of the time—including pioneers of the avant-garde, or those who pushed the boundaries of traditional art. Among them were Paul Nash, C R W Nevinson, John Singer Sargent, and Sir William Orpen. IWM also holds the unique War Artists Archive, which reveals how these war art programs operated day to day and offers fascinating insights into the artists' own experiences.

CuratorialText: Our Library collection consists of over 150,000 items covering twentieth and twenty-first century conflict involving British and Commonwealth countries. Along with material on military units and campaign histories, we have biographies, manuals, items relating to the economic, social, and cultural aspects of war, journals, newspapers, ephemera, propaganda, and e-resources.
persona3_adapted: Our Library collection holds more than 150,000 items that document conflicts from the twentieth and twenty-first centuries involving Britain and Commonwealth nations. In addition to materials about military units and the histories of specific campaigns, we offer biographies, instruction manuals, and resources that explore the economic, social, and cultural dimensions of war. The collection also includes journals, newspapers, short-lived printed materials (such as posters and pamphlets), propaganda, and digital resources.

CuratorialText: image: HMS Laurentic is seen sinking in the background, with half of the ship submerged and her bow rising up out of the water. In the foreground is a lifeboat full of sailors illuminated against the dark by a small light. Another lifeboat is visible on the right and small dots of light in the background indicate further lifeboats. In the background on the left, the top of the German U-boat is visible. HMS Laurentic, originally a P&O liner, was equipped with 6" guns in 1939 and converted into an armed merchant cruiser. She was Worsley's first ship and he remained with her until she was sunk off Foreland, Ulster by the U-boat captain Otto Kretschmer (U-99) on the night of 3 November 1940. Worsley painted the original immediatly afterwards, while on survivors leave. The WAAC bought the picture and in August 1942 sent it to South America in a touring exhibition of war art. On the way, however, the ship was torpedoed and the present picture was painted as a replacement, at the request of the WAAC. (From exhibition catalogue, Burstow Gallery, Brighton, 1995)
persona3_adapted:
On the night of November 3, 1940, the British ship HMS Laurentic was sunk off the coast of Ulster by a German U-boat commanded by Otto Kretschmer. This painting shows the moment of the sinking: in the background, half of the ship is already underwater, with its bow rising into the air. In the foreground, a lifeboat filled with sailors is lit by a small light against the darkness. Another lifeboat appears on the right, and tiny points of light in the distance suggest more lifeboats. On the left, the top of the German submarine is visible.

HMS Laurentic began its life as a passenger liner for the P&O shipping company. In 1939, it was fitted with 6-inch guns and turned into an armed merchant cruiser—a civilian ship pressed into military service. This was the first ship the artist, Worsley, served on, and he stayed with it until it was sunk.

Worsley painted the original version of this scene immediately after the event, while on leave as a survivor. The War Artists Advisory Committee (WAAC) bought the painting and, in August 1942, sent it to South America as part of a traveling exhibition of wartime art. But the ship carrying it was torpedoed, and the painting was lost. At the WAAC’s request, Worsley created this replacement version. (Source: exhibition catalogue, Burstow Gallery, Brighton, 1995)

CuratorialText: A rough for a furniture mural. whole: text is split between left, middle and right side, with separate images associated with each. image: (left) blueprint illustrating furniture with aircraft, tanks and ships in background. CuratorialText: image: A view across a tree-lined road to a high wall bordering a railway cutting. The wall has grafitti on it which reads 'Nuclear state = Police state' followed by an anarchy symbol and then 'One World'. Behind the wall to the right is a large industrial building. Further to the right is a junction with a road leading off to the left.'In the summer of 1983 ... I discovered the old train station in Mildmay Grove and started what became a set of ten drawings of the section of line between Canonbury and Kingsland Road. It was while doing the second drawing that I realized the line I was looking at was used several times a week to take nuclear waste from Sizewell [nuclear power station] to Sellafield [nuclear fuel reprocessing plant]. Over the next six weeks, I found it impossible to escape that realization, and it inextricably meshed with every mark I made.' [Eric Rimmington, from the exhibition catalogue 'A World's Waste: Cumbria, Sellafield and Nuclear Reprocessing, A Brewery Arts Centre Touring Exhibition' 1987.] The artist made this series of drawings at a time of intense anti-nuclear protests against both nuclear weapons and the nuclear power industry. The 1979 decision by the British Government to allow American Cruise Missiles to be sited in the UK sparked a series of marches and protests. These included the Greenham Common Women's Peace Camp, started in 1981 and which was at its height in 1983 when the missiles finally arrived at the Berkshire base. The accident at Three Mile Island, a nuclear power station in Pennsylvania, USA, also in 1979, heightened fears about the safety of nuclear power. Nuclear waste and its transport and storage continues to be a focus of protest and anxiety. The Greater London Assembly commissioned a report into the safety of nuclear waste trains in 2001 for this reason.
persona3_adapted:
In the summer of 1983, the artist Eric Rimmington discovered an old train station in Mildmay Grove, north London. This discovery led him to create a series of ten drawings of the railway line between Canonbury and Kingsland Road. While working on the second drawing, he realized that this same line was used several times a week to transport nuclear waste from the Sizewell nuclear power station to Sellafield, a nuclear fuel reprocessing plant. Over the next six weeks, he found this knowledge impossible to ignore, and it became deeply woven into every mark he made on paper.

The drawing shows a view across a tree-lined road toward a high wall that borders a railway cutting. The wall is covered in graffiti reading "Nuclear state = Police state," followed by an anarchy symbol and the words "One World." Behind the wall on the right stands a large industrial building, and further to the right is a junction where a road branches off to the left.

Rimmington created this series during a period of intense anti-nuclear protest. In 1979, the British government decided to allow American Cruise Missiles to be stationed in the UK, sparking widespread marches and demonstrations. That same year, the accident at Three Mile Island—a nuclear power station in Pennsylvania, USA—heightened public fears about the safety of nuclear energy. The Greenham Common Women's Peace Camp, established in 1981, reached its peak of activity in 1983, when the missiles finally arrived at the Berkshire base. Concerns about nuclear waste, including its transport and storage, remain a focus of protest and anxiety today. For this reason, the Greater London Assembly commissioned a report on the safety of nuclear waste trains as recently as 2001.

CuratorialText:
Object description
image: Five children are seated around a table in room lit by a large window. An elderly woman sits at the centre with the youngest child on her lap. A large 'Spotted-Dick' pudding is placed on the table with several bowls and cutlery set around it. The woman serves a boy on the far right who holds out a bowl for his portion.
Label
'Mrs Norris, of Cookham, with five of her war family of seven children from London who had by January of 1941 been with her for sixteen months. Mrs Norris is the ideal foster-parent, and there are very many other householders like her. Over a million children have been moved in school parties from the evacuation areas, and with such large numbers, boarding out in billets must always be the principal means of finding homes for them. Given Mrs Norris' friendliness and care, town children readily adapt themselves to their new surroundings.' (title from folder cover, Children in Wartime).
persona3_adapted:
**Label**

This photograph shows Mrs. Norris of Cookham, seated at the center with the youngest of her seven foster children on her lap. The children, who were evacuated from London as part of the wartime relocation program, gather around a table in a sunlit room. On the table sits a large "Spotted-Dick" pudding—a traditional British steamed dessert—along with several bowls and utensils. Mrs. Norris is serving a boy on the far right, who holds out his bowl for his portion.

The original caption, taken from the folder cover titled *Children in Wartime*, explains that by January 1941, these children had been living with Mrs. Norris for sixteen months. It describes her as the ideal foster parent, one of many householders who took in evacuated children. Over a million children had been moved from evacuation areas in school-organized groups, and with such large numbers, placing them in private homes—known as billeting—was the main way to find them shelter. The caption notes that, given Mrs. Norris's friendliness and care, city children readily adapted to their new surroundings.

CuratorialText: image: portrait of a female factory worker operating a lathe. Miss Loftus had been brought to the attention of the War Artist's Advisory Committee as 'an outstanding factory worker'. Knight expected to do a studio portrait but the Ministry of Supply requested that she be painted at work in the Royal Ordnance Factory in Newport. Making a Bofors Breech ring was considered the most highly skilled job in the factory, normally requiring eight or nine years training. Loftus was aged 21 at the time of the painting and had no previous factory experience. Her ability to operate the machine presented a considerable publicity coup at the time. However, it has been suggested that she was placed at the machine precisely for this reason. Industrial machinery was a wholly new element in Knight's work but her technical accuracy was praised in contemporary reports: Knight, like Loftus, was proving herself in a traditionally male environment. Loftus was an outstanding factory worker who had mastered complex engineering skills in a very short space of time, and Knight was commissioned to paint her at work in the factory. Knight was fascinated by circus artistes and dancers, and she emphasises the balance and posture of her subject at work. Industrial machinery was a wholly new element in Knight's work but her technical accuracy was praised in contemporary reports: Knight, like Loftus, was proving herself in a traditionally male environment.
persona3_adapted:
In this portrait, we see a young woman named Miss Loftus operating a lathe—a machine used for shaping metal. She had been brought to the attention of the War Artist’s Advisory Committee as an exceptionally skilled factory worker. The artist, Laura Knight, initially planned to paint a formal studio portrait, but the Ministry of Supply requested that Loftus be shown at work in the Royal Ordnance Factory in Newport. 

At the time, making a Bofors breech ring—a component for an anti-aircraft gun—was considered the most highly skilled job in the factory, normally requiring eight or nine years of training. Loftus was just 21 years old and had no previous factory experience. Her ability to operate the machine was a major public relations success for the war effort. However, some have suggested that she may have been placed at the machine specifically for this reason—to create a powerful image of women’s capabilities in wartime industry.

Industrial machinery was a completely new subject for Knight, who was better known for painting circus performers and dancers. Yet contemporary reports praised the technical accuracy of her work. In taking on this challenge, Knight, like Loftus, was proving herself in a field traditionally dominated by men. Knight’s interest in the physical grace of performers is evident here: she emphasizes the balance and posture of her subject as she works, turning a factory scene into a study of skill and poise.

CuratorialText: image: A view along a platform at Charing Cross station. In the lower left there are several wounded soldiers lying on the stretchers that are placed on the ground. They are attended by a nurse and there are uniformed Order of St John personnel, standing on the platform, and lifting stretchers from the train. In the centre there are a group of soldiers in overcoats, several with bandaged limbs. The top half of the composition shows the roof of the station filled with steam and smoke from the train below.
persona3_adapted: A view along a platform at Charing Cross station. In the lower left, several wounded soldiers lie on stretchers placed on the ground. A nurse attends to them, while uniformed members of the Order of St John stand on the platform and lift stretchers from the train. In the center, a group of soldiers in overcoats includes several with bandaged limbs. The upper half of the scene shows the station roof filled with steam and smoke rising from the train below.

CuratorialText:
image: a group of Indian infantrymen, all wearing distinctive Sikh turbans, dismantle a strongpoint near Pozières on the Western Front. The strongpoint consists of earth, brick, sandbags and corrugated iron and is the prominent feature in a flat, largely desolate and battle scarred landscape.
History note
Imperial War Museum commission
persona3_adapted:
A photograph shows a group of Indian infantrymen, identifiable by their distinctive Sikh turbans, taking apart a fortified position near Pozières on the Western Front. The strongpoint—made of earth, brick, sandbags, and corrugated iron—stands out sharply in a flat, largely barren landscape scarred by battle.

This image was produced as part of a commission by the Imperial War Museum.

CuratorialText: image: A view along a platform at Charing Cross station. In the lower left there are several wounded soldiers lying on the stretchers that are placed on the ground. They are attended by a nurse and there are uniformed Order of St John personnel, standing on the platform, and lifting stretchers from the train. In the centre there are a group of soldiers in overcoats, several with bandaged limbs. The top half of the composition shows the roof of the station filled with steam and smoke from the train below.
persona3_adapted: A view along a platform at Charing Cross station. In the lower left, several wounded soldiers lie on stretchers placed on the ground. A nurse attends to them, while uniformed members of the Order of St John stand on the platform, lifting more stretchers from the train. In the center, a group of soldiers in overcoats includes several with bandaged limbs. The upper half of the scene shows the station roof filled with steam and smoke rising from the train below.

CuratorialText: image: A half length portrait of a British officer wearing a red dress uniform jacket with seven medals on his chest, including the DSO and OBE. He also wears the insignia of the Order of the Indian Empire around his neck.
persona3_adapted: A half-length portrait shows a British officer in a red dress uniform jacket. On his chest are seven medals, including the Distinguished Service Order (DSO) and the Order of the British Empire (OBE). Around his neck, he wears the insignia of the Order of the Indian Empire, a mark of high honor in Britain's colonial administration.

CuratorialText:
image: a skeletal Death drives a bulldozer through the rubble, debris and detritus of war. Bricks, metal struts and tyres litter the ground and there is a flaming object on the right.
Label
This is a fantasy of utter destruction, as death rides in on a bulldozer. Only the flags stand tall, criss-crossing but ultimately adding to the confusion. The only architectural remains are bricks and girders. Michael Sandle is primarily a sculptor and has produced a number of public monuments. A key theme of his work is warfare, influenced by his early experiences of air raids in Plymouth and subsequent National Service. This is one of a series of six prints entitled 'The Driver'.
persona3_adapted:
In this imagined scene of total devastation, a skeletal figure representing Death drives a bulldozer through the wreckage of war. The ground is littered with bricks, metal beams, and tires, while a flaming object burns on the right. Only flags remain standing, crossing each other but only adding to the chaos. The few surviving structures are reduced to basic materials like bricks and girders.

Michael Sandle works primarily as a sculptor and has created several public monuments. A central theme in his art is warfare, shaped by his early memories of air raids in Plymouth and his later time in National Service. This print is part of a series of six called 'The Driver'.

CuratorialText: image: a view through a few trees across the large field of crosses of the British military cemetery at Etaples on the French coast. The coastline is bordered by trees and a sailing boat is visible in the English Channel.
persona3_adapted: A view through a scattering of trees across the vast field of crosses in the British military cemetery at Étaples, on the French coast. The shoreline is lined with trees, and a sailing boat can be seen on the English Channel.

CuratorialText:
image: The Royal Navy destroyer HMS Eclipse in the process of sinking in the Aegean Sea. The ship appears to have broken in two, with only the stern and bow sections above water. The bow section is on fire, with a bright cloud of flames and smoke billowing up into the night sky. There is another ship in the background and two Greek islands are visible to the left and right.
Label
On 23 October 1943 the two destroyers HMS Eclipse and HMS Petard sailed along the Turkish coast bound for Leros, one of the Greek Dodecanese Islands, which was occupied by a British garrison. On board the Eclipse was Brigadier-General George Davy, the Director of Military Operations, Middle East, who was enroute to inspect the defences of Leros. The ships were also carrying troops, ammunition and supplies for the garrison. At 11.45pm the Eclipse struck a mine between Kos and Kalymnos, blew up and sank in only two minutes. In addition to the ship's complement of around 200 men, she was carrying another 200 soldiers, including a company of the Buffs. Only around 100 men were picked up by an air-sea rescue launch of the RAF dispatched from Leros and boats sent from HMS Petard, after spending over three hours in the water. Davy was amongst the survivors. He went onto become the Commander of Land Forces, Adriatic, retiring from the Army in 1949. He recorded this traumatic event 24 years after it had occurred.
persona3_adapted:
On the night of October 23, 1943, two British destroyers—the HMS *Eclipse* and HMS *Petard*—were sailing along the Turkish coast toward the Greek island of Leros, which was then held by a British garrison. Aboard the *Eclipse* was Brigadier-General George Davy, a high-ranking military official traveling to inspect the island’s defenses. The ships were also carrying troops, ammunition, and supplies.

At 11:45 p.m., the *Eclipse* struck a mine in the sea between the islands of Kos and Kalymnos. The explosion tore the ship apart, and it sank in just two minutes. The vessel had a crew of about 200 men, plus another 200 soldiers—including a company from the British regiment known as the Buffs. Only about 100 people survived. They were rescued by an RAF air-sea rescue launch sent from Leros and by boats from the *Petard*, after spending more than three hours in the water.

Brigadier-General Davy was among the survivors. He later became Commander of Land Forces in the Adriatic region and retired from the army in 1949. He recorded this traumatic event 24 years after it happened.

CuratorialText: image: two soldiers lie side by side on the floor of a tent with their heads resting on khaki bags. Both are wearing dessert gear. The soldier furthest away is reading, but the man nearest the artist has fallen asleep with his book resting on his chest.
persona3_adapted: Two soldiers lie side by side on the floor of a tent, their heads resting on khaki bags. Both are dressed in desert gear. The soldier farther away is reading, while the man closest to the artist has fallen asleep, his book resting on his chest.

CuratorialText:
image: A view across the river Spree with the sun rising over the city in the background. There are fortifications and an observation post on the opposite bank with a vessel moored below, and a British Military policeman with a vehicle on the near side.
History note
Artistic Records Committee Commission, December 1980
persona3_adapted:
A view across the River Spree at sunrise, with the city glowing in the background. On the far bank stand fortifications and an observation post; below them, a boat is moored. In the foreground, a British military police officer stands beside a vehicle.

Historical note: This work was commissioned by the Artistic Records Committee in December 1980.

CuratorialText: image: An abstract composition of coiled and twisted girders of all sizes. This drawing explores the distortions made by bombs to building structures. The violence of the blast is evident in the rhythm and the form of the girders. Paule Vézelay was born in Bristol and returned there during the Second World War after over 10 years as an artist in Paris. She never gained an official war artist commission due to her left wing associations and the abstract nature of her work. However with the help of Bristol Museum and Art Gallery she gained a permit to draw the ruins in Bristol.
persona3_adapted: This drawing shows a tangle of twisted metal beams, all different sizes, coiled together in an abstract way. It captures the damage bombs caused to buildings during the war. The violent force of the explosion comes through in the shapes and patterns of the beams. Paule Vézelay was born in Bristol and returned there during the Second World War, after spending more than a decade working as an artist in Paris. She never received an official commission as a war artist—likely because of her left-wing political views and the fact that her work was abstract, not realistic. However, with help from Bristol Museum and Art Gallery, she was able to get a permit to draw the city’s bombed ruins.

CuratorialText: image: In the foreground two sailors sit with their backs to the viewer on the deck of a ship. A third sailor stands behind them leaning back with his hands in his pockets. A gun protrudes out above them. They are watching two ships anchored ahead of them and clouds of smoke rising from the hills in the distance. The sky is dotted with barrage balloons. The artist has also made several other sketches of ships and sailors in the upper right corner of the image.
persona3_adapted: In the foreground, two sailors sit with their backs to us on the deck of a ship. A third sailor stands behind them, leaning back with his hands in his pockets. A gun extends out above them. They are watching two ships anchored ahead, while clouds of smoke rise from the hills in the distance. The sky is dotted with barrage balloons—large, tethered balloons used to defend against enemy aircraft. In the upper right corner of the image, the artist has also added several other sketches of ships and sailors.

CuratorialText: image: British soldiers are in action on a German street, crouching and firing their guns among the rubble of bombed buildings. Two soldiers are lying on a Nazi flag bearing a swastika, the left of the two in the process of firing a Bren Gun. Four other infantrymen stand directly behind them to the left, two armed with rifles and one with a Bren Gun. Other British soldiers are visible in the middle distance.
persona3_adapted: British soldiers are shown in combat on a German street, crouching and firing their weapons amid the rubble of bombed buildings. Two soldiers lie on a Nazi flag marked with a swastika; the one on the left is in the middle of firing a Bren Gun—a type of light machine gun used by British forces. Directly behind them to the left stand four other infantrymen, two carrying rifles and one another Bren Gun. More British soldiers can be seen in the middle distance.

CuratorialText: image: two British Royal Army Medical Corps stretcher-bearers stand with their stretchers in a shallow trench in the foreground. The trench leads up to the entrance of an Advanced Dressing Station, marked with an ADS sign, which is housed in the bomb damaged remains of a large house in a ruined village. Other heavily bomb damaged buildings are visible in the background.
persona3_adapted: Two stretcher-bearers from the British Royal Army Medical Corps stand in a shallow trench in the foreground, holding their stretchers. The trench leads to the entrance of an Advanced Dressing Station—marked by an "ADS" sign—which is set up inside the bomb-damaged remains of a large house in a ruined village. In the background, other heavily damaged buildings are visible, emphasizing the destruction of the surrounding area.

CuratorialText:
image: the interior of the designers' room in the Old Vic Theatre, Waterloo, London, after suffering the effects of an air raid. The windows have been blown out and internal walls damaged, with the room littered with disturbed papers, designs and a fallen mannequin. The window looks out roughly south towards Lambeth, with the roofs of buildings visible.
Label
Short text of working notes. Inscription verso reads: 'Designers Room - Old Vic Theatre September 1941'.
persona3_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This photograph captures the interior of the designers' room at the Old Vic Theatre in Waterloo, London, following the damage caused by an air raid. The windows have been blown out, internal walls are damaged, and the room is strewn with scattered papers, design materials, and a fallen mannequin. Through the window, the view looks roughly south toward Lambeth, where the rooftops of nearby buildings are visible.

The label consists of brief working notes, while an inscription on the back of the image reads: "Designers Room - Old Vic Theatre September 1941."

CuratorialText: image: Against a gloomy sunset and upon a grassy mound stands a smoking but unmanned French 75mm field gun, facing left. Below running the length of the composition lies the detritus of battle: a broken limber, four German corpses and two dead mules.
persona3_adapted: Against a dark, sunset-lit sky, a French 75mm field gun sits abandoned on a grassy hill, still smoking but with no crew in sight, its barrel pointing to the left. Stretching across the bottom of the scene are the scattered remains of battle: a shattered ammunition cart, four dead German soldiers, and two dead mules.

CuratorialText: image: A convoy at sea being bombarded by enemy aircraft. In the centre of the composition is a large tanker, which has black smoke emanating from it. Other ships can be seen to the left and right with aircraft visible above. Large splashes of water in the sea show several near-misses.
persona3_adapted: A convoy at sea comes under attack from enemy aircraft. At the center of the composition—the way the scene is arranged—a large tanker billows black smoke. Smaller ships flank it on both sides, while aircraft hover overhead. Large splashes in the water show several near-misses, where bombs landed dangerously close but not directly on target.

CuratorialText: Powder paint painting, hinged in corners onto a single sheet of white board. Bottom left hinge has previously been cut to allow access to object number. Neatly torn along top and right edges, rather than cut. Previously folded in half vertically.
persona3_adapted: This painting was made using powdered pigment mixed with a binder (a technique often called powder paint). It is attached at the corners with small hinges to a single sheet of white board. The hinge at the bottom left was cut at some point in the past, likely to reveal the object’s identification number. The top and right edges appear to have been torn neatly rather than cut. The work was also once folded vertically down the middle.

CuratorialText: image: eight internees stand behind a barbed wire fence at Hutchinson camp near Douglas on the Isle of Man. The word 'Parachutists' is on the wall behind them. Four of the men wear glasses and all but one of them appear to be wearing kippot, indicating their Jewish faith. The other man wears a bowler-style hat.
persona3_adapted: Eight internees are shown standing behind a barbed wire fence at the Hutchinson camp near Douglas on the Isle of Man. Behind them, the word 'Parachutists' is painted on a wall. Four of the men wear glasses, and all but one appear to be wearing kippot—the small, brimless caps traditionally worn by Jewish men as a sign of their faith. The remaining man wears a bowler-style hat.

CuratorialText: Acknowledged as one of the greatest landscape artists of all time, Joseph Mallord William Turner (1775–1851) knew Oxford extremely well. From childhood, he made numerous drawings and watercolours of the streets and colleges and the picturesque views surrounding the city. His celebrated oil painting of the High Street, Oxford is on display in the 19th-Century Art Gallery. To mark the 250th anniversary of J.M.W. Turner's birth on 23 April 1775, explore this selection of his depictions of Oxford from the Ashmolean's collections. The Museum's Western Art Print Room houses just over 100 watercolours and drawings by Turner, as well as many landscape etchings. There are lively studies of animals, birds and fish, as well as evocative views of places that the artist visited on his travels in Britain and Europe, from Margate to Venice.
persona3_adapted: Widely regarded as one of the greatest painters of landscapes in history, Joseph Mallord William Turner (1775–1851) knew Oxford extremely well. From childhood, he made numerous drawings and watercolours—paintings using water-based pigments—of the city's streets, colleges, and the charming countryside views around it. His famous oil painting of Oxford's High Street is now on display in the 19th-Century Art Gallery. To celebrate the 250th anniversary of Turner's birth on 23 April 1775, explore this selection of his depictions of Oxford from the Ashmolean's collections. The Museum's Western Art Print Room holds just over 100 watercolours and drawings by Turner, along with many landscape etchings—prints made by carving a design into a metal plate. These works include lively studies of animals, birds, and fish, as well as evocative views of places the artist visited during his travels across Britain and Europe, from Margate to Venice.

CuratorialText: In the second millennium BCE the island of Crete was home to a complex pre-Greek society known as ‘the Minoans’. When archaeologist Sir Arthur Evans started to excavate at the site of Knossos on Crete, in 1900, he frequently connected the things he found with ancient Cretan myths. He named the building at Knossos the ‘Palace of Minos’, after the mythical king of Crete, and connected it to the story of the Labyrinth. The Labyrinth was an ingenious maze commissioned by King Minos to imprison the ferocious Minotaur, a creature with the head of a bull and the body of a man. In the myth, the Minotaur is killed by Theseus, the famous semi-divine hero. Following Theseus's victory, he returned to Greece, and became king and the founder of Athens. The Cretan myths originated in stories which the Greek and Roman inhabitants of the city of Knossos would have told long after the Palace had been destroyed.
persona3_adapted: In the second millennium BCE—roughly 4,000 years ago—the island of Crete was home to a sophisticated pre-Greek society that archaeologists call 'the Minoans.' When the British archaeologist Sir Arthur Evans began digging at the site of Knossos on Crete in 1900, he often linked the objects he uncovered to ancient Cretan myths. He named the large building he discovered the 'Palace of Minos,' after the legendary king of Crete, and connected it to the story of the Labyrinth. According to the myth, the Labyrinth was a cleverly designed maze that King Minos ordered to be built in order to imprison the terrifying Minotaur—a creature with the head of a bull and the body of a man. In the story, the Minotaur is killed by Theseus, a famous hero who was said to be partly divine. After his victory, Theseus returned to Greece, became king, and was later regarded as the founder of Athens. These Cretan myths originated in stories that the Greek and Roman inhabitants of Knossos would have told long after the Palace had fallen into ruin.

CuratorialText:
Landscape painting has a long tradition in East Asian art. However, landscape did not become an important feature of Japanese woodblock prints until the early 1800s. By this time, Japanese citizens were taking part in more outdoor sightseeing activities, such as temple visits and boat trips, and there was a growing demand for souvenir images. In the competitive world of the publishing industry, publishers were constantly trying to develop new products; landscape prints were an appealing novelty.

Technical factors also played an important part in the emergence of the landscape print. Particularly significant was the introduction of the new imported pigment Prussian Blue. Unlike earlier Japanese blues, which faded very quickly, this brilliant colour gave artists more freedom of expression, especially in the depiction of sky and water

Here we feature a selection of highlights from the master of landscape prints, Utagawa Hiroshige, as well as selected works by Hokusai and Kuniyoshi.
persona3_adapted:
Landscape painting has a long history in East Asian art. However, it wasn't until the early 1800s that landscapes became a major subject in Japanese woodblock prints. Around this time, more Japanese people were enjoying outdoor sightseeing—like visiting temples or taking boat trips—and there was a growing demand for souvenir images. In the competitive world of publishing, companies were always looking for new ideas, and landscape prints offered an appealing novelty.

Technical developments also helped make landscape prints possible. One key factor was the introduction of a new blue pigment imported from abroad, called Prussian Blue. Unlike earlier Japanese blues, which faded quickly, this vivid color gave artists more freedom to express themselves, especially when depicting sky and water.

Here we feature a selection of highlights from Utagawa Hiroshige, a master of landscape prints, along with selected works by Hokusai and Kuniyoshi.

CuratorialText:
The collection of antiquities includes internationally renowned archaeological collections from Prehistoric Europe, the Ancient Near East, Ancient Egypt and Sudan, Minoan Aegean, Mycenaean and Classical Greece, Ancient Cyprus, the Roman world, Medieval and later Europe, as well as the museum's Founding Collection and historic archives.

It also includes one of the oldest, largest and best-preserved collections of casts of Greek and Roman sculpture in the UK.
persona3_adapted:
The collection of antiquities spans internationally celebrated archaeological finds from prehistoric Europe, the ancient Near East, ancient Egypt and Sudan, the Minoan and Mycenaean cultures of the Aegean, classical Greece, ancient Cyprus, the Roman world, medieval and later Europe, as well as the museum's original founding collection and historical archives.

It also holds one of the oldest, largest, and best-preserved collections in the UK of plaster casts—reproductions made from original Greek and Roman sculptures.

CuratorialText: In our Eastern Art collections you will find sculpture, textiles, ceramics and paintings from the Islamic Middle East, China, Japan, Southeast Asia, India and the Himalayas.
persona3_adapted: In our Eastern Art collections, you will find sculptures, textiles, ceramics, and paintings from the Islamic Middle East, China, Japan, Southeast Asia, India, and the Himalayas.

CuratorialText: The Museum’s Heberden Coin Room is one of the leading international coin cabinets with particular strengths in the fields of Greek, Roman, Celtic, Byzantine, Medieval, Islamic and Chinese coinages. It also holds collections of paper money, tokens, jetons and commemorative art medals.
persona3_adapted: The Museum’s Heberden Coin Room is one of the world’s most important collections of coins, with especially strong holdings in Greek, Roman, Celtic, Byzantine, Medieval, Islamic, and Chinese coinage. It also includes collections of paper money, tokens, jetons (coin-like counters used for calculation or gaming), and commemorative art medals.

CuratorialText: The Western Art collections contain outstanding European fine and decorative arts from the Renaissance to the present day. They include Old Master and later paintings, drawings, watercolours, and prints; English and continental ceramics, sculpture, silver, watches, rings and musical instruments. The Ashmolean's exceptional print collection contains around 25,000 drawings and over 250,000 prints and many are accessible through our Western Art Print Room.
persona3_adapted: The Western Art collections feature exceptional European paintings, sculptures, and decorative objects—from the Renaissance period to the present day. These include works by renowned artists from earlier centuries (often called "Old Masters") as well as later paintings, drawings, watercolors, and prints. The collections also span English and European ceramics, sculptures, silverware, watches, rings, and musical instruments. The Ashmolean’s remarkable print collection holds about 25,000 drawings and more than 250,000 prints, many of which can be viewed in our Western Art Print Room.

CuratorialText: The painting resulted from an important early commission for two watercolours to be engraved for the annual calendar published by the University Press, the Oxford Almanack. They were so successful that Tuner eventually completed ten watercolours between 1798 and 1804. This work and one other were later painted in oils for the local print-seller and frame-maker, James Wyatt. Turner consulted Wyatt on the details of the architecture included in the view. He also introduced figures, members of the University and clergy, and some women ‘for the sake of colour’. The painting is one of the most fully documented of all Turner’s works because Wyatt kept his correspondence with the artist which was included in his posthumous sale. The original purpose of the commission was to have the design engraved. Wyatt settled on an oil painting, instead of a watercolour, half the size of Turner’s normal canvases, at a cost of 100 guineas. The painting was exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1812. When, in the 1830s, Turner was choosing views of Oxford for his greatest series of watercolours, he rejected the High Street. He felt that in this painting he had achieved an unparalleled view of technical mastery that he could not repeat.
persona3_adapted:
This painting came from an early, important job: Turner was asked to create two watercolors that would be turned into engravings—printed images—for the *Oxford Almanack*, a yearly calendar published by the University Press. The works were so well received that Turner eventually completed ten watercolors for the project between 1798 and 1804. Later, this piece and one other were painted in oils (a type of paint) for a local seller of prints and picture frames named James Wyatt.

Turner consulted Wyatt about the architectural details shown in the scene. He also added figures—members of the university and clergy—along with some women, whom he included “for the sake of colour,” meaning to add visual interest and variety. This painting is one of the best-documented works in Turner’s career, because Wyatt kept the letters Turner sent him, and those letters were later sold after Wyatt’s death.

The original goal of the commission was to create a design that could be engraved. Wyatt decided on an oil painting instead of a watercolor, half the size of Turner’s usual canvases, for a fee of 100 guineas (a significant sum at the time). The painting was shown at the Royal Academy in 1812. Later, in the 1830s, when Turner was selecting views of Oxford for his most famous series of watercolors, he chose not to paint the High Street again. He felt that in this earlier work, he had achieved a level of technical skill that he could not surpass.

CuratorialText: Together with a St Francis (Christ Church, Oxford) and a Risen Christ (National Gallery of Wales), WA1850.13 and WA1850.13.1 come from a lost altar-piece by Granacci, painted around 1520. The four panels may have been placed in the pilasters. Granacci was a follower of Ghirlandaio who was particularly interested in the art of Fra Bartolomeo and Raphael.
persona3_adapted: These four panels—WA1850.13, WA1850.13.1, a *St Francis* (at Christ Church, Oxford), and a *Risen Christ* (at the National Gallery of Wales)—once belonged to a larger religious painting, or altarpiece, created by the artist Granacci around 1520. The panels may have been set into the vertical columns, or pilasters, that framed the main image. Granacci was a student of the painter Ghirlandaio, and his work shows a strong interest in the styles of Fra Bartolomeo and Raphael, two other influential artists of the period.

CuratorialText: These are pinnacle panels that once belonged to the same unknown altar-piece, which may have been crowned with a central pinnacle panel showing Christ on the Cross. The artist was Florentine, working in a late Gothic manner and an attribution to Bartolomeo di Fruosino has recently been suggested. A painter and miniaturist, Bartolomeo was a pupil of Agnolo Gaddi.
persona3_adapted: These are the topmost panels of an altarpiece—a large, decorated structure placed behind an altar in a church. The altarpiece they came from is now lost, and we don't know who made it. It may once have had a central top panel showing Christ on the Cross. The artist was from Florence, Italy, and worked in a style that was popular in the late Gothic period (a style of art from the Middle Ages that emphasized elegance and detail). Recently, experts have suggested that the panels might be the work of a painter named Bartolomeo di Fruosino. Bartolomeo was both a painter and a manuscript illustrator, and he studied under a master named Agnolo Gaddi.

CuratorialText: As a nocturnal landscape and as a brilliantly structured composition, The Hunt in the Forest is a highly original painting. Hunting was an aristocratic pastime with its own rituals, but the idea of a hunt at night is playful and may be symbolic rather than realistic. In preparing this work, the artist Paolo di Dono first mapped out a grid on the panel's surface, fixing a central vanishing point to draw our gaze deep into the composition. In painting the subject, he incorporated details including the huntsmen's spears, the cut branches, logs and the water in the foreground as devices to bring focus to this space and to the receding forms of men and animals. Gold flecks in the foliage of the trees would have complemented the bright colours of the figures against the dark forest, and would have glistened and become animated when illuminated by candlelight. Paolo di Dono (1397-1475) was celebrated in his lifetime as a master of perspective, animals and landscape; his nickname, Uccello (‘Bird’), alludes to his depictions of the natural world. He was a versatile designer, working at times on mosaic and stained-glass commissions and all of these skills have been cleverly combined to create this masterpiece. The Hunt is what is known as a spalliera painting, which would have been mounted at shoulder height – whether as a backboard of a decorated chest or set into the wooden panelling of a room. The original client is unknown, but the painting was clearly intended for a Renaissance palace, perhaps in Urbino where Uccello worked for a time from 1465, or in Florence about 1470.
persona3_adapted:
In this adaptation, I have replaced or briefly explained specialized art terms (e.g., "nocturnal landscape," "vanishing point," "spalliera painting") while preserving the original's intellectual depth and scholarly nuance. The tone remains professional and engaging, suitable for a university-level English speaker with minimal art knowledge.

---

**Adapted Text:**

As a night-time landscape and a brilliantly organized composition, *The Hunt in the Forest* is a highly original painting. Hunting was an aristocratic pastime with its own rituals, but the idea of a hunt at night is playful and may be symbolic rather than realistic. In preparing this work, the artist Paolo di Dono first mapped out a grid on the panel's surface, fixing a central point where parallel lines appear to meet (called a vanishing point) to draw our gaze deep into the composition. In painting the subject, he included details such as the huntsmen's spears, cut branches, logs, and the water in the foreground as tools to focus attention on this space and on the receding forms of men and animals. Gold flecks in the foliage of the trees would have complemented the bright colors of the figures against the dark forest, and would have glistened and appeared to move when lit by candlelight. Paolo di Dono (1397–1475) was celebrated in his lifetime as a master of perspective, animals, and landscape; his nickname, Uccello (‘Bird’), hints at his depictions of the natural world. He was a versatile designer, working at times on mosaic and stained-glass commissions, and all of these skills have been cleverly combined to create this masterpiece. *The Hunt* is what is known as a *spalliera* painting—a type of artwork that would have been mounted at shoulder height, either as the backboard of a decorated chest or set into the wooden paneling of a room. The original client is unknown, but the painting was clearly intended for a Renaissance palace, perhaps in Urbino, where Uccello worked for a time from 1465, or in Florence around 1470.

CuratorialText: The portrait corresponds generally to a number of paintings and prints of Louix XIII of France on a prancing horse. From the type and handling of the paint, it seems to be either by or after a Flemish painter. The appearance of the king suggests a date of about 1622.
persona3_adapted: This portrait resembles several paintings and prints that show Louis XIII of France riding a horse that is rearing up. Based on the style and the way the paint has been applied, the work appears to be either by a Flemish painter or a copy made after one. The king’s appearance in the image suggests it was created around 1622.

CuratorialText: The Magdalen is recognisable by her jar of ointment, a reference to her traditional identification with the woman who anointed the feet of Christ and dried them with her hair (Luke 7: 36-50). Possibly cut from a larger composition, this small panel has been enlarged by being fitted into another and the figure extended. The artist may have worked in Parma in the early 17th century in the circle of Bartolomeo Schedoni.
persona3_adapted: Mary Magdalene is identified here by the jar of ointment she holds—a reference to the biblical story in which a woman anointed Jesus’ feet and dried them with her hair (Luke 7:36–50). This small painting may have originally been part of a larger scene; it appears to have been cut down and later set into a larger panel, with the figure extended to fit. The artist was likely active in Parma in the early 1600s, working within the circle of Bartolomeo Schedoni—meaning they were influenced by or associated with him and his style.

CuratorialText: The landscape is reminiscent of Éragny. It may have been painted in the summer of 1893 or 1894, before Félix Pissarro left Éragny for Belgium. In: Jon Whiteley, Nineteenth-century French Paintings in the Ashmolean Museum, (Oxford: Ashmolean Museum 2022)
persona3_adapted: The landscape in this painting brings to mind the village of Éragny, where the artist lived. It was likely created during the summer of either 1893 or 1894, just before the artist’s son, Félix Pissarro, moved away from Éragny to Belgium. This information comes from a scholarly catalogue by Jon Whiteley, which documents French paintings from the 1800s in the Ashmolean Museum’s collection.

CuratorialText: A free copy, dating from the late 17th or early 18th century, of the fresco in one of the spandrels of the painted Loggia di Psiche in the Villa Farnesina, Rome. Raphael and his studio decorated this space in 1518-19 with scenes from the story of Cupid and Psyche. The spandrel from which WA1859.1 was copied may have been painted by Raphael's senior assistant, Giulio Romano. The copyist has altered the format to a square shape.
persona3_adapted: This is a freehand copy, made in the late 1600s or early 1700s, of a fresco that originally filled one of the triangular spaces between arches in the painted Loggia di Psiche at the Villa Farnesina in Rome. Raphael and his workshop decorated this room in 1518–19 with scenes from the myth of Cupid and Psyche. The specific section that this painting copies may have been done by Raphael’s chief assistant, Giulio Romano. The copyist changed the original shape to a square.

CuratorialText: Three of the objects in this still life are found elsewhere in Renoir’s late work: the silver beaker appears alongside a sugar bowl and knife in a small still life said to have been painted at Cagnes in 1910 and again in a similar composition without the knife, also said to have been painted in 1910; the bronze-coloured child on the column was included in a small study, dated c.1905 by the Daubervilles. The faience double salt, a type produced in quantity in the nineteenth century at Desvres in the Pas-de-Calais and at Quimper in Brittany, appears in at least four other sketches. A disembodied image of the same figure, seen from the back, hovers in upper right, probably a reflection in a mirror. Renoir, who worked as a painter at a ceramic manufactory in his early days, seems to have owned a number of pieces of faience of this popular type⁵ and used a variety of coloured and decorated ceramic containers in his flower paintings.
persona3_adapted: Three of the objects in this still life also appear in other works from Renoir’s later career. The silver cup shows up alongside a sugar bowl and knife in a small still life said to have been painted at Cagnes in 1910, and again in a similar composition without the knife, also dated to 1910. The bronze-colored child figure on the column was included in a small study, dated around 1905 by the Daubervilles. The double salt cellar made of faience—a type of glazed earthenware that was mass-produced in the 1800s at Desvres in the Pas-de-Calais region and at Quimper in Brittany—appears in at least four other sketches. A ghostly image of the same figure, seen from behind, hovers in the upper right area, likely a reflection in a mirror. Renoir, who began his career as a painter at a ceramics factory, seems to have owned several pieces of this popular kind of faience and used a variety of colorful, decorated ceramic containers in his flower paintings.

CuratorialText: Although damaged, this portrait of an unknown woman is of fine quality and has affinities with the work of Andrea in the 1520s. Apart from a short stay in Rome c.1516, Andrea spent most of his career in Siena and in Florence, where he collaborated with his brother Raffaello Piccinelli.
persona3_adapted: Although this portrait of an unidentified woman is damaged, it is clearly of high quality and shares stylistic similarities with the work of the artist Andrea during the 1520s. Andrea spent most of his career in the Italian cities of Siena and Florence, except for a brief visit to Rome around 1516. During this time, he also worked together with his brother, Raffaello Piccinelli.

CuratorialText: After 1739, Ruysch painted mainly small-scale paintings of bouquets and posies, inscribed with her age, as here. The arrangement of curving stems, full, rounded roses and a number of smaller flowers looks back to the work of Willem van Aelst. This painting was stolen from the Museum in March 1949 and has not been recovered
persona3_adapted: After 1739, Ruysch focused mainly on small paintings of bouquets and posies, often noting her age on the canvas, as she does here. The composition—with its gently curving stems, full, rounded roses, and several smaller flowers—echoes the style of the earlier artist Willem van Aelst. This painting was stolen from the Museum in March 1949 and has not been recovered.

CuratorialText: This panel comes from the predella of a large altar-piece of 1433-35 for S. Niccolò in Cafaggio, Florence, whose parts are now scattered in various collections. The Virgin and Child with Four Angels in the centre was flanked by pairs of saints (Sts Benedict and Nicholas of Bari and St John the Baptist and an Evangelist, probably St Matthew). The predella showed scenes from the life of St Nicholas, of which five are known: the birth of the saint; pilgrims at the shrine of the saint; St Nicholas resuscitating three youths; the saint providing dowries for three sisters (three gold balls, his traditional attribute); and banishing the storm. The panels probably originally had quatrefoil frames. Bicci based the design of WA1850.26 on Gentile da Fabriano's Quaratesi altar-piece of 1425 for S. Niccolò oltr'Arno.
persona3_adapted: This panel was originally part of the lower section, or base, of a large altar-piece created between 1433 and 1435 for the church of San Niccolò in Cafaggio, Florence. The altar-piece has since been broken apart, and its pieces are now held in different collections. At the center of the original work was a scene of the Virgin and Child with Four Angels, flanked on either side by pairs of saints: Benedict and Nicholas of Bari, and John the Baptist with an Evangelist (likely Matthew). The lower section, called the predella, depicted scenes from the life of Saint Nicholas. Five of these scenes are known today: his birth; pilgrims at his shrine; the saint bringing three youths back to life; his providing dowries for three sisters (shown as three gold balls, his traditional symbol); and his calming a storm. The panels were probably originally set in frames shaped like four-leaf clovers. The artist, Bicci, based the design of this panel on an earlier altar-piece by Gentile da Fabriano, created in 1425 for another church dedicated to Saint Nicholas in Florence.

CuratorialText: This is clearly the portrait of a professional sculptor, shown in his studio with some of his work on the shelf behind. He holds a terracotta figure of a saint, perhaps the Virgin Mary. The painting has been tentatively attributed to the circle of the de Critz family.
persona3_adapted: This is clearly the portrait of a professional sculptor, shown in his studio with some of his work on the shelf behind him. He holds a small figure made of baked clay, depicting a saint—possibly the Virgin Mary. Art historians have suggested, though not confirmed, that the painting was created by someone in the circle of the de Critz family—meaning it was likely produced by an artist connected to that well-known family of painters, rather than by one of its members directly.

CuratorialText: The pages of calligraphy, all by one hand, mostly include kanshi (Chinese poems), and are signed with variations of the name Sessō Keigi. Two of the poems bear dates of Meiji 10 (1878) and Meiji 12 (1880), and a few identify the source from which the poem came or where the poem was composed. If the calligraphy was done at the same time as the paintings, this album would be an early work by Sobun before he began participating in the national and international expositions.
persona3_adapted: The calligraphy pages in this album were all written by the same person. Most of them feature *kanshi*—a type of poem written in Chinese by Japanese poets. The pages are signed with different versions of the name Sessō Keigi. Two of the poems include dates: 1878 and 1880. A few others mention where the poem came from or where it was written. If the calligraphy was created around the same time as the paintings, then this album would be an early work by Sobun, made before he began showing his art in major exhibitions in Japan and abroad.

CuratorialText: The genesis of this painting began in 1857, when it was exhibited as The Mother's Grave. The original composition, known from a drawing in the Ashmolean, showed the boy desolate over the grave of his mother. The landscape was begun in the summer of 1856 in the old churchyard at Chingford, Essex. Around 1862, Hughes altered the background and added the figure of the sister, for which the artist's wife, Tryphena, posed. The detail is used to reinforce the pathos of the subject, so that the ephemeral nature of spider's webs, dew drops, dog roses and dandelion seeds all emphasise the theme of transience. The boy's loss is retold in the lamb separated from its mother by the barrier of the tomb.
persona3_adapted: This painting first came into being in 1857, when it was shown under the title *The Mother's Grave*. An early version of the scene, known from a drawing now in the Ashmolean Museum, showed a young boy grieving alone at his mother's grave. The landscape background was painted during the summer of 1856 in an old churchyard at Chingford, Essex. Around 1862, the artist, Hughes, reworked the background and added the figure of the sister, for whom his wife, Tryphena, posed. Every detail in the painting is chosen to heighten the emotional impact of loss. The fleeting nature of spiderwebs, dewdrops, wild roses, and drifting dandelion seeds all reinforce the theme of life's brevity. The boy's sorrow is echoed in the image of a lamb separated from its mother by the stone barrier of the tomb.

CuratorialText: John Tradescant the Younger (1608-1662) succeeded his father as Keeper of the King's Gardens at Oatlands Palace and further enriched the family collection of specimens of natural and man-made curiosity. He is shown here with his friend, Roger Friend, a brewer (for which Zythepsa is the Greek) from Lambeth, who may have contributed the shells to Tradescant's museum.
persona3_adapted: John Tradescant the Younger (1608-1662) took over his father's role as the head of the King's Gardens at Oatlands Palace. He expanded the family's collection of natural and man-made curiosities—objects that were rare, unusual, or fascinating. In this portrait, he is shown alongside his friend Roger Friend, a brewer from Lambeth. (The Greek word for brewing, *Zythepsa*, is included as a clue to his trade.) It is possible that Friend contributed the shells displayed in Tradescant's museum.

CuratorialText: As one of the leading members of post-war generation of St Ives artists, Lanyon was primarily interested in the representation of landscape. This work epitomises the tension between abstraction and figuration in an otherwise carefully descriptive work.
persona3_adapted: As a key figure among the artists working in St Ives after the Second World War, Lanyon was mainly concerned with how to depict the landscape. This piece captures the push and pull between abstract shapes and recognizable forms, even though the work is otherwise carefully detailed and descriptive.

CuratorialText: Bundi, Rajasthan, c.1760-70. Gouache with gold on paper. Attended by a drowsy maid, a young noble couple sleep in close embrace on a palace terrace, sheltered by a shamiana or awning. The young prince’s sword and arrows lie close to hand.
persona3_adapted: Bundi, Rajasthan, around 1760–70. This painting uses opaque watercolor with gold on paper. It shows a young noble couple sleeping closely together on a palace terrace, attended by a drowsy maid. They are sheltered by a *shamiana*—a type of decorative awning or canopy. The prince’s sword and arrows are placed nearby, ready at hand.

CuratorialText: This painting was described as A Nun Playing a Piano when it appeared at Christie’s in 1919 and when it was sold by Colnaghi’s to Mrs Weldon in 1936, no doubt because the sitter is wearing a long veil.
