However, there is no reason otherwise to suppose the sitter is a nun. It is almost certainly a portrait of Desirée-Marie Ribot, known as Louise, the artist’s daughter whom he often used as a model. Born in 1857, her youthful appearance supports the evidence of style which suggests a date in the late 1860s or early 1870s before Ribot’s touch became thicker and more painterly. The dark background and domestic theme recall the work of François Bonvin (1817–1887), an early supporter of Ribot and of Alphonse Legros (1837–1911), who shared Bonvin’s interest in Spanish art.
persona3_adapted: This painting was once called *A Nun Playing a Piano* when it appeared at auction in 1919 and again when it was sold to a collector in 1936. That title likely came from the long veil the woman is wearing. But there is no real reason to think she is a nun. In fact, this is almost certainly a portrait of the artist’s daughter, Desirée-Marie Ribot, known as Louise, whom he often used as a model. She was born in 1857, and her youthful appearance supports what the painting’s style tells us: that it was likely made in the late 1860s or early 1870s, before the artist’s brushwork became heavier and more visibly textured. The dark background and the quiet, domestic scene recall the work of François Bonvin (1817–1887), an early supporter of Ribot, and of Alphonse Legros (1837–1911), who shared Bonvin’s interest in Spanish art.

CuratorialText: The inscription on this painting reads ‘Wind from the west is blowing through layers of curtains on doors, the proud [chrysanthemum] are falling but their beauty remains. In fading moonlight I [stayed] with these flowers while they cooled, versing through this cool evening in autumn.’
persona3_adapted: The inscription on this painting reads: 'A wind from the west blows through the layered curtains on the doors. The proud chrysanthemums are falling, yet their beauty remains. In the fading moonlight, I stayed with these flowers as they cooled, composing verses through this cool autumn evening.'

CuratorialText: Sinnott is well known for his complex figurative compositions. He often deals with the human figure in a context. Although the same fluid brush-strokes can be seen in the background and in the figures, the two wrestling men can be distinguished by a lighter touch and a slight variation in the use of paint. The underpainting, in places, appears through the upper layers, accentuating the sense of speed in the painting process.
persona3_adapted: Sinnott is known for his intricate paintings of the human form, often placing figures in a specific setting or situation. While the same flowing brushwork appears in both the background and the figures, the two wrestling men stand out because of a lighter, more delicate handling and a subtle difference in how the paint is applied. In some areas, the initial layer of paint shows through the top layers, which adds to the feeling that the painting was created quickly and with energy.

CuratorialText: The only securely documented painting by the artist is an altar-piece of 1447 now in the Museo Civico, Padua. A follower of Michele Giambono, Francesco may have collaborated with Antonio Vivarini in Venice. Together with WA1850.10, this panel was once part of an unknown altar-piece possibly for a church in Padua or Venice. St Catherine, of royal birth, wears a crown and carries her martyr's palm; the wheel refers to earlier attempts at her martyrdom.
persona3_adapted: The only painting that can be confidently attributed to this artist is an altar-piece from 1447, now housed in the Museo Civico in Padua. Francesco was a follower of Michele Giambono, meaning he was influenced by Giambono's style, and he may have worked alongside Antonio Vivarini in Venice. Along with another panel (WA1850.10), this piece was originally part of an unknown altar-piece, possibly created for a church in Padua or Venice. Saint Catherine, who was of royal birth, is shown wearing a crown and holding a martyr's palm—a symbol of her sacrifice. The wheel beside her refers to the earlier, unsuccessful attempts to kill her for her faith.

CuratorialText: The grief-stricken figures of the Virgin and St John the Evangelist make this small panel a powerful devotional image; it may have been the right wing of a portable diptych, although no hinge-marks are now visible. The anonymous artist was very close to Jacopo di Cione (to whom WA1921.3 has also been attributed) and would have trained in the large workshop of Orcagna, the leading artist in mid 14th-century Florence
persona3_adapted: The sorrowful figures of the Virgin Mary and St John the Evangelist make this small panel a powerful image for private prayer. It may have been the right half of a portable two-panel painting, though no hinge marks are visible today. The unknown artist was very close to Jacopo di Cione (another work in the collection has also been linked to him) and would have trained in the large workshop of Orcagna, the leading artist in Florence during the mid-1300s.

CuratorialText: Veronese's celebrated Last Supper of 1573 for the refectory of SS Giovanni e Paolo, Venice, caused him to be examined by the Inquisition on the grounds of blasphemy; he changed the title to The Feast in the House of Levi, a subject which allowed for much colourful detail (Luke 6: 27-30). Numerous copies are known of this large painting, now in the Accademia, Venice. WA1855.159 is a reduced copy of fine quality by a mid-18th-century Venetian artist, probably Francesco Fontebasso.
persona3_adapted: Veronese's famous painting *The Last Supper* from 1573, created for the dining hall of a Venetian monastery, led to him being questioned by the Inquisition—the Church's court for religious offenses—on charges of blasphemy. To avoid controversy, he changed the title to *The Feast in the House of Levi*, a biblical story that permitted the lively, colorful details he had included (based on Luke 6:27-30). Many copies exist of this large work, which now hangs in the Accademia museum in Venice. WA1855.159 is a smaller, high-quality version painted in the mid-1700s by a Venetian artist, likely Francesco Fontebasso.

CuratorialText: A companion to Opie's portrait of the sitter's husband, Francis Smith (1764-1815), WA1934.422. Both paintings were exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1800.
persona3_adapted: A companion piece to Opie's portrait of the sitter's husband, Francis Smith (1764–1815), also in the museum's collection (WA1934.422). Both paintings were shown at the Royal Academy's annual exhibition in 1800.

CuratorialText: Painted in 1886 at a time when both Camille and Lucien Pissarro were working with the pointillist technique. The view, taken from a bend on the path leading from the Pré du Chêne across the Epte from Éragny to Bazincourt, includes the spire of Saint-Martin in Éragny in the centre with the sixteenth-century brick manor house on the right. The Epte runs along the line of trees that crosses the composition. In 1887 Lucien painted an almost identical view in watercolour and bodycolour. The buildings seen from this stretch of the path are now largely concealed by the trees. However, it is clear that in both works Lucien has greatly enlarged the size of the church and manor and suppressed the farm buildings that lie in front of the manor. His father, as Lucien must have known, had painted the same view from the same spot in 1884, using the more improvised style of the early 1880s.
persona3_adapted:
Painted in 1886, this work dates from a period when both Camille Pissarro and his son Lucien were using a technique known as pointillism, which involves building up an image from small, distinct dots of color. The scene is taken from a bend in the path that leads from the Pré du Chêne, crossing the river Epte between the villages of Éragny and Bazincourt. In the center of the composition, you can see the spire of the Saint-Martin church in Éragny, while the sixteenth-century brick manor house appears on the right. The river Epte runs along the line of trees that cuts across the painting.

In 1887, Lucien created an almost identical view using watercolor and bodycolor (a type of opaque watercolor). The buildings visible from this part of the path are now mostly hidden by trees. However, it is clear that in both works, Lucien significantly enlarged the church and manor, while leaving out the farm buildings that originally stood in front of the manor. His father, as Lucien would have known, had painted the same view from the same spot in 1884, using a looser, more spontaneous style typical of his early 1880s work.

CuratorialText: The painting shows a distant view of the village of Lauris in the Vaucluse, with its distinctive eighteenth-century château and garden terrace on the left and the mountains of the Petit Luberon in the background. Guigou was seated or standing on one of the many sandbanks interspersed with the channels of the river Durance as it passes between Lauris and La Roque-d’Anthéron during the dry season. As in most of the artist’s Provençal landscapes, the air is clear and the sunlight strong. The example of Corot or Daubigny may have had some influence on the choice of the panoramic view that Guigou seems to have favoured above all others, but his manner of painting is distinctive. The sky, sand and river are thinly brushed and enlivened with spots of impasto and small, short strokes with the tip of the brush in the artist’s characteristic manner.
persona3_adapted:
The painting offers a distant view of the village of Lauris in the Vaucluse region of France. On the left, you can see its distinctive eighteenth-century castle and garden terrace, with the mountains of the Petit Luberon rising in the background. The artist, Guigou, was either seated or standing on one of the many sandbanks that appear among the channels of the Durance River during the dry season, where it flows between Lauris and La Roque-d’Anthéron.

Like most of Guigou’s landscapes of Provence, the air is clear and the sunlight intense. The choice of a wide, sweeping view—a type of composition that Guigou seems to have preferred above all others—may have been influenced by artists such as Corot or Daubigny. However, his painting technique is distinctive. The sky, sand, and river are painted with thin layers of color, brought to life by small, raised dabs of thicker paint and short, delicate strokes made with the tip of the brush, in a style that is uniquely his own.

CuratorialText: This painting, which is a cosmic landscape with Daoist undertones, was produced in 1988 for Khoan Sullivan (1919-2003) who wished for something with a predominantly blue colour. The inscription reads ‘To Mr. and Mrs. Sullivan. Affectionately, Chu Teh-Chun 1988.’
persona3_adapted: This painting, created in 1988, depicts a vast, otherworldly landscape that subtly reflects Daoist ideas—a Chinese philosophy centered on harmony with nature and the universe. It was made for Khoan Sullivan (1919–2003), who requested a work dominated by the color blue. The inscription on the piece reads: “To Mr. and Mrs. Sullivan. Affectionately, Chu Teh-Chun 1988.”

CuratorialText: One of Lucien's most extrovert works, painted in June 1925 and illustrating the broom in full flower at Le Brusq.
persona3_adapted: One of Lucien's most lively and expressive works, painted in June 1925, it shows the broom plant in full bloom at Le Brusq.

CuratorialText: Joseph Warton (1722-1800) was educated at Oriel College, Oxford, before entering the church. He was Headmaster of Winchester College between 1766 and 1793 and an accomplished poet. This portrait was painted and exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1777, the year in which Warton was also elected to 'The Club'. He was the brother of Thomas Warton, the Poet Laureate, whose portrait Reynolds also painted (Trinity College, Oxford; Mannings 2000, no. 1838).
persona3_adapted: Joseph Warton (1722-1800) studied at Oriel College, Oxford, before becoming a clergyman. He served as Headmaster of Winchester College from 1766 to 1793 and was also a skilled poet. This portrait was painted and shown at the Royal Academy in 1777—the same year Warton was elected to 'The Club,' a famous London social group for writers, artists, and intellectuals. He was the brother of Thomas Warton, who held the official title of Poet Laureate (the nation's leading poet). Reynolds also painted Thomas's portrait, which now hangs at Trinity College, Oxford.

CuratorialText: The influence of Vuillard is strongly marked. There is a less finished version of the same composition on the back
persona3_adapted: Vuillard’s influence is clearly visible here. On the back of the canvas, there is a less detailed version of the same scene.

CuratorialText: Bollongier was one of the few painters of the first half of the 17th century in Haarlem who specialised in painting flowers. His work seems to have been influenced by the Bosschaert school but with an added naturalism in the arrangement of the flowers. The glass container in this painting was a favourite accessory. It appears first in a work of 1627 and for the last time in a flower piece of 1639 (Rijksmuseum, Amsterdam).
persona3_adapted: Bollongier was one of the few painters in Haarlem during the early 1600s who focused almost entirely on flower paintings. His style appears to have been influenced by the Bosschaert school—a group of artists known for their detailed floral works—but he brought a more natural, lifelike quality to how he arranged the flowers. The glass vase shown here was one of his favorite props. It first appears in a painting from 1627 and shows up for the last time in a flower piece from 1639, which is now in the Rijksmuseum in Amsterdam.

CuratorialText: When Pharaoh ordered all new-born Hebrew sons to be killed, Moses's mother put him in an ark of bulrushes in the Nile. His sister stayed to watch while Pharaoh's daughter came to the river with her maidens and found the child (Exodus 2: 2-4). WA1950.169 is striking for its emotional intensity, with the focus on Moses's grief-stricken parents. The joyful scene of the finding of Moses was a popular one, whereas this subject is far less common (Poussin earlier treated it c. 1627). Painted in 1654 for Jacques Stella, WA1950.169 was greatly admired by contemporaries for the artist's learned treatment of the subject, his skill in painting reflections and the beauty of the landscape. Pharoah's city is based on Pirro Ligorio's reconstruction of some of the most famous buildings of ancient Rome. A sphinx accompanied by a river-god and cornucopia identifies the Nile. Different explanations, based on Poussin's antiquarian interests and wide-ranging visual references, have been proposed for the significance of the pipes of Pan that hang from a tree behind this group. Two painted copies are recorded in the 17th century; other copies and variations are known (including a tapestry version of the right side) and three engraved versions were made, the first by Stella's niece, Claudine Bouzonnet Stella, in 1672.
persona3_adapted: When Pharaoh ordered the killing of all newborn Hebrew sons, Moses’s mother placed him in a basket made of reeds and set it on the Nile. His sister stayed to watch as Pharaoh’s daughter came to the river with her attendants and found the child (Exodus 2: 2-4). This painting is striking for its emotional intensity, focusing on Moses’s grief-stricken parents. The joyful scene of Moses being found was a popular subject in art, but this more sorrowful moment is far less common (the artist had treated the happier version earlier, around 1627). Painted in 1654 for the artist Jacques Stella, the work was greatly admired by contemporaries for its learned approach to the biblical story, the artist’s skill in painting reflections, and the beauty of the landscape. The city in the background is based on a reconstruction of some of the most famous buildings of ancient Rome, created by the architect and antiquarian Pirro Ligorio. A sphinx, along with a river-god and a cornucopia (a horn-shaped symbol of abundance), identifies the scene as the Nile. Behind this group, a set of Pan pipes hangs from a tree; scholars have offered various explanations for their meaning, based on the artist’s interest in ancient history and his wide-ranging visual references. Two painted copies were made in the 1600s, and other versions and variations are known (including a tapestry of the right side). Three engraved copies were also produced, the first by Stella’s niece, Claudine Bouzonnet Stella, in 1672.

CuratorialText: The brother of Masaccio, Lo Scheggia was an eclectic artist who painted numerous works for domestic settings. This type of decorative, ceremonial subject is often found on cassone panels. Although the picture is much abraded, it has sufficient affinities with the mature work of Lo Scheggia to make the attribution convincing.
persona3_adapted: The brother of Masaccio, Lo Scheggia was a versatile artist who created many paintings intended for homes. This kind of decorative, ceremonial scene is commonly seen on the panels of cassoni—elaborately painted chests used for weddings or special occasions. Although the painting is heavily worn over time, it shares enough similarities with Lo Scheggia’s later work to make the attribution to him convincing.

CuratorialText: Lemon studied in Rome and in Paris with Carolus-Duran, but spent his early years in California. He exhibited at the Royal Academy from 1880 and came into contact with Giovanni Costa and his circle of Etruscans in Italy. This slight sketch from nature probably dates from the last years of the 19th century.
persona3_adapted: Lemon trained in Rome and Paris under the French painter Carolus-Duran, but spent his early years in California. He began showing his work at the Royal Academy in London in 1880, and in Italy he became part of a group of artists led by Giovanni Costa, known as the Etruscans. This small, informal study from life was likely made in the late 1890s.

CuratorialText: The subject, the flat and dry handling and the monumental quality of the figures show Shannon's debt to Puvis de Chavannes. The painting has been dated to c.1910 and is a variation on one of the artist's favourite themes.
persona3_adapted: The subject matter, the flat and dry painting style, and the monumental quality of the figures reveal Shannon's debt to Puvis de Chavannes—an earlier French painter known for his simplified, mural-like compositions. This work has been dated to around 1910 and is a variation on one of the artist's favorite themes.

CuratorialText: Views in and around the village of Berck in northern France appear in many of the artist's paintings from 1870 until his death in 1898. This view across the dunes is precisely dated. The unbroken horizon and solitary figure in the foreground emphasize the windswept emptiness of the site.
persona3_adapted: The village of Berck, in northern France, and its surroundings appear often in this artist’s paintings from 1870 until his death in 1898. This particular view across the sand dunes is marked with an exact date. The uninterrupted horizon and the single figure in the foreground draw attention to the site’s windswept, empty quality.

CuratorialText: Traditionally but wrongly said to be a portrait of Antonio Stradivari, A863a is a portrait of a musician or instrument-maker of the late 16th century and identifications of Claudio Monteverdi (1567-1643) and Gasparo da Salo (1540-1609) have been proposed.
persona3_adapted: This portrait, often mistakenly thought to show the famous violin maker Antonio Stradivari, actually depicts a musician or instrument-maker from the late 1500s. Over the years, experts have suggested it might be the composer Claudio Monteverdi or the instrument maker Gasparo da Salo, though neither identification has been confirmed.

CuratorialText: During his later years, Calvert produced many small works in thin oil colours on paper, the restrained colours and muted harmonies evocative of a dream world. They are generally peopled with figures from Greek mythology. Painted on a pink ground.
persona3_adapted: In his later years, Calvert created many small works on paper using thin oil paints. The colors are subdued and the overall effect is quiet and harmonious, suggesting a dreamlike world. These pieces often feature figures from Greek mythology. The paintings are done on a pink background.

CuratorialText: Traditionally attributed to Guido Reni as the Head of a Sibyl, WA1926.9 was recently identified as a copy after Cerrini. The same expressive head appears in his altar-piece of The Holy Family with Sts Catherine of Alexandria and Agnes painted for San Carlo alle Quattro Fontane, Rome (consecrated in 1646) and now in the convent there.
persona3_adapted: This painting was long thought to be by the celebrated Baroque artist Guido Reni and was known as *Head of a Sibyl*. However, recent scholarship has identified it as a copy after the lesser-known painter Giovanni Domenico Cerrini. The same striking face appears in one of Cerrini’s altarpieces—a large religious painting placed above an altar—titled *The Holy Family with Sts Catherine of Alexandria and Agnes*. That work was created for the church of San Carlo alle Quattro Fontane in Rome, which was consecrated in 1646, and it still hangs in the convent attached to that church today.

CuratorialText: One of Linnell's most important religious paintings, this was exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1835 and sold to the Art Union for the purposes of engraving in 1838. It was based on sketches made in 1829, when Linnell was closely associated with the 'Ancients' at Shoreham. Samuel Palmer was the model for the disciple on the right. The source is Luke 24: 13-35.
persona3_adapted: One of Linnell's most significant religious works, this painting was displayed at the Royal Academy in 1835 and sold to the Art Union in 1838 so it could be reproduced as an engraving. The composition draws from sketches made in 1829, when Linnell was closely connected with a group of artists known as the 'Ancients' in Shoreham. The disciple on the right was modeled after Samuel Palmer, a fellow artist. The scene is based on a passage from the Gospel of Luke (24: 13-35), which describes the encounter on the road to Emmaus.

CuratorialText: Design is an impulse that unifies us across time and culture. This installation unifies two traditionally disparate collecting fields, fashion and textiles with decorative and industrial arts to better understand what underlies our motivations and capacity for design. Ensembles from the Iris Apfel Rare Bird of Fashion collection celebrate the exuberant remixing and inventive styling of one of the world’s most prominent fashion icons, while constellations of unique and culturally significant works of design, fashion, and textiles explore distinctive and resourceful forms of creative expression. A recent gallery highlight is the display of 38 vibrant and flamboyant ensembles from the collection of Boston-based entrepreneur and fashion icon Yolanda Cellucci. Yolanda’s, based in Waltham, Massachusetts, was a hotspot for lavish evening and wedding ensembles and that became a household name throughout the region, and a shorthand for glitz and glamor in the late 20th century. Cellucci generously donated 57 works of fashion and accessories to PEM in 2021, along with archival photographs and materials related to the legacy of her bridal shop.
persona3_adapted: Design is a creative drive that connects people across different times and cultures. This exhibition brings together two areas that museums usually keep separate—fashion and textiles on one hand, and decorative and industrial arts on the other—to explore what motivates us to design and how we are capable of doing so. Outfits from the Iris Apfel Rare Bird of Fashion collection celebrate the bold mixing of styles and inventive way of dressing from one of the world’s most famous fashion figures. At the same time, groupings of unique and culturally important works of design, fashion, and textiles highlight creative approaches that are both distinctive and resourceful. A recent highlight in the gallery is the display of 38 vibrant and eye-catching outfits from the collection of Boston-area businesswoman and fashion icon Yolanda Cellucci. Her store, Yolanda’s, based in Waltham, Massachusetts, was known for luxurious evening wear and wedding dresses, becoming a well-known name across the region and a symbol of glamour and excitement in the late 20th century. In 2021, Cellucci generously donated 57 fashion pieces and accessories to the Peabody Essex Museum, along with archival photographs and materials related to the history of her bridal shop.

CuratorialText: Born in Greenbush, New York in 1844, Lewis became the first sculptor of Black and Indigenous (Mississauga) descent to achieve international recognition. Beginning her career in Boston in 1863, she traveled to Rome in 1866 to join the leading American sculptors of her generation, breaking international, racial and gender barriers. "Sometimes the times were dark and the outlook was lonesome, but where there is a will, there is a way,” Lewis recalled in 1878. “That is what I tell my people whenever I meet them, that they must not be discouraged, but work ahead until the world is bound to respect them for what they have accomplished.” Following her death in 1907, Lewis’ legacy endured in Black communities, yet her contribution to American sculpture has largely been underrecognized. Some of her great masterpieces were rediscovered decades later, while others remain lost today. Her vivid, naturalistic stone sculptures feature abolitionists and social reformers, engage with Indigenous identity and give new life to religious and mythological figures.
persona3_adapted: Born in Greenbush, New York in 1844, Lewis became the first sculptor of Black and Indigenous (Mississauga) descent to achieve international recognition. She began her career in Boston in 1863, then traveled to Rome in 1866 to join the leading American sculptors of her generation, breaking barriers of race, gender, and nationality. "Sometimes the times were dark and the outlook was lonesome, but where there is a will, there is a way," Lewis recalled in 1878. "That is what I tell my people whenever I meet them, that they must not be discouraged, but work ahead until the world is bound to respect them for what they have accomplished." After her death in 1907, Lewis’ legacy continued in Black communities, yet her contribution to American sculpture has largely gone unrecognized. Some of her greatest works were rediscovered decades later, while others remain lost today. Her vivid, lifelike stone carvings depict abolitionists and social reformers, explore Indigenous identity, and breathe new life into religious and mythological figures.

CuratorialText: Home to 3.7 million people, 85 species of animals, 32,000 species of insects and 2 billion migratory birds, the boreal forest spans nearly the entire Northern Hemisphere, just below the Arctic Circle, and makes up one of the world’s last remaining stretches of true wilderness. Discover the biodiversity and global importance of our northernmost forests through first-person stories, commissioned objects, interactive experiences and exquisite photography and videography. The boreal forest is critical to the health of our entire planet. It helps regulate Earth’s climate, is the world’s largest land biome, is a critical water reservoir and holds two to three times as much carbon as tropical forests.
persona3_adapted: Home to 3.7 million people, 85 species of animals, 32,000 species of insects, and 2 billion migratory birds, the boreal forest stretches across nearly the entire Northern Hemisphere, just below the Arctic Circle. It is one of the world’s last remaining expanses of true wilderness. Through firsthand accounts, specially commissioned artworks, hands-on activities, and stunning photography and video, this exhibition explores the biodiversity and global significance of our northernmost forests. The boreal forest is essential to the health of our entire planet: it helps regulate Earth’s climate, is the largest land-based ecosystem on the planet, serves as a critical freshwater reservoir, and stores two to three times more carbon than tropical forests.

CuratorialText: Salem’s connections to the Declaration of Independence center around Ezekiel Russell, a local printer who played a key role in publishing and disseminating the Declaration in 1776. This exhibition coincides with the 250th anniversary of the American Revolution as well as the 400th anniversary of the European settlement of Salem. Works on view in the intimate James Duncan Phillips Trust Gallery include two of the earliest broadside editions of the Declaration of Independence alongside Revolutionary-era manuscripts, newspapers, pamphlets and broadsides that showcase the nation’s founding values of democracy: freedom, liberty and equality.
persona3_adapted: Salem’s link to the Declaration of Independence centers on Ezekiel Russell, a local printer who played a key role in publishing and spreading the Declaration in 1776. This exhibition marks the 250th anniversary of the American Revolution and the 400th anniversary of European settlement in Salem. The works displayed in the intimate James Duncan Phillips Trust Gallery include two of the earliest printed poster versions of the Declaration of Independence, along with Revolutionary-era manuscripts, newspapers, pamphlets, and posters that highlight the nation’s founding democratic values: freedom, liberty, and equality.

CuratorialText: These films honor the lesser-known stories of Salem and offer a behind-the-scenes look at what makes this city so remarkable. Produced over the course of more than seven years by Perry Hallinan, Joe Cultrera and many other filmmakers for presentation at the Salem Film Fest, these short films span topics from breakfast at Red’s to the annual Black Picnic at Salem Willows. For longtime Salem residents, these stories might spark nostalgia; for new residents and tourists, they illuminate Salem’s vibrant communities, bygone businesses and traditions that local filmmakers have long worked to record and preserve. Beyond the Broom showcases the vibrant community of Salem, one with an identity that extends beyond its witch trials history. This exhibition is part of the 2026 celebration of Salem 400+, which commemorates the quadricentennial of the city’s settlement while honoring the preexisting Indigenous community. The initiative aims to engage the Salem community and ensure a vibrant and sustainable city for generations to come.
persona3_adapted: These films tell the lesser-known stories of Salem, giving viewers a glimpse behind the scenes of what makes this city so remarkable. Created over more than seven years by Perry Hallinan, Joe Cultrera, and many other filmmakers for the Salem Film Fest, these short works cover topics ranging from breakfast at Red’s to the annual Black Picnic at Salem Willows. For longtime residents, these stories may bring back memories; for newcomers and visitors, they reveal Salem’s lively communities, vanished businesses, and traditions that local filmmakers have worked hard to record and preserve. *Beyond the Broom* showcases Salem’s vibrant community—a place with an identity that goes beyond its famous witch trials history. This exhibition is part of the 2026 celebration of Salem 400+, which marks the 400th anniversary of the city’s settlement while also honoring the Indigenous community that lived here long before. The initiative aims to bring the Salem community together and help ensure a lively, sustainable city for future generations.

CuratorialText: Explore several hundred fascinating objects inside the historic hall, representing a sampling of the many collections that were displayed in the museum during its earliest years. This installation gives voice to early collectors, trading partners and museum visitors about these objects — with some accounts extracted from records in the museum’s vast archive — and shares perspectives from a range of people living today. These captivating stories and works that reflect local and global history, as well as the impact of maritime trade, recenter East India Marine Hall as the heart of the museum. From its inception, this space and the objects in it have elicited empathy, curiosity and wonder and transported visitors to places around the world. This project has been made possible in part by a grant from the National Endowment for the Humanities: Democracy demands wisdom. Major support is provided by Mr. Ulf B. CuratorialText: Renowned for its expansive holdings of decorative arts and paintings, PEM’s American art collection spans four centuries of creative expression in the United States. Rooted in our historic holdings that celebrate the rich and diverse artistic and cultural heritage of Essex County, Massachusetts, the collection has continued to grow to embrace a broader representation of the art of this country. The artists represented in this Seamans gallery use a variety of materials — wood, metal, glass, clay, paint, fiber and more — to convey their creative ambitions. Many of the artistic practices and decorative inspirations behind these works have long histories, with their origins traceable to Europe, Asia and the Americas. These examples of formal perfection, domestic utility and luxury, and innovative twists on cultural traditions tell a larger story of our global connectedness. Together, these works tell compelling stories of American life and the ongoing cultural exchanges between the people of the region, the country and the wider world. Located in the Seamans Gallery.
persona3_adapted: PEM’s American art collection is known for its wide range of decorative objects and paintings, covering four centuries of creative work in the United States. The collection began with pieces that celebrate the rich and varied artistic and cultural history of Essex County, Massachusetts, and has since expanded to include a broader view of American art. The artists featured in this Seamans gallery work with many different materials—wood, metal, glass, clay, paint, fiber, and more—to express their creative goals. Many of the techniques and design ideas behind these pieces have long histories, with roots in Europe, Asia, and the Americas. These objects, whether they show perfect craftsmanship, serve everyday or luxury purposes, or put a new spin on cultural traditions, reveal a larger story of how we are all connected across the globe. Together, they tell powerful stories about American life and the ongoing exchange of ideas among people in this region, the country, and the wider world. Located in the Seamans Gallery.

CuratorialText: Persian and Turkish architecture, textiles and miniature paintings inspire the precise, stylized floral forms that compose Anila Quayyum Agha’s sculptural chamber of light and shadow. This luminous installation located in the Wheatland Family gallery provides an opportunity to contemplate the differences and commonalities that shape our lives and relationships. Originally from Lahore, Pakistan, and now living in the US, Agha is acutely attuned to the social codes that inform the lives of Muslim women and all immigrants. She describes this work as her effort to create a sense of how women can reclaim and safely open up private space to welcome others. Located in the Wheatland Family Gallery.
persona3_adapted: Persian and Turkish architecture, textiles, and miniature paintings inspire the carefully arranged floral patterns that make up Anila Quayyum Agha’s sculptural room of light and shadow. This glowing installation, located in the Wheatland Family Gallery, invites visitors to reflect on the differences and shared experiences that shape our lives and relationships. Originally from Lahore, Pakistan, and now living in the United States, Agha is deeply aware of the unwritten social rules that affect the lives of Muslim women and all immigrants. She describes this work as her attempt to show how women can reclaim and safely open up private spaces to welcome others.

CuratorialText:
Merchants across the globe went to great lengths to acquire these spectacular commodities. Now known as Asian export art, these objects connected societies and created a complex global economy that continues to shape our world to this day.

PEM’s Asian Export Art collection, foremost in the world, explores cross-cultural exchange as a catalyst for creativity and celebrates the interplay of commerce and creative expression. The gallery features more than 200 works of art made in diverse media by artists in China, Japan, and South Asia. These transcultural objects demonstrate the beauty and ingenuity that can be created through blending artistic traditions, materials, and technologies. Porcelain, textiles, tea, ivory, and silver were the focus of intensive trade activity between Asia and the rest of the world. This installation in the Sean M. Healey Family gallery also addresses the uncomfortable truth that many of these works of art were originally purchased with profits derived from the illegal opium trade. During the 1800s, millions of Indian and Chinese lives were devastated by opium, a foreshadowing of today’s opioid crisis.
persona3_adapted:
Merchants around the world went to extraordinary lengths to obtain these remarkable goods. Today we call them Asian export art—objects that linked different societies and helped create a complex global economy whose effects are still felt today.

PEM’s Asian Export Art collection, the most important of its kind in the world, examines how cultural exchange can spark creativity and highlights the relationship between commerce and artistic expression. The gallery displays more than 200 works made from a wide range of materials by artists in China, Japan, and South Asia. These objects, shaped by multiple cultures, show the beauty and inventiveness that can result when artistic traditions, materials, and techniques are blended together. Porcelain, textiles, tea, ivory, and silver were the main goods traded intensively between Asia and the rest of the world. This exhibition in the Sean M. Healey Family Gallery also confronts a difficult reality: many of these works were originally bought with money earned from the illegal opium trade. In the 1800s, opium devastated millions of lives in India and China, a dark precedent for the opioid crisis we face today.

CuratorialText: This exhibition explores America as a place where disparate viewpoints are brought together: America as both longed for and greatly revered. Previously on view in the context of PEM's 2019 Jacob Lawrence: The American Struggle exhibition, Bethany Collins, America: A Hymnal presents a solo exhibition of Collins’ art as an immersive experience. Explore 100 different versions of the popular patriotic anthem “My Country ‘Tis of Thee,” rewritten between the 18th and 20th centuries in support of various American causes. Versions of the lyrics are bound together chronologically in Collins’ artist book, where she burns away the musical notes with a laser — chronicling an idea of America through the history of one song’s varied verses. A range of these powerful voices and expressions are heard throughout the gallery in a sound piece, what Collins calls, “a familiar chaos of dissenting versions of what it means to be … American bound together.” The gallery is wallpapered with a newly commissioned work of flocked flower silhouettes. Each flower contains a meaning as the artist uses floriography, the symbolic language of flowers, to relay messages of hope, longing and loss to further illustrate the evolution and complexity of American experiences. In addition to the artwork on view, a study center offers deeper dives into Collins' artistic approaches and historical sources. Through interactives, PEM collection objects and media components, Bethany Collins, America: A Hymnal inspires us to activate our own civic mindset. Located in the Jurrien Timmer Gallery.
persona3_adapted: This exhibition looks at America as a place where different, often conflicting, viewpoints come together—a country that people both long for and deeply admire. *Bethany Collins, America: A Hymnal* was first shown as part of PEM’s 2019 exhibition *Jacob Lawrence: The American Struggle*, and now returns as a solo show that surrounds visitors in an immersive experience. Here, you can explore 100 different versions of the patriotic song “My Country ‘Tis of Thee,” rewritten between the 1700s and 1900s to support a wide range of American causes. Collins has bound these lyrics together in chronological order in an artist’s book, where she uses a laser to burn away the musical notes. This process creates a record of how one song’s changing words have shaped—and been shaped by—different ideas of America. Throughout the gallery, a sound piece brings together many of these voices and perspectives, creating what Collins calls “a familiar chaos of dissenting versions of what it means to be … American bound together.” The walls are covered with a newly commissioned wallpaper featuring flower silhouettes made of flocked fabric. Each flower carries a specific meaning, using the symbolic language of flowers (known as floriography) to convey messages of hope, longing, and loss. These floral symbols help illustrate how American experiences have evolved and remain complex. In addition to the artwork, a study center offers deeper exploration of Collins’ creative methods and the historical sources she draws on. Through interactive displays, objects from PEM’s collection, and media components, *Bethany Collins, America: A Hymnal* encourages us to think about our own role as citizens. This exhibition is located in the Jurrien Timmer Gallery.

CuratorialText: Encompassing everyday objects and the fine arts, the museum’s Japanese collection is distinguished for its range, from paintings and sculpture to decorative arts and costumes and textiles. Spanning Hokkaido in the North to Okinawa in the South, and prehistory to the present, these objects illuminate the varied artistic and cultural traditions of Japan. Toward the end of the 19th century, Edward Sylvester Morse, director of the Peabody Academy of Science (a precursor to PEM), became one of the first Western scholars to visit modern Japan after it opened to travelers. Morse was highly influential in encouraging American interest in Japanese art and culture, and helped create this remarkable Japanese collection.
persona3_adapted: The museum’s Japanese collection brings together both everyday objects and fine art, and stands out for its wide range—from paintings and sculptures to decorative arts, costumes, and textiles. Spanning from Hokkaido in the north to Okinawa in the south, and from prehistoric times to the present day, these works reveal the diverse artistic and cultural traditions of Japan. In the late 1800s, Edward Sylvester Morse, director of the Peabody Academy of Science (a forerunner of today’s PEM), became one of the first Western scholars to visit modern Japan after it opened to foreign travelers. Morse played a key role in sparking American interest in Japanese art and culture, and his efforts helped build this remarkable collection.

CuratorialText: From the late Joseon dynasty (1392–1910) to today, PEM’s exceptional collection explores art, culture, and life in Korea during a time of significant transition and global connection. PEM’s early Korean collection was primarily formed by Edward Sylvester Morse, director of the Peabody Academy of Science (a predecessor organization to PEM), who proactively pursued Korean acquisitions during his tenure from 1880 to 1914. Among his most significant acquisitions from this period is a set of eight Korean musical instruments played at the 1893 World’s Columbian Exposition. PEM’s connection with Korea was also strengthened by Yu Kil-Chun (1856-1914), the reformist scholar and politician who came to the U.S. as a member of the first Korean delegation and lived near Salem for nearly two years. Yu Kil-Chun and other early travelers between the U.S. and Korea donated many meaningful and foundational works to the museum’s collection. PEM’s Yu Kil-Chun Gallery of Korean Art and Culture brings to life the compelling stories of these early pioneers, presented alongside the museum’s renowned Korean textile collection, exquisite 19th-century paintings — including the Welcoming Banquet of the Governor of Pyeongan screen — and inspiring contemporary works by Nam June Paik (1932–2006) and other Korean and Korean American artists.
persona3_adapted: From the late Joseon dynasty (1392–1910) to the present day, PEM’s outstanding collection explores art, culture, and daily life in Korea during a period of major change and growing connections with the wider world. The museum’s early Korean collection was largely built by Edward Sylvester Morse, director of the Peabody Academy of Science (an organization that came before PEM). Morse actively sought out Korean objects during his time as director, from 1880 to 1914. Among his most important finds from this era is a set of eight Korean musical instruments that were played at the 1893 World’s Columbian Exposition in Chicago. PEM’s ties to Korea were also strengthened by Yu Kil-Chun (1856–1914), a reform-minded scholar and politician who came to the United States as part of the first official Korean delegation and lived near Salem for nearly two years. Yu Kil-Chun and other early travelers between the U.S. and Korea donated many meaningful and foundational works to the museum’s collection. PEM’s Yu Kil-Chun Gallery of Korean Art and Culture brings to life the compelling stories of these early pioneers, displayed alongside the museum’s renowned Korean textile collection, exquisite 19th-century paintings—including a large folding screen titled *Welcoming Banquet of the Governor of Pyeongan*—and inspiring contemporary works by Nam June Paik (1932–2006) and other Korean and Korean American artists.

CuratorialText: PEM’s Maritime art collection, which is the finest in the country, frames the sea as an enduring source of opportunity as well as peril, a force that inspires creativity and innovation, and encourages engagement with the wider world. This installation located in the Byrne Family gallery offers a global perspective on our creative response to the sea, from a 17th-century brass Pakistani astrolabe to Valerie Hagerty’s monumental Shipwrecked Armoire with Barnacles (2012) and artworks that reflect Salem’s rich history of maritime trade and exploration. Immersive digital media amplify compelling stories that lay behind unassuming objects, like a calendar stick from 1803 whose carved notches record the long days that Rhode Island native, James Drown, spent shipwrecked and left for dead on Tristan da Cunha, a remote speck island in the South Atlantic.
persona3_adapted: PEM’s Maritime art collection, the finest of its kind in the country, presents the sea as both a lasting source of opportunity and danger—a force that sparks creativity and innovation, and encourages people to connect with the wider world. This exhibition in the Byrne Family gallery takes a global look at how we have creatively responded to the sea, featuring objects that range from a 17th-century brass astrolabe from Pakistan (a device used to measure the positions of stars and planets) to Valerie Hagerty’s large-scale *Shipwrecked Armoire with Barnacles* (2012), as well as artworks that tell the story of Salem’s rich history in maritime trade and exploration. Interactive digital displays bring to life the powerful stories behind seemingly simple objects, such as a calendar stick from 1803. Its carved notches record the long days that James Drown, a Rhode Island native, spent shipwrecked and abandoned on Tristan da Cunha, a remote, tiny island in the South Atlantic.

CuratorialText: Bringing two extraordinary collections of Native American and American art together for the first time in our institution's history, this long-term installation celebrates artistic achievements across time, space, and worldviews. Located in the Nancy and George Putnam Gallery and Barbara Weld Putnam Gallery, the 250 artworks on display span in time from 10,000 years ago to the present, and demonstrate a range of voices, modes of expression, cultures, and media including: sculpture, paintings, textiles and costumes, furniture, decorative arts, works on paper, installations, video, and a re-envisioned period room. The gallery also includes original documents and objects from the Salem witch trials, one of the most iconic dramas in American history. Between May 1692 and March 1693, the court sentenced 25 innocent people in the Salem region to death. The new installation will be responsive to these urgent times by giving our public an opportunity to grapple with the links, continuities, and disjunctions of our complex histories in America in order to shape a more connected and empathetic present and future. By rethinking America’s histories, visitors are asked to consider how we might envision a better future together.
persona3_adapted: Bringing together two major collections—one of Native American art and one of American art—for the first time in our museum's history, this long-term exhibition celebrates artistic achievements across different times, places, and ways of seeing the world. Located in the Nancy and George Putnam Gallery and Barbara Weld Putnam Gallery, the 250 artworks on display span from 10,000 years ago to the present day. They show a wide range of perspectives, styles, cultures, and materials, including: sculpture, paintings, textiles and clothing, furniture, decorative objects, works on paper, video installations, and a reimagined period room (a room designed to evoke a specific historical era). The gallery also includes original documents and objects from the Salem witch trials, one of the most famous events in American history. Between May 1692 and March 1693, the court sentenced 25 innocent people in the Salem area to death. This new installation responds to our current moment by giving visitors a chance to reflect on the connections, continuities, and breaks in America’s complex histories—with the goal of shaping a more connected and empathetic present and future. By rethinking America’s past, visitors are invited to consider how we might imagine a better future together.

CuratorialText: These figures, from disparate cultures around the world and across time, embody power as both a fundamental social dynamic and a part of our innate wiring to respond to figures and faces. Curved niches encourage visitors to engage with each artwork, decluttering the field of view and focusing attention. Poetically concise labels heighten the emotional tenor of the experience. Located in the Louise Du Pont Crowninshield Gallery.
persona3_adapted: These figures, drawn from different cultures and historical periods around the world, represent power in two ways: as a basic force that shapes how societies work, and as something rooted in our natural instinct to react to human forms and faces. Curved display spaces invite visitors to focus on each artwork one at a time, clearing away visual distractions and sharpening attention. Short, thoughtfully written labels add an emotional layer to the experience. This gallery is named after Louise Du Pont Crowninshield.

CuratorialText: The exhibition starts at “A is for Always Indigenous” to acknowledge the Native communities who have lived for millennia on the land where the museum now sits. It ends with Z is for Zoology and coincides with the return to the galleries of a leatherback turtle specimen captured in 1885, a favorite of longtime visitors. Salem is a city with many stories of local, national and international significance. Alexander Graham Bell completed the first successful long-distance telephone call from Salem in 1877. Parker Brothers produced Monopoly here. And in 2013, President Obama signed legislation recognizing the city as the birthplace of the United States National Guard. Using selections from PEM’s vast collection, Salem Stories features more than 100 works, including paintings, sculpture, textiles, decorative arts, photographs, natural history specimens, manuscripts, posters, books, eyewitness accounts, and even a murder weapon. The A–Z structure creates an accessible and entertaining way to engage with Salem’s history, from past to present day. This is an evolving story, one that will continue to unfold throughout the life of the exhibition. Located in the Peggy and Lloyd Hilborn Gallery.
persona3_adapted: The exhibition begins with “A is for Always Indigenous,” honoring the Native communities who have lived on this land for thousands of years, long before the museum was built. It ends with “Z is for Zoology,” timed to coincide with the return of a leatherback turtle specimen—captured in 1885 and a longtime favorite of regular visitors—to the galleries. Salem is a city rich with stories that matter locally, nationally, and internationally. In 1877, Alexander Graham Bell made the first successful long-distance telephone call here. Parker Brothers created the board game Monopoly in Salem. And in 2013, President Obama signed a law recognizing the city as the birthplace of the United States National Guard. Drawing from PEM’s extensive collection, *Salem Stories* brings together more than 100 objects: paintings, sculptures, textiles, decorative arts, photographs, natural history specimens, manuscripts, posters, books, firsthand accounts, and even a murder weapon. The A-to-Z format offers a straightforward and engaging way to explore Salem’s history, from the past to the present. This is an evolving story—one that will continue to develop throughout the exhibition’s run. The show is located in the Peggy and Lloyd Hilborn Gallery.

CuratorialText: Bridging myth with social and political history, the Chester and Davida Herwitz Gallery tells the story of nation-building and self-discovery through works by India's most celebrated artistic geniuses of the 20th century. Following independence from British rule in 1947, artists in India aimed to uncover a visual language that was uniquely Indian in inspiration to convey their experiences, struggles, ambitions and dreams. The Prashant H. Fadia Foundation and Deshpande Foundation Gallery features a selection of objects from PEM's extensive collection of historical material from India. Focused primarily on the 19th century, the gallery considers India's long and complex history of foreign occupation, and its troubling impact on the representation of Indian people in art. Featuring some of the earliest objects to come to PEM, including unfired clay sculpture, mica paintings and Kalighat paintings, the gallery considers and questions the timeless tropes of India that persist even today.
persona3_adapted:
Bridging myth with social and political history, the Chester and Davida Herwitz Gallery tells the story of nation-building and self-discovery through works by India's most celebrated artistic geniuses of the 20th century. After gaining independence from British rule in 1947, artists in India sought to create a visual style—a way of seeing and representing the world—that was uniquely Indian in its inspiration. Through this new visual language, they aimed to express their experiences, struggles, ambitions, and dreams.

The Prashant H. Fadia Foundation and Deshpande Foundation Gallery features a selection of objects from PEM's extensive collection of historical material from India. Focused mainly on the 19th century, the gallery examines India's long and complex history of foreign occupation and its troubling impact on how Indian people have been portrayed in art. Featuring some of the earliest objects to come to PEM—including unfired clay sculptures, paintings on mica (a thin, shiny mineral), and Kalighat paintings (a style of watercolor painting on paper that emerged in 19th-century Kolkata)—the gallery considers and questions the timeless stereotypes of India that persist even today.

CuratorialText: This diverse selection of contemporary glass represents the work of more than 40 of the most influential glass artists from the 20th century to today. Donated to PEM in 2022 by New York-based collectors Carl and Betty Pforzheimer, this collection is remarkable for its range of forms, scale and techniques in glass. The Pforzheimers’ gift significantly expands PEM’s noted historic glass collection while celebrating the extraordinary aesthetic possibilities that arise from the combination of sand and fire.
persona3_adapted: This selection of contemporary glass showcases work by over 40 of the most influential glass artists from the 1900s to the present day. Donated to PEM in 2022 by New York-based collectors Carl and Betty Pforzheimer, the collection stands out for its variety of shapes, sizes, and methods used in working with glass. The Pforzheimers’ gift greatly expands PEM’s well-known historic glass collection while celebrating the remarkable artistic potential that comes from combining sand and fire.

CuratorialText: The Pod is part of PEM's Dotty Brown Art & Nature Center, which investigates our place in nature through contemporary art, memorable objects and interactive experiences. Designed for visitors of all ages, the family-friendly center features installations, changing exhibitions, hands-on displays and art-making spaces. Grow your awareness of nature, try on new ways of being, and tap into your own creativity. Discover a life-size wire tree by sculptor Elizabeth Keithline modeled on a live tree from PEM’s campus. Watch dancer Laura Kathrein move as different animals and embody local habitats. And create your own imaginary creature inspired by real animals and fantastical beasts from around the globe. What creative sense will be sparked in you on your visit to The Pod? What comes to mind when you think of a pod? A pea pod, an iPod or maybe a futuristic orb? Jane Winchell, the Sarah Fraser Robbins Director of the Art & Nature Center and curator of The Pod, describes her idea of a pod and what you’ll encounter when you visit our new multisensory exhibition space. There is something for everyone here, ranging from fabulous, nature-inspired contemporary and historic artworks to stations where you can explore nature up close or make your own art. Families with young children will discover the Wonder Zone which includes activity bins, a light table and picture books just for them

Families with young children will find the Wonder Zone, which includes activity bins, a light table, and picture books designed just for them.

CuratorialText: This extraordinary series of events between June 1692 and March 1693 led to the deaths of 25 innocent women, men and children who were wrongfully convicted of crimes. More than 300 years later, the personal tragedies and grievous wrongs that occurred still provoke us to reflect and reckon with the experiences of those involved. Learn the true story of this tragedy as told through the voices and with the possessions of those directly involved. This ongoing installation tells this story through court documents and authentic historic objects presented as tangible fragments directly tied to people in Salem and nearby communities in the late 17th century. A handwritten petition, a carved loom, a walking stick — each illuminates an aspect of individuals who lived through Salem’s witch trials and serves as a reminder of the real people impacted by these harrowing events. The Salem Witch Trials 1692 is organized by the Peabody Essex Museum. This exhibition is made possible by Carolyn and Peter S. Lynch and The Lynch Foundation. We thank Jennifer and Andrew Borggaard, James B. and Mary Lou Hawkes, Chip and Susan Robie, and Timothy T. Hilton as supporters of the Exhibition Innovation Fund. We also recognize the generosity of the East India Marine Associates of the Peabody Essex Museum.

More than 300 years later, the personal tragedies and serious injustices that took place still push us to reflect on and come to terms with the experiences of those involved. Learn the true story of this tragedy, told through the voices and personal belongings of the people directly affected. This ongoing exhibition presents the story using court documents and authentic historical objects—tangible fragments directly connected to individuals in Salem and nearby communities in the late 17th century. A handwritten petition, a carved loom, a walking stick—each item reveals something about the lives of those who lived through Salem’s witch trials and reminds us of the real people impacted by these terrifying events. *The Salem Witch Trials 1692* is organized by the Peabody Essex Museum. Hilton as supporters of the Exhibition Innovation Fund. We also recognize the generosity of the East India Marine Associates of the Peabody Essex Museum.

CuratorialText: The artist researched the museum’s vast and varied holdings, diving deep to find unexpected connections across time, cultures, and materials. Platacis turned her findings into 210 canvas stencils—all drawn and cut by hand with X-Acto blades. After applying the stencils to the walls, she and her assistant used a variety of spray-paint and graffiti techniques, including drop shadows, high contrast, and layering, to apply color to the walls and give dimensionality and life to her forms. Organic forms and curvilinear lines emerged as unifying design motifs that speak to the natural world. As you move deeper into these galleries, floral forms give way to enchanting fauna and larger-than-life presentations of familiar and unexpected objects. Platacis’ labor-intensive contemporary approach to painting connects to the artistry and skill embedded within the historical objects in PEM’s collection, creating a fascinating interplay between artistic practice then and now. What discoveries await? Located in the H.A. Crosby Forbes and Pamela Cunningham Copeland galleries.
persona3_adapted: The artist explored the museum’s extensive and diverse collection, looking deeply to find surprising links across different time periods, cultures, and materials. She turned what she discovered into 210 canvas stencils—all drawn and cut by hand with precision knives. After placing these stencils on the walls, she and her assistant used various spray-paint and street-art methods, such as adding shadows, creating strong contrasts, and building up layers, to apply color and give the forms a sense of depth and energy. Organic shapes and flowing, curved lines became the main visual themes, echoing the natural world. As you move further into these rooms, flower-like shapes give way to enchanting animals and larger-than-life versions of both familiar and surprising objects. Platacis’s hands-on, time-intensive approach to painting connects to the craftsmanship and skill found in the historical items in the museum’s collection, creating a compelling conversation between artistic methods from the past and present. What discoveries await you? Located in the H.A. Crosby Forbes and Pamela Cunningham Copeland galleries.

CuratorialText: As one of the 20th century’s most critically acclaimed and genre-blurring artists, Noguchi created a remarkable design legacy encompassing sculpture, lighting, furniture, ceramics and designs for public spaces such as gardens, playgrounds and plazas. Organized by the High Museum of Art in Atlanta, Isamu Noguchi: "I am not a designer" shines a light on the artist’s limitless creativity and social consciousness. Discover the evolution of Noguchi’s design work, particularly the way he created environments through theatrical set design, furniture and inclusive public spaces — all of which were intentionally designed to improve the way people lived. Many of his designs remain incredibly popular, such as the celebrated coffee table and Akari light sculptures that you may recognize in stores today. As Noguchi once said, “Everything is sculpture. Any material, any idea without hindrance born into space, I consider sculpture.” Noguchi also excelled at working collaboratively and forming close relationships with other creative professionals, particularly with modern dancer and choreographer Martha Graham, American architect R. Buckminster Fuller and Romanian sculptor and painter Constantin Brâncuși. These fruitful creative relationships are also highlighted within the exhibition.
persona3_adapted: As one of the most celebrated and genre-defying artists of the 20th century, Noguchi built a remarkable body of work that includes sculpture, lighting, furniture, ceramics, and designs for public spaces like gardens, playgrounds, and plazas. Organized by the High Museum of Art in Atlanta, *Isamu Noguchi: "I am not a designer"* explores the artist’s boundless creativity and his deep concern for society. The exhibition traces how Noguchi’s design work evolved—especially his approach to creating environments through theater set design, furniture, and public spaces that were open to everyone. All of these were intentionally shaped to improve the way people lived. Many of his designs remain hugely popular today, such as the well-known coffee table and the Akari light sculptures you might recognize in stores. As Noguchi once said, “Everything is sculpture. Any material, any idea without hindrance born into space, I consider sculpture.” Noguchi also had a gift for collaboration, forming close working relationships with other creative professionals—most notably modern dancer and choreographer Martha Graham, American architect R. Buckminster Fuller, and Romanian sculptor and painter Constantin Brâncuși. These fruitful partnerships are also highlighted in the exhibition.

CuratorialText: In the nearly two decades since PEM featured the crowd-pleasing exhibition Origami Now!, contemporary origami has continued to expand into exciting new styles of expression and invention. This multigenerational exhibition in the Dotty Brown Art & Nature Center highlights origami as not just an exciting art form, but also an important influence on new innovations in science, engineering, math and more. See works from leading origami artists, including Michael G. LaFosse, one of the world’s top origami masters and a Massachusetts resident. In addition to showcasing masterworks of origami, PEM will highlight what is emerging in contemporary origami and design, ranging from incredibly complex artistic models and folding nano structures, to origami-inspired emergency shelters. Investigate origami-inspired breakthroughs in robotics, medicine and sustainability. Interactive displays and video tutorials will give visitors of all ages hands-on opportunities to investigate and participate in the artistic process of origami.
persona3_adapted:
In the nearly two decades since PEM presented the popular exhibition *Origami Now!*, the art of paper folding has continued to evolve into exciting new forms of creativity and problem-solving. This exhibition, which brings together artists from different generations, is on view in the Dotty Brown Art & Nature Center. It shows that origami is not only a fascinating art form, but also an important source of ideas for new developments in science, engineering, mathematics, and other fields.

You’ll see works by leading origami artists, including Michael G. LaFosse, one of the world’s top origami masters and a Massachusetts resident. Alongside these masterpieces, the exhibition explores what’s new in contemporary origami and design—from highly complex artistic models and tiny folded structures at the nanoscale, to emergency shelters inspired by origami techniques. Discover how origami has led to breakthroughs in robotics, medicine, and sustainability. Interactive displays and video tutorials offer hands-on opportunities for visitors of all ages to explore and take part in the creative process of origami.

CuratorialText: View iconic contemporary artworks that reach back into millennia-old art practices. The Stars We Do Not See: Australian Indigenous Art is the largest exhibition of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander art ever presented in North America, representing the incredible breadth and depth of Contemporary Indigenous Australian art. The exhibition charts watershed moments in Australian Indigenous art, celebrating a vivid history of creativity that predates the arrival of Europeans by many thousands of years. The exhibition features nearly 200 artworks by more than 130 artists, among them several undisputed masterworks from the National Gallery of Victoria’s collection. Many of these significant works have never before left the country. The Stars We Do Not See is curated especially to introduce North American audiences to customary forms and styles, such as the conceptual map paintings of the Central and Western Desert Regions (sometimes colloquially referred to as “dot paintings”), ochre on bark paintings from far Northern Australia and ambitious experimental weavings. The exhibition also presents powerful works in neon, video and photography that acknowledge and innovate upon tradition.
persona3_adapted: View iconic contemporary artworks that draw on art-making traditions stretching back thousands of years. *The Stars We Do Not See: Australian Indigenous Art* is the largest exhibition of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander art ever presented in North America, showcasing the remarkable range and depth of contemporary Indigenous Australian art. The exhibition highlights key turning points in Australian Indigenous art, celebrating a vibrant history of creativity that began many thousands of years before Europeans arrived. It features nearly 200 works by more than 130 artists, including several widely recognized masterpieces from the National Gallery of Victoria’s collection. Many of these important works have never before left Australia. *The Stars We Do Not See* has been curated especially to introduce North American audiences to traditional forms and styles—such as the conceptual map paintings of the Central and Western Desert Regions (sometimes called “dot paintings”), paintings using natural ochre pigments on bark from far northern Australia, and bold experimental weavings. The exhibition also includes powerful works in neon, video, and photography that both acknowledge and build upon tradition.

CuratorialText: Come and experience the liveliness of a drinking party, the opulence of a royal wedding and poetic evocation of spring on a delicate dish. Discover plants and animals, myths and symbols and decipher the Chinese character for "Double Happiness." With more than 30 highlights from the museum's wide-ranging Chinese collection spanning 3,000 years, this exhibition celebrates China's artistic achievements crystallized in seasonal festivals, religious ceremonies and celebrations. Located in the Yin Yu Tang Interpretive Gallery.

Explore plants and animals, myths and symbols, and learn to recognize the Chinese character for "Double Happiness." Featuring more than 30 standout pieces from the museum's extensive Chinese collection, which spans 3,000 years, this exhibition celebrates China's artistic achievements as they come to life in seasonal festivals, religious ceremonies, and celebrations. The exhibition is located in the Yin Yu Tang Interpretive Gallery.

CuratorialText:
Thomas Mifflin (1744-1800) and his wife, Sarah Morris Mifflin (1747?-1790), were the only Philadelphians painted by John Singleton Copley, the greatest artist in the American colonies prior to the Revolution. Mifflin was an ardent patriot and by the time this portrait was made, had established himself as a successful merchant; later he rose to the rank of major general in the Continental Army, and was elected the first governor of Pennsylvania after the United States achieved independence. Sarah Morris, who married Thomas in 1767, was an accomplished, witty, and supportive partner. In the portrait, Sarah is weaving a decorative fringe on a portable loom, which may symbolize the couple's endorsement of the colonists' boycott of English goods.
Famous as one of Copley's finest works soon after it was painted, this portrait shows the artist at the height of his powers. Born in Boston in 1738 into a poor immigrant family, Copley was self-taught. He developed a highly finished style that rendered the features, costumes, and settings of his subjects with remarkable accuracy. Copley always kept a keen eye on his competition, and he may have been inspired to paint both Sarah and Thomas Mifflin on a single canvas by his contemporary Charles Willson Peale, who had recently completed a group portrait of the Philadelphia Cadwalader family that was admired for its portrayal of family unity and affection. Here, Copley depicts not only the features and costumes of his sitters with his famed skill, but creates an image of marriage as an affectionate, equal partnership--an innovative concept in American portraiture at the time.
persona3_adapted:
Thomas Mifflin (1744-1800) and his wife, Sarah Morris Mifflin (1747?-1790), were the only residents of Philadelphia painted by John Singleton Copley, widely considered the most accomplished artist in the American colonies before the Revolutionary War. Mifflin was a passionate supporter of American independence, and by the time this portrait was made, he had already built a successful career as a merchant. He later rose to the rank of major general in the Continental Army and was elected the first governor of Pennsylvania after the United States gained independence. Sarah Morris, who married Thomas in 1767, was a talented, witty, and supportive partner. In the portrait, Sarah is weaving a decorative fringe on a small, portable loom—a detail that may symbolize the couple’s support for the colonists’ boycott of British goods.

Famous as one of Copley’s finest works soon after it was painted, this portrait shows the artist at the peak of his abilities. Born in Boston in 1738 into a poor immigrant family, Copley taught himself to paint. He developed a highly polished style that captured the facial features, clothing, and surroundings of his subjects with remarkable precision. Copley always paid close attention to what other artists were doing, and he may have been inspired to paint both Sarah and Thomas Mifflin together on a single canvas by his contemporary Charles Willson Peale. Peale had recently completed a group portrait of the Philadelphia Cadwalader family, which was praised for showing family unity and affection. Here, Copley not only depicts the faces and clothing of his subjects with his famous skill, but also creates an image of marriage as a loving, equal partnership—a fresh and innovative idea in American portraiture at the time.

CuratorialText: Better known as a patriot during the American Revolution, Paul Revere was the most prolific and accomplished silversmith in late 1700s Boston. His shop produced domestic and ecclesiastical objects for clients belonging to the same civic, military, political, and religious organizations as Revere. The teapot’s inverted pear shape and engraved ornament of C-scrolls, foliage, and shells epitomizes the Rococo style of his early career. Equally renowned as an engraver of bookplates, trade cards, magazine illustrations, and political prints, Revere probably executed this decoration himself. John Singleton Copley’s famous 1768 portrait of Revere (Museum of Fine Arts, Boston) depicts the silversmith with engraving tools, holding a teapot with the same low, domed cover and slightly pointed, pinecone finial as this example. Could they be one and the same?
persona3_adapted: Better known as a patriot during the American Revolution, Paul Revere was the most productive and skilled silversmith in Boston during the late 1700s. His workshop created both everyday household items and objects for churches, serving clients who belonged to the same civic, military, political, and religious groups as Revere himself. The teapot’s inverted pear shape and its engraved decoration—featuring curving C-shaped scrolls, leaves, and shells—perfectly captures the Rococo style, a highly ornamental and playful artistic movement popular in Europe and America during his early career. Revere was also famous as an engraver, making personalized bookplates, business cards, magazine illustrations, and political cartoons. He likely created this decoration himself. John Singleton Copley’s famous 1768 portrait of Revere (Museum of Fine Arts, Boston) shows the silversmith holding engraving tools and a teapot with the same low, domed lid and slightly pointed, pinecone-shaped knob on top as this example. Could they be one and the same?

CuratorialText: Human rights and social issues often figure in Pippin’s work, but this painting is particularly overt in its treatment of racism. Clouds hover over a hooded member of the Ku Klux Klan and a burly white man holding a noose. The two menacing figures stand opposite the Statue of Liberty, here painted brown instead of green. At the center, Mr. Prejudice drives a wedge into a symbolic V for victory, segregating white and black machinists and servicemen. Pippin includes himself as one of the soldiers, wearing a World War I uniform, with his wounded right arm hanging at his side.
persona3_adapted: Human rights and social issues are common themes in Pippin’s work, but this painting addresses racism in a particularly direct way. Dark clouds hang over a hooded member of the Ku Klux Klan and a large, threatening white man holding a noose. These two menacing figures stand opposite the Statue of Liberty, which Pippin has painted brown instead of its usual green. At the center of the composition, a figure called Mr. Prejudice drives a wedge into a symbolic V for victory, splitting apart white and Black machinists and soldiers. Pippin includes himself among the soldiers, shown in a World War I uniform, with his wounded right arm hanging at his side.

CuratorialText: While the bed chambers of chic early Philadelphia houses often featured a high chest (for storing textiles; 1957-129-1) and dressing table (for cosmetics and hair accessories; 2012-59-1), this pairing represents the pinnacle of design, cabinetmaking, and carved ornament. The raised space underneath the high chest distinguishes it as a confection of North America since the British had abandoned that design by the 1730s. While most high chests and dressing tables fit within well-defined groups, the proportions and shaping of the rails and the legs of this pairing relate to only one other dressing table, making it difficult to identify the maker. The choice mahogany, with an even surface and swirling figure, signifies the status of the commission. And a highly skilled carver ornamented it profusely—most conspicuously with a narrative from Aesop’s fable of “The Fox and the Grapes” on the central bottom drawers. Aesop’s moralistic tales were the only fiction Quakers were allowed to read.
persona3_adapted:
In the bedrooms of fashionable early Philadelphia homes, it was common to find a tall chest for storing linens and a matching dressing table for cosmetics and hair accessories. However, the pair of furniture pieces described here represents the highest level of design, woodworking, and decorative carving. The raised space beneath the tall chest is a distinctly North American feature, because by the 1730s, British furniture makers had stopped using this design.
