While most tall chests and dressing tables fall into recognizable categories, the proportions and shaping of the horizontal rails and legs of this set are so unusual that they match only one other known dressing table. This makes it difficult to determine who made them. The use of high-quality mahogany, with its smooth surface and swirling grain pattern, signals the importance of the commission. A highly skilled carver decorated the piece extensively—most notably with a scene from Aesop’s fable “The Fox and the Grapes” on the central bottom drawers. For the Quaker families who owned such furniture, Aesop’s moral tales were the only fiction they were allowed to read.

CuratorialText: Pennsylvania-made Kleiderschranken (clothes presses) functioned, as did the earlier models in northern Europe, to hold hanging clothes and store cherished textiles. This schrank is notable for its extraordinary decoration—bold architectural paneling, carving, and lettering that records the name of its owner, Georg Huber of Manheim, Pennsylvania, and the date it was made, 1779, only a year before he married Barbara Oberholtzer. Rather than a lightwood inlay, the lettering and floral designs were made by pouring molten sulfur into channels carved in the walnut. Swiss and German designs inspired the decorative motifs, many of which are commonly found on other Pennsylvania German furniture as well as ceramics and fraktur, and helped to connect the owners and makers to their cultural roots. The wardrobe’s monumental size, bold composition, and profuse ornament demonstrate not only Huber’s aesthetic taste and wealth but also the skills and sensibilities of the Germanic artisans who made it.
persona3_adapted: This Pennsylvania-made *Kleiderschrank*—a type of large wardrobe or clothes press—served the same purpose as earlier versions from northern Europe: storing hanging clothes and safeguarding treasured textiles. What makes this particular piece stand out is its remarkable decoration: bold architectural paneling, carved details, and lettering that records the name of its owner, Georg Huber of Manheim, Pennsylvania, along with the date it was made, 1779—just a year before he married Barbara Oberholtzer. Instead of using lighter wood for inlay, the lettering and floral designs were created by pouring molten sulfur into channels carved into the walnut surface. The decorative patterns were inspired by Swiss and German designs, many of which appear frequently on other Pennsylvania German furniture, as well as on ceramics and a type of decorative manuscript known as *fraktur*. These motifs helped connect the owners and makers to their cultural heritage. The wardrobe’s enormous size, bold arrangement, and lavish ornamentation not only reflect Huber’s personal taste and wealth, but also showcase the skill and cultural background of the Germanic artisans who crafted it.

CuratorialText: Inscribed (translation): 1786 G. H. From the earth with sense the potter makes everything. Cadarina Raeder, her dish. German-speaking immigrants to Pennsylvania maintained the artistic traditions they brought from Europe, often retaining art forms and styles over several generations. This dish features decoration made using the centuries-old sgraffito technique, achieved by cutting through a slip coating to expose the red clay underneath. The verse in German around the border, extolling the skill of the potter, also shows an adherence to tradition: language provided an immediate means of holding onto ethnic identity. With such rich ornamentation, this dish was clearly not intended for use.
persona3_adapted:
Inscribed (translation): 1786 G. H. From the earth with sense the potter makes everything. Cadarina Raeder, her dish.

German-speaking immigrants who settled in Pennsylvania kept alive the artistic traditions they had brought from Europe, often preserving these art forms and styles across several generations. This dish is decorated using a centuries-old technique called sgraffito, in which the potter scratches through a thin layer of light-colored liquid clay (called slip) to reveal the darker red clay beneath. The German verse around the border, which praises the potter's skill, also reflects a commitment to tradition: language offered an immediate way to hold onto ethnic identity. Given its elaborate ornamentation, this dish was clearly not meant for everyday use.

CuratorialText: In 1795 the Philadelphia artist Charles Willson Peale painted this illusionistic portrait to showcase his artistic skills. At the same time, he wished to represent the roles played by two of his sons at Peale’s Museum, the first American museum created to educate and entertain the public. Titian Ramsay Peale, who crafted natural science displays, gestures at the top of the stairs as his older brother, Raphaelle, an artist with his palette in hand, strides upward just above a real step attached to the base of the canvas. The life-size portrait is known to have startled and delighted visitors, reputedly even George Washington, before the Peale Museum’s collection was dismantled and finally sold in 1854. Acquired by the Philadelphia Museum of Art in 1945, the picture continues to engage and welcome visitors.
persona3_adapted: In 1795, Philadelphia artist Charles Willson Peale painted this portrait, which uses a clever visual trick to make the scene look three-dimensional. He did this to show off his artistic skill, while also highlighting the different roles of two of his sons at Peale’s Museum—the first American museum created to both educate and entertain the public. In the painting, Titian Ramsay Peale, who built displays of natural science, gestures from the top of the stairs. His older brother, Raphaelle, an artist holding his palette, strides upward just above a real step attached to the bottom of the canvas. This life-size portrait reportedly startled and delighted visitors, even George Washington, before the Peale Museum’s collection was broken up and eventually sold in 1854. Acquired by the Philadelphia Museum of Art in 1945, the painting still engages and welcomes viewers today.

CuratorialText: A rare early representation of ethnic and religious diversity in the United States, and an outstanding example of Peale’s late naturalistic style, the picture is distinguished by the direct and sympathetic encounter between the artist and his subject and a skilled rendering of details. Yarrow’s knit cap suggests a kufi, a hat traditionally worn by African Muslim men to assert their religion or African identity, but Peale artfully employed its yellow band to highlight his sitter’s steady gaze with its glint of humor and wisdom. Seventy-seven years old when he created this portrait, Peale was seeking to record the traits that he believed supported a long life. In his writings and museum displays, Peale celebrated making wise choices to maintain good health and a positive attitude. He perceived Yarrow’s perseverance in the face of racism and enslavement as a model of resourcefulness, industriousness, and sobriety.
persona3_adapted: A rare early depiction of ethnic and religious diversity in the United States, this painting is also a fine example of Charles Willson Peale’s later naturalistic style—meaning he aimed to portray people and objects as they truly appeared, without idealizing them. What makes the work stand out is the direct, warm connection between the artist and his subject, as well as Peale’s skillful attention to detail. The knit cap worn by Yarrow suggests a kufi, a type of hat traditionally worn by African Muslim men to express their faith or African heritage. But Peale cleverly used the cap’s yellow band to draw attention to Yarrow’s steady gaze, which carries a hint of both humor and wisdom. At seventy-seven years old when he painted this portrait, Peale was interested in capturing the qualities he believed helped people live long lives. In his writings and museum displays, he celebrated making wise choices to maintain good health and a positive outlook. He saw Yarrow’s ability to endure racism and enslavement as a model of resourcefulness, hard work, and self-discipline.

CuratorialText: This chair is part of the elegantly painted furniture Latrobe designed for the Walns' drawing room. Decorated in rich red, yellow, and gilt on a black ground and incorporating classical motifs and symbols, the set of drawing room furniture consisted of a pier table, Grecian sofas, a pair of card tables, window benches, and chairs. Its legs that curved inwards make the chair the earliest American example of a Klismos chair, a forerunner of the classical style that enjoyed widespread popularity in the late 1810s and 1820s. In addition to this chair from the Waln set, the Museum's collection includes six more chairs, a Grecian sofa, a card table, and a pier table with a mirrored back.
persona3_adapted: This chair is part of a set of elegantly painted furniture that Latrobe designed for the Waln family’s drawing room. The pieces were decorated in rich red, yellow, and gold on a black background, and they featured designs and symbols from ancient Greek and Roman art. The set included a table meant to stand against a wall, sofas in the Greek style, a pair of card tables, window benches, and chairs. Because its legs curve inward, this chair is the earliest known American example of a Klismos chair—a type that helped introduce the classical style to the United States, which became very popular in the late 1810s and 1820s. Along with this chair from the Waln set, the Museum’s collection also holds six more chairs, a Grecian sofa, a card table, and a wall table with a mirrored back.

CuratorialText:
The Gross Clinic is recognized as one of the greatest American paintings ever made. The young and little-known Eakins created it specifically for Philadelphia’s 1876 Centennial Exhibition, intending to showcase his talents as an artist and to honor the scientific achievements of his native Philadelphia.
Choosing the city’s world-famous surgeon and teacher Dr. Samuel Gross as his subject, Eakins sets the scene in Jefferson Medical College’s surgical amphitheater. Dr. Gross is shown leading a clinic of five doctors operating on the left thigh of a patient. At the same time Gross is demonstrating to students the relatively new surgical procedure he had developed to treat bone infections. In contrast to the recoiling woman to the left—traditionally identified as the patient’s mother—Gross embodies the confidence that comes from knowledge and experience. Casting himself as a witness, Eakins can be seen seated to the right of the tunnel railing, sketching or writing.
persona3_adapted:
**The Gross Clinic** is widely regarded as one of the greatest American paintings ever created. The young and relatively unknown artist Thomas Eakins made it specifically for Philadelphia’s 1876 Centennial Exhibition, aiming to display his artistic skill and to celebrate the scientific achievements of his hometown.

Eakins chose as his subject Dr. Samuel Gross, a world-famous surgeon and teacher from Philadelphia. The scene takes place in the surgical amphitheater of Jefferson Medical College—a large, theater-like room where students could watch operations. Dr. Gross is shown leading a team of five doctors as they operate on a patient’s left thigh. At the same time, he is teaching the students about a relatively new surgical method he had developed for treating bone infections.

In contrast to the woman on the left, who recoils in distress—traditionally thought to be the patient’s mother—Dr. Gross embodies the calm confidence that comes from knowledge and experience. Eakins places himself in the scene as a witness; you can see him seated to the right of the tunnel-like railing, sketching or writing.

CuratorialText: The dramatic rescue from a foundering ship shown here was made possible by a recent innovation in lifesaving technology, the breeches buoy. Secured firmly to ship and shore, the device permitted the transfer of stranded passengers to safety by means of a pulley that was hauled back and forth by crews at either end. Cropped down to its essentials, Homer's composition thrusts us into the midst of the action with massive waves rolling past, drenching the semiconscious woman and her anonymous savior. The Life Line was immediately recognized by critics as a major contribution to American art, portraying a heroic, contemporary subject with both painterly virtuosity and detailed observation.
persona3_adapted: The dramatic rescue from a sinking ship shown here was made possible by a recent breakthrough in lifesaving technology: the breeches buoy. This device, securely fastened to both the ship and the shore, allowed stranded passengers to be moved to safety using a pulley system that crews on either end pulled back and forth. By trimming the scene down to its most essential elements, Homer's composition pulls us directly into the action, with massive waves rolling past and soaking the barely conscious woman and her unknown rescuer. *The Life Line* was immediately hailed by critics as a landmark work in American art, portraying a heroic, everyday subject with both impressive technical skill and careful attention to detail.

CuratorialText: Mary Cassatt’s flair for digging into an ordinary moment and distilling its emotions stands out in this portrayal of three individuals lulled by a carriage ride into silent reverie. Once titled simply Driving, the painting depicts an outing in the Bois de Boulogne, a large wooded park on the western edge of Paris. The driver of the carriage is Lydia Cassatt, sister of the artist, and the girl seated primly beside her is Odile Fèvre, niece of the painter Edgar Degas. The bored-looking groom is unidentified. Usually associated with domestic interiors and the private lives of women and children, here Cassatt explored an outdoor setting and a degree of autonomy enjoyed by upper-class women. Driving in the park was something the Cassatt sisters enjoyed, particularly after 1879, when the family acquired a small carriage and Bichette, the horse partly depicted here
persona3_adapted: Mary Cassatt had a gift for capturing an ordinary moment and drawing out its emotional depth—something that shines in this scene of three people quietly lost in thought during a carriage ride. Originally titled simply *Driving*, the painting shows an outing in the Bois de Boulogne, a large wooded park on the western edge of Paris. The driver is Lydia Cassatt, the artist’s sister, and the girl sitting neatly beside her is Odile Fèvre, the niece of painter Edgar Degas. The bored-looking groom is not identified. While Cassatt is best known for scenes set inside homes, focusing on the private lives of women and children, here she turns to an outdoor setting and explores a kind of freedom that upper-class women could enjoy. Driving in the park was something the Cassatt sisters liked to do, especially after 1879, when the family bought a small carriage and the horse partly shown here, named Bichette.

CuratorialText: Tanner painted The Annunciation soon after returning to Paris from a trip to Egypt and Palestine in 1897. The son of a minister in the African Methodist Episcopal Church, Tanner specialized in religious subjects, and wanted to experience the people, culture, architecture, and light of the Holy Land. Influenced by what he saw, Tanner created an unconventional image of the moment when the angel Gabriel announces to Mary that she will bear the Son of God. Mary is shown as an adolescent dressed in rumpled Middle Eastern peasant clothing, without a halo or other holy attributes. Gabriel appears only as a shaft of light. Tanner entered this painting in the 1898 Paris Salon exhibition, after which it was bought for the Philadelphia Museum of Art in 1899, making it his first work to enter an American museum.
persona3_adapted: Tanner painted *The Annunciation* shortly after returning to Paris from a trip to Egypt and Palestine in 1897. The son of a minister in the African Methodist Episcopal Church, Tanner focused on religious subjects and wanted to experience the people, culture, architecture, and light of the Holy Land firsthand. Influenced by what he saw, Tanner created an unconventional image of the moment when the angel Gabriel tells Mary she will give birth to the Son of God. Mary is shown as a young teenager dressed in rumpled Middle Eastern peasant clothing, without a halo or other traditional signs of holiness. Gabriel appears only as a shaft of light. Tanner entered this painting in the 1898 Paris Salon, a major annual art exhibition, after which it was bought for the Philadelphia Museum of Art in 1899—making it his first work to enter an American museum.

CuratorialText: A father’s love radiates from this iridescent painting of sunlight and youth. Shaped by the realist tradition of Thomas Eakins at the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts, Daniel Garber adopted Impressionist color and handling during a two-year sojourn in Europe. Returning in 1907, he settled into a farm along Cuttalossa Creek in Bucks County, Pennsylvania, where he harmoniously merged nature, art, and domestic life. In this portrait of his daughter, Tanis, light from the garden outside his studio renders her smock translucent, while the composition’s serene geometry captures the shimmer of an endless summer afternoon.
persona3_adapted: A father’s love shines through this luminous painting of sunlight and youth. Daniel Garber was trained in the realistic style of Thomas Eakins at the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts, but during a two-year stay in Europe, he began to adopt the vivid colors and loose brushwork of the Impressionists. When he returned to the United States in 1907, he settled on a farm along Cuttalossa Creek in Bucks County, Pennsylvania. There, he blended the natural world, his art, and family life into a seamless whole. In this portrait of his daughter, Tanis, light from the garden outside his studio makes her smock appear almost see-through, while the calm, balanced arrangement of shapes and forms captures the glow of a seemingly endless summer afternoon.

CuratorialText: Georgia O’Keeffe once remarked, “What is my experience of the flower if not color?” This painting of two calla lilies is an extraordinary example of her floral compositions, made of sweeping, broad waves of subtly blended hues. The white petals, highlighted in green, are arranged against a pink backdrop, and from each one emerges a bright yellow pistil. Many have interpreted O’Keeffe’s depictions of floral anatomy in relation to sexuality and gender, but the artist always resisted these interpretations, considering them too specific and limiting.
persona3_adapted: Georgia O’Keeffe once said, “What is my experience of the flower if not color?” This painting of two calla lilies is a remarkable example of her flower-themed works, built from flowing, wide strokes of gently blended colors. The white petals, touched with green, stand out against a pink background, and from each petal rises a bright yellow central part of the flower (called a pistil). Many viewers have linked O’Keeffe’s close-up images of flowers to ideas about sexuality and gender, but the artist always rejected these readings, feeling they were too narrow and limiting.

CuratorialText: Wharton Esherick’s carved interiors are among his most important early sculptural work. Inspired by the angular shapes and intersecting lines of the Cubist movement, the artist incorporated the natural patterns and grains of assorted woods into his pieces to emphasize their strong geometric and organic forms. Born in Philadelphia in 1887, Esherick studied painting at the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts from 1908–10, and discovered wood as a medium for artistic expression when he began to make carved frames for his canvases. By the mid-1920s he was fully committed to sculpture and extended his definition of the medium to the carving of furniture and architectural interiors. His work is often seen as a forerunner to the contemporary studio furniture movement.
persona3_adapted: Wharton Esherick’s carved interiors rank among his most significant early works as a sculptor. Inspired by the sharp angles and intersecting lines found in Cubism—an early 20th-century art movement that broke subjects down into geometric shapes—Esherick used the natural patterns and grain lines of different types of wood to highlight the strong, balanced shapes and organic curves in his pieces. Born in Philadelphia in 1887, he first studied painting at the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts from 1908 to 1910. He discovered wood as a material for artistic expression when he began carving frames for his own paintings. By the mid-1920s, he had fully dedicated himself to sculpture, expanding his idea of what sculpture could be to include carved furniture and entire room interiors. His work is often considered an early influence on what is now known as the studio furniture movement, in which artists create one-of-a-kind furniture pieces as works of art.

CuratorialText: Andrew Wyeth shared the realist approach of many mid-twentieth-century painters of the American scene, although his process of simplification and synthesis often led to haunting, surrealist effects. His picture of his neighbor Karl Kuerner’s light-filled kitchen holds an air of something missing. Wyeth explained that the painting was about “the winter light, the dishes ready for Karl Kuerner’s lunch—that is, peace, yes, but to me behind it is violence suppressed.” The snarling log outside the window, like the knife on the table, connected in the artist’s mind to Kuerner’s wartime experience, his violent dog, and his strong hand on the entire farm. Said Wyeth, “I wanted to get down to the very essence of the man who wasn’t there.”
persona3_adapted: Andrew Wyeth shared the realist approach of many mid-20th-century American painters, meaning he aimed to depict everyday scenes with clarity and accuracy. However, his way of simplifying and combining elements often created strange, dreamlike effects. This painting shows the sunlit kitchen of his neighbor, Karl Kuerner, yet it feels like something is absent. Wyeth explained that the work was about “the winter light, the dishes ready for Karl Kuerner’s lunch—that is, peace, yes, but to me behind it is violence suppressed.” The twisted, gnarled log visible outside the window, along with the knife on the table, were linked in the artist’s mind to Kuerner’s experiences in war, his aggressive dog, and his firm control over the entire farm. Wyeth said, “I wanted to get down to the very essence of the man who wasn’t there.”

CuratorialText: In November 1943 this painting was included in Jackson Pollock's stunning solo exhibition at Art of This Century, the innovative New York art gallery run by legendary art patron Peggy Guggenheim. As its title suggests, the work depicts a male figure, embodied in the black columnar form at right, with its mysterious arithmetic graffiti, and a curvy female figure at left, with marvelous catlike eyelashes. Standing on tiny triangular feet, both figures are surrounded by splashed and smeared oil paint that enhances the sense of energy inherent in the two vertical forms, while also foreshadowing the famous "drip" technique that Pollock would develop later that decade.
persona3_adapted: In November 1943, this painting was featured in Jackson Pollock's remarkable solo exhibition at Art of This Century—a cutting-edge New York gallery run by the legendary art collector and supporter Peggy Guggenheim. As the title suggests, the work shows a male figure, represented by the dark, column-like shape on the right, covered in mysterious, number-like markings. On the left, a curvy female figure appears, with striking, catlike eyelashes. Both figures stand on tiny, triangular feet and are surrounded by splashed and smeared oil paint. This energetic application of paint heightens the sense of movement in the two upright forms, while also hinting at the famous "drip" technique Pollock would develop later in the 1940s.

CuratorialText: Here you will find Benjamin Franklin as you’ve never seen him before. Philadelphia-born artist Roberto Lugo riffs on the traditional representations of Franklin found throughout the city—ones that celebrate him as one of the Founding Fathers of the United States. You can see one such stately portrayal of Franklin directly behind you, in gallery 384. Lugo places Franklin within a constellation of songs, symbols, and rappers’ names—ones that shaped his childhood and adult life. The artist also includes his own self-portrait on the other side of the vase, creating an imagined dialogue between himself and Franklin around the themes of money, history, violence, and success.
persona3_adapted: Here you will find Benjamin Franklin as you’ve never seen him before. Philadelphia-born artist Roberto Lugo takes inspiration from the traditional images of Franklin found throughout the city—images that honor him as one of the Founding Fathers of the United States. (You can see one such dignified portrait of Franklin directly behind you, in gallery 384.) Instead of repeating that familiar version, Lugo places Franklin within a constellation of songs, symbols, and the names of rappers—cultural references that shaped his own childhood and adult life. The artist also includes a self-portrait on the other side of the vase, creating an imagined conversation between himself and Franklin about money, history, violence, and success.

CuratorialText: Florine Stettheimer set this scene like a stage, framed by a grand red staircase at left and a long red drape mimicking a theater curtain at right. The depicted customers of Bendel’s, the famous Manhattan department store, lack the decorum and manners expected of women in this period. Instead, they frantically dive for clearance items, change clothes, inspect garments, and examine their mirrored reflections. Stettheimer lets us see "behind the curtain" to observe these women during a moment of unabashed liberation. The action-packed canvas contains an astonishing thirty-two people and two dogs, including the Pekingese at the forefront who serves as a surrogate for the artist, as indicated by the "FS"-monogrammed sweater. Henri Bendel, the store’s queer owner, stands in the left foreground. Opposite is an androgynous salesperson, whose appearance and pose—slicked-back platinum hair, rosy lips, and delicate hand gesture—align with how white gay men often presented themselves at the time.
persona3_adapted:
Florine Stettheimer arranged this scene like a stage performance, with a grand red staircase on the left and a long red curtain on the right that mimics a theater drape. The shoppers shown here are at Bendel’s, a famous department store in Manhattan, but they do not behave with the restraint and good manners expected of women at the time. Instead, they frantically grab sale items, change clothes, inspect garments, and study their own reflections in mirrors. Stettheimer allows us to see "behind the curtain" and observe these women in a moment of open, unapologetic freedom.

The busy painting includes an astonishing thirty-two people and two dogs. One of the dogs, a Pekingese in the foreground, wears a sweater with the initials "FS," suggesting it stands in for the artist herself. Henri Bendel, the store’s owner, who was gay, appears in the left foreground. Across from him is a salesperson whose appearance is androgynous—with slicked-back platinum hair, rosy lips, and a delicate hand gesture—matching the way many white gay men of the time chose to present themselves.

CuratorialText: Three female immortals wear cloaks and skirts made of grasses and leaves. Their hairy bodies identify them as the Feathered Women (mao nü) of Daoist lore, who live deep in the mountains searching for herbs. Here they show each other what they have gathered. The figure at the far left holds a lingzhi fungus (thought to bestow longevity if ingested) and a basket of plants is draped over her arm. The focus of the painting is on a large health-benefiting ginseng root displayed in the hand of the central immortal. Other herbs depicted include leaves of banana and palm tree carried in the baskets on the women’s backs. The representation of Feathered Women exhibiting precious medicinal items functions as a special talisman, protecting the owner from illness.
persona3_adapted: Three female immortals wear cloaks and skirts made of grasses and leaves. Their hairy bodies identify them as the Feathered Women (mao nü) from Daoist tradition, who live deep in the mountains searching for herbs. Here they show each other what they have gathered. The figure on the far left holds a lingzhi fungus—believed to grant long life if eaten—and a basket of plants hangs from her arm. The painting’s main focus is a large ginseng root, known for its health benefits, displayed in the hand of the central immortal. Other herbs include banana and palm leaves carried in the baskets on the women’s backs. The depiction of Feathered Women presenting precious medicinal items serves as a special talisman, meant to protect the owner from illness.

CuratorialText:
This delicate porcelain lantern is so light in the hand that it feels almost weightless. Candles placed within it would have glowed and flickered through the intricate lattice designs, which required great skill to achieve due to the extremely thin clay. It was not until the early 1700s that artists developed this piercing technique.

Auspicious Decoration

The four flowers within the central medallions on the six sides—the camellia, rose, peony, and lily—convey peace, unity, and longevity.
persona3_adapted:
This delicate porcelain lantern is so light in the hand that it feels almost weightless. When candles were placed inside, their light would have glowed and flickered through the intricate lattice patterns—a design that required great skill to create because the clay had to be extremely thin. Artists did not develop this technique of piercing the clay until the early 1700s.

Auspicious Decoration

On each of the six sides, four flowers appear inside central medallions: the camellia, rose, peony, and lily. Together, they are meant to convey peace, unity, and longevity.

CuratorialText:
Various deva, or guardian deities, are depicted on this fragment from the outer section of a large mural that once decorated the hall of a Buddhist temple. Murals representing such figures, richly dressed in costumes and adorned with jewelry, are typical of northern Chinese temples built from the late Yuan (1271–1368) to the early Ming dynasties (1368–1644), when the ruling elite embraced a mix of Chinese and Tibetan Buddhism. Although there is some loss of color, we can identify several of the deities:
(Far left) The guardian of Buddhist temples and protector of the faith, Weituo (Skanda in Sanskrit), wears a winged helmet. His diamond sword destroys demons and Buddhism’s enemies.
(Center) The Heavenly King of the West brandishes a snake, while the Heavenly King of the North holds an umbrella and stupa/pagoda.
(Right) The protective goddess Mārīcī has three faces—one frontal and two in profile—and multiple arms.
persona3_adapted:
Various guardian deities, or deva, appear on this fragment from the outer section of a large wall painting that once decorated the hall of a Buddhist temple. Wall paintings showing such figures, richly dressed in costumes and adorned with jewelry, are typical of northern Chinese temples built from the late Yuan (1271–1368) to the early Ming dynasties (1368–1644), when the ruling elite embraced a mix of Chinese and Tibetan Buddhism. Although some of the color has faded, we can identify several of the deities:

(Far left) Weituo (known as Skanda in Sanskrit), the guardian of Buddhist temples and protector of the faith, wears a winged helmet. His diamond sword destroys demons and Buddhism’s enemies.

(Center) The Heavenly King of the West brandishes a snake, while the Heavenly King of the North holds an umbrella and a stupa—a dome-shaped structure that serves as a Buddhist shrine.

(Right) The protective goddess Mārīcī has three faces—one facing forward and two in profile—and multiple arms.

CuratorialText: The Zhihua monastery is one of the great Buddhist temple compounds in the Chinese capital of Beijing. Conceived on a grand scale, the compound was built in the early 1400s by the eunuch Wang Zhen. This ceiling comes from the second of five main halls on the monastery's central axis—the Hall of Great Wisdom. To give visitors a sense of the original context, the Museum installed the ceiling with architectural elements reconstructed from measured drawings made at the original site. In the center is a writhing imperial dragon surrounded by clouds, bracketing, and eight canted panels, each bearing a smaller dragon among clouds. Lotuses, apsaras (Buddhist flying musicians), and other Buddhist religious symbols are carved in the surrounding panels. The Chinese name for this central part of the ceiling is tianjing, or "well of heaven." While most of the original red lacquer is well preserved, much of the rich overlay of gold leaf has been lost. A series of model temples supported by cloud-decorated brackets encompass the central element; within these miniature structures are delicately carved sculptures of Buddhas and bodhisattvas. The remainder of the ceiling consists of square panels painted with stylized lotus flowers that show the influence of Tibetan Buddhism (Lamaism). Each bears a character from a North Indian script (called Lantsha by the Tibetans) that symbolically recreates the universe. The model temples and panels may have been added during repairs made in the Kangxi period (1662–1722).
persona3_adapted:
The Zhihua Monastery is one of the great Buddhist temple complexes in Beijing, China’s capital. Built on a grand scale, it was constructed in the early 1400s by a eunuch named Wang Zhen. The ceiling you see here originally came from the second of five main halls along the monastery’s central axis—the Hall of Great Wisdom. To help visitors imagine the original setting, the Museum has installed the ceiling with reconstructed architectural elements based on detailed drawings made at the original site.

At the center of the ceiling is a twisting imperial dragon surrounded by clouds, structural brackets, and eight angled panels, each featuring a smaller dragon among clouds. Carved into the surrounding panels are lotuses, apsaras (Buddhist flying musicians), and other Buddhist religious symbols. The Chinese name for this central part of the ceiling is *tianjing*, or "well of heaven." While most of the original red lacquer is still in good condition, much of the rich gold leaf that once covered it has worn away.

Around this central element is a series of model temples, supported by cloud-decorated brackets. Inside these miniature structures are delicately carved sculptures of Buddhas and bodhisattvas—enlightened beings who delay their own salvation to help others. The rest of the ceiling is made up of square panels painted with stylized lotus flowers, which show the influence of Tibetan Buddhism (also known as Lamaism). Each panel contains a character from a North Indian script (called Lantsha by Tibetans) that symbolically recreates the universe. The model temples and panels may have been added during repairs made in the Kangxi period (1662–1722).

CuratorialText: Educator and collector Ernest Fenollosa installed this monumental painting by Kano Hōgai in his home in Tokyo, where it held pride of place. The artist’s highly naturalistic rendering of intertwined dragons reflects a fusion of East and West. While the subject of dragons dates back to ancient China, the three-dimensionality and foreshortening of the animals’ forms reflect Hōgai’s familiarity with the Western use of perspective.
persona3_adapted: Educator and collector Ernest Fenollosa displayed this large-scale painting by Kano Hōgai in his Tokyo home, where it occupied the most prominent position. The artist’s remarkably lifelike depiction of intertwined dragons shows a blending of Eastern and Western artistic traditions. Although dragons as a subject have ancient Chinese origins, the animals appear three-dimensional and their forms are shown at sharp angles—techniques that reveal Hōgai’s knowledge of Western perspective.

CuratorialText: Fifty-eight sixteenth-century granite architectural elements are presented within the museum gallery in an arrangement designed to resemble a South Indian temple hall (mandapam). In 1913 these pieces lay discarded at the small Madanagopalaswamy Temple in Madurai, where a Philadelphia couple purchased them while on their two-year-long honeymoon. But recent research supports that the majority were created for the large Kudal Alagar Temple nearby. There they had been part of a shrine to the goddess Lakshmi, wife of the Hindu deity Vishnu. This shrine had become dilapidated, and the temple authorities dismantled it by 1907, completing its replacement by 1923. Stone slabs between the lion brackets depict several scenes from the Ramayana, the epic tale of one of Vishnu's earthly incarnations. They once formed parts of a carved frieze showing the entire story. The life-size figures projecting from pillars are deities and mythological characters, including from the Ramayana. The many small images on the pillars include gods and heroes as well as humans (like an architect with his measuring stick).
persona3_adapted:
Fifty-eight granite architectural pieces from the 1500s are displayed in the museum gallery, arranged to resemble a temple hall from South India, known as a *mandapam*. In 1913, these fragments were found discarded at the small Madanagopalaswamy Temple in Madurai, where a Philadelphia couple purchased them during their two-year honeymoon. However, recent research suggests that most of these pieces were originally made for the larger Kudal Alagar Temple nearby. There, they had been part of a shrine dedicated to the goddess Lakshmi, the wife of the Hindu god Vishnu. That shrine had fallen into disrepair, and the temple authorities took it apart by 1907, completing its replacement by 1923.

Stone slabs placed between the lion-shaped brackets show several scenes from the *Ramayana*, the epic story of one of Vishnu’s earthly incarnations. These slabs were once part of a carved band, or frieze, that depicted the entire tale. The life-sized figures projecting from the pillars represent gods and mythological characters, including some from the *Ramayana*. The many small images carved into the pillars include gods, heroes, and even human figures—such as an architect holding his measuring stick.

CuratorialText: Enslaved Black and free white craftsmen worked side by side across the southern landscape.  Over the past thirty years researchers at MESDA have identified nearly 6500 craftsmen who enslaved people.  In fact, this fact is among the most common things we know about an identified craftsman.  Often anonymous, those who were enslaved by craftsmen were often at work on building sites, at the potters’ wheels, in cabinet shops, at iron foundries, behind the counters of silversmiths’ shops, and at dozens of other trades.  Their skills are revealed in the objects that they helped to fashion.  Their identities are often lost to history. A line in a legal document, or a brief mention in an advertisement is often all we know about an enslaved craftsman – but careful reading of period documents by MESDA researchers has identified nearly 3000 enslaved craftsmen by name.  The exhibition “Black and White all Mix’d Together”: The Hidden Legacy of Enslaved Craftsmen looks at icons of southern decorative arts through the lens of research conducted by MESDA over more than thirty years.  It recovers the stories of the many named and nameless individuals whose hidden hands shaped the southern landscape.
persona3_adapted: Enslaved Black and free white craftsmen worked side by side across the southern landscape. Over the past thirty years, researchers at MESDA have identified nearly 6,500 craftsmen who enslaved people. In fact, this is one of the most common facts we know about a craftsman once we identify him. Often anonymous, those enslaved by these craftsmen were hard at work on building sites, at potters’ wheels, in furniture workshops, at iron foundries, behind the counters of silversmiths’ shops, and in dozens of other trades. Their skills are revealed in the objects they helped to create. Their identities, however, are often lost to history. A line in a legal document, or a brief mention in an advertisement, is often all we know about an enslaved craftsman—but careful reading of historical documents by MESDA researchers has identified nearly 3,000 enslaved craftsmen by name. The exhibition *“Black and White all Mix’d Together”: The Hidden Legacy of Enslaved Craftsmen* examines iconic examples of southern decorative arts through the lens of research conducted by MESDA over more than thirty years. It recovers the stories of the many named and nameless individuals whose hidden hands shaped the southern landscape.

CuratorialText: North Carolina’s Piedmont region is known around the world for the manufacture of fine furniture.  However, long before the factories of Lexington, Thomasville, Hickory, and High Point came into existence, the Piedmont was home to a diverse group of cabinetmakers who crafted distinctive regional furniture. By the middle of the eighteenth century, immigrants from Pennsylvania, Maryland, and Virginia began pouring into the North Carolina Piedmont in search of inexpensive yet fertile land.  By 1800 about 250,000 Scotch-Irish, English, and German settlers called the region home.  Cabinetmakers immediately utilized the abundant walnut, cherry, pine and poplar trees to produce well-made, stylish furniture for their homes.  In most cases, these craftsmen combined national trends with local idiosyncratic preferences to produce regional furniture with a distinctive style and appearance. This exhibit is drawn from MESDA’s extensive collection of Piedmont North Carolina furniture made between 1780 and 1860.
persona3_adapted: The Piedmont region of North Carolina is famous worldwide for producing high-quality furniture. But long before the big furniture factories in towns like Lexington, Thomasville, Hickory, and High Point were built, the area was home to a wide range of skilled woodworkers who made furniture with a distinct local character. In the mid-1700s, immigrants from Pennsylvania, Maryland, and Virginia began moving into the North Carolina Piedmont in large numbers, looking for land that was both affordable and rich for farming. By 1800, around 250,000 settlers—mostly of Scotch-Irish, English, and German background—lived in the region. These woodworkers quickly made use of the plentiful walnut, cherry, pine, and poplar trees to create well-crafted, fashionable furniture for their homes. In most cases, they blended popular styles from across the country with their own local tastes and preferences, resulting in furniture that had a unique look and feel. This exhibit features pieces from MESDA’s large collection of Piedmont North Carolina furniture made between 1780 and 1860.

CuratorialText: Masterfully decorated slipware, sculptural bottles, refined creamware, and faience are but part of the rich artistic legacy of North Carolina’s first earthenware potters. During the last half of the eighteenth century, artisans of European descent introduced a variety of Old World ceramics traditions to the Carolina backcountry. From both an artistic and technical standpoint, the work of these craftsmen often surpassed that of their Middle Atlantic and New England contemporaries. North Carolina potters transformed the simplest of materials into vessels of practical utility, astonishing beauty, and deep cultural significance. This exhibit is the first major survey of this work an the context in which it was created.
persona3_adapted: Masterfully decorated pottery with painted designs, sculptural bottles, refined tableware with a smooth, cream-colored finish, and tin-glazed earthenware are just part of the rich artistic legacy of North Carolina’s first potters who worked in earthenware—a type of clay fired at relatively low temperatures. During the last half of the eighteenth century, artisans of European descent brought a variety of Old World ceramic traditions to the Carolina backcountry. From both an artistic and technical standpoint, the work of these craftsmen often surpassed that of their counterparts in the Middle Atlantic and New England regions. North Carolina potters transformed the simplest of materials into vessels that were practical for everyday use, astonishingly beautiful, and deeply meaningful within their culture. This exhibit is the first major survey of this work and the context in which it was created.

CuratorialText: Salem was the largest of North Carolina’s Moravian communities and from the moment of arrival the earliest settlers documented their landscape with a variety of detailed maps, surveys, drawings, and paintings. Salem was founded in 1766 as a congregation town with the Church overseeing all activities—financial, social, and spiritual. The Church owned all land and leased it to individuals who owned only the improvements. Salem was a bustling trade center with a mill, pottery, tannery, brewery, and store. But the earliest landscapes emphasize the centrality of the Church. They depict the town’s conscious design around a central square, the heart of the community. The square was home to the Gemein Haus (and later Home Church), where religious services were held; the large communal dwellings for Single Brothers and Single Sisters; the schools for young boys and girls; and the store. Gardens and fields appear nestled in and around the most prominent buildings in early views, suggesting the town’s growing success both agriculturally and commercially. As the nineteenth century progressed, the residents lobbied for more personal control. In 1849, the Church agreed to sell land to create the neighboring town of Winston, and in 1856 it abandoned the lease-hold system in favor of private land ownership. Manufacturing enterprises grew, and the railroad’s arrival in 1873 set the stage for tremendous growth in local industries. Salem—once closed to outsiders—became closely intertwined with the town of Winston. The textile and tobacco industries flourished, and by 1900 the twin cities of Winston and Salem had become the largest metropolitan area in North Carolina.
persona3_adapted:
Salem was the largest of North Carolina’s Moravian communities, and from the moment the first settlers arrived, they recorded their surroundings through a wide range of detailed maps, surveys, drawings, and paintings. Founded in 1766 as a congregation town, the Church oversaw all aspects of life—financial, social, and spiritual. The Church owned all the land and leased it to individuals, who owned only the buildings and improvements they made on it.

Despite being a busy trade center with a mill, pottery workshop, tannery, brewery, and store, the earliest images of the town focus on the central role of the Church. These pictures highlight how the town was deliberately arranged around a central square, which served as the community’s heart. This square contained the Gemein Haus (later called Home Church), where religious services were held; the large communal homes for Single Brothers and Single Sisters; the schools for young boys and girls; and the store. In these early views, gardens and fields appear nestled in and around the most important buildings, suggesting the town’s growing success in both farming and commerce.

As the 1800s progressed, residents pushed for more personal control over their lives and property. In 1849, the Church agreed to sell land to create the neighboring town of Winston, and in 1856 it abandoned the lease-only system in favor of allowing private land ownership. Manufacturing businesses grew, and when the railroad arrived in 1873, it set the stage for a huge expansion of local industries. Salem—once closed to outsiders—became closely connected with the town of Winston. The textile and tobacco industries thrived, and by 1900 the twin cities of Winston and Salem had become the largest metropolitan area in North Carolina.

CuratorialText:
James River Valley pottery has been the subject of archaeological and collector interest for over half a century.  The James River and its tributaries were home to more than a dozen potteries before the Civil War.  Potters hailing from New York, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, Maryland, Ireland and England, created a rich  hybrid tradition with forms and decorations adapted from their regional styles and wrought in local clays dug from Kaolin to Claymount.

Transcending their status as utilitarian objects, these pots, many exhibited here for the first time, provide a remarkable record and a unique glimpse into a significant and long-lived concentration of pottery manufacturing in eastern Virginia.  Viewed collectively, they present a bold decorative aesthetic rivaling long-recognized and celebrated schools of production seen elsewhere in the commonwealth and the Nation.
Today, many of these important historic sites are threatened by development.  Fortunately, archaeologists and decorative arts scholars are working ahead of the bulldozers to document and understand the important legacy of the James River Potteries before they are lost forever.  At the end of 2013 the Chipstone Foundation journal Ceramics in America will publish an extensive article about the James River Valley stoneware potteries based on this ongoing research.
This exhibition was on view at MESDA from November 2012 through March 2012. It was co-curated by Kurt Russ, Oliver Mueller-Heubach, Marshall
persona3_adapted:
James River Valley pottery has drawn the interest of archaeologists and collectors for more than fifty years. Before the Civil War, the James River and its surrounding waterways were home to over a dozen pottery workshops. Potters who came from New York, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, Maryland, Ireland, and England created a rich blend of traditions. They shaped their pots using forms and decorations borrowed from their home regions, but made them from local clays—ranging from a fine white clay called kaolin to a darker clay from a place called Claymount.

These pots go beyond being simple everyday objects. Many are on display here for the first time, and together they offer a remarkable record and a unique window into a major, long-lasting center of pottery production in eastern Virginia. Seen as a group, they show a bold decorative style that can stand alongside the well-known and celebrated pottery traditions found elsewhere in Virginia and across the country.

Today, many of these important historical sites are at risk from new construction. Fortunately, archaeologists and experts in decorative arts are working ahead of the bulldozers to document and understand the lasting importance of the James River Potteries before they are lost forever. At the end of 2013, the Chipstone Foundation’s journal *Ceramics in America* will publish a detailed article about the James River Valley stoneware potteries, based on this ongoing research.

This exhibition was on view at MESDA from November 2012 through March 2012. It was co-curated by Kurt Russ, Oliver Mueller-Heubach, and Marshall.

CuratorialText:
An Irish schoolmaster. A German Jewish silversmith. An African Muslim scholar. A multi-national female pastelist. A Scottish cabinetmaker. A potter, a carpenter, an engraver. The lives of these individuals share a common thread: they were “lately from” somewhere else and came to the American South in the 18th and early 19th centuries.

This exhibition features objects created by these craftspeople whose origins spanned continents and oceans. As much as possible, it connects those objects with the words of their makers through letters, inscriptions, autobiographies, account books, advertisements, and even drawings. Together, they tell the story of journeys taken, communities forged in new homes, responses to changing political climates, and the creative blending of traditions into something new.
persona3_adapted:
An Irish schoolmaster. A German Jewish silversmith. An African Muslim scholar. A female pastelist from multiple countries. A Scottish cabinetmaker. A potter, a carpenter, an engraver. The lives of these individuals share a common thread: they were “lately from” somewhere else and came to the American South in the 1700s and early 1800s.

This exhibition features objects made by these skilled workers, whose origins spanned continents and oceans. Where possible, it connects those objects with the makers’ own words—through letters, inscriptions, autobiographies, account books, advertisements, and even drawings. Together, these items and writings tell the story of journeys taken, communities built in new homes, responses to changing political climates, and the creative blending of traditions into something new.

CuratorialText: Early Southerners drank a lot — nearly twenty gallons of alcohol each — every year. They drank when they woke up in the morning, as they ate and worked during the day, and when they socialized at night. But how people drank was as important as what they drank. From imported Madeira sipped at mahogany tables to local whisky out of redware jugs, this exhibit explores the decorative arts that defined the early Southern drinker.
persona3_adapted: Early Southerners consumed a great deal of alcohol—nearly twenty gallons per person each year. They drank from the moment they woke up, throughout meals and work during the day, and during evening social gatherings. Yet the way people drank mattered just as much as what they drank. This exhibit examines the decorative objects—from imported Madeira wine served at polished mahogany tables to locally made whisky poured from redware jugs—that shaped and revealed the identity of the early Southern drinker.

CuratorialText: this exhibit in MESDA’s Whitehall Gallery brings together rare maps from the 18th and early 19th centuries to explore how maps were made, used, and did work in the early American South. Rivers of Ink, Mountains of Type: Maps at Work in the Early American South includes maps from the MESDA and Old Salem collections as well as major loans from private collections, Salem Congregation, and the Moravian Archives of the Southern Province. The exhibit includes two early surveys of the Wachovia Tract made by Andreas Höger; a rare first-state copy of Joshua Fry and Peter Jefferson’s map of Virginia; two copies of Lewis Evans’s General Map of the Middle British Colonies produced at Ben Franklin’s press in Philadelphia; and surveys by George Washington of lands he owned along the Ohio River.
persona3_adapted: This exhibit in MESDA’s Whitehall Gallery brings together rare maps from the 1700s and early 1800s to explore how maps were made, used, and actively shaped life in the early American South. *Rivers of Ink, Mountains of Type: Maps at Work in the Early American South* includes maps from the MESDA and Old Salem collections, as well as major loans from private collections, Salem Congregation, and the Moravian Archives of the Southern Province. Among the highlights are two early surveys of the Wachovia Tract by Andreas Höger; a rare first printing of Joshua Fry and Peter Jefferson’s map of Virginia; two copies of Lewis Evans’s *General Map of the Middle British Colonies*, produced at Benjamin Franklin’s press in Philadelphia; and surveys by George Washington of lands he owned along the Ohio River.

CuratorialText: Alabama’s pottery traditions reflect the distinctive combination of people “thrown together” across its landscape. This exhibition explores the human hands that made, held, and preserved these objects. Across the present-day state of Alabama, Indigenous people including the Muscogee (Creek), Choctaw, Cherokee, and Chickasaw, practiced ceramic traditions brought by their ancestors into the region over 10,000 years ago. Colonial and American settlers were both a threat and a market for these craftspeople, who produced hybrid wares that meshed traditional techniques and designs that appealed to these foreign consumers. The geography of Alabama influenced how and where diverse potters and people came together. A tail of the Appalachian Mountains runs from south to north across the middle of the state. Most of the 19th century potters north of the mountains came from Tennessee, Virginia, and the Ohio River Valley. South of the mountains, the majority arrived from Georgia or South Carolina. The Gulf Coast was the site of early interactions between French, Spanish and British colonists and the Indigenous people of Alabama. Whether they came from the north, east, or across the Atlantic, immigrants brought with them enslaved people of African descent, including skilled ceramists. Together, their hands shaped the clay of the American South.
persona3_adapted: Alabama’s pottery traditions reflect the unique mix of people who were “thrown together” across its landscape. This exhibition explores the human hands that made, held, and preserved these objects. Across what is now the state of Alabama, Indigenous peoples—including the Muscogee (Creek), Choctaw, Cherokee, and Chickasaw—practiced pottery-making traditions brought by their ancestors into the region more than 10,000 years ago. Colonial and American settlers were both a threat and a source of business for these craftspeople, who created hybrid wares that blended traditional techniques and designs with those that appealed to these foreign buyers. Alabama’s geography shaped how and where different potters and communities came together. A spur of the Appalachian Mountains runs from south to north through the middle of the state. Most 19th-century potters north of these mountains came from Tennessee, Virginia, and the Ohio River Valley. South of the mountains, the majority arrived from Georgia or South Carolina. The Gulf Coast was an early meeting point for French, Spanish, and British colonists and the Indigenous peoples of Alabama. Whether they came from the north, east, or across the Atlantic, immigrants brought with them enslaved people of African descent, including skilled potters. Together, their hands shaped the clay of the American South.

CuratorialText:
The Carolyn and Mike McNamara Gallery at the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts (MESDA) exhibits some of the furniture, ceramics, paintings, prints, metalwork, and textiles from the early American South.  From fine art to folk art, these objects tell the stories of the diverse craftsmen and consumers who called the South home for more than four centuries.

The early South is usually divided into three major cultural regions: the Chesapeake, the Lowcountry, and the Backcountry.  The rivers, roads, and natural resources of these regions defined their economic and settlement patterns.  Each was home to a unique blend of ethnicities and religions: Native Americans, British Anglicans and Quakers, Scotch-Irish Presbyterians, French Huguenots, Moravians, German Lutherans and Reformed, and free and enslaved peoples of African descent.  The dynamic interactions between and among these distinct groups flavored the decorative arts of the early South.
persona3_adapted:
The Carolyn and Mike McNamara Gallery at the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts (MESDA) showcases furniture, ceramics, paintings, prints, metalwork, and textiles from the early American South. Ranging from refined, high-quality pieces to more rustic, everyday creations, these objects reveal the stories of the diverse makers and buyers who lived in the South for over four hundred years.

The early South is typically divided into three major cultural areas: the Chesapeake, the Lowcountry, and the Backcountry. The rivers, roads, and natural resources in each of these regions shaped how people settled and built their economies. Each area also had its own unique mix of ethnic and religious groups: Native Americans; British Anglicans and Quakers; Scotch-Irish Presbyterians; French Huguenots; Moravians; German Lutherans and Reformed Christians; and free and enslaved people of African descent. The lively exchanges and interactions among these different communities gave the decorative arts of the early South their distinctive character.

CuratorialText:
The William C. and Susan S. Mariner Southern Ceramics Gallery is home to the most comprehensive collection of antebellum pottery made in Maryland, Virginia, the Carolinas, Georgia, Kentucky, and Tennessee.

Among the decorative arts, pottery is the one media that is most intimately connected to place.  The southern pottery in this gallery reflects the interplay between maker, consumer, culture, and geography.  It is a tangible reflection of how potters responded to specific cultural needs to craft vessels out of local materials that were both useful and beautiful.

This virtual gallery offers two ways to explore the southern ceramics on exhibit in the Mariner Gallery.  The first is through a series of virtual galleries curated by the noted ceramics scholar Robert Hunter, Editor of Ceramics in America that explore important themes—tradition, design, expression, purpose, faith, war, and freedom—as seen through the lens of southern pottery.  You can also browse the objects in the Mariner Gallery by media: earthenware, salt-glazed stoneware, and alkaline glazed stoneware.  Within these pages you are also able to filter the objects by maker, place, and time
persona3_adapted:
The William C. and Susan S. Mariner Southern Ceramics Gallery holds the largest collection of pottery made in the American South before the Civil War—specifically from Maryland, Virginia, the Carolinas, Georgia, Kentucky, and Tennessee.

Among decorative arts—objects made to be both useful and beautiful—pottery is the type most closely tied to a specific place. The southern pottery in this gallery shows how makers, buyers, local culture, and geography all influenced one another. Each piece is a physical record of how potters met particular community needs, shaping local materials into vessels that served a purpose and also pleased the eye.

This virtual gallery offers two ways to explore the southern ceramics on display in the Mariner Gallery. First, you can view a series of virtual galleries curated by Robert Hunter, a well-known ceramics scholar and editor of *Ceramics in America*. These galleries explore important themes—tradition, design, expression, purpose, faith, war, and freedom—as seen through southern pottery. Alternatively, you can browse the objects by type of material: earthenware (pottery fired at lower temperatures), salt-glazed stoneware (stoneware with a textured, glass-like coating created by throwing salt into the kiln), and alkaline-glazed stoneware (stoneware with a glossy finish made using wood ash or other alkaline materials). Within these pages, you can also filter objects by maker, location, and time period.

CuratorialText: The Thomas A. Gray Rare Book and Manuscript Collection is home to rare and important works about the early American South. The collection represents a lifetime of connoisseurship for Tom Gray, a former director of the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts and cousin of MESDA founder Frank L. Horton. What began as an collection focused on Tom’s home state of North Carolina gradually expanded to include important texts from all seven of MESDA’s southern states. With books and manuscripts spanning four centuries – from 1585 to 1865 – the Gray collection is a perfect complement to the MESDA and Old Salem collections.  Books and manuscripts from the collection are exhibited in the MESDA galleries and are available to researchers.  A selection of highlights are shown below.
persona3_adapted: The Thomas A. Gray Rare Book and Manuscript Collection holds rare and historically significant works about the early American South. This collection reflects a lifetime of careful, expert selection by Tom Gray, a former director of the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts and a cousin of the museum’s founder, Frank L. Horton. What began as a collection focused on Tom’s home state of North Carolina gradually grew to include important texts from all seven southern states represented by the museum. With books and manuscripts spanning four centuries—from 1585 to 1865—the Gray collection perfectly complements the museum’s and Old Salem’s holdings. Items from the collection are displayed in the museum’s galleries and are also available for researchers. A selection of highlights is shown below.

CuratorialText: MESDA’s collection includes architectural woodwork collected from houses in Maryland, Virginia, North Carolina, South Carolina, and Georgia.  This collection was originally assembled by Frank L. Horton to serve as backdrops for period room installations.  Today, in addition to providing scale and context for our study galleries, MESDA’s architectural collections are also studied as important Southern objects in their own right that that can tell stories of style, craftsmanship, migration, ethnicity, and race.
persona3_adapted: MESDA’s collection includes architectural woodwork—such as carved moldings, doorways, and paneling—taken from houses in Maryland, Virginia, North Carolina, South Carolina, and Georgia. This collection was originally put together by Frank L. Horton to serve as the settings for rooms arranged to look like historical interiors. Today, in addition to providing a sense of size and context for our study galleries, MESDA’s architectural pieces are also studied as important Southern objects in their own right. They can reveal stories about changing styles, the skills of craftspeople, patterns of migration, and the roles of ethnicity and race.

CuratorialText: Old Salem Museums & Gardens and its Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts are home to one of country’s most important collections of early Southern longrifles, swords, and related objects.  Longrifles were some of the most complicated objects crafted in the early American South.  Artistic masterworks, they required craftsmen skilled in woodworking, silversmithing, ironworking, and the mechanical arts.  The rich tradition of early Southern rifle making is kept alive today by the talented craftsmen of the Timothy Vogler Gun Shop, located directly across the street from the Frank L. Horton Museum Center at Old Salem Museums & Gardens.
persona3_adapted: Old Salem Museums & Gardens, along with its Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts, holds one of the nation’s most significant collections of early Southern longrifles, swords, and related items. Longrifles were among the most complex objects ever made in the early American South. These artistic masterpieces required craftsmen skilled in woodworking, silversmithing, ironworking, and mechanical design. The rich tradition of early Southern rifle making continues today through the talented artisans at the Timothy Vogler Gun Shop, located directly across the street from the Frank L. Horton Museum Center at Old Salem Museums & Gardens.

CuratorialText: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts and Old Salem Museums & Gardens is home to an exceptional collection of Southern bedcoverings including quilts and coverlets.  The collection also has a significant number of textiles made and used by the women of Salem, North Carolina.
persona3_adapted: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts and Old Salem Museums & Gardens holds an outstanding collection of Southern bedcoverings—such as quilts and woven coverlets—that were both made and used in the region. The collection is especially notable for its many textiles created and used by the women of Salem, North Carolina.

CuratorialText: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts at Old Salem Museums & Gardens is home to an extensive collection of rare books and manuscripts prints that document the people and places of the early American south.  Many of these books and manuscripts are part of the Thomas A. Gray Rare Book and Manuscript Collection.
persona3_adapted: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts, located within Old Salem Museums & Gardens, holds a large collection of rare books, handwritten documents, and printed materials that record the lives and locations of people in the early American South. Many of these items belong to the Thomas A. Gray Rare Book and Manuscript Collection.

CuratorialText: MESDA is home to an extensive collection of southern brass, copper, and pewter.
persona3_adapted: MESDA houses a large collection of metal objects made in the South, including items crafted from brass, copper, and pewter.

CuratorialText: Old Salem Museums & Gardens, its Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts, and The William C. and Susan S. Mariner Southern Ceramics Collection are home to the most comprehensive collection of Southern ceramics in existence.
persona3_adapted: Old Salem Museums & Gardens, along with its Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts and The William C. and Susan S. Mariner Southern Ceramics Collection, holds the largest and most complete collection of pottery and porcelain made in the American South that exists anywhere.

CuratorialText: MESDA is home to a collection of clocks, surveyors compasses, and sundials that span all seven of our constituent states.
persona3_adapted: MESDA houses a collection of clocks, surveyors' compasses, and sundials—instruments used for measuring time and direction—that come from all seven states represented in our collection.

CuratorialText: German speaking communities throughout the South created richly illuminated documents celebrating births, baptisms, marriages, and deaths.
persona3_adapted: German-speaking communities across the American South created beautifully decorated documents to celebrate life events such as births, baptisms, marriages, and deaths.

CuratorialText: For over forty years the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts has collected many of the finest examples of southern-made furniture.  These objects demonstrate how the early South evolved, inform us about the diverse peoples who came here, and illuminate the artisans who were integral to the process. Whether they were English, Scottish, Irish, German, African, or French, Southern cabinetmakers utilized their materials at hand, both native and imported, to create furniture that speaks to us about their heterogeneous backgrounds and the multifaceted aspects of their everyday lives. These craftsmen contributed to the dynamic amalgamation of art, culture, and history that forms the basis for MESDA’s ongoing study of the early South.
persona3_adapted: For over forty years, the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts has gathered many of the finest examples of furniture made in the American South. These objects show how the early South developed, tell us about the many different groups of people who came to the region, and highlight the skilled workers who were essential to that process. Whether they were English, Scottish, Irish, German, African, or French, Southern furniture makers used the materials available to them—both local and brought from elsewhere—to create pieces that reveal their diverse backgrounds and the many sides of their daily lives. These craftspeople contributed to a dynamic blend of art, culture, and history that forms the foundation for MESDA’s ongoing study of the early South.

CuratorialText: MESDA’s collection of iron represents the work of furnaces and blacksmiths across all seven southern states.
persona3_adapted: MESDA’s collection of iron objects showcases the work of furnaces and blacksmiths from all seven southern states.

CuratorialText: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts at Old Salem Museums & Gardens is home to one of the most important collections of southern of maps.  On the surface maps appear scientific and authoritative, useful devices for governmental officials and colonial expansionists to claim ownership of land and exert power and authority over competing nationalities.  Below the surface, however, maps often provide tremendous insights into a myriad of social, political, and economic forces that shaped Southern culture.
persona3_adapted: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts at Old Salem Museums & Gardens holds one of the most significant collections of southern maps. At first glance, maps seem like objective, factual tools—useful for government officials and colonial expansionists to claim land and assert power over rival nations. But beneath that surface, maps often reveal deep insights into the many social, political, and economic forces that helped shape Southern culture.

CuratorialText: Music is an important part of Southern culture.  In Salem, North Carolina, music was intrgal to Moravian worship and daily life.  Salem had its own orchestra by the 1780s and brass bands played regularly as part of religious services and to announce births and deaths in the community.  Old Salem Museums & Gardens collection of musical instruments and related materials reflects the important role played by music in the community.  Other objects in the collection reflect musical craftsmanship elsewhere in the American South.
persona3_adapted: Music has always been a vital part of life in the American South. In Salem, North Carolina, it was woven into both religious practice and everyday routines for the Moravian community. By the 1780s, Salem had its own orchestra, and brass bands performed regularly—not only during worship services but also to mark major life events, such as births and deaths. The collection at Old Salem Museums & Gardens includes musical instruments and related items that show just how central music was to this community. Other objects in the collection also highlight musical craftsmanship from across the broader American South.

CuratorialText: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts has led the way in researching and collecting work by Southern artisans, allowing us to better understand the rich cultural complexity of artistic production in the South. While it is true that with only a handful of significant urban centers, fewer painters settled and remained in the South, the painting collection at MESDA ably demonstrates the diversity of training and styles with works by European-trained and native-born artists living in urban and rural settings
persona3_adapted: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts has been a pioneer in studying and collecting the work of Southern craftspeople, helping us better understand the rich cultural mix that shaped art-making in the South. It's true that, because the South had only a few major cities, fewer painters chose to settle there permanently. However, MESDA's painting collection clearly shows a wide range of artistic backgrounds and styles—featuring works by both European-trained artists and those born in America, living in cities as well as rural areas.

CuratorialText: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts at Old Salem Museums & Gardens is home to an extensive collection of prints that document the people and places of the early American south.  These views documented English perceptions of America and served as vehicles to convey a host of attitudes and values and provide tremendous insights into a myriad of social, political, and economic forces that shaped Southern culture.
persona3_adapted: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts at Old Salem Museums & Gardens holds a large collection of prints that record the people and places of the early American South. These images captured how the English saw America and were used to express a range of beliefs and values. As a result, they offer deep insights into the many social, political, and economic forces that helped shape Southern culture.

CuratorialText: Pastel portrait of Frances L’Escott. Half-length pastel portrait of young woman with body turned slightly to the right and head slightly to the left. She has auburn hair, brown eyes, with loosely fitted ecru and blue gown. The original wood frame retains its original spruce back with the following inscriptions: “Henrietta Johnston Fecit/ South Carolina Ano 1716.” “I/ Frances Lescott/ Married Peter Villep—.”
persona3_adapted: Portrait of Frances L’Escott, created in pastel—a medium made from powdered pigment pressed into a stick, similar to chalk. The half-length portrait shows a young woman with her body turned slightly to the right and her head angled a little to the left. She has auburn hair and brown eyes, and wears a loosely fitted gown in ecru (a pale, beige-like color) and blue. The original wooden frame still has its original spruce back, which bears two inscriptions. One reads: “Henrietta Johnston Fecit / South Carolina Ano 1716,” identifying the artist and the year and place of creation. The other reads: “I / Frances Lescott / Married Peter Villep—,” which likely records her name and marriage.

CuratorialText: Portrait of man, bust length, seated in a red upholstered chair, body half-left, eyes and head forward, brown hair, blue-gray eyes, ruddy complexion, wearing a black coat, yellow vest and white stock and tie, brass buttons. The background is a greenish-gray.
persona3_adapted: This man sits in a red upholstered chair, his body turned slightly to the left while his head and eyes face directly toward us. He has brown hair, blue-gray eyes, and a ruddy complexion. He wears a black coat over a yellow vest, with a white stock and tie fastened by brass buttons. Behind him, the background is a muted greenish-gray.

CuratorialText: Portrait of man in brown coat and yellow vest with white stock. He has blue-gray eyes and dark hair. He is a half-length figure, facing half left, posed with hand in his vest. He stands in front of a column and what must be a balustraded porch with green trees and a greenish ground in the distance.
persona3_adapted: A man is shown from the chest up, his body angled slightly to the left, with one hand tucked into his vest. He wears a brown coat, a yellow vest, and a white neckcloth. His eyes are blue-gray, and his hair is dark. Behind him, a column and what appears to be a porch with a railing frame the scene, while in the distance, green trees and a patch of green ground are visible.

CuratorialText: Portrait of a woman seated in a Chippendale type chair, half-length, facing forward and dressed with a white high crowned cap, gray dress and white neck piece with a black ribbon and jewel at her throat. She has brown eyes, dark hair. She holds a reddish-brown book in her right hand and is seated at a column base to the right, with red drapery and tassel, and with a balustraded rail and trees in the background to the left.
persona3_adapted: This portrait shows a woman seated in a chair typical of the Chippendale style—an elegant, carved wooden design popular in the 1700s. She is shown from the waist up, facing the viewer directly. She wears a tall white cap, a gray dress, and a white neckpiece accented with a black ribbon and a jewel at her throat. Her eyes are brown, her hair dark. In her right hand, she holds a reddish-brown book. She sits beside the base of a column on the right side of the image, while red drapery with a tassel hangs nearby. On the left side, a railing and trees appear in the background.
