CuratorialText: Full length portrait of a man in blue uniform with red trim at lapel and cuffs, white vest and breeches with black boots trimmed with gold at the top, white epaulets and neck piece, and black tircorner hat decorated with silver cord. He is posed with sword in right upraised hand and a dagger-like knife in the left, facing half-left, with a tree at either side to frame a fight between American troops and Indians in the background. The soldiers in the back wear round hats, short blue jackets with red facings, belly boxes, and linen overalls. The retreating Indians to the left rear of the painting appear with topknots or scalp locks, and wear battle dress of breechcloths and leggings.
persona3_adapted: A full-length portrait of a man in a blue uniform with red trim on the lapel and cuffs, a white vest and breeches, black boots trimmed with gold at the top, white shoulder ornaments (epaulets) and neck piece, and a black three-cornered hat decorated with silver cord. He is posed with a sword raised in his right hand and a dagger-like knife in his left, facing slightly to the left. Trees on either side frame a battle scene in the background, where American troops fight against Native Americans. The soldiers in the back wear round hats, short blue jackets with red trim, cartridge boxes worn on the belt (belly boxes), and linen overalls. The retreating Native Americans, shown to the left rear of the painting, have topknots or scalp locks and wear battle dress consisting of loincloths (breechcloths) and leggings.

CuratorialText: Charcoal drawing of the profile of Joseph Lewis, Jr., facing left on pink paper. The drawing shows a gentleman with light hair tied at the back with dark ribbon who is wearing a dark high coat with a white stock. The painting is mounted in the original gold frame with black and gold painted glass.
persona3_adapted: Charcoal drawing on pink paper of Joseph Lewis, Jr., shown in profile facing left. The subject is a gentleman with light hair tied back with a dark ribbon, wearing a dark high-collared coat and a white neckcloth. The piece is displayed in its original gold frame, which includes painted glass in black and gold.

CuratorialText: Watercolor on paper of a plantation with an inscription at the base of the painting. The house is a white frame two-story central hall structure with its chimneys on the rear elevation. A fence runs in front of the house with a bridge to the right in the background, indicating an inlet of water. An outbuilding stands behind some trees standing behind the bridge. Several large trees are located in the left foreground, along with some moss, grass and other foliage. A path leads to the house, and two men, one mounted upon a horse, the other standing below him are talking to one another. A body of water is in the background.
persona3_adapted: This watercolor painting on paper depicts a plantation estate. At the bottom of the artwork, there is a brief written note. The main house is a white, two-story wooden building with a central hallway and chimneys located at the back. A fence runs across the front of the property, and in the background to the right, a bridge suggests the presence of a narrow waterway, like an inlet. Behind some trees near the bridge, a smaller outbuilding is visible. In the lower left corner of the scene, several large trees stand alongside patches of moss, grass, and other plants. A path leads up to the house, where two men are shown talking: one is seated on a horse, while the other stands beside him. In the distance, a body of water completes the landscape.

CuratorialText: Charcoal drawing, profile of man, facing right, on faded pink paper. The gentleman wears the uniform of an officer, a dark coat with light facings with buttons at the outside of long buttonholes on the lapel and collar, and epaulets of gold bullion. A black inner collar is over a white stock. The man’s hair is graying and is tied in a knot at the back with a black ribbon.
persona3_adapted: A charcoal drawing on faded pink paper shows a man in profile, facing to the right. He is dressed in an officer’s uniform: a dark coat with lighter trim, where buttons sit along the outer edge of long buttonholes on the lapel and collar. His shoulders are adorned with gold-braided ornaments known as epaulets. A black inner collar rises above a white neckcloth. The man’s graying hair is pulled back and tied in a knot with a black ribbon.

CuratorialText: Oil on canvas portrait of a gentleman with dark eyes, dark complexion and a shaven head, who is facing half-right with hands folded resting on a table . His right hand holds a quill pen and the left is holding a document with a name written thereon. He wears a white loose collar shirt with deep red robe and turban of the same red material, and sits in a chair upholstered in a red-brown fabric with brass tacks. The background is a dark green drapery to the left and books on the right.
persona3_adapted: Oil on canvas portrait of a gentleman with dark eyes, dark complexion, and a shaven head. He faces slightly to the right, his hands folded and resting on a table. In his right hand, he holds a quill pen; his left hand rests on a document with a name written on it. He wears a loose white collar shirt, a deep red robe, and a turban made of the same red fabric. He sits in a chair upholstered in a red-brown material, studded with brass tacks. The background features dark green drapery on the left and a stack of books on the right.

CuratorialText: Bust portrait, oil on canvas, of woman wearing a pearl necklace and blue dress with lace trim, in mahogany architectural frame with cut-out corners.
persona3_adapted: A painted portrait, created with oil on canvas, shows a woman from the chest up. She wears a pearl necklace and a blue dress trimmed with lace. The painting is set in a dark reddish-brown wooden frame designed with cut-out corners, giving it an architectural look.

CuratorialText: Painting, oil on canvas, of Richard Sprigg of Strawberry Hill, Maryland. Young man facing forward, his body is slightly to the right, his left hand in his vest, wearing a dark beige coat and light silk vest, with white stock. He has a ruddy complexion, dark eyes, brown hair. The background varies from greenish-gray to the lower right to very dark gray in upper left and has lunettes in the corners.
persona3_adapted: This is a portrait of Richard Sprigg, who lived at Strawberry Hill in Maryland. Painted in oil on canvas, it shows a young man facing forward, with his body turned slightly to the right. His left hand is tucked into his vest, and he wears a dark beige coat, a light silk vest, and a white neckcloth. He has a ruddy complexion, dark eyes, and brown hair. The background shifts from a greenish-gray in the lower right to a very dark gray in the upper left, and the corners of the painting are shaped like half-moons, known as lunettes.

CuratorialText: Oil on canvas portrait of a woman facing half-right. The subject has dark hair combed upward to expose her left ear, and pearls in her hair that hang down over her right shoulder. She wears a blue silk cross-bodice dress tied with a dark red sash. The pinned-up sleeves of the garment revealing a light under-garment. She is posed with her left arm upon a table, the right at her hip, and foliage painted in the background.
persona3_adapted: Oil on canvas portrait of a woman turned slightly to the right. Her dark hair is swept upward, revealing her left ear, and pearls woven into her hair cascade over her right shoulder. She wears a blue silk dress with a cross-shaped bodice, fastened with a dark red sash. The dress's sleeves are pinned up, exposing a lighter fabric underneath. She rests her left arm on a table, places her right hand on her hip, and foliage fills the background.

CuratorialText: A watercolor “en grisaille” of Williamsville, a home in Hanover County, Virginia. The house was built between 1794 and 1803 for William Pollard, II, the second Clerk of Court of Hanover County. It passed to his son George William Pollard in 1840 and remained in the Pollard family until after the death of Harry Todd Pollard, the amateur artist who drew and signed the watercolor.
persona3_adapted: A watercolor painting in shades of gray, known as "grisaille," depicts Williamsville, a home in Hanover County, Virginia. The house was built between 1794 and 1803 for William Pollard II, who served as the second Clerk of Court for Hanover County. In 1840, the property passed to his son, George William Pollard, and stayed in the Pollard family until after the death of Harry Todd Pollard—the amateur artist who drew and signed this watercolor.

CuratorialText: Painting, done in oil on mahogany panel, of a young man facing half-right. The gentleman has dark hair and side-burns, and is dressed in a white shirt, tie and vest, and a black coat supporting a high collar. There is an inscription on the back of the panel.
persona3_adapted: This portrait, painted in oil on a mahogany panel, shows a young man facing slightly to the right. He has dark hair and sideburns, and wears a white shirt, tie, and vest, along with a black coat with a high collar. An inscription is written on the back of the panel.

CuratorialText: Oil on canvas painting of a man facing half-left. The work is a half-length portrait. The subject has gray hair, dark gray eyes, wears a dark brown coat with a high turned-down collar, a yellow vest, and white stock at the neck.
persona3_adapted: A painting in oil on canvas shows a man turned slightly to the left, captured from the chest up. He has gray hair and dark gray eyes, and wears a dark brown coat with a high, turned-down collar, a yellow vest, and a white neckcloth.

CuratorialText: This painting depicts a bend in the Cumberland River near the Hermitage, the home of Andrew Jackson (1767-1845). It was a scene that the artist returned to regularly. Another version of this composition is in the collection of the Greenville County Museum of Art.
persona3_adapted: This painting shows a curve in the Cumberland River near the Hermitage, the home of Andrew Jackson (1767-1845). It was a view the artist revisited many times. A similar version of this scene is held in the collection of the Greenville County Museum of Art.

CuratorialText: Painting, oil on canvas, of Charles Town showing the city waterfront as seen from across the Cooper River. A large sailing ship is in the left foreground, the opposite shore in foreground with various figures along the shore.
persona3_adapted: A painting in oil on canvas depicts the waterfront of Charles Town as seen from across the Cooper River. In the left foreground, a large sailing ship is prominent, while the opposite shore appears in the foreground with several figures scattered along the water's edge.

CuratorialText: Painting, oil on canvas, waist-length portrait of a lady wearing a gray satin dress with gray stomacher in front and white lace at neck and sleeves, her hair is pulled tightly back, a loop of pearls in it on the left side of the portrait. Her right hand holds a pink rose.
persona3_adapted: This is a waist-length portrait of a woman, painted in oil on canvas. She wears a gray satin dress, with a matching panel—called a stomacher—at the front, and white lace trim at her neck and sleeves. Her hair is pulled back tightly, and on the left side of the painting, a loop of pearls is woven into it. In her right hand, she holds a pink rose.

CuratorialText: Painting, oil on canvas, of a man facing half-left and seated in a green painted chair. The man has a high foreheard, dark hair, dark blue eyes, ruddy complexion, and is wearing a dark coat mounted with gray buttons, a white stock, and a yellow vest. A red and pearl pin is on his neck cloth. The painting is signed in the lower left corner.
persona3_adapted: A man is seated in a green painted chair, turned slightly to the left, in this oil painting on canvas. He has a high forehead, dark hair, dark blue eyes, and a ruddy complexion. He wears a dark coat with gray buttons, a white stock (a type of neckcloth popular at the time), and a yellow vest. A red and pearl pin adorns his neckcloth. The artist’s signature appears in the lower left corner.

CuratorialText: Painting, oil on canvas, of lady sitting half right, face to front. She wears a high dark brown hair style, has brown eyes, and wears a dark high gathered dress with puff short sleeves, a loose arrangement of net at a square cut neck line, a red paisley shawl over her left hand and a red cloth at her right hand in the lower left corner. The painting is signed in the lower left hand corner.
persona3_adapted: This portrait, painted in oil on canvas, shows a woman seated at a slight angle to the right, though she faces the viewer directly. Her dark brown hair is styled high on her head, and her brown eyes meet ours. She wears a dark, high-necked dress with short, puffed sleeves and a square neckline, where a loose net-like fabric is gathered. A red paisley shawl drapes over her left hand, while a red cloth rests near her right hand in the lower left corner of the composition. The artist’s signature appears in the same lower left area.

CuratorialText: Pastel portrait on paper of a young man. He sits in profile to the left, and he has light brown hair, a black coat, a white shirt and stock. The work was done on white paper. The side profile view of this portrait seems inspired by the work of British-born artist James Sharples (d. 1811), instead of the three-quarter views preferred by his son Felix.
persona3_adapted: This pastel portrait on paper shows a young man seated in profile, facing left. He has light brown hair and wears a black coat, a white shirt, and a stock—a type of neckcloth common in the period. The artist worked on white paper. The choice of a side profile view appears to be influenced by the style of British-born artist James Sharples (who died in 1811), rather than the three-quarter view—where the subject’s face is turned slightly toward the viewer—that was favored by his son, Felix.

CuratorialText: A bust lenth portrait in crayon/ pastel on grey-pink paper of a young woman in a three-quarter pose to the left. She has brown eyes, brown hair in a bun held high on her head with a black comb, and wears a high organdy collar and white blouse. The portrait is marked on the back with the inscription “Miss Lucy Ann Bou….” and “H.E. H.”.
persona3_adapted: A bust-length portrait, drawn in crayon or pastel on grey-pink paper, shows a young woman facing slightly to the left. She has brown eyes and brown hair pinned high on her head in a bun, held in place with a black comb. She wears a high, stiff collar made of organdy—a fine, sheer cotton fabric—and a white blouse. On the back of the portrait, two inscriptions are written: “Miss Lucy Ann Bou….” and “H.E. H.”

CuratorialText: Pastel on white paper, of young woman, three-quarter pose to the right. She has brown hair, brown eyes, and wears a pink dress of a low cut design. It is marked on the back with the sitter’s name, “Miss Susan Whiting.” In a lower corner, upside down to the sitter’s name is another inscription which notes that the portrait was done at “North End” on “November 21, 1814.” North End was home of artist Hester Eliza Henrietta (Van Bibber) Tabb, whose initials “H.E.H.V.B.” are also inscribed on the verso.
persona3_adapted: A pastel portrait on white paper shows a young woman in a three-quarter pose, meaning her body is turned slightly to the right while her face may face the viewer more directly. She has brown hair and brown eyes, and wears a pink dress with a low neckline. On the back of the artwork, the sitter’s name is written: “Miss Susan Whiting.” In one corner, upside down relative to that name, another inscription notes that the portrait was created at “North End” on “November 21, 1814.” North End was the home of the artist, Hester Eliza Henrietta (Van Bibber) Tabb, whose initials “H.E.H.V.B.” are also inscribed on the back.
